sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/AADSTS Errors/AADSTS65004

symptom: AADSTS75011: Authentication method mismatch during SAML SSO. User authenticates with X509/MultiFactor but app requests Password/ProtectedTransport in RequestedAuthnContext.
rootCause: The SAML application includes RequestedAuthnContext specifying a specific auth method (e.g. Password). User authenticates with a different method (e.g. certificate/MFA). With Comparison=exact, methods must match exactly.
solution: Contact app vendor to: 1) Remove RequestedAuthnContext from SAML request (optional element), OR 2) Set to Unspecified. Workaround: user signs out and signs in with the method requested by the app. Long-term: use Conditional Access instead of RequestedAuthnContext.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/AADSTS Errors/AADSTS75011

symptom: AADSTS75011 when using Passkeys (FIDO2/Authenticator passkey) to access SAML app. Auth method MultiFactor/Fido does not match Password. SAML request has multiple AuthnContextClassRef including Password.
rootCause: When multiple AuthnContextClassRef values present in RequestedAuthnContext, Entra ID prioritizes Password if present regardless of order. FIDO2 produces MultiFactor/Fido which does not match Password with Comparison=exact. By design.
solution: Recommended: Remove RequestedAuthnContext entirely from SAML request. Alternative: Remove Password from list and use only Unspecified. This allows stronger auth methods like passkeys. Verify: 1) Check AADSTS75011, 2) Decode SAML request, 3) Confirm Password present, 4) Check sign-in logs for MultiFactor/Fido.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/AADSTS Errors/AADSTS75011:

symptom: AADSTS50199: CmsiInterrupt - For security reasons, user confirmation is required for this request. A dialog appears asking 'Are you trying to sign in to {appName}' when user is already authenticated and has consented, using OAuth 2.0 authorization code through system browser.
rootCause: Application uses system browser with an unapproved redirect URI scheme (e.g., custom schemes like myapp://, oauthredirect://). Only http://, https://, msauth:// (iOS), msauthv2:// (iOS), com.apple.preferences:// (iOS/macOS), and chrome-extension:// (desktop Chromium) are considered safe. Unsafe schemes trigger CMSI confirmation dialog. On mobile, localhost URLs for public clients also trigger CMSI even with safe schemes.
solution: Use approved redirect URI schemes. For MAUI/Xamarin apps, use .WithUseEmbeddedWebView(true) to force embedded webview (bypasses CMSI). For iOS, use Microsoft Authenticator as broker (only solution for iOS). For Android, use a supported browser. Check ReplyAddressHash (SHA256) in ASC Diagnostics to verify the redirect URI sent to eSTS.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/AADSTS Errors/AADSTS50199

symptom: AADSTS50214: This web native bridge call resulted in an error from the operating system without external error data. Also AADSTS50207 in ASC Diagnostics: web native bridge interrupt shown during login when app requests login through native broker.
rootCause: The app MSAL.js integration uses allowNativeBroker flag, which enforces login requests through the native broker (Web Account Manager / WAM) for MFA. MSAL.js acquires tokens from WAM on Windows which are device-bound and not cached in browser storage.
solution: For custom apps: set system.allowNativeBroker to false in MSAL config. Other options: (1) Update WAM and Azure AD Broker Plugin to latest, install latest Microsoft Authenticator on mobile. (2) Clear cached tokens, sign out all Microsoft accounts, clear cookies, restart browser. (3) Verify device meets CA compliance. (4) Try InPrivate/Incognito to bypass cached broker tokens.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/AADSTS Errors/AADSTS50214

symptom: AADSTS530004: AcceptCompliantDevice setting is not configured for this organization. External (guest) users blocked from accessing protected resources that require device compliance via Conditional Access.
rootCause: Three causes: (1) Resource tenant inbound XTAP does not trust compliant device claims from external user home org while CA requires device compliance. (2) External user token not from Intune-joined device in home tenant (only Intune-joined devices send IsCompliantDeviceAccepted:true claim). (3) Legacy/classic CA policy with RequiredDeviceState=Known or resource app legacy auth policy with same config.
solution: For XTAP: enable Trust compliant device in resource tenant inbound cross-tenant access settings. Ensure guest device is Intune-joined in home tenant, user signs in with PRT (not incognito). For legacy CA: migrate classic policies to modern CA. For legacy auth policies: app owner should migrate to modern CA. Use ASC Auth Troubleshooter Expert View (XTAP + CA Diagnostic tabs) for diagnosis.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/AADSTS Errors/AADSTS530004

symptom: AADSTS53003: Access has been blocked by Conditional Access policies. The access policy does not allow token issuance. The capolids claim in error response contains the ObjectId of the blocking CA policy.
rootCause: A Conditional Access policy blocked authentication. Common in non-interactive (daemon/background) flows where the user/service account does not meet CA requirements (MFA, device compliance, location restrictions).
solution: For non-interactive flows: (1) Add service account as exception to the CA policy. (2) Switch to client credentials flow (app-only token) - note each resource may have additional requirements. (3) Implement interactive sign-in capability. For interactive flows: ensure app handles this error and prompts for interactive sign-in. Use capolids value from error claims to identify blocking CA policy.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/AADSTS Errors/AADSTS53003

symptom: AADSTS54005: OAuth2 Authorization code was already redeemed. Error invalid_grant when application attempts to reuse an authorization code during OAuth code flow to get tokens for multiple resources.
rootCause: Application reuses authorization codes for multiple resources (codes are single-use). Common in ASP.NET apps with: misconfigured distributed token cache (mixing AddDistributedTokenCaches with AddInMemoryTokenCaches), multi-instance deployments without ARR Affinity, or .NET 6/7 known bug with OpenIdConnectOptions race condition in Microsoft Identity Web.
solution: Use auth code to get a refresh token first, then use refresh token for additional resources. For ASP.NET Core: (1) Use Microsoft Identity Web. (2) Enable ARR Affinity for multi-instance deployments. (3) Configure distributed token cache correctly (use only one impl). (4) Upgrade from .NET 6/7 to .NET 8. For .NET 6/7: apply FixMicrosoftIdentityOptionsMonitorRaceCondition workaround. Install correct IIS Hosting Bundle for target .NET version.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/AADSTS Errors/AADSTS54005
Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/AADSTS Errors/AADSTS9002325
symptom: Applications integrated with Entra ID fail during sign-in using OAuth 2.0 Authorization Code flow with error: AADSTS9002325: Proof Key for Code Exchange is required for cross-origin authorization code redemption.
rootCause: The application is configured as a public client (SPA) with redirect URI under Single Page Application, but attempts to redeem the authorization code without using PKCE (Proof Key for Code Exchange).
solution: For SPA/public client: implement the Authorization Code flow with PKCE. Reference: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/entra/identity-platform/reference-third-party-cookies-spas

sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/AADSTS Errors/AADSTS9002325
symptom: Confidential client (Web App) using client secret for authorization code redemption gets error AADSTS9002325: Proof Key for Code Exchange is required for cross-origin authorization code redemption.
rootCause: The redirect URI is incorrectly configured under SPA section in the app registration instead of Web, causing Entra ID to treat the confidential client as a public client requiring PKCE.
solution: Remove the redirect URI from the SPA configuration and add it under Web in the application registration. This ensures Entra ID correctly treats the app as a confidential client, allowing authorization code redemption with client secret.

sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/ADFS and WAP/ADFS Based SSO for Windows Virtual Desktop
symptom: AVD SSO with ADFS fails with errors: MSIS9623 GlobalAuthenticationPolicy doesn't allow OAuth JWT Bearer request; or MSIS9265 client not configured to authenticate using client secret post. CertLogon/SecretLogon requests fail.
rootCause: ADFS SsoSecretType configuration does not match what is stored in Azure Key Vault. E.g., SsoSecretType is set to SharedKeyInKeyVault but Key Vault has a certificate, or vice versa.
solution: Set SsoSecretType on ADFS to match Key Vault content. For shared key: Update-AzWvdHostPool with -SsoSecretType SharedKeyInKeyVault. For certificate: Update-AzWvdHostPool with -SsoSecretType CertificateInKeyVault.

sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/ADFS and WAP/ADFS Based SSO for Windows Virtual Desktop
symptom: AVD SSO fails with error: Cannot find certificate or public key to validate message/token signature obtained from OAuth Client https://client.wvd.microsoft.com.
rootCause: An ADFS Client was not created with the identifier https://client.wvd.microsoft.com. As of April 18, 2022, WVD started automatically redirecting the Web Client to this URL, but the ADFS setup script did not include this new client identifier.
solution: Update the ADFS Client setup script to include the new client identifier https://client.wvd.microsoft.com and re-run to create the missing ADFS Client registration.

sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/ADFS and WAP/ADFS Based SSO for Windows Virtual Desktop
symptom: AVD SSO CertLogonRequest times out after ~30 seconds with TaskCanceledException / IOException. ADFS service consumes extremely high virtual memory (tens of GB). Resource-Exhaustion-Detector warnings in System log for Microsoft.IdentityServer.ServiceHost.exe.
rootCause: ADFS uses Certificate Enrollment API for user logon certificates. The API installs certificates into the ADFS service account personal certificate store, which grows unbounded. When the store accumulates thousands of certificates, enumeration becomes extremely slow, causing timeouts and memory exhaustion.
solution: Clean up accumulated certificates from the ADFS service account personal certificate store (under the service account profile AppData). Consider implementing periodic certificate store cleanup automation.

symptom: Cannot switch between federatedIdpMfaBehavior and SupportsMfa for domain federation. Setting SupportsMfa after federatedIdpMfaBehavior is set has no effect.
rootCause: federatedIdpMfaBehavior is the evolved version of SupportsMfa. Once federatedIdpMfaBehavior is set to any value, Azure AD permanently ignores SupportsMfa. Switching between the two is not supported by design.
solution: Use federatedIdpMfaBehavior exclusively via MS Graph internalDomainFederation API or Microsoft.Graph PowerShell (Update-MgDomainFederationConfiguration). Do not use Convert-MsolDomainToFederated with SupportsMfa after federatedIdpMfaBehavior has been set.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/ADFS and WAP/Manage Domain Federation Settings with MS Graph

symptom: Creating new Internal Domain Federation via API fails with error: FederatedIdpMfaBehavior cannot be empty.
rootCause: Microsoft blocks creation of new internal domain federation configurations when FederatedIdpMfaBehavior property is not specified.
solution: Include the federatedIdpMfaBehavior property in the POST request body. Choose one of: acceptIfMfaDoneByFederatedIdp, enforceMfaByFederatedIdp, or rejectMfaByFederatedIdp.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/ADFS and WAP/Manage Domain Federation Settings with MS Graph

symptom: SAML 2.0 federation with Entra ID: FederationMetadata and Signed SAML AuthnRequests are only signed with SHA1. Third-party IdP rejects the request due to weak signature algorithm.
rootCause: Entra ID only supports SHA1 for signing FederationMetadata and SAML AuthnRequests as of Oct 2024. SHA-256 is not yet configurable (platform limitation).
solution: No workaround - known limitation. Configure third-party IdP to accept SHA1 signatures. Feature request: Engineering work item 2799795. ICM: 555588341.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/Azure AD Authentication General/Azure AD : Use a SAML 2.0 Identity Provider (IdP) for Single Sign On

symptom: MFA claim requirements unclear for SAML 2.0 federated IdP. Third-party SAML IdP sends MFA claim but Entra ID does not recognize it, or MFA still required despite IdP performing MFA.
rootCause: For SAML 2.0 federation, the expected MFA claims behavior differs from WS-Fed. Product team clarifying exact requirements. ICM: 556339192.
solution: Ensure federated IdP includes proper MFA claims in SAML assertion. Configure federatedIdpMfaBehavior appropriately. Monitor ICM 556339192 for product team clarification.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/Azure AD Authentication General/Azure AD : Use a SAML 2.0 Identity Provider (IdP) for Single Sign On

symptom: Admin blocked from disabling auth methods in SSPR blade. Error: Number of methods chose is less than number of methods required to reset. Save button unavailable.
rootCause: Migration state is Pre-migration, which respects legacy MFA/SSPR policies and enforces strict method count validation in SSPR blade.
solution: Change migration state from Pre-migration to Migration in Progress (Security > Authentication methods > Manage migration). After that, SSPR blade shows warning instead of blocking error.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/Azure AD Authentication General/Azure AD Authentication Method Policies

symptom: Users can still register authenticator apps as software OATH tokens even after disabling in legacy MFA settings (Notification through mobile app / Verification code).
rootCause: SSPR still has Mobile app code/notification enabled. In Pre-migration state, legacy policies for both MFA and SSPR are respected independently.
solution: Disable Mobile app code and Mobile app notification in SSPR Authentication Methods blade. If blocked by method count error, first change migration state to Migration in Progress.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/Azure AD Authentication General/Azure AD Authentication Method Policies

symptom: After using Begin automated guide wizard to migrate to Authentication methods policy, Microsoft Authenticator authentication mode changed to Push. Passwordless sign-in stops working.
rootCause: Bug in automated migration guide (bug 3091679). When any method is available through legacy MFA settings, the wizard replaces Authenticator auth mode with Push.
solution: After migration via automated guide, manually edit Microsoft Authenticator auth methods policy: change Authentication mode from Push back to Passwordless. Bug tracked as 3091679.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/Azure AD Authentication General/Azure AD Authentication Method Policies

symptom: Legacy MFA (Per-user MFA Verification Options) and SSPR auth method policies can no longer be edited in Entra ID portal after Sep 30, 2025.
rootCause: Microsoft retired editing of legacy MFA and SSPR policies starting Sep 30, 2025. UX changes enforce migration to Authentication Methods Policy (AMP).
solution: Use Authentication Methods Policy (Entra ID > Authentication Methods > Policies). Follow migration guide. Request exception (valid until Jan 2026) via support. Auto-migration planned for 2026.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/Azure AD Authentication General/Azure AD Authentication Method Policies

symptom: Federated user authentication fails with redirect loop between Azure AD and ADFS after successful ADFS authentication. User is redirected back and forth between Azure AD and ADFS.
rootCause: The user account has not been synced from on-premises Active Directory to Azure AD. After ADFS authenticates the user, Azure AD cannot find the corresponding user object, causing the redirect loop.
solution: Verify user exists in Azure AD using Microsoft Graph PowerShell: Connect-MgGraph (requires GA credentials), then Get-MgUser to check if the affected user appears. If not synced, check Azure AD Connect sync status and ensure the user is in scope for synchronization. Note: MSOnline module is deprecated; use Microsoft Graph PowerShell instead.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/ADFS and WAP/ADFS workflow - Check if user is synced to AAD

symptom: ADFS WAP (Web Application Proxy) proxy trust relationship cannot be established or starts failing. HTTP.sys uses wrong certificate for client certificate authentication on port 443.
rootCause: A specific IP:Port SSL binding exists on port 443 that takes highest precedence over the ADFS Hostname:Port binding in HTTP.sys priority order (IP:Port > SNI/Hostname:Port > CSS > wildcard). This causes HTTP.sys to use the wrong certificate and CTL store for WAP proxy trust client certificate validation.
solution: Three options: (1) Remove the specific IP:Port binding (check it does not recreate automatically). (2) Use a 2nd IP address for ADFS traffic and resolve ADFS service FQDN to this IP. (3) Configure the AdfsTrustedDevices store as the CTL Store for the specific IP:Port binding. Check current bindings with netsh http show sslcert.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/ADFS and WAP/ADFS workflow - Creating Check if proxy trust certificate settings between AD FS and Proxy are correct

symptom: ADFS WAP proxy trust fails. The SSL certificate binding for the ADFS service FQDN hostname:port on 443 does not have CTL Store Name set to AdfsTrustedDevices (visible in netsh http show sslcert output).
rootCause: The ADFS hostname:port SSL binding CTL Store is not pointing to AdfsTrustedDevices store, preventing proper client certificate validation for WAP proxy trust. The AdfsTrustedDevices store contains the self-signed proxy trust certificates from WAP servers.
solution: If Azure AD Connect is installed, use AAD Connect to update the SSL certificate bindings on all servers. Otherwise, run Set-AdfsSslCertificate -Thumbprint <thumbprint> on the ADFS server to regenerate the ADFS certificate bindings with correct CTL store configuration.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/ADFS and WAP/ADFS workflow - Creating Check if proxy trust certificate settings between AD FS and Proxy are correct

symptom: ADFS WAP proxy trust fails. A CA-issued (non-self-signed) certificate is found in the AdfsTrustedDevices certificate store on the ADFS server.
rootCause: The AdfsTrustedDevices store should only contain self-signed certificates (MS-Organization-Access cert for Workplace Join and proxy trust certs for each WAP server). A CA-issued certificate in this store corrupts the Certificate Trust List (CTL), causing it to only contain the CA-issued cert and breaking proxy trust validation.
solution: Delete the non-self-signed SSL Server Certificate from the AdfsTrustedDevices store. Only self-signed certs should remain. Verify with PowerShell: Get-ChildItem cert:\LocalMachine\AdfsTrustedDevices | Where-Object {$_.Issuer -ne $_.Subject} - any results indicate CA-issued certs that must be removed.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/ADFS and WAP/ADFS workflow - Creating Check if proxy trust certificate settings between AD FS and Proxy are correct

symptom: ADFS WAP proxy trust communication breaks. SSL termination is happening on a network device between AD FS servers and WAP servers.
rootCause: WAP and ADFS communication is based on client certificates. SSL termination on an intermediate network device (load balancer, firewall, etc.) strips the client certificate, breaking the proxy trust handshake.
solution: Disable SSL termination on the network device between the ADFS and WAP servers. ADFS-WAP communication requires end-to-end SSL/TLS for client certificate authentication to function correctly.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/ADFS and WAP/ADFS workflow - Creating Check if proxy trust certificate settings between AD FS and Proxy are correct

symptom: Azure AD Connect Health Risky IP report for ADFS shows no data, missing IP activities, or alert email notifications not received.
rootCause: Multiple causes: (1) AD FS auditing is not enabled, required for Risky IP to collect data. (2) Alert email notification is off by default. (3) Exchange IP addresses and private IP addresses are excluded from the report (but included in CSV export). (4) Only activities exceeding the designated threshold appear in the list.
solution: Enable AD FS auditing per docs. Enable alert notification and configure proper thresholds (avoid noise). Note: report goes back max 30 days, Exchange/private IPs excluded from report but included in CSV export. For large tenants, CSV download can take up to 3 minutes. All daily events generated at midnight UTC.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/ADFS and WAP/Azure AD Connect Health for ADFS

symptom: When connecting to Azure Virtual Desktop (AVD) with smart card or user certificate via ADFS federated CBA, the Session Desktop prompts for password credential instead of presenting smart card authentication.
rootCause: Forms-based authentication is enabled as an authentication method on the ADFS External (Extranet) interface, causing a credential prompt at the STS before certificate authentication can occur.
solution: Remove/uncheck Forms-based authentication from the Extranet authentication methods in ADFS. Also ensure Certificate authentication is enabled on the Extranet. Users should click More choices and select the smart card credential, then enter PIN. Note: smart card users will still see a PIN prompt at the STS; this unlocks the smart card and is not part of the authentication pipeline.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/ADFS and WAP/Azure AD Federated Certificate Based Auth For Azure Virtual Desktop

symptom: Kerberos tickets not issued via KDCProxy for Azure Virtual Desktop. Network Level Authentication (NLA) fails when connecting to AVD host pool using smart card/certificate authentication.
rootCause: After KDCProxy is configured, domain controllers have stale Domain Controller Authentication certificates that prevent Kerberos ticket issuance via the KDC Proxy service.
solution: Request new Domain Controller Authentication certificates on each domain controller, delete the old certificates, and reboot all domain controllers. Enable Kerberos-KDCProxy Operational event logging for diagnosis. Also verify: (1) Server Authentication certificate is bound to TCP 443 (netsh http show sslcert ipport=0.0.0.0:443). (2) KDCProxy is properly configured per docs.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/ADFS and WAP/Azure AD Federated Certificate Based Auth For Azure Virtual Desktop

symptom: Azure AD Federated Certificate Based Authentication fails for Azure Virtual Desktop. Smart card or certificate sign-in to Azure AD for AVD does not work despite ADFS CBA being configured.
rootCause: The issuer and serialnumber AD FS claim rules are configured on the wrong Relying Party Trust. When AVD SSO is configured, a separate Windows Virtual Desktop ADFS Logon SSO RP Trust is created, but the CBA claims must be on the Microsoft Office 365 Identity Platform Worldwide RP Trust instead.
solution: Add issuer and serialnumber AD FS claim rules to the Microsoft Office 365 Identity Platform Worldwide Relying Party Trust, NOT the Windows Virtual Desktop ADFS Logon SSO RP Trust. Deploy root and intermediate CA certificates to Azure AD using Microsoft Graph PowerShell cmdlets. Ensure WAP certauth endpoint (port 443 or 49443) is accessible, with hosts file entry for certauth.<adfs-farm-name> if WAP is domain-joined.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/ADFS and WAP/Azure AD Federated Certificate Based Auth For Azure Virtual Desktop
Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/Azure AD Authentication General/Azure Portal Redirect to Wrong Tenant
symptom: Users are redirected to an unintended tenant when accessing Azure Portal. Government tenant users may be redirected to commercial tenants. The issue occurs when user email domain suffix is verified in a different tenant.
rootCause: During silent token request for Azure Management API, the X-AnchorMailbox parameter contains the user email attribute, which populates login_hint. Home realm discovery finds the domain suffix verified in a different tenant, causing redirect to the wrong tenant.
solution: Portal fix deployed: authentication logic updated to use preferred_username instead of email for tenant routing. Preview portal (preview.portal.azure.com) already has the fix. Workaround: remove or update email addresses with domain suffixes not verified in the users authoritative tenant.

sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/Azure AD Authentication General/Azure Portal MFA Enforcement
symptom: Global Administrator receives error "You do not have access" when trying to request MFA enforcement grace period at aka.ms/managemfaforazure. Error says page can only be accessed by tenant admin.
rootCause: Non-Global Administrator account is attempting to access the grace period request page, or the Global Administrator has not completed the Elevated Access process.
solution: Log in as a Global Administrator and complete the Elevated Access process (aka.ms/enableelevatedaccess) before accessing the grace period page. The admin must elevate access on each tenant separately.

sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/Azure AD Authentication General/Azure Portal MFA Enforcement
symptom: Error "We were unable to fetch the MFA enforcement status details for your tenant" when accessing MFA enforcement management page (aka.ms/managemfaforazure).
rootCause: Global Administrator has not completed the Elevated Access process before accessing the grace period page. Elevated access is required to read/write MFA enforcement status.
solution: Complete the Elevated Access process at aka.ms/enableelevatedaccess, then retry accessing aka.ms/managemfaforazure. After completing the grace period action, remove elevated access.

sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/Azure AD Authentication General/Chrome SameSite
symptom: Applications using Azure AD or ADFS authentication go into browser redirect loop, show broken/partially rendered pages, or fail to display signed-in experience. Issue occurs only in Chrome 80+ and not in Edge/IE/Firefox.
rootCause: Chrome 80+ enforces SameSite cookie attribute changes. Cookies without SameSite=None and Secure flag are treated as SameSite=Lax, breaking cross-site authentication flows. ASP.NET OpenIdConnect cookies (Nonce, State, Oidc) and similar framework cookies lack these attributes.
solution: Verify SameSite issue: 1) Check if issue repros on Edge/IE/Firefox (if yes, not SameSite). 2) Confirm Chrome 80+ via chrome://settings/help. 3) Check chrome://flags for SameSite flags. 4) Use F12 console to look for cookie warnings. Fix: Update website to set SameSite=None and Secure on auth cookies per Chrome/ASP.NET SameSite cookie recipes. Temporary: set Chrome group policy to disable SameSite enforcement.

sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/Azure AD Authentication General/Legally Mandated Authentication Block for Tenants/Ephemeral Tenant Authentication Block
symptom: Users get AADSTS5000229 error: "We are sorry, this resource is not available" when authenticating. All authentication methods and paths to the tenant are blocked, including user sign-in, API/PowerShell, and filing support cases.
rootCause: Tenant identified as a Microsoft-owned ephemeral tenant created outside supported processes. The tenant has been blocked by Microsoft posture management. Through end of 2025, AADSTS5000224 may also be used for expired ephemeral tenants.
solution: For Microsoft employees: follow aka.ms/TenantMgmt to create a new ephemeral tenant; can request one-time 90-day extension via aka.ms/TenantExtension with EVP approval (max 90+90 days). For external customers: create IcM via aka.ms/TenantReAuth template (enter "Customer Tenant" in approver field); PG will verify and perform re-auth. Root Cause coding: CID Sign In and MFA > Tenant DeAuth > AADSTS5000229 - Ephemeral Tenant Block.

sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/Azure AD Authentication General/Legally Mandated Authentication Block for Tenants/Fraud or Security based Authentication Blocks for Tenants
symptom: Users get AADSTS5000224 error: "We are sorry, this resource is not available" when authenticating. All authentication blocked including all resources, users, apps. ASC may fail to load the tenant or show 5000224 message. DirectoryFeatures shows TenantAuthBlockReasonFraud (66).
rootCause: Tenant blocked by Identity Security Investigation team (FROG team) due to fraud or security violation. The block is applied via TenantAuthBlockReasonFraud (DirectoryFeature 66) on the tenant object.
solution: For MS employees: use aka.ms/TenantMgmt. For external customers: create IcM via aka.ms/TenantReAuth. If PG confirms re-auth issue, unblock and close case. If PG detects fraud: (1) get TA confirmation, (2) send CELA-mandated message with tenant name, (3) if customer appeals, TA creates IcM to FROG team (Service: Fraud Response Operations Group, Team: Triage) using IcM template W13H15. Contact sasrequests@microsoft.com. Root Cause: fraud false positive -> AADSTS5000224 - Fraud FALSE Positive; confirmed fraud -> AADSTS5000224 - Fraud Block.

sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/Azure AD Authentication General/Legally Mandated Authentication Block for Tenants/Legally Mandated - Disabled User
symptom: Individual user account disabled and cannot authenticate. Audit log shows: "Core Directory, User Management, Disable account, Success, <user name>, PTSS". The accountEnabled property is set to false. All authentication methods blocked for that user.
rootCause: User is subject to government trade sanctions. The PTSS (trade screening) application automatically disabled the user account by setting accountEnabled=false, per Microsoft compliance obligations with governmental sanctions decisions.
solution: Verify via audit log (look for PTSS as disabling app) or CMAT portal (cmat.azure.com -> Email Address tab -> check Trade Screening Status = Blocked). Get TA confirmation first. If blocked user != requester: send CELA message about government restriction. If blocked user = requester: send CELA message and close immediately. Do NOT re-enable account. Escalation: tradehlp@microsoft.com with Tenant Name, Tenant ID, user ID, audit log entry.

sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/Azure AD Authentication General/Legally Mandated Authentication Block for Tenants/Legally Mandated Block - Global Trade and Compliance IP Restrictions
symptom: AADSTS500531 error: "The sign-in was blocked because it came from an IP blocked for legal reasons". Browser and application flows both blocked. All authentication from embargoed country IPs completely blocked.
rootCause: Authentication originates from an IP address in a country under US trade embargo (currently North Korea/KP). Azure AD blocks all authentication and API access from embargoed country IPs per US Global Trade Law compliance.
solution: Get TA confirmation. Send CELA-mandated response about non-compliant authentication activity. Do NOT unblock. If customer claims NGO status: escalate to expcomp@microsoft.com (CC Hank.Corscadden@microsoft.com). General trade queries: tradehlp@microsoft.com. If customer claims IP geolocation incorrect: escalate to Protections and Signals Intelligence > OnCall team.

sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/Azure AD Authentication General/Legally Mandated Authentication Block for Tenants/Multi-Domain Satellite Tenant Remediation
symptom: AADSTS5000224 error on tenant with @microsoft.com accounts and customer domains. Customer received action-required email about authentication block. Tenant de-authed after May 28, 2024 deadline for not removing @microsoft.com accounts from admin roles.
rootCause: Multi-domain satellite tenant containing @microsoft.com accounts in privileged roles (Global Admin, Auth Admin, etc.), technical/security contacts, or billing contacts. Microsoft posture management blocks these tenants to prevent unauthorized co-tenancy of Microsoft corporate accounts with external domains.
solution: Before de-auth: remove all @microsoft.com accounts from (1) admin roles (Global Admin, Auth Admin, Privileged Role Admin, User Admin), (2) Technical Contacts in tenant properties, (3) Billing contacts in M365 Admin Center. After de-auth: create IcM via aka.ms/TenantReAuth to unblock, then walk through remediation. MS employees: aka.ms/TenantMgmt. IcM escalation: Service=Identity Reputation Management and Abuse Prevention, Team=Triage, template=aka.ms/reauth.

symptom: Application or Relying Party receives SAML tokens signed with an unexpected hash algorithm (e.g., SHA-1 instead of SHA-256), causing signature validation failures or application errors.
rootCause: The Secure Hash Algorithm configured on the Relying Party Trust in ADFS does not match the algorithm expected by the application.
solution: Open ADFS Management Console > expand Relying Party Trusts > select the target RP > Advanced tab > change the Secure hash algorithm dropdown to the required value (e.g., SHA-256). The change takes effect immediately for new tokens issued to that RP.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/ADFS and WAP/ADFS Workflow - Update Signing Algorithm as required by application_ RP

symptom: Federation/SSO breaks after ADFS token-signing or token-decrypting certificate is renewed. Partner applications or relying parties reject tokens signed with the new certificate.
rootCause: Federation partners (relying party trusts and claims provider trusts) still trust the old certificate and have not been updated with the new one after ADFS auto-rollover or manual renewal.
solution: For partners who CANNOT consume federation metadata: manually export the new token-signing certificate public key (.cer or .p7b including the chain) and send it to them for import on their side. For partners who CAN consume federation metadata: ensure they re-fetch the updated federation metadata URL to pick up the new certificate automatically. Verify all RP and CP trusts reflect the new primary certificate before decommissioning the old one.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/ADFS and WAP/ADFS Workflow - Update application_RP to use new TS certificate

symptom: Windows user in EEA region receives AADSTS9002341 error (User is required to permit SSO) when signing in to apps. The error appears in AAD Operational logs as EventID 1098.
rootCause: Digital Markets Act (DMA) compliance requires Windows to prompt EEA users for SSO consent before sharing credentials across apps. This is by-design behavior triggered when x-ms-SsoFlags header indicates EEA device region. The block exposes WAM UI, which may fail if other issues exist (e.g., AV blocking AAD WAM plugin).
solution: User must click Continue on the SSO consent notice. This is a legal requirement - do NOT recommend disabling WAM or falling back to ADAL. If user clicks dont sign in, the prompt reappears next time. For S500 customers with SevA, a KIR (Known Issue Rollback) is available per Windows version. Check for underlying WAM issues if the UI flow itself fails.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/Azure AD Authentication General/Digital Markets Act SSO Sign In Prompt

symptom: User receives AADSTS9002342 error (User denied SSO permission) in Azure sign-in logs after clicking dont sign in on the DMA SSO consent prompt.
rootCause: User actively chose to decline SSO permission on the DMA consent notice. This choice is not persisted - the prompt will appear again next time an app requests SSO.
solution: This is expected behavior. The user can click Continue on the next prompt to grant SSO permission. If the user wants SSO, they need to accept the notice. The decline only applies to that single prompt instance.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/Azure AD Authentication General/Digital Markets Act SSO Sign In Prompt

symptom: Users authenticating with Certificate-Based Authentication (CBA) on Windows repeatedly see the DMA SSO consent prompt, up to once every 4 hours.
rootCause: Known bug: WinLogon from CBA does not carry over the DMA accept bit when Windows refreshes the PRT (every 4 hours). Tracked in OS bug 50237170.
solution: No workaround currently available. Fix has been implemented in in-progress Windows builds and a backport is in progress. No definitive timeline for general availability of the fix.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/Azure AD Authentication General/Digital Markets Act SSO Sign In Prompt

symptom: User sees a TypeError dialog instead of the DMA SSO consent prompt when signing in on Windows in EEA region.
rootCause: Underlying bug in the WebView stack where Intl API is not available. Occurs when the machine timezone settings do not match the geographic region. Bug 51735640 - will not be fixed.
solution: Check Windows timezone settings (Settings > Time and Language > Language and Region > Device Setup Region) and ensure the machine is set to the same timezone as the geographic region the user is in.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/Azure AD Authentication General/Digital Markets Act SSO Sign In Prompt

symptom: User receives AADSTS90023 error (Invalid request. The request specified session id but did not specify issuer) during logout from Entra ID application.
rootCause: An app registration has its Front Channel Logout URL configured to point to Entra ID endpoints (e.g., https://login.microsoftonline.com/ or https://login.windows.net/), creating a recursive logout loop.
solution: Remove the Front Channel Logout URL that points to login.microsoftonline.com or login.windows.net from the app registration. If the failing request comes from the application instead, do not send a sid parameter in the logout request - use logout_hint instead. Allow up to 30 minutes for propagation.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/Azure AD Authentication General/Entra ID Logout TSG

symptom: Sign-out process hangs or fails because a Front Channel Logout URL returns HTTP 404 or other errors during the logout flow.
rootCause: One or more app registrations that the user signed into have an invalid or non-functional Front Channel Logout URL. During logout, Entra ID sends POST calls in hidden iframes to all configured Front Channel Logout URLs, and failures in these calls disrupt the logout flow.
solution: Take a Fiddler/HAR capture during logout. Look for requests with sid parameter that return errors (e.g., 404). Extract the hostname from the failing URL and use PowerShell (Connect-MgGraph -Scopes Application.Read.All) to find all apps and service principals with matching logoutUrl. Update or remove the invalid Front Channel Logout URL. Wait 30 minutes for propagation.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/Azure AD Authentication General/Entra ID Logout TSG

symptom: Users see excessive implicit sign-in events to Microsoft App Access Panel in the My Sign-ins page, creating noise that makes it hard to identify real sign-in activity.
rootCause: The My Apps Secure Sign-in Extension browser extension triggers repeated silent sign-in events that are all logged in the My Sign-ins activity.
solution: Disable or uninstall the My Apps Secure Sign-in Extension while reviewing My Sign-ins activity. Future filtering and grouping improvements planned by PG will reduce this noise.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/Azure AD Authentication General/Entra ID My Signins

symptom: Application receives HTTP 403 Forbidden when calling Microsoft Graph authentication method management APIs (e.g., updating phone auth method, managing FIDO2 keys) after January 26, 2026.
rootCause: Starting January 26, 2026, Microsoft Graph requires users to have completed MFA within the last 10 minutes when managing their own authentication methods programmatically via Graph API.
solution: Handle 403 responses by initiating a new interactive OAuth 2.0 authorization request with claims parameter: {access_token:{amr:{essential:true,values:[ngcmfa]}}}. After user completes MFA, retry the credential management request with the new access token.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/Azure AD Authentication General/Entra ID My Signins

symptom: QR code authentication method stops working for users after admin disables and re-enables it in the authentication method policy, or after admin removes and reassigns QR Code auth method to a user.
rootCause: When admin disables/re-enables the QR code auth method or removes/reassigns it to a user, the auth method policy refresh only occurs when the user signs in with a different method. This is consistent behavior across all auth methods in Entra ID.
solution: Have the affected user sign in using a different authentication method first (e.g., password). After successful sign-in with another method, QR code authentication will work again. Note: this only applies when removing the user from the auth method itself - deleting and recreating just the QR Code does not cause this issue.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/Azure AD Authentication General/Entra ID QR Code Authentication Method

symptom: User receives Incorrect QR code error when trying to sign in with QR code authentication. No entries appear in sign-in logs for the failed attempt.
rootCause: The QR code validation fails before credential verification, so nothing is logged in sign-in logs. Five possible causes: (1) QR code physically damaged, (2) QR code deleted from user account, (3) QR code expired, (4) QR code not yet within active start date/time, (5) QR code not provisioned by administrator.
solution: Check if user has an active QR code via Graph API: GET https://graph.microsoft.com/beta/users/{upn}/authentication/qrCodePinMethod. Check expireDateTime and startDateTime. Review user audit logs for recent QR code changes. If QR code is invalid or expired, admin must create a new QR code and distribute to the user. Policy changes may take up to 15 minutes to propagate.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/Azure AD Authentication General/Entra ID QR Code Authentication Method

symptom: Authentication failures to Microsoft Entra services (login.microsoftonline.com, login.live.com, login.windows.net, autologon.microsoftazuread-sso.com, graph.windows.net) starting January 7, 2026. Clients that pin to DigiCert G1 root CA or do not trust DigiCert G2 root CA experience TLS/authentication failures. Major browsers planning to distrust G1 root after April 2026.
rootCause: Microsoft migrating DigiCert certificates from G1 root CA (DigiCert Global Root CA) to G2 root CA (DigiCert Global Root G2) for improved security and compliance. Affects DigiCert, DigiCert EV, Thawte, Thawte EV, GeoTrust, GeoTrust EV, and RapidSSL certificate families. Clients with certificate pinning to G1 or missing G2 trust anchors will fail validation.
solution: Trust all Root and Subordinate CAs listed in Azure Certificate Authority details (https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/security/fundamentals/azure-ca-details). Trust DigiCert Global Root G2 and its subordinate CAs. Remove client-side pinning to DigiCert Global Root CA. Ensure pinned certificates can be updated on short notice (24hr per CA/Browser Forum). ICM: AAD Gateway/Gateway.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/Azure AD Authentication General/Migration of Microsoft’s DigiCert certificates from the G1 root CA to the G2 root CA

symptom: AADSTS70000 'The provided authorization code has expired' or AADSTS70002 'Error validating credentials due to invalid authorization code' during OAuth2 authorization code flow. Applications fail to complete token redemption. Occurs after January 2026 rollout.
rootCause: Microsoft reduced OAuth2 authorization code lifetime from 10 minutes to 1 minute for all tenants (Phase 2, January 2026). Applications with high-latency redirects, deep links, VPNs, slow mobile networks, embedded browsers, or long client-side processing exceed the 1-minute redemption window. ~0.12% impact expected.
solution: Redeem authorization codes immediately after issuance. Use MSAL or modern auth libraries. Keep redirect paths fast. Remove unnecessary processing before token request. PKCE still requires 1min redemption. Collect: time between auth response and token redemption, app logs, error messages. ~100 high-risk 3P apps temporarily exempted. Escalate if customer redeems within timeframe but still gets errors. ICM: eSTS/ESTS.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/Azure AD Authentication General/OAuth2 Auth Code Validity Improvements

symptom: User deletion or revocation takes longer than expected (up to 2 hours). Access tokens have unexpectedly extended lifetime (2hr or 24hr for CAE). Policy changes not effective immediately (1-15min delay). CTL overwritten issues.
rootCause: COVID-19 load reduction on ESTS: (1) S2S token lifetime increased to 24hr, (2) AT lifetime 1hr to 2hr for Teams/Office without CA policies, (3) Emergency CAE: AT to 24hr with real-time enforcement, (4) Policy polling increased to 1min (premium) or 15min (free), (5) OWA/SPO polling 1hr to 24hr.
solution: Expected behaviors for tenants without CA policies. Root cause coding: CID Sign In and MFA > Change to address capacity due to Covid-19 > Token Lifetime Increase / Revocation Delay / Policy Change Effective Delay / CTL overwritten. Most info NOT customer-ready - do not share without PG approval. Consult TA.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/Azure AD Authentication General/Reducing COVID-19 Load on ESTS

symptom: Certificate validation failures or connectivity disruptions to Azure services (including Azure SQL) after October 1, 2025. Custom TLS implementations or strict nonce validation logic reject OCSP responses. Legacy OSS SDKs referencing OneOCSP endpoint may break.
rootCause: MSPKI OCSP responders removed nonce extension support starting October 1, 2025 (RFC 5019/6960 alignment). OCSP responses valid but nonce ignored. Legacy OneOCSP service retired. Custom clients enforcing nonce match reject response. Risk from outdated libraries, inherited configs, or 3P integrations relying on deprecated OCSP behavior.
solution: Review revocation logic to ensure clients do not reject OCSP responses lacking nonce. Notify downstream partners. Standard TLS stacks (Windows, OpenSSL, Chrome, Edge, Firefox) NOT impacted. Only MSPKI certificates affected - DigiCert/private/AME PKI unaffected. Use PKI team TSG. ICM: MS PKI Incident DRI.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/Azure AD Authentication General/Removal of Nonce Support in OCSP Service for Microsoft PKI

symptom: Users receive AADSTS5000225 error: This tenant has been blocked due to inactivity. ASC may show Error code 67: TenantAuthBlockReasonLifecycle. All resources, users, apps blocked. Cases must be filed from different tenant.
rootCause: Microsoft lifecycle policy blocks tenants inactive >200 days past billing cycle. Day 0: notification email. Day 30: login block AADSTS5000225 by OMS Commerce. Day 60: tenant permanently deleted.
solution: 1) Collect tenant ID, correlation ID, timestamp. 2) SE files ICM using ASC template [ID][IDM] AADSTS5000225 Tenant Lifecycle Reauth Request. 3) TA confirms in DS Explorer: _IsDeleted=False, DirectoryFeatures has TenantAuthBlockReasonLifecycle[67]. 4) Check USGov via OpenID metadata - if USGov, transfer to Azure GSX Identity Gov. 5) TA runs Geneva action LifeCycle - Extend Tenant Lifecycle For Dormancy with Is Reactivate Forever checked (requires AME groups TM-SCIMID-CMRC-OPS and AP-SCIMID-CMRC-OPS). 6) Re-auth takes up to 15 min. SAP: Azure/Microsoft Entra Directories, Domains, and Objects/Directory Management/Blocked Tenant (AADSTS5000225).
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/Azure AD Authentication General/Lifecycle Based Authentication Blocks

symptom: Users receive AADSTS165000 (InvalidRequestCanary) during sign-in. Variants: request did not include required tokens (missing canary), tokens do not match user context (validation failed), or tokens were incorrect/invalid (corrupted).
rootCause: Login CSRF protection validation fails - CSRF double submit cookie/form canary missing, mismatched, or corrupted. Typically caused by custom apps or scripts posting credentials directly to login.microsoftonline.com instead of AAD-managed sign-in UX.
solution: Remove custom calls modifying requests to login.microsoftonline.com. Ensure Entra ID executes entire auth flow E2E. If federated or customer needs time, escalate to ESTS > ESTS UX for tenant allowlisting. ASC SAP: Azure/Azure AD Sign-In and MFA/Passwords/Stay Signed In (KMSI).
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/Azure AD Authentication General/Login CSRF

symptom: Users receive AADSTS165001 (CanaryExpired) or AADSTS165003 (SessionContextExpired): Session Expired - request tokens have expired and are no longer valid.
rootCause: CSRF canary token or session context cookie has expired due to session timeout, part of Login CSRF protection mechanism.
solution: Clear browser cookies and retry sign-in from the beginning to reset CSRF tokens and session context.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/Azure AD Authentication General/Login CSRF

symptom: Users see AADSTS399218 (CsrfSpeedbumpConfirmationRequired): domain confirmation dialog/speedbump during federated sign-in with auto-acceleration or SmartLinks. May repeat if cookies cleared.
rootCause: Login CSRF protection inserts domain confirmation speedbump for federated auto-acceleration (HRD with login_hint/domain_hint) or SmartLinks. These flows skip initial Entra ID sign-in page preventing CSRF cookie drop. Persistent csrfspeedbump cookie should suppress future prompts.
solution: Speedbump is expected security feature, not an error. If repeating, ensure csrfspeedbump cookie persists. To suppress org-wide: implement Tenant Restrictions v2 (TRv2) policies. Suppressed for: native WebView2/Electron/mobile clients, active SSO, active TRv2. Once per session.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/Azure AD Authentication General/Login CSRF

symptom: Apps receive AADSTS165004 (InvalidApiCanary) on async API calls to Entra ID. Error indicates missing, mismatched, or corrupted user context values (cookies, form fields, headers).
rootCause: CSRF violation on async API endpoint calls. Canary attributes missing, session mismatch, or corrupted. Usually caused by customer automation making direct API calls to login.microsoftonline.com bypassing AAD auth flow.
solution: Remove custom API calls bypassing AAD auth flow. Ensure AAD manages entire auth flow E2E. For federated or extended remediation, escalate to ESTS > ESTS UX for tenant whitelisting.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/Azure AD Authentication General/Login CSRF

symptom: Authentication to login.microsoftonline.com fails with SSL/TLS certificate validation errors after Microsoft introduces new Azure Certificate Authorities alongside existing DigiCert certificates.
rootCause: Microsoft implements new Azure CAs for login.microsoftonline.com. Systems with outdated OS, custom trust stores, disconnected environments, or unsupported platforms may not trust new Azure CAs. Min versions: Windows XP SP3, Firefox 32, iOS 7, macOS 10.9, Android SDK 5.x, Java JRE 1.8.0_101.
solution: 1) Update to supported OS. 2) If cannot update, manually add Azure CAs to trusted root store. 3) Linux: add CAs to /etc/ssl/certs. 4) Java: update key store. 5) Immediate mitigation: update hosts file for login.microsoftonline.com to DigiCert-only IPs: EU=20.190.177.19, APAC=20.190.141.32, NA=20.190.152.19. Ref: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/security/fundamentals/azure-ca-details
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/Azure AD Authentication General/Microsoft Entra Certificate Authorities

symptom: Apps using certificate pinning for login.microsoftonline.com fail authentication after new Azure CAs introduced. Only DigiCert-pinned certificates accepted.
rootCause: Apps explicitly pin to DigiCert CAs for login.microsoftonline.com. New Azure CAs rejected because not in pinned list.
solution: Update pinned certificate list to include both DigiCert and new Azure CAs. Ensure pins can be updated on short notice (24h per CA/Browser Forum). Ref: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/security/fundamentals/certificate-pinning. ICM: AAD Gateway > Gateway.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/Azure AD Authentication General/Microsoft Entra Certificate Authorities
Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/Azure AD Conditional Access Policy/Azure AD Conditional Access Attribute Based Cloud App Targeting
symptom: Conditional Access policy with Dynamic Targeting (app filter) is read-only in the Azure portal for Global Administrators and Conditional Access Administrators. They see the banner: This CA Policy is read-only because it has an App filter configured and you do not have permission to alter that filter. They can Delete but not Save the policy.
rootCause: Dynamic targeting (attribute-based cloud app targeting) requires membership in Attribute Definition Administrator or Attribute Definition Reader role in addition to GA/CA Admin role. Graph API PATCH calls from GA without these roles may still succeed, creating inconsistency with portal behavior.
solution: Assign the Attribute Definition Administrator or Attribute Definition Reader role to the administrator in addition to their existing Global Administrator or Conditional Access Administrator role.

sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/Azure AD Conditional Access Policy/Azure AD Conditional Access Attribute Based Cloud App Targeting
symptom: Users with only Attribute Definition Administrator or Reader roles cannot access the Security blade in Azure AD portal and cannot configure Dynamic Targeting in CA policies.
rootCause: Attribute definition roles alone do not grant access to the Security blade. Access requires Conditional Access Administrator or equivalent role.
solution: Assign both roles: Conditional Access Administrator (for Security blade access) AND Attribute Definition Administrator/Reader (for dynamic targeting rule management).

sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/Azure AD Conditional Access Policy/Azure AD Conditional Access Attribute Based Cloud App Targeting
symptom: Dynamic targeting (attribute-based cloud app filtering) in Conditional Access is not applying correctly when the rule contains multiple expressions.
rootCause: Bug in the ESTS DLL that reads Dynamic target rule expressions - filter evaluation fails with multiple expressions. Tracked in bug 1659158.
solution: At Public Preview, expressions limited to String type. Workaround: use single expression rules or simplify filter. Boolean and Integer types supported at GA.

sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/Azure AD Conditional Access Policy/Azure AD Conditional Access Authentication Contexts
symptom: First Authentication Context created in the tenant does not appear in the Azure portal. Only the second one is visible with the C1 ID that belonged to the first.
rootCause: API bug allows the second authentication context creation to override/reuse the C1 ID assigned to the first authentication context, effectively replacing it.
solution: Check Audit logs filtering by Activity Add AuthenticationContextClassReference to verify. Re-create the missing context with a new ID (C2+). Note: authentication contexts cannot be deleted once created.

sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/Azure AD Conditional Access Policy/Azure AD Conditional Access Authentication Flows
symptom: User is blocked when signing into a non-device-code-flow app (e.g. Exchange) after initially using device code flow to sign into an allowed app (e.g. SharePoint), even though the CA policy only blocks device code flow for non-SharePoint apps.
rootCause: Protocol tracking: when a user authenticates via device code flow, the session is marked as protocol tracked. Any subsequent sign-in within that session is subject to Authentication Flows CA policy enforcement, even for non-device-code flows. State persists through token refreshes.
solution: By design. Use a separate session (different browser/device) not protocol-tracked to access other apps. Or configure CA policy to allow device code flow for all apps the user needs in the same session.

sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/Azure AD Conditional Access Policy/Azure AD Conditional Access Sign-in Logs
symptom: Sign-in event for blocked EAS legacy authentication shows Conditional Access status as Failure but Session status as Success in Azure AD sign-in logs, causing confusion when customer has configured CA to block EAS legacy authentication
rootCause: For Exchange ActiveSync CA policies, Azure AD relies on Exchange to enforce CA. Azure AD sends a signal in the response for Exchange to block the request on their end. Because ESTS issued a token and did not block on CA, the session field shows Success instead of Failed
solution: This is by design in current implementation. Azure AD does not enforce CA directly for EAS - it delegates enforcement to Exchange. The session status will be corrected to show Failed in a future update (tracked via Epic 1071328). Explain to customer that the actual enforcement happens at Exchange side and the sign-in log status is a logging discrepancy, not a security gap

sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/Azure AD Conditional Access Policy/Conditional Access 'Microsoft Admin Portals' app group
symptom: In Azure Government (govcloud), Azure Portal is not protected when targeting the Microsoft Admin Portals app group in Conditional Access policy during public preview
rootCause: During public preview, the govcloud appid for Azure Portal was not included in the Microsoft Admin Portals app group. Other portals are not affected because they do not have different appids for govcloud instances
solution: Fixed at General Availability (around end of September 2023). If customer is still on preview, upgrade to GA version of the Microsoft Admin Portals app group

sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/Azure AD Conditional Access Policy/Conditional Access 'Microsoft Admin Portals' app group
symptom: Users navigating to compliance.microsoft.com or security.microsoft.com encounter error AADSTS50076: Due to a configuration change made by your administrator, you must use multi-factor authentication to access '00000006-0000-0ff1-ce00-000000000000', when Microsoft Admin Portals app group is added to CA policy
rootCause: Security and Microsoft Purview compliance portal (AppID 80ccca67-54bd-44ab-8625-4b79c4dc7775) was not yet included in the Microsoft Admin Portals app group, but it requests a token for Microsoft Admin Center (00000006-0000-0ff1-ce00-000000000000) which IS in the group. This dependency triggers CA policy evaluation and requires controls to be satisfied
solution: Workaround 1: Have user click OK on the error (possibly twice due to Security Center retry bug) - user will then be prompted for MFA. Workaround 2: Exclude user from the Microsoft Admin Portals CA policy until the compliance portal is fully added. Permanent fix: Deployed end of July 2023 when Security and Purview compliance portal was added to the Microsoft Admin Portals app group

sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/Azure AD Conditional Access Policy/Conditional Access Optimization Agent
symptom: Customers with Security Copilot see 'Security Copilot agents are coming' under What's new in Entra portal but cannot find or enable the Conditional Access Optimization Agent tile
rootCause: The Conditional Access Optimization Agent was deployed via phased rollout, not immediately available to all tenants
solution: Phased rollout completed on June 2, 2025. The agent tile should now be visible to all eligible tenants in the Agents blade of the Entra portal. If still not visible, verify tenant has Entra P1+ license and Security Copilot subscription with provisioned Compute Units

sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/Azure AD Conditional Access Policy/Conditional Access Optimization Agent
symptom: Admin clicking Run Agent for the Conditional Access Optimization Agent encounters error 'The agent encountered an error and could not complete its run'. Browser trace may show 500 Internal Server Error on POST to api.securitycopilot.microsoft.com
rootCause: The tenant has run into overage of Security Copilot Compute Units (SCUs). Each agent run consumes approximately 3 SCUs on average
solution: Navigate to Usage monitoring in Security Copilot portal (securitycopilot.microsoft.com/usage-monitoring) to check remaining provisioned SCUs. If a 500 Internal Server Error is present in browser trace, submit an ICM to the Security Copilot team. If no 500 error, verify the tenant still has available SCUs and has not exhausted the allocation

sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/Azure AD Authentication General/Unable to login to Azure Portal or Office 365 portal from Domain joined machines
symptom: Unable to login to Azure Portal or Office 365 portal from domain-joined Windows machines. login.microsoftonline.com is blocked. Error: your Internet access is blocked. Disjoining the domain resolves it.
rootCause: Proxy, firewall, or Group Policy settings on the domain-joined machine block access to login.microsoftonline.com.
solution: Check proxy/firewall settings on the domain. Ensure login.microsoftonline.com and required M365 endpoints are allowed through corporate proxy/firewall. Verify GPO-applied proxy settings.

sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/Azure AD Authentication General/Updates to OAuth 2.0 Authorization Code Grant Flow for Native Apps
symptom: Native apps relying on OAuth 2.0 Authorization Code Grant flow break after security update. Browser shows warning page instead of blank page with auth code, then redirects to /common/wrongplace after ~3 seconds.
rootCause: Security update from Dec 2025. Auth codes previously visible in browser address bar indefinitely (phishing risk). New: warning page then auto-redirect removes the code.
solution: Native apps must retrieve the authorization code from browser in under 3 seconds automatically. Must NOT rely on manual code retrieval. ICM path: eSTS team.

sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/Azure AD Conditional Access Policy/AAD CA KNOWN ISSUES
symptom: CA reports sign-in as Successful with no policy enforcing controls, but sign-in still challenged (device-based) and might fail. CA Diagnostics may be broken.
rootCause: Classic CA policies still enabled on tenant. Classic policies not shown in standard CA list and not captured by new CA diagnostics.
solution: Check ASC > Conditional Access > Classic policies tab for enabled classic policies. Migrate per: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/entra/identity/conditional-access/policy-migration-mfa

sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/Azure AD Conditional Access Policy/AAD CA KNOWN ISSUES
symptom: Admin unable to edit Microsoft-managed CA policy. Error 1184: You cannot change the properties of this Microsoft-managed policy, except for excluding users, groups, roles, or changing the state.
rootCause: Deleted users in policy cause UX automatic cleanup during edit, triggering error since modifying other properties of Microsoft-managed policies is not allowed.
solution: Click Edit button below Excluded identities instead of main Edit button. This avoids triggering deleted user cleanup.

sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/Azure AD Conditional Access Policy/AAD CA KNOWN ISSUES
symptom: CA blocks access because device state not shared with Windows Server 2016 + Edge v115. Other client apps pass device state normally.
rootCause: Bug in Edge 115 prevents device state from being shared on Windows Server 2016.
solution: Rollback Edge to v114. Verify: 1) edge://settings/help for version, 2) dsregcmd /status for PRT. Transfer to Browsers team via SAP.

sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/Azure AD Conditional Access Policy/AAD CA KNOWN ISSUES
symptom: Cloud app names changed in CA app picker. Microsoft Azure Management -> Windows Azure Service Management API, Universal Store Service APIs -> Windows Store for Business, Azure Windows VM Sign-In -> Microsoft Azure Windows VM Sign-In.
rootCause: CA app picker UX updated to call MSGraph directly instead of legacy IAMUX backend with different app names.
solution: Expected behavior. AppIds unchanged (797f4846, 45a330b1, 372140e0). Name change is cosmetic, does not affect policy enforcement.

sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/Azure AD Conditional Access Policy/AAD Conditional Access Application Condition
symptom: CA policy with include/exclude selected cloud apps does not block access from native application. CA Diagnostic: This resource was not included in the policy application condition.
rootCause: For native apps, CA evaluates against the resource (AAD Graph, MS Graph, EXO), NOT the client app. Only confidential clients requesting id_token are evaluated directly. Scope User.Read on MSGraph is exempt from CA.
solution: Target the resource app in CA policy instead of native client app. Check ClientTypeForConditionalAccess in ASC Expert View > PerRequestLogs. For V1 endpoint: 1) Include All Apps, 2) Add response_type=code+id_token, or 3) Migrate to V2 endpoint.

sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/Azure AD Conditional Access Policy/App protection policy for Edge on Windows
symptom: Users on Win10 with MAM for Edge in endless Switch Edge profile loop. Error 53003. MamLog.txt: application did not supply access token, MTD report launch fails.
rootCause: KB5031445 (Oct 2023 Preview) not installed. This enables wdmam:// protocol handler for Edge desktop MAM.
solution: Install KB5031445 (OS Build 19045.3636). Verify MAM enrollment in MamLog.txt. Requirements: Win10 20H2+ with KB5031445 or Win11 22H2+ with KB5031455. Edge v115+. Device must be workplace-joined only.

sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/Azure AD Conditional Access Policy/Authentication Strengths Conditional Access CBA Advanced Options
symptom: User authenticates with certificate but does not satisfy custom authentication strength CBA issuer/policyOID restrictions. Or cannot switch to different certificate within same browser session.
rootCause: Only one cert per browser session (cached). Certs with >5 Policy OIDs: best-effort selection of 5. External users cannot use CBA restrictions on resource tenant. Cert must have valid AKI per X.509 v3.
solution: For cert switching: close all browser windows, open new session. For >5 Policy OIDs: contact PG. For external users: home tenant cross-tenant only. Verify cert AKI.

symptom: Creating Terms of Use fails with error 'There was a problem creating terms of use <DisplayName>' when the combined PDF file size for all selected languages exceeds 2MB.
rootCause: The initial Create of a TOU term is written as a single blob. There is a 2MB limitation at the gateway, so if the total size of the files exceeds 2MB the Create will fail.
solution: If only one language file exceeds 2MB, use a smaller file. If creating a TOU with multiple languages where each file is <2MB but the total exceeds 2MB, create the TOU with only one language first, then add each additional language file afterward (subsequent additions are not subject to the same limit).
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/Azure AD Conditional Access Policy/Entra ID Terms of Use

symptom: Users are blocked from enrolling their devices when 'Consent per device' setting is enabled on Terms of Use and the Conditional Access policy has the Intune enrollment app in scope.
rootCause: The 'Consent per device' TOU setting is incompatible with Intune enrollment app as the CA policy target. Device enrollment flow cannot satisfy the per-device consent requirement through the Intune enrollment app.
solution: Option 1: Turn the 'Consent per device' setting off on the TOU. Option 2: Update the CA policy app targeting from Intune enrollment to the actual resource apps the users will be accessing (e.g., SharePoint Online, Exchange Online).
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/Azure AD Conditional Access Policy/Entra ID Terms of Use

symptom: Sign-in logs show AADSTS50158 'External security challenge not satisfied' failures when Terms of Use Conditional Access policy is applied, even though users can sign in successfully. Customer may report this as a CRI believing authentication is broken.
rootCause: Expected behavior. Terms of Use implements Conditional Access extensibility which requires an interactive sign-in. During a non-interactive sign-in, CA cannot verify whether the TOU agreement is satisfied, so it returns error 50158. The client app needs to re-initiate the request in an interactive sign-in session for ESTS/CA to prompt the user for the required TOU challenge.
solution: Query ASC sign-in logs via correlation ID. If two entries exist for the same correlation ID (one 50158 failure followed by a Success(0)), the TOU was satisfied - this is normal. If only 50158 appears with no subsequent success, the client application is not handling the interaction_required correctly. The app needs to support interactive sign-in flow per MSAL error handling docs (https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/entra/identity-platform/msal-error-handling-js#errors-that-require-interaction). Transfer to Partner team if the issue is app-side.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/Azure AD Conditional Access Policy/Entra ID Terms of Use

symptom: Terms of Use PDF fails to render on Android devices. On iOS, the PDF loads but lacks 'Back' navigation, forcing users to cancel the application. 'Pinch to zoom' does not work on either Android or iOS. The 'Having trouble viewing? Click here' link does nothing when clicked.
rootCause: Bugs in ADAL (Azure AD Authentication Library) on mobile platforms. Android does not support PDF rendering in the ADAL webview, iOS lacks proper back navigation UI, and pinch-to-zoom is not implemented in the ADAL window.
solution: These are known ADAL issues pending engineering fixes. For the 'Having trouble viewing' link, a private preview feature with an allow list can remove the link and add 'Zoom in'/'Zoom out' buttons. Since ADAL is deprecated, upgrading the application to use MSAL (Microsoft Authentication Library) may resolve these rendering issues. Customers should migrate to MSAL for the best experience.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/Azure AD Conditional Access Policy/Entra ID Terms of Use
Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/Azure AD Identity Protection/AAD ID Protection KNOWN ISSUES
symptom: Identity Protection Risk Detections blade sorting by Detection Time is incorrect - clicking descending shows older dates mixed in with newer ones
rootCause: Bug in portal UX sorting logic for the Risk Detections blade (tracked by Engineering work item 2371055)
solution: Known product bug. Inform customer this is a known issue being tracked by engineering. As workaround, export data and sort locally.

sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/Azure AD Identity Protection/AAD ID Protection KNOWN ISSUES
symptom: Customer receives user at risk notification for users who have been at risk for a long time - notification appears delayed or duplicated
rootCause: Bug in Signals Pipeline: when recalculating user risk level on new sign-in risk, notifications are resent even when risk level did not increase to high. IsDuplicateEvent flag is not working correctly when old user risk is carried over.
solution: Known bug (tracked by work item 1528221). Inform customer this is expected to be fixed. Similar to ICM 258327060 response pattern.

sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/Azure AD Identity Protection/AAD ID Protection KNOWN ISSUES
symptom: Admin disables user via Identity Protection portal, but user account re-enables itself after next AAD Connect sync cycle
rootCause: Expected behavior: when user is synced from on-prem AD and still enabled there, the AAD Connect sync cycle will flip the user state back to enabled because on-prem AD is the source of authority.
solution: Disable the user in on-premises Active Directory first, then wait for sync cycle to propagate. Identity Protection portal disable alone is insufficient for hybrid users.

sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/Azure AD Identity Protection/AAD ID Protection KNOWN ISSUES
symptom: Azure AD Threat Intelligence appears twice in the detection type filter under Risk Detections blade. Selecting one duplicate shows Something went wrong error.
rootCause: Deployment error exposed UX changes intended for a new signal prematurely, causing duplicate filter entries.
solution: Resolved by deployment fix. Use the other (working) duplicate option as workaround. If seen again, report to engineering.

sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/Azure AD Identity Protection/Azure AD ID Protection FAQ
symptom: Unfamiliar sign-in properties risk detection triggered on failed sign-in attempts. Customer thinks failed logins cause risk detections.
rootCause: Risk detections are NOT triggered by failed logins. Smart Lockout locks account after several failed attempts (AADSTS50053). Once locked, even a correct password attempt can trigger unfamiliar sign-in properties, but these are two independent events with no causation.
solution: Check for Account Lockout errors (AADSTS50053) in sign-in logs. Explain that unfamiliar sign-in properties detection and smart lockout are independent mechanisms. A CA policy failure (device/location) can also cause this pattern.

sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/Azure AD Identity Protection/Azure AD ID Protection FAQ
symptom: Admin received email notification about a high risky user, but upon browsing to AADIP Risky Users blade, no new risky users found.
rootCause: The Risky User report defaults to showing only at-risk users. If user has already self-remediated risk as directed by policy, they appear under Remediated users instead.
solution: Check if Remediated/Dismissed filter is unchecked in the Risky User report. The user likely already remediated their risk via policy-directed actions and will appear in the Remediated Users list.

sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/Azure AD Identity Protection/Azure AD ID Protection FAQ
symptom: Number of risky users and sign-ins in the risk alert email does not match what is displayed in the Risky Users/Sign-ins reports in portal.
rootCause: Multiple causes: (1) report filters may differ from landing page defaults (30 days), (2) known latency in user risk dismissal flow causing sync delays, (3) offline detections may not have updated on landing page yet.
solution: Ensure reports have matching time-span and filter settings as the landing page. The Risky User/Sign-in reports should be taken as authoritative over the landing page counts.

sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/Azure AD Identity Protection/Azure AD ID Protection FAQ
symptom: Risky user/risky sign-in/risk detection data is displayed for deleted users in Identity Protection reports.
rootCause: Risk data is not cleaned until the user is hard deleted. Soft-deleted users still have associated risk data. Service-side limitation may also prevent automatic cleanup even after soft delete.
solution: Step 1: Verify user is truly deleted (not soft-deleted) via ASC - if soft-deleted, admin can hard delete or wait 30 days for auto hard-delete. Step 2: If user is hard-deleted but risk data remains, file IcM using the designated template for manual cleanup.

sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/Azure AD Identity Protection/Azure AD ID Protection FAQ
symptom: Guest user unable to perform MFA registration with error AADSTS53004: Cannot Configure Multi-Factor Authentication Methods Due To Suspicious Activity.
rootCause: Guest user has not registered MFA in home tenant but got elevated to high risk in resource tenant. Identity Protection reports do not show this scenario, creating a diagnostic black hole. MFA registration is blocked when user is at high risk.
