solution: Alleviate user risk via API first, then have user complete MFA and SSPR registration in their home tenant before allowing access attempts to the resource tenant.

sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/Azure AD Identity Protection/Azure AD ID Protection FAQ
symptom: MCAS-triggered Impossible Travel risk detections are false positives caused by users hopping on/off VPN. Customer has added VPN IPs to Named IP Locations but detections persist.
rootCause: Impossible Travel is an offline detection consuming MCAS alert pipeline input. MCAS maintains its own safe IP list separate from Azure AD Named IP Locations. The two IP lists are not automatically synced.
solution: Sync MCAS safe IP list with Azure AD Named IP Locations. MCAS team offers a Python script to bulk update IPs/subnets downloaded from AAD Named IP locations via the data enrichment API. Automatic sync is in backlog with no ETA.

sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/Azure AD Identity Protection/Azure AD ID Protection FAQ
symptom: Atypical travel risk detection triggers even though one of the locations in the travel pair is configured as a trusted Named Location.
rootCause: By design: Atypical travel only whitelists when the second (destination) IP is part of a trusted location, since that is the IP from which the actual login is performed. The first/origin IP being trusted does not suppress the detection.
solution: Explain to customer this is by design. Only the destination/second IP in the atypical travel pair is evaluated against trusted locations. If the destination IP should be trusted, add it to Named Locations.

sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/Azure AD Identity Protection/Azure AD IP Lockout
symptom: Users blocked from signing in with error code 50053 (IdsLocked). Kusto shows ErrorCode=IdsLocked, RamRecommendedAction=100 (Blocked), RamRecommender=2 (IPBlocking).
rootCause: Azure AD IP Lockout (IP Blocking) detected overwhelmingly unhealthy traffic from the user IP address. This is an always-on cloud protection mechanism that blocks all traffic from bad IPs. Legitimate users on shared/corporate IPs can be caught as false positives.
solution: Step 1: Verify in Kusto (PerRequestTableIfx) whether the block is IP Lockout (ErrorCode=IdsLocked, RamRecommender=2). Step 2: Check if the affected IP is registered in Azure AD Named Locations. Step 3: If not, add the IP range to a Named Location marked as trusted - this exempts it from IP Lockout. Microsoft will not block sign-ins from trusted corporate IPs.

sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/Azure AD Kerberos/Azure AD Kerberos Authentication TSG
symptom: dsregcmd /status shows OnpremTGT: NO and klist cloud_debug shows Cloud Primary (Hybrid logon) TGT available: 0. ESTS KerberosOps status codes show 27 and/or 28 instead of expected value 1.
rootCause: Status code 27 = TgtUserDnsDomainNameMissing (user lacks DnsDomainName attribute), code 28 = TgtRodcKrbtgtNotFound (Kerberos domain missing keys). Most commonly, AAD Connect is missing transform rules to sync the DnsDomainName attribute (exposed as onPremisesDomainName in Graph).
solution: Step 1: Check sign-in logs for Windows Sign In app in ASC. Step 2: Use Authentication Troubleshooter Expert view, filter PerRequestLogs by KerberosOps. Step 3: Decode status codes (1=SuccessGeneratedRodcTgt, 2=SuccessGeneratedCloudTgt, 27=DnsDomainNameMissing, 28=KrbtgtNotFound, 112=KeyNotRotated). Step 4: For code 27, verify onPremisesDomainName in Graph Explorer and add missing transform rules in AAD Connect. For code 28, provision Kerberos keys using Set-AzureADKerberosServer.

symptom: Users unable to complete MFA voice call authentication - strong authentication phone number is empty. Affects cloud-only users (since Nov 2020) and DirSync users (since May 2021) who were relying on public profile contact numbers for MFA.
rootCause: Azure MFA disabled fallback to public profile contact numbers for security. Previously, if a strong auth phone method was set without a phone number, the service fell back to public profile numbers. This fallback was insecure because cloud-only users could update public profile numbers without MFA.
solution: Set phone numbers directly in strong authentication methods: (1) Azure Portal - Authentication methods blade under user, (2) PowerShell: New-MgUserAuthenticationPhoneMethod -UserId user@contoso.net -phoneType mobile -phoneNumber "+1 1112223333", (3) Graph API: PUT /beta/users/{userId}/authentication/phoneMethods/{id}. Check empty numbers: Get-MgUserAuthenticationPhoneMethod -UserId user@contoso.net.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/Azure AD MFA/Azure AD MFA Phone number fallback

symptom: After migrating from legacy MFA to Voice Call authentication method policy, users with Office phone defined as an authentication method cannot see their office phone in the MFA prompt list.
rootCause: When Voice Call authentication method policy is enabled, the Office phone option must be separately enabled under the Configure tab. If not enabled, users with Office phone method on their user object will not see it during MFA.
solution: In Entra admin center, go to Authentication methods > Voice call policy > Configure tab, enable the Office phone option. If tenant uses SSPR with Office phone enabled, enable Voice calls auth method policy with Office phone option enabled.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/Azure AD MFA/Azure AD MFA Voice Call Authentication Method Policy

symptom: Custom greeting audio in MFA voice call authentication stops working or cannot be updated. After Oct 31 2025 cannot update via Entra admin center. After Feb 28 2026 feature fully retired.
rootCause: Microsoft is retiring the custom greeting feature in Entra voice call authentication. Oct 31 2025: last day to update via portal. Feb 28 2026: full retirement - all voice calls use default Microsoft recordings.
solution: Before Oct 31 2025: update custom greetings via Entra admin center. Between Oct 31 2025 and Feb 28 2026: contact Microsoft Customer Support to make changes. After Feb 28 2026: no workaround, voice calls will use Microsoft default recordings.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/Azure AD MFA/Azure AD MFA Voice Call Authentication Method Policy

symptom: Users stuck in MFA verification loop - keep getting prompted for MFA but cannot complete it. Occurs when using ADFS with both Keep me signed in (KMSI) and Remember my device enabled, and ADFS performs MFA via MFA Server or 3rd party MFA solutions.
rootCause: Remember my device is incompatible with ADFS KMSI when ADFS performs MFA via MFA Server or 3rd party. When remember device days expire, Azure AD requests fresh MFA, but ADFS returns its existing cached token instead of performing new MFA, creating an infinite loop.
solution: Disable either Keep me signed in on ADFS or Remember my device - do not use both simultaneously when ADFS is performing MFA. If using Azure AD Premium, migrate to Conditional Access sign-in frequency and persistent browser policies instead.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/Azure AD MFA/Remember Multi-Factor Authentication

symptom: Users getting prompted for MFA more frequently than expected despite Remember my device being configured. Conditional Access Sign-in Frequency policy and Remember my device interfere with each other.
rootCause: Remember my device setting conflicts with Conditional Access Sign-in Frequency policy. Both control MFA prompt behavior but in incompatible ways, causing unexpected prompt frequency.
solution: Use only one mechanism: (1) Azure AD Premium customers should use Conditional Access sign-in frequency and/or persistent browser policies. (2) Microsoft 365 Apps/Azure AD Free customers should use Remember my device. Do not mix both. Check SourceofMFAExpiry via AuthTroubleshooter in sign-in logs to identify which policy is expiring the MFA claim.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/Azure AD MFA/Remember Multi-Factor Authentication

symptom: Rich client (non-browser) applications prompt for MFA more often than expected after configuring Remember my device with duration less than 90 days.
rootCause: Rich clients use refresh tokens or PRTs. Azure AD validates the MFA Auth instance timestamp on the token against the Remember my device days. When set lower than 90 days, the MFA claim expires faster, causing more frequent prompts for rich client apps.
solution: Set Remember my device duration to 90 days or more (default was increased to 90 in Sep 2020, range 1-365 days). Navigate to Azure AD > Users > Multi-Factor Authentication > Service Settings > remember multi-factor authentication, set days to 90+.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/Azure AD MFA/Remember Multi-Factor Authentication

symptom: Admin clicks Revoke MFA sessions for a user but it has no effect - action returns success without actually revoking MFA sessions. Affects users with Conditional Access-based MFA enforcement.
rootCause: Revoke MFA sessions is a legacy capability tied only to per-user MFA enforcement. For CA-based MFA users, the portal previously returned success without performing actual revocation. Bug corrected late 2025; portal now returns proper error for CA users. Starting Feb 2026, button replaced by Revoke sessions.
solution: Use Revoke sessions instead (Users blade > select user > Overview > Revoke sessions), which invalidates all sessions including MFA regardless of enforcement method (CA or per-user). For API: use Graph revokeSignInSessions: POST /v1.0/users/{id}/revokeSignInSessions. The legacy Revoke MFA sessions button will be replaced by Revoke sessions starting Feb 2026.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/Azure AD MFA/Revoke MFA Sessions Replaced with Revoke Sessions
Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/Azure AD Conditional Access Policy/Tenant Lockout TA Instructions
symptom: Tenant admin receives AADSTS5000224 error during sign-in and is locked out of the tenant
rootCause: The tenant is part of Microsoft de-authentication initiative for Microsoft-sponsored/repro tenants
solution: Validate if the tenant is part of the de-auth initiative at the SharePoint page; if included in de-auth scope, do not bypass the lockout; provide the MS POC with the de-auth reference article

sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/Azure AD Conditional Access Policy/Tenant Lockout TA Instructions
symptom: Geneva Action tenant lockout bypass fails with error: Latest Azure Portal login was blocked because of per-user MFA
rootCause: The admin is blocked by per-user MFA, not Conditional Access policies; the Geneva Action (ESTSConditionalAccessExtension) can only bypass CA/IPC/SD policies, not per-user MFA
solution: Contact the Data Protection Team to help with per-user MFA bypass; the CA policy exclusion action cannot exempt per-user MFA. DPT will bypass if lockout is due to Per-User MFA or if lockout can be corrected by re-registering MFA method

sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/Azure AD Conditional Access Policy/Tenant Lockout TA Instructions
symptom: Tenant admin is locked out of Azure Portal despite having admin role; admin account flagged as GDPR minor
rootCause: The admin account has been flagged as a GDPR minor which blocks access to Azure Portal regardless of admin role, preventing admin actions
solution: Elevate another account (not flagged as minor) via Data Protection Team; if no other account available, engage MSODS team for service-side edit to remove the minor flag from the account

sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/Azure AD ESTS/FIC + MSI ESTS Troubleshooting Guide
symptom: Workload identity federation fails with AADSTS7002121: No matching federated identity record found for presented assertion audience
rootCause: The FIC audience, subject, or issuer does not match the configuration on the user-assigned managed identity or app registration; matching is case-sensitive
solution: Verify FIC config: 1) Audiences must be api://AzureADTokenExchange for public clouds; 2) Compare Issuer/Subject/Audience from ASC sign-in logs against FIC config; 3) Matching is case-sensitive; 4) Ensure ManagedIdentityResourceID exists for user-assigned MI; 5) If FIC token missing from incoming credentials, issue is on app side

sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/Azure AD ESTS/FIC + MSI ESTS Troubleshooting Guide
symptom: Authentication fails with AADSTS700231: Token obtained using another federated identity credential may not be used as federated identity credential
rootCause: Customer code uses chained FIC flow (FIC1 -> FIC2 -> Access Token) which is not supported; an app with FIC obtains a token that is then used as another FIC input
solution: Modify code to use FIC directly to get access token for target Azure resource without chaining. Use DefaultAzureCredential with ManagedIdentityClientId. Chained FIC is not a supported scenario; code correction is not AAD team scope

sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/Azure AD ESTS/How ESTS detects Client IP
symptom: Non-interactive sign-in IP in Entra sign-in logs does not match the app server IP for confidential client apps using refresh token grant
rootCause: By design: ESTS uses OriginalIPAddressClaim (ClientIPSource=4) for refresh token grant of confidential client apps, showing IP from original token issuance instead of current app server IP
solution: Expected behavior. To find actual app server IP, search XFwdIp in ASC PerRequestLogs column. The displayed Client IP comes from the incoming Refresh Token

sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/Azure AD ESTS/How ESTS detects Client IP
symptom: In device code flow, client IP in sign-in logs reflects the remote device that initiated device code request, not the device where user performed interactive login
rootCause: By design: ESTS uses DeviceFlowUserCode (ClientIPSource=6) to reflect remote device IP since final tokens are returned to original/remote device; CA IP policies evaluated for that device
solution: Expected behavior. Confirm by checking ClientIPSource in ASC sign-in diagnostic logs (should show DeviceFlowUserCode/6). CA IP-based policies are correctly evaluated against the original/remote device IP

sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/Azure AD ESTS/How ESTS detects Client IP
symptom: Azure VM sign-in logs show IPv6 client IP instead of expected IPv4; CA policies based on IPv4 VNet named location fail to apply
rootCause: Due to SFI effort, Azure Network added AzureActiveDirectory service endpoint tag to 1st party subscriptions, routing requests through VNet Service Tunnel using IPv6 converted from IPv4
solution: Check if tenant/VNet is in VnetIdsThatCanOverrideClientIp list in ESTS. If listed, ESTS overrides client IP with IPv4 from Gateway x-ms-vnet-client-ip header (ClientIPSource=VnetClientIp). If not listed, escalate to ESTS to add exception

sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/Azure AD ESTS/How ESTS detects Credential Type
symptom: During Windows OOBE for Microsoft Entra Join, federated domain user sees account does not exist error when entering UPN on sign-in screen
rootCause: SAML-based federation is not supported for Entra Join scenario; GetCredentialType API returns IfExistsResult=1 (NotExists) even though UPN exists in Entra ID
solution: Use WS-Fed protocol instead of SAML for federation if Entra Join is required. SAML federation does not support OOBE device join scenario. Ref: Plan your Microsoft Entra join deployment

sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/Azure AD ESTS/How ESTS detects Credential Type
symptom: Guest user (B2B) sees account does not exist error when trying to authenticate, even though the guest account exists in the tenant
rootCause: Email OTP authentication method is disabled in the tenant authentication methods policy; with no available auth method for the guest, GetCredentialType returns IfExistsResult=1 (NotExists)
solution: Enable Email OTP in the authentication methods policy; verify the authentication scenario and methods are supported and enabled by tenant admins

sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/Azure AD ESTS/Understanding AAD Gateway and Backup Authentication in Microsoft Entra ID
symptom: Authentication request returns HTTP 403 or 500 error without standard AADSTS error codes; STS-specific headers (x-ms-request-id, x-ms-ests-server) missing from response
rootCause: Request was routed to AAD Gateway backup authentication system (CCS) instead of primary STS endpoints; happens during preventive testing or outage resilience routing. Header x-ms-gateway-slice=CCS confirms this
solution: 1) Check response headers for x-ms-gateway-slice=CCS and X-FEServer; 2) Query addgwwst Kusto cluster AllRequestSummaryEvents to verify TargetService=CSS; 3) Check application-side logs; 4) Missing STS headers confirms request did not reach STS

sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/Azure AD MFA/AAD MFA KNOWN ISSUES
symptom: Phone number authentication method does not appear under Authentication Methods in Azure portal for legacy users, even though it is visible in MySecurityInfo.
rootCause: PhoneMethod entry was never migrated from legacy backend to StrongAuthenticationMethod schema. Incomplete method displays in MySecurityInfo but not under Authentication Methods in portal.
solution: No customer-facing workaround. PG backlog item exists to address parity between both views (ICM 672804544). Inform customer this is a known issue affecting a subset of legacy users.

sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/Azure AD MFA/AAD MFA KNOWN ISSUES
symptom: Admin user gets 403 error when calling UserAuthenticationMethods Graph API or viewing user authentication methods in portal. mysecurityinfo cannot load user method detail. Fiddler/HAR shows 403 on /api/authenticationmethods/availablemethods.
rootCause: User or group is assigned to Microsoft Graph service principal (appId 00000003-0000-0000-c000-000000000000) with appRoles. Access token contains both roles and scp claims. When both present, Graph API uses intersection to check access, resulting in 403.
solution: Check Graph SP appRoleAssignedTo: GET /servicePrincipals(appId='00000003-0000-0000-c000-000000000000')/appRoleAssignedTo?$top=200&$count=true. Either add proper role assignments or remove all appRole assignments for the affected user/group.

sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/Azure AD MFA/AAD MFA KNOWN ISSUES
symptom: User cannot use TOTP code for MFA sign-in. After entering TOTP code in sign-in UI, nothing happens. ESTS/SAS log shows error code PhoneAppOTPOathCodeCorrectbutDeviceNotallowed.
rootCause: User entered a valid TOTP code matching a registered device, but the TOTP device flavor (e.g., third-party software TOTP) is not allowed for MFA authentication. Registration is allowed for both SSPR and MFA, but sign-in only checks MFA policy.
solution: Check user in ASC -> Users -> Authentication methods to verify which TOTP device types are allowed for MFA. Ensure user is using an allowed device flavor (e.g., Microsoft Authenticator TOTP vs. third-party TOTP).

sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/Azure AD MFA/AAD MFA KNOWN ISSUES
symptom: Users unable to complete MFA on NPS Extension deployment. NPS Extension event logs (AuthZOptCh) show Access Rejected with Azure MFA response: RequestRedirected and message: The authentication has been redirected to the appropriate region.
rootCause: Multiple simultaneous MFA authentication attempts for the same user on NPS Extension trigger a redirect conflict in the Azure MFA backend.
solution: Workaround: Configure NPS/user experience to prevent duplicate MFA attempts on the same account within a 2-minute timeframe. PG fix pending (bug 3113001, ICM 572758874).

sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/Azure AD MFA/AAD MFA KNOWN ISSUES
symptom: Users trying to change an already-registered phone number in My Security Info using Change get error: Phone number is already registered for this account, please enter a different phone number - even when entering a different number.
rootCause: Bug in My Security Info Change flow for phone number authentication method (ICM 559885796).
solution: Workaround: Delete the registered phone number using Delete, then register the new number using Add sign-in method.

sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/Azure AD MFA/AAD MFA KNOWN ISSUES
symptom: User is redirected to register MFA method despite having usable methods registered, or stuck in redirect loop to My Security Info page with no method for registration.
rootCause: User default method is non-usable (disabled in Authentication methods policy) and system-preferred method is disabled. Also triggered when user is enabled for External Authentication Method (EAM).
solution: Delete or change the non-usable default method to a usable one. Prevention: before disabling an auth method policy or excluding a user, verify no affected users have that method as default.

sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/Azure AD MFA/AAD MFA KNOWN ISSUES
symptom: After admin disables third-party software TOTP or hardware TOTP authentication method policy, users who previously registered can still use third-party TOTP codes for MFA successfully.
rootCause: Disabling the authentication method policy does not revoke already-registered third-party TOTP methods. Known bug tracked by PG (work item 2953713).
solution: No customer-facing workaround currently available. Admin must manually remove the registered third-party TOTP methods from affected users.

sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/Azure AD MFA/AAD MFA KNOWN ISSUES
symptom: B2B guest users who are also GDAP admins unable to register MFA methods on aka.ms/mysecurityinfo. Users see Keep your account secure page with no methods, We ran into a problem error, or redirect loop.
rootCause: GDAP guest admins cannot manage their own security information at My Security Info (by design, bug 2863208). User is both CSP (DAP/GDAP) and B2B guest.
solution: Configure cross-tenant access policies to trust MFA from the trusted CSP tenant. Otherwise, tenant admin must register methods (SMS or Voice) on behalf of the GDAP guest admin. Ref: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/partner-center/customers/gdap-faq

sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/Azure AD MFA/AAD MFA KNOWN ISSUES
symptom: Users unable to register account in Microsoft Authenticator app on Android device. Auth app logs show PAD_VALIDATION_FAILED_NO_NOTIFICATION_ONDEVICE and TOO_MANY_REGISTRATIONS com.google.firebase error.
rootCause: Firebase Cloud Messaging (FCM) returns TOO_MANY_REGISTRATIONS error when device has too many apps registered with FCM. Not a Microsoft Authenticator issue.
solution: Delete unused apps from device to free FCM registration slots, or factory reset if device was cloned. For FCM support, contact Google/Firebase.

sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/Azure AD MFA/AAD MFA KNOWN ISSUES
symptom: User cannot register MFA. MFA registration fails silently or with error when user belongs to many groups.
rootCause: Service limitation: if user has more than 2049 transitive group memberships, MFA registration fails.
solution: Reduce user transitive group membership below 2049. Check count: Graph Explorer GET /users/{id}/transitiveMemberOf/$count. Ref: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/entra/identity/users/directory-service-limits-restrictions

sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/Azure AD MFA/AAD MFA KNOWN ISSUES
symptom: Guest users stuck in redirect loop or blocked when trying to register security info. User is interrupted to register, redirected to My Security Info, then redirected back to interrupt repeatedly.
rootCause: Circular dependency when guest users are included in Identity Protection MFA registration policy AND inbound MFA trust is configured in Cross-tenant access policies.
solution: Exclude external users from Identity Protection MFA registration policy when trusting MFA for external users. Ref: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/entra/external-id/cross-tenant-access-overview

sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/Azure AD MFA/AAD MFA KNOWN ISSUES
symptom: User in scope of CBA (Certificate Based Authentication) and SSPR/MFA combined registration cannot complete registration, stuck in loop. System-preferred MFA methods is enabled.
rootCause: When system-preferred methods is enabled and user is in scope of CBA policy, registration flow requires CBA challenge which creates circular dependency preventing SSPR/MFA method registration.
solution: Exclude the CBA policy group from MFA system-preferred methods. Or use Temporary Access Pass (TAP) to satisfy MFA requirement and complete SSPR registration.

sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/Azure AD MFA/AAD MFA KNOWN ISSUES
symptom: MFA or PSI registration in Microsoft Authenticator app fails during interactive token acquisition step for Auth Connector resource.
rootCause: Tenant admin has Conditional Access policy targeting all cloud apps, which started being applied to Auth Connector resource used by Authenticator app for MFA/PSI registration.
solution: Exclude the Auth Connector resource from the Conditional Access policy that targets all cloud apps. ICM 373439038.

sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/Azure AD MFA/AAD MFA KNOWN ISSUES
symptom: NPS/MFA extension on Windows Server 2022 crashes after processing one MFA request. DllHost.exe crash in msvcrt.dll with exception code 0x40000015. On service restart works for one request then crashes again.
rootCause: Incompatibility between NPS Extension DLLs (mfanpsauthnext.dll, mfanpsauthzext.dll) and newer Visual C++ runtime installed on Windows Server 2022.
solution: Update NPS Extension to version 1.2.1959.1 or later which resolves the crash.

sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/Azure AD MFA/AAD MFA KNOWN ISSUES
symptom: Using Graph API to register mobile phone number for MFA sends SMS to user even when SMS is disabled as auth method in tenant. Impacts NPS Extension and ADFS MFA Adapter.
rootCause: Graph API phone registration defaults to SMS delivery regardless of tenant SMS policy setting.
solution: Workaround: Set the phone number in the Office phone field instead of mobile phone, which triggers a phone call instead of SMS. PG tracking: work item 1268750.

symptom: PTA agent appears as inactive on the Azure portal. The Microsoft Azure AD Connect Authentication Agent connector status shows inactive under Azure AD Connect > Pass-through authentication.
rootCause: The SSL client certificate used by the PTA agent has expired and could not be renewed automatically. The certificate is required for the agent to communicate with the Azure AD service via the bootstrap endpoint.
solution: Verify SSL client certificate existence and validity in the local machine certificate store. Follow Microsoft docs to check Application Proxy trust certificate support. If the certificate is expired or missing, uninstall the PTA connector and reinstall it to generate a new certificate.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/Azure AD Pass-Through Authentication/Azure AD Pass-Through Authentication (PTA) - PTA agent appears as "inactive" on the portal

symptom: PTA agent appears as inactive on the Azure portal. The connector cannot reach the bootstrap endpoint {TENANTID}.pta.bootstrap.his.msappproxy.net.
rootCause: Network communication issue prevents the PTA agent from completing the periodic bootstrap call (every 10 minutes). Possible causes: TCP 80/443 blocked for outbound traffic, SSL inspection intercepting traffic, required Azure URLs not whitelisted, or DNS resolution failure for the bootstrap endpoint.
solution: Verify: (1) TCP 80/443 are open outbound towards Azure endpoints. (2) SSL inspection is disabled for PTA agent traffic. (3) All URLs from the PTA Quick Start prerequisites are accessible. (4) DNS resolves {TENANTID}.pta.bootstrap.his.msappproxy.net (test with ping). If using proxy, ensure correct proxy configuration in AzureADConnectAuthenticationAgentService.exe.config.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/Azure AD Pass-Through Authentication/Azure AD Pass-Through Authentication (PTA) - PTA agent appears as "inactive" on the portal

symptom: PTA agent appears as inactive on the Azure portal. The agent server accesses the Internet through an outbound proxy or has unexpected proxy settings.
rootCause: Proxy misconfiguration in one or more locations: (1) AzureADConnectAuthenticationAgentService.exe.config has incorrect proxy syntax or unwanted proxy setting; (2) WinHTTP proxy configured (netsh winhttp show proxy); (3) machine.config under Framework64/v4.0.30319/Config has proxy setting; (4) IE proxy set in Network Service context. The proxy address must use format protocol://proxy:port.
solution: For direct Internet access: remove proxy config or add <defaultProxy enabled='false'></defaultProxy> to the config file. For proxy access: verify proxy URL format is protocol://proxy:port (not bare hostname). Check all proxy sources (config file, WinHTTP, machine.config, IE). Restart the Microsoft Azure AD Connect Authentication Agent service after any changes.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/Azure AD Pass-Through Authentication/Azure AD Pass-Through Authentication (PTA) - PTA agent appears as "inactive" on the portal

symptom: PTA agent appears as inactive on the Azure portal. The Microsoft Azure AD Connect Authentication Agent service is not running on the connector server.
rootCause: The Microsoft AAD Authentication Agent service has been manually stopped or has crashed. Without the service running, the connector cannot perform the periodic bootstrap call and will show as inactive.
solution: Open services.msc and restart the Microsoft Azure AD Connect Authentication Agent service. If the service keeps crashing, collect data using the Data Collector script (PTA-tracingVxx.ps1 -ServiceTraceOn), restart the service, wait 60 seconds, stop the collection, and upload traces for analysis.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/Azure AD Pass-Through Authentication/Azure AD Pass-Through Authentication (PTA) - PTA agent appears as "inactive" on the portal

symptom: PTA agent appears as inactive on the Azure portal intermittently, even though authentication through PTA is working correctly.
rootCause: Azure Portal occasionally displays incorrect inactive status for the connector despite the bootstrap communication happening at regular 10-minute intervals. This is a known portal display issue (rare).
solution: Use Kusto to query BootstrapRootOperationEvent table in AADHIS database (Idsharedwus cluster) to verify bootstrap is occurring every 10-11 minutes. If bootstrap is normal, collect a Fiddler trace while portal shows inactive and look for the GET request to main.iam.ad.ext.azure.com/api/Directories/PassThroughAuthConnectorGroups to confirm portal-side issue.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/Azure AD Pass-Through Authentication/Azure AD Pass-Through Authentication (PTA) - PTA agent appears as "inactive" on the portal
Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/Azure AD MFA/TSG: Self-mitigation steps for users that cannot setup MFA because they already have 5 device or hardware tokens
symptom: User cannot set up MFA authenticator app. Error: 'You cannot have more than 5 hardware tokens or authenticator apps' or 'You have too many devices registered.'
rootCause: User previously registered 5 different phones for MFA. Device tokens persisted in directory user object StrongAuthenticationPhoneAppDetail property, hitting the 5-device limit.
solution: 1) Admin: Azure portal > AAD user > Authentication methods > 'Require re-register MFA'. 2) User: navigate to aka.ms/mysecurityinfo to delete old device tokens. 3) Alternative: Graph API DELETE https://graph.microsoft.com/beta/users/{UPN}/authentication/microsoftAuthenticatorMethods/{id} (requires UserAuthenticationMethod.ReadWrite.All). 4) If only admin on tenant: create ICM to Credential Enforcement and Provisioning/Triage.

sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/Azure AD Pass-Through Authentication/Azure AD Pass-Through Authentication (PTA) - Active Directory Errors and Root Causes
symptom: PTA sign-in fails with AADSTS50126 (AD error 1326): 'The user name or password is incorrect.'
rootCause: Incorrect password or user cannot be located in AD based on the username from Azure AD.
solution: Verify correct password. Confirm user object exists in on-premises AD with matching UPN/sAMAccountName. Check AD domain reachability from PTA agent.

sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/Azure AD Pass-Through Authentication/Azure AD Pass-Through Authentication (PTA) - Active Directory Errors and Root Causes
symptom: PTA sign-in fails with AADSTS80012 (AD error 1328): 'Your account has time restrictions that keep you from signing in right now.'
rootCause: User AD account has logon hour restrictions configured preventing sign-in at current time.
solution: AD Users and Computers > user Properties > Account tab > 'Logon Hours...' > adjust allowed logon days/hours.

sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/Azure AD Pass-Through Authentication/Azure AD Pass-Through Authentication (PTA) - Active Directory Errors and Root Causes
symptom: PTA sign-in fails with AADSTS80007 (AD error 1329): 'This user is not allowed to sign in to this computer.'
rootCause: User AD account has workstation logon restrictions; PTA server not in allowed computer list.
solution: AD Users and Computers > user Properties > Account tab > 'Log On To...' > add PTA agent server to allowed list.

sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/Azure AD Pass-Through Authentication/Azure AD Pass-Through Authentication (PTA) - Active Directory Errors and Root Causes
symptom: PTA sign-in fails with AADSTS50057 (AD error 1331): 'This user cannot sign in because this account is currently disabled.'
rootCause: The user account is disabled in Active Directory.
solution: Check and re-enable the user account on the Domain Controller if disabled unintentionally.

sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/Azure AD Pass-Through Authentication/Azure AD Pass-Through Authentication (PTA) - Active Directory Errors and Root Causes
symptom: PTA sign-in fails with AADSTS80007 (AD error 1384): 'During a logon attempt, the user security context accumulated too many security IDs.'
rootCause: User is member of over 1024 groups (including nesting and SID history from migration), exceeding security token max size.
solution: Remove user from some global/local groups. Common after user migration (SID History). Ref: https://learn.microsoft.com/troubleshoot/windows-server/windows-security/logging-on-user-account-fails

sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/Azure AD Pass-Through Authentication/Azure AD Pass-Through Authentication (PTA) - Active Directory Errors and Root Causes
symptom: PTA sign-in fails with AADSTS80007 (AD error 1385): 'Logon failure: the user has not been granted the requested logon type at this computer.'
rootCause: GPO policy 'Access this computer from the network' / 'Deny access to this computer from the network' blocks the user from network logon to PTA agent server.
solution: Check local and domain GPO: Computer Configuration > Windows Settings > Security Settings > Local Policies > User Rights Assignment. Ensure user is in 'Access this computer from the network' and not in 'Deny access'.

sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/Azure AD Pass-Through Authentication/Azure AD Pass-Through Authentication (PTA) - Active Directory Errors and Root Causes
symptom: PTA sign-in prompts 'The user password must be changed before signing in' (AD error 1907).
rootCause: 'User must change password at next logon' flag is set in Active Directory.
solution: User needs to change password. If cannot sign in, admin can clear the flag or reset password directly in AD.

sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/Azure AD Pass-Through Authentication/Azure AD Pass-Through Authentication (PTA) - Active Directory Errors and Root Causes
symptom: PTA sign-in fails with AD error 1909: 'The referenced account is currently locked out and may not be logged on to.'
rootCause: User account is locked out in Active Directory due to exceeding lockout threshold.
solution: Unlock user account in AD. Investigate lockout source (brute force, stale credentials, service accounts). Consider adjusting Azure AD Smart Lockout to protect on-premises accounts.

sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/Azure AD Pass-Through Authentication/Azure AD Pass-Through Authentication (PTA) - Active Directory Errors and Root Causes
symptom: PTA sign-in fails with AADSTS80018: 'Unauthorized or forbidden access of encryption keys.'
rootCause: No PTA agent is properly installed and registered with Azure AD, or agent encryption keys are invalid/expired.
solution: Ensure at least one PTA agent is installed and registered. Re-register agent if needed. Verify agent service is running and can reach Azure AD on port 443.

sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/Azure AD Pass-Through Authentication/Azure AD Pass-Through Authentication (PTA) - Active Directory Errors and Root Causes
symptom: PTA sign-in fails with AADSTS80015: 'Validation request budget exceeded.' Authentication times out.
rootCause: HIS service has a 14-second authentication budget (eSTS waits 15s). Network issues cause initial auth to fail, retries within the window also fail.
solution: Investigate underlying auth failure. Check network between PTA agent and DC. Review PTA agent trace logs and DC event logs for the actual error. AADSTS80015 is a timeout wrapper around the real issue.

sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/Azure AD Pass-Through Authentication/Azure AD Pass-Through Authentication (PTA) - Active Directory Errors and Root Causes
symptom: PTA sign-in fails with AADSTS80007 (AD error 1787): 'The security database on the server does not have a computer account for this workstation trust relationship.'
rootCause: PTA agent server's computer account trust relationship with the domain is broken.
solution: Re-establish trust: 1) Remove and re-join server to domain, 2) Reset-ComputerMachinePassword, or 3) netdom resetpwd. Note: root cause under PG investigation.

sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/Azure AD Pass-Through Authentication/Azure AD Pass-Through Authentication (PTA)
symptom: PTA sign-in fails with AADSTS80001: 'Unable to connect to Active Directory.'
rootCause: PTA connector machine is not domain-joined or cannot reach a Domain Controller.
solution: Ensure PTA connector is domain-joined with valid computer account. Verify DNS resolution and network connectivity to DCs.

sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/Azure AD Pass-Through Authentication/Azure AD Pass-Through Authentication (PTA)
symptom: PTA sign-in fails with AADSTS80002: 'A timeout occurred connecting to Active Directory.'
rootCause: AD unavailable or not responding. Network latency, DC overload, or firewall between connector and DC.
solution: Check AD availability. Verify network connectivity and latency between PTA connector and DCs. Ensure no firewall blocking LDAP/Kerberos traffic.

sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/Azure AD Pass-Through Authentication/Azure AD Pass-Through Authentication (PTA)
symptom: AAD Connect PTA configuration fails: 'Showing a modal dialog box or form when the application is not running in UserInteractive mode is not a valid operation.'
rootCause: MFA is enabled for the Global Administrator account used to configure PTA, triggering an interactive MFA prompt in non-interactive context.
solution: Temporarily disable MFA for the cloud-only Global Administrator account before enabling PTA feature in Azure AD Connect. Re-enable MFA after configuration completes.

symptom: GSA BYOD - B2B Guest access "Switch tenant" option is not visible in the Global Secure Access client on registered (non-joined) Windows devices.
rootCause: Intermittent UI issue in the GSA client where the B2B Guest access switch tenant option may fail to render after initial sign-in.
solution: Restart the Windows device. After restart, the switch tenant option should become visible in the GSA client.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/Microsoft Entra Global Secure Access (ZTNA)/GSA Bring Your Own Device (BYOD)

symptom: GSA DLP/ATP - Alert events appear in Traffic logs but are missing from the Alerts blade in the Global Secure Access portal.
rootCause: The alert action was logged in traffic logs but the traffic may not have been properly routed to Netskope, or Netskope did not generate a corresponding alert entry. The transactionId in traffic logs maps to partnerTransactionId in the Alerts blade.
solution: Query TalonOperationEvent table in Kusto (idsharedwus cluster, NaasProd database) to check if VendorName contains "Netskope" and review securityProfileMetadata. If Netskope is present but alert is still missing from the Alerts blade, file an ICM to GSA Control Plane team.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/Microsoft Entra Global Secure Access (ZTNA)/GSA Data Loss Prevention

symptom: GSA TLS Inspection - Every TLS connection fails with "Your connection isn't private" (NET::ERR_CERT_AUTHORITY_INVALID). Clicking Advanced shows the TLS certificate is not trusted. Stopping the Global Secure Access Engine Service resolves the error. Intune sync may also fail with 0x80072f8f.
rootCause: The public .cer file for the trusted root certificate configured in GSA Settings > Session management > TLS Inspection tab is either missing from the client computer's Trusted Root Certificate store, or the .cer file present does not match the certificate in the TLS Inspection tab.
solution: Download the .CER file from the certificate in the TLS Inspection tab (GSA portal > Settings > Session management > TLS Inspection) and import it into the Trusted Root Certificate store of the client computer.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/Microsoft Entra Global Secure Access (ZTNA)/GSA Data Loss Prevention

symptom: GSA TLS Inspection - Intune Policy Sync fails with "The sync could not be initiated (0x80190194 Not found (404))" after enabling TLS inspection in Global Secure Access.
rootCause: Intune sync endpoints (checkin.dm.microsoft.com, r.manage.microsoft.com) are incorrectly intercepted by TLS inspection policy as Internet traffic instead of being bypassed as M365 traffic.
solution: Create a Custom Bypass rule: Navigate to Connect > Traffic forwarding > Internet access profile > Internet access policies > View > Custom Bypass > Add rule. Set Destination type to FQDN and enter: checkin.dm.microsoft.com,r.manage.microsoft.com. Save and disable/re-enable the GSA client to pick up the change.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/Microsoft Entra Global Secure Access (ZTNA)/GSA Data Loss Prevention

symptom: GSA TLS Inspection - Windows Update fails with error 0x801901f6 ("There were some problems installing updates") after enabling TLS inspection in Global Secure Access.
rootCause: Windows Update endpoints (fe2cr.update.microsoft.com, slscr.update.microsoft.com, fe3cr.delivery.mp.microsoft.com) are incorrectly intercepted by TLS inspection policy as Internet traffic instead of being bypassed.
solution: Create a Custom Bypass rule: Navigate to Connect > Traffic forwarding > Internet access profile > Internet access policies > View > Custom Bypass > Add rule. Set Destination type to FQDN and enter: fe2cr.update.microsoft.com,slscr.update.microsoft.com,fe3cr.delivery.mp.microsoft.com. Save and disable/re-enable the GSA client to pick up the change.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/Microsoft Entra Global Secure Access (ZTNA)/GSA Data Loss Prevention
Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/Microsoft Entra Global Secure Access (ZTNA)/GSA Deployment Logs
symptom: Clicking Request Id link in GSA Deployment Logs blade returns error "The requested feature isn't implemented" (HTTP 501 Not Implemented). Browser trace shows GET call to graph.microsoft.com/beta/networkaccess/deployments/{requestId}?$expand=configuration/microsoft.graph.networkaccess.profileConfiguration/current fails with 501.
rootCause: During Private Preview, the Deployment logs feature does not support Security Provider policies. The event details endpoint is not yet implemented for these policy types.
solution: Engineering investigating (ICM-629567797). A fix is being deployed to support all policy types in the system; expected completion by end of month. No customer-side workaround available during preview.

sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/Microsoft Entra Global Secure Access (ZTNA)/GSA External User Access (B2B)
symptom: External users (B2B guests) cannot access on-premises resources requiring Kerberos authentication (e.g., SMB file shares, applications using Integrated Windows Authentication) in the resource tenant via Global Secure Access Private Access.
rootCause: Microsoft Entra B2B collaboration does not provide Kerberos tickets. Global Secure Access Private Access does not proxy Kerberos or support Kerberos Constrained Delegation (KCD) for external users.
solution: For web applications, the only supported method is publishing the app through Application Proxy with KCD. See: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/entra/identity/app-proxy/how-to-configure-sso-with-kcd. SMB file shares are not supported for B2B users via GSA.

sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/Microsoft Entra Global Secure Access (ZTNA)/GSA External User Access (B2B)
symptom: Switching GSA client account to resource tenant fails when the resource tenant is configured for required MFA in cross-tenant settings and the home tenant uses passwordless sign-in (PSI) on the Microsoft Authenticator app.
rootCause: Known limitation: the external user access feature does not support the combination of required MFA in resource tenant cross-tenant config with passwordless sign-in (PSI) on Authenticator in home tenant.
solution: No workaround currently available. This is a documented known limitation. Avoid enforcing MFA in cross-tenant settings when home tenant users rely on passwordless sign-in via Authenticator.

sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/Microsoft Entra Global Secure Access (ZTNA)/GSA External User Access (B2B)
symptom: External user access in resource tenant fails when compliant network policies are enforced for private applications. External users from other tenants are blocked despite having correct permissions.
rootCause: External users cannot meet the compliant network requirement from an external tenant. The compliant network check does not recognize external tenant GSA connections.
solution: External users must be excluded from compliant network Conditional Access policies applied to private applications. Create an exclusion group for B2B guests in the CA policy conditions.

symptom: GSA health check for 'NRPT rules set' reports False; Private DNS not working because DNS suffix search list on the Windows client does not include the required single label suffix
rootCause: The single label suffix required by GSA Private DNS is missing from the DNS Suffix Search List on the client machine, preventing NRPT rules from being applied correctly
solution: Run IPCONFIG /ALL to verify DNS Suffix Search List. If suffix is missing, collect Advanced log collection + Process Monitor while disabling/re-enabling GSA client to investigate why the suffix is not being added
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/Microsoft Entra Global Secure Access (ZTNA)/Internal Docs/Global Secure Access - NRPT issues

symptom: GSA Private DNS not working; `netsh namespace show policy` returns empty output even though NRPT registry entries exist under HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Services\Dnscache\Parameters\DnsPolicyConfig
rootCause: A malformed Group Policy Object (GPO) creates an empty DnsPolicyConfig key at HKLM\SOFTWARE\Policies\Microsoft\Windows NT\DNSClient\DnsPolicyConfig, which overrides the local NRPT rules configured by the GSA client
solution: On domain controller, run the SYSVOL scanning PowerShell script to identify GPOs with empty DnsPolicyConfig. Use Set-GPRegistryValue + Remove-GPRegistryValue with the GPO GUID to fix the malformed policy. Run gpupdate /force on client, then disable/re-enable GSA client. Ref: internal KB ce0714d9-819f-f3fa-c8e2-e8c8c8dd4735
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/Microsoft Entra Global Secure Access (ZTNA)/Internal Docs/Global Secure Access - NRPT issues

symptom: GSA health check 'NRPT rules set' reports False on Windows computers with non-English language settings, even though Private DNS functions correctly and netsh namespace show policy displays policies
rootCause: Known product bug - the NRPT health check logic fails to parse output correctly on non-English OS locale (tracked: identitydivision work item 3099172)
solution: Confirm Private DNS is actually working by verifying DNS suffix search list and netsh output. This is a false positive from the health check. Track engineering fix via work item: https://identitydivision.visualstudio.com/Engineering/_workitems/edit/3099172
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/Microsoft Entra Global Secure Access (ZTNA)/Internal Docs/Global Secure Access - NRPT issues

symptom: After signing out from GSA client, DNS resolution fails for destinations with publicly resolvable names (e.g., ADFS) because NRPT configuration added by GSA client is not cleared on sign-out
rootCause: Known product bug - GSA client sign-out does not remove the NRPT rules it created, causing DNS traffic to still be directed through GSA tunnels that are no longer active (tracked: identitydivision work item 3180409)
solution: Workaround: disable/re-enable GSA client or manually clear NRPT config after sign-out. Track engineering fix via work item: https://identitydivision.visualstudio.com/Engineering/_workitems/edit/3180409
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/Microsoft Entra Global Secure Access (ZTNA)/Internal Docs/Global Secure Access - NRPT issues

symptom: GSA Threat Intelligence policy not blocking malicious sites immediately after configuration - users can still access threat sites for up to 90 minutes after policy creation and CA linkage
rootCause: Security profiles in GSA are enforced via access tokens. New or updated security profiles take 60-90 minutes to propagate and become effective across all user sessions.
solution: Wait 60-90 minutes after configuring the Threat Intelligence policy and linking it to a Conditional Access policy. Clear browser cache on test devices. Verify enforcement by navigating to test threat sites (e.g., entratestthreat.com, smartscreentestratings2.net). Check GSA Network Traffic Logs for action taken (may take up to 5 minutes to appear).
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/Microsoft Entra Global Secure Access (ZTNA)/GSA Threat Intelligence Filtering

symptom: GSA Web Category Checker API (Graph beta) returns only one category for a URL even though the URL may belong to multiple web categories
rootCause: Known limitation - the Web Category Checker tool (microsoft.graph.networkaccess.getWebCategoryByUrl) currently returns only the first category. This is API-only with no UI in the Entra admin center.
solution: For full category information, check the Web Category column in GSA Traffic Logs instead of relying solely on the API. If a URL needs encoding (spaces/query strings), URL-encode the @url parameter. To dispute categorization, email gsawebcatdispute@service.microsoft.com with FQDN, Category Returned, Category Expected, Justification.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/Microsoft Entra Global Secure Access (ZTNA)/GSA Web Category Checker

symptom: GSA blocks Copilot Studio agent traffic but shows 502 Bad Gateway for HTTP Actions or 403 Forbidden for connectors instead of a proper GSA block page
rootCause: Known issue - the block experience for Copilot Studio agents does not yet show a standard GSA block page. HTTP Node traffic returns 502 Bad Gateway and connector traffic returns 403 Forbidden when blocked by GSA security policies.
solution: This is a known issue and improvements are coming. To verify the block was from GSA, check GSA Traffic Logs (Entra portal > Global Secure Access > Monitor > Traffic Logs). Use Kusto query on TalonOperationEvent table to see PolicyAction and ResponseCode. Filter by TunnelType==AiAgentPlatform and TenantId.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/Microsoft Entra Global Secure Access (ZTNA)/GSA for Copilot Studio Agents

symptom: GSA security policies linked to Conditional Access security profiles are not applied to Copilot Studio agent traffic - only baseline profile policies take effect
rootCause: Known limitation - GSA for Copilot Studio Agents only supports the baseline profile for enforcement. Security profiles linked to Conditional Access policies are not currently supported for agent traffic. Policies are per-tenant only.
solution: Configure all security policies for Copilot Studio agents (web content filtering, threat intelligence, network file filtering) under the baseline profile in GSA. Do not rely on CA-linked security profiles for agent traffic. After enabling GSA for Agents in Power Platform, create or update custom connectors for them to route through GSA.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/Microsoft Entra Global Secure Access (ZTNA)/GSA for Copilot Studio Agents

symptom: GSA Prompt Shield policy does not detect or block malicious prompts in AI apps that use URL-based encoding (e.g., Google Gemini) - only JSON-based GenAI apps are protected
rootCause: Known limitation - Prompt Shield currently supports only JSON-based generative AI apps. Apps using URL-based encoding for prompt submission are not supported. Only text prompts are supported (not files).
solution: Verify the customer AI app uses JSON-based communication. If using Gemini or other URL-based AI apps, Prompt Shield cannot protect them. For custom LLMs, configure a custom type model with exact URL and JSON path for optimal performance. For unsupported app types, recommend alternative security controls at the app layer.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/Microsoft Entra Global Secure Access (ZTNA)/Global Secure Access AI Prompt Shield Gateway

symptom: GSA Prompt Shield does not fully scan long prompts - prompts exceeding 10000 characters are silently truncated, potentially allowing malicious content in the truncated portion
rootCause: Known limitation - Prompt Shield supports prompts up to 10000 characters. Anything longer is truncated before scanning. Rate limits also apply when scanning requests for specified conversation schemes.
solution: Be aware that only the first 10K characters of prompts are scanned. For apps generating very long prompts, recommend implementing app-level prompt validation for content beyond 10K chars. To optimize performance for custom LLMs, specify the exact URL and JSON path for each conversation scheme.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/Microsoft Entra Global Secure Access (ZTNA)/Global Secure Access AI Prompt Shield Gateway

symptom: GSA Mac client fails to tunnel FQDN-based traffic when DNS over HTTPS (DoH/Secure DNS) is enabled on the browser or macOS - traffic bypasses GSA and goes directly to internet
rootCause: The GSA Mac client relies on DNS interception to capture FQDN-based traffic. When DNS over HTTPS is enabled (browser or OS level), DNS queries bypass the client interception. IP-based traffic acquisition is not affected.
solution: Disable DNS over HTTPS on both the browser (Chrome: chrome://settings/security, Edge: edge://settings/privacy) and macOS system DNS settings. Verify GSA client status shows connected (green tick in system tray). Network traffic acquired by IP rules is unaffected by DoH.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/Microsoft Entra Global Secure Access (ZTNA)/Global Secure Access For Mac

symptom: GSA Mac client does not tunnel UDP traffic on ports 80/443 (QUIC protocol) for Internet Access workload - QUIC traffic bypasses GSA and goes directly to the destination
rootCause: QUIC protocol is not supported for Internet Access on Mac. Traffic to UDP 80 and 443 cannot be tunneled. QUIC is supported for Private Access and M365 workloads only.
solution: Disable QUIC protocol on browsers to force fallback to HTTPS over TCP (fully supported). For Chrome/Edge: navigate to chrome://flags or edge://flags, search QUIC and set to Disabled. Administrators can also deploy browser policies to disable QUIC organization-wide. Private Access and M365 workloads are not affected.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/Microsoft Entra Global Secure Access (ZTNA)/Global Secure Access For Mac

symptom: Admin configures a Conditional Access policy to block Global Secure Access M365 traffic profile, but users can still access M365 destinations bypassing the policy
rootCause: M365 forwarding traffic policies have hardening set to bypass by default. When a user cannot acquire a token for the M365 traffic profile, traffic goes over the internet without flowing through NaaS. Hardening=tunnel mode is not yet supported.
solution: Confirm the M365 forwarding profile hardening setting is set to bypass (default). If customer requires full blocking, they must wait for tunnel hardening support in GA. Note GSA resource tokens have approximately 3-hour lifetime; admin may need to wait for token expiry or manually clear the token cache (e.g., switch user) to observe policy taking effect.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/Microsoft Entra Global Secure Access (ZTNA)/Global Secure Access as Target Resource in CA

symptom: Admin cannot configure a Conditional Access policy with Global Secure Access as target resource and Compliant Network location condition simultaneously
rootCause: By design: a user cannot come from a compliant network before connecting to one. The Compliant Network location condition in CA can only be used for destination resources after the user and traffic is already flowing through the NaaS service.
solution: Inform customer this is by design. Use the Compliant Network location condition only for destination resource CA policies (after user is connected to NaaS), not for the Global Secure Access target resource itself.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/Microsoft Entra Global Secure Access (ZTNA)/Global Secure Access as Target Resource in CA

symptom: MFA requirement for user groups in Global Secure Access Conditional Access policy causes certain users to get blocked instead of being prompted for MFA registration
rootCause: Known limitation: users who are not MFA registered are not prompted to register for MFA when accessing Global Secure Access. Instead of receiving a registration prompt, they are blocked.
solution: Ensure all users in the targeted user group are MFA registered before applying the CA policy requiring MFA for Global Secure Access. Pre-register users for MFA before enabling the policy.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/Microsoft Entra Global Secure Access (ZTNA)/Global Secure Access as Target Resource in CA

symptom: Global Secure Access tile does not appear in the Microsoft Defender for Endpoint iOS app after onboarding the tenant to Global Secure Access
rootCause: The Defender for Endpoint app may not refresh its UI to show the GSA tile immediately after onboarding is completed.
solution: Force stop the Microsoft Defender for Endpoint app and relaunch it. The GSA tile should then appear on the Defender dashboard.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/Microsoft Entra Global Secure Access (ZTNA)/Global Secure Access client for iOS

symptom: Access to Private Access application shows a connection time-out error on iOS after a successful interactive sign-in via Global Secure Access
rootCause: The initial connection may not fully establish after the first interactive sign-in, causing a transient timeout on the Private Access tunnel.
solution: Reload the application or refresh the web browser. The connection should establish successfully on the second attempt.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/Microsoft Entra Global Secure Access (ZTNA)/Global Secure Access client for iOS

symptom: GSA App Discovery workbook in Log Analytics fails to load data or displays only partial data after configuring diagnostic settings to export Global Secure Access traffic logs.
rootCause: The traffic log export to the Log Analytics workspace may be misconfigured, stale, or incomplete. The Discovered Application Segments workbook relies on properly exported traffic logs in the workspace.
solution: Re-export the Global Secure Access traffic logs by going to Monitor > Diagnostic settings and reconfiguring the export. Then reload the workbook from Monitor > Workbooks. If the issue persists, open an IcM to Microsoft Network as a Service/ZTNA Data Pipeline (template: j1L1U3) with a PSR of the repro and a HAR file.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/Microsoft Entra Global Secure Access (ZTNA)/Microsoft Entra GSA App Discovery

symptom: Private DNS resolution not working for GSA Private Access; internal domain names are not resolved when accessed through Global Secure Access client.
rootCause: Prerequisites not met for Private DNS feature. Requires: (1) Private Access forwarding profile enabled, (2) Microsoft Entra Private Network Connector >= 1.5.3829.0, (3) GSA client >= 2.0.0.0 (version 1.7.376+), (4) IPv6 and Secure DNS / DNS over TCP must be disabled on the client device.
solution: Verify all prerequisites: (1) Enable the Private Access traffic forwarding profile at Global Secure Access > Connect > Traffic Forwarding. (2) Update Private Network Connector to >= 1.5.3829.0 (check via connector server file properties). (3) Update GSA client to >= 2.0.0.0 (check via Advanced Diagnostics > Overview). (4) Disable IPv6 and Secure DNS on each client per https://learn.microsoft.com/entra/global-secure-access/how-to-install-windows-client. (5) Verify DNS suffixes are configured under Quick Access > Private DNS tab. (6) Check NRPT rules with "netsh name show policy" - entries should show DNS server 6.6.255.254.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/Microsoft Entra Global Secure Access (ZTNA)/Private DNS Support for GSA

symptom: SourceIP address is not getting restored in Entra sign-in logs after enabling the Source IP restoration feature in Global Secure Access.
rootCause: Either CA-signaling is disabled/not yet in effect, or the M365 traffic profile is not enabled. There is a known 15-20 minute delay from toggling the SourceIP restoration feature before changes appear in sign-in logs.
solution: Verify: (1) CA Signaling is enabled in the Admin portal and in MS-Graph. (2) CA Signaling is enabled at the NaaS Edge. (3) M365 traffic profile is enabled at Global Secure Access > Connect > Traffic Forwarding. (4) Use the GSA client Health Check tool to confirm the destination URL is flowing through the NaaS service. (5) Wait at least 15-20 minutes after toggling the feature before checking sign-in logs.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/Microsoft Entra Global Secure Access (ZTNA)/SourceIP Restoration

symptom: CAE (Continuous Access Evaluation) strict enforcement is not working with SourceIP restoration in Global Secure Access; IP change from allowed to disallowed range does not trigger token revocation.
rootCause: CAE with SourceIP restoration only supports SharePoint, Exchange, and Microsoft Graph. If the target application is not one of these, CAE enforcement will not work. Also, M365 traffic profile may not be enabled, or CAE session may be disabled in the Conditional Access policy.
solution: Verify: (1) The target application is SharePoint, Exchange, or Microsoft Graph (CAE SourceIP enforcement is not supported for other apps). (2) M365 traffic profile is enabled. (3) CAE session is not disabled in the CA policy. (4) Note that the CAE real-time event trigger typically takes 40-50 seconds after the IP change event occurs.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/Microsoft Entra Global Secure Access (ZTNA)/SourceIP Restoration

symptom: CA Signaling header is missing from Global Secure Access requests, causing SourceIP restoration or compliant network checks to fail.
rootCause: CA Signaling may not be properly enabled or propagated. The header is required for SourceIP restoration and compliant network CA policies to function.
solution: Verify CA Signaling configuration using the dedicated Kusto query on the Datapath-CA-Signaling wiki page (https://supportability.visualstudio.com/AzureAD/_wiki/wikis/AzureAD/1001342/Datapath-CA-Signaling). Check that CA Signaling is enabled both in the Admin portal and at the NaaS Edge.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/Microsoft Entra Global Secure Access (ZTNA)/SourceIP Restoration

symptom: When adding a network segment (FQDN or IP) to a GSA Private Access application, error 'Fully qualified domain name and port overlaps with an application' or 'IP address and port overlaps with an application' appears. Clicking the overlapping application link leads to an empty GSA Private Access configuration page.
rootCause: The original application was deleted via App Registrations blade instead of GSA Enterprise Applications blade, creating an orphaned object that still holds the network segment reservation but is no longer manageable through GSA.
solution: Search the Application ID in App Registrations > Deleted Applications. If found, click Restore App Registration to restore it, then delete or modify via GSA Enterprise Application blade. If permanently deleted, wait at least 1 day before re-adding the segment. Collect browser/Fiddler/HAR trace and use Kusto query on MsgraphApplicationOperationEvent to validate. Escalate via ICM if still unresolved.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/Microsoft Entra Global Secure Access (ZTNA)/Internal Docs/Global Secure Access - Portal issues/Error When Adding a Network Segment

symptom: Entra Private Access application does not appear under Global Secure Access > Enterprise Application. The app may show as a Non-Gallery App or Application Proxy App instead. Users may get AADSTS50105 error. Service principal tags are missing IsAccessibleViaZTNAClient, HideApp, PrivateAccessNonWebApplication.
rootCause: A race condition during GSA application creation prevents the updating of service principal tags. The service principal is created with incorrect tags (missing ZTNA-specific tags), causing it to not appear in the GSA Enterprise Application blade.
solution: PATCH the service principal tags via Graph API: PATCH https://graph.microsoft.com/beta/servicePrincipals/{objectId} with body containing tags: IsAccessibleViaZTNAClient, HideApp, PrivateAccessNonWebApplication, WindowsAzureActiveDirectoryCustomSingleSignOnApplication, WindowsAzureActiveDirectoryIntegratedApp. Alternatively use Update-MgServicePrincipal PowerShell cmdlet, or delete and recreate the application from Enterprise Application blade.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/Microsoft Entra Global Secure Access (ZTNA)/Internal Docs/Global Secure Access - Portal issues/Incorrect Registration of Entra Private Access Apps

symptom: Overlapping network segments in GSA Private Access cause unexpected access behavior. Traffic intended for a specific FQDN (e.g., app1.contoso.com) is matched against a broader wildcard segment (e.g., *.contoso.com) in a different Enterprise Application, leading to access being allowed or blocked unexpectedly.
rootCause: Network segments in GSA Private Access are evaluated by priority on the GSA client from lowest to highest; first applicable segment wins. Priority is determined by creation order, not by URL specificity. Segments created earlier always have lower priority numbers and are evaluated first. QuickAccess segments always have higher priority than Enterprise Application segments.
solution: Recreate the Enterprise Applications in the desired priority (creation) order, as recreating segments within an application does not change priority. If recreation is not feasible due to scale, engage the Product Group via IcM.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/Microsoft Entra Global Secure Access (ZTNA)/Internal Docs/Global Secure Access - Understanding Private Access Application Network Segment Priority

symptom: Private network connector configured with outbound proxy for GSA Private Access fails. gRPC traffic to porto backend attempts to go directly instead of using the outbound proxy, leading to access failures. Connector debug log: Initializing the tunnel failed with [ProxyEndpointError] [DefaultEndpointError] Error parsing default endpoint.
rootCause: The connector does not respect outbound proxy settings for gRPC traffic unless the proxy is configured at both the connector configuration file level AND the WinHTTP level. If the outbound proxy uses default ports 80/443, the port is stripped before reaching the connector.
solution: 1) Upgrade connector to latest version. 2) Add proxy to connector configuration file per docs. 3) Configure proxy at WinHTTP level: netsh winhttp set proxy http://proxy:port. If proxy uses default ports 80/443, share the private connector version with the fix under NDA.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/Microsoft Entra Global Secure Access (ZTNA)/Internal Docs/Microsoft Entra Private Access: Known Issues

symptom: Users with GSA client running receive ESTS login error AADSTS10000108: The policy {guid} provided in the sec-Restrict-Tenant-Access-Policy header did not match a policy ID in tenant. Affects customers who enabled TRv2 signaling before creating the TRv2 policy (before June 2023 fix).
rootCause: Global Secure Access TRv2 signaling was enabled before the TRv2 policy was created. GSA cached the incorrect (empty/default) policy ID and adds it to every HTTP header request to ESTS, causing ESTS to reject it.
solution: Disable TRv2 signaling on the Session Management blade. Create the TRv2 policy in Cross-Tenant Access Settings if not already created. Re-enable TRv2 signaling. This forces GSA to cache the correct policy ID.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/Microsoft Entra Global Secure Access (ZTNA)/Internal Docs/Microsoft Entra Private Access: Known Issues

symptom: Customer encounters Access denied error when attempting to access the Global Secure Access blade in MS Entra Portal. Fiddler trace shows AADSTS500014: The service principal for resource 9d4afbbc-06a4-49e0-8005-4e5afd1d4fec is disabled, with 400 error on call to graph.microsoft.com/beta/networkaccess/tenantStatus.
rootCause: The service principal for ZTNA Network Access Control Plane (AppId: 9d4afbbc-06a4-49e0-8005-4e5afd1d4fec) has been disabled in the tenant, preventing token issuance for the GSA portal.
solution: Locate the Enterprise Application ZTNA Network Access Control Plane (AppId: 9d4afbbc-06a4-49e0-8005-4e5afd1d4fec) under Enterprise Applications blade and re-enable the Service Principal.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/Microsoft Entra Global Secure Access (ZTNA)/Internal Docs/Microsoft Entra Private Access: Known Issues

symptom: Remote Desktop Connection (TCP 3389) fails on first attempt when MFA is enforced by Conditional Access for GSA Private Access. RDP client shows: Remote Desktop cant connect to the remote computer. Subsequent attempts succeed after MFA is completed.
rootCause: By default, the Remote Desktop Client waits only 8 seconds for the TCP connection. When MFA is required through CA and GSA Private Access, the authentication flow takes longer than 8 seconds, causing the first RDP connection to time out.
solution: Set registry key HKLM\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Terminal Server Client\TimeoutTcpDirectConnection (REG_DWORD) to 60 (decimal). PowerShell: Set-ItemProperty -Path HKLM:\Software\Microsoft\Terminal Server Client -Name TimeoutTcpDirectConnection -Value 60 -Type DWord. This is a temporary workaround; PG is working on an enterprise-ready solution.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/Microsoft Entra Global Secure Access (ZTNA)/Internal Docs/Microsoft Entra Private Access: Known Issues

symptom: Private DNS resolution over GSA intermittently fails or times out. DNS resolution only succeeds after multiple attempts. Connector debug log: DNS request failure: No records found for given DNS query. (os error 9501). Backend shows same errors.
rootCause: The issue is related to IPv6 on the connector. When IPv6 is enabled on the connector or client machine, DNS queries may intermittently fail due to IPv6-related resolution issues.
solution: Disable IPv6 on both the client machine and the connector via registry: Set-ItemProperty -Path HKLM:\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Services\Tcpip6\Parameters -Name DisabledComponents -Value 0x20. After applying the change, private DNS resolution should become consistent.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/Microsoft Entra Global Secure Access (ZTNA)/Internal Docs/Microsoft Entra Private Access: Known Issues

symptom: Private Access Connector service crashes on Windows Server 2025 when Private Access DNS is used. Faulting module: pap_connector_lib.dll. Application Event log shows crash events. Kusto BootstrapRootOperationEvent shows unusually high number of initialBootstrap=true requests.
rootCause: Crashes on Server 2025 correlate to HTTPS and SVCB DNS queries and how these are handled by newer OS versions. Only Server 2025 is known to have this issue. The connector service auto-recovers after each crash.
solution: Contact Arpad Gulyas or Shawn Gray to deploy a private Connector version that fixes this issue. Validate using Kusto query on BootstrapRootOperationEvent with initialBootstrap==true to confirm abnormal restart pattern.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/Microsoft Entra Global Secure Access (ZTNA)/Internal Docs/Microsoft Entra Private Access: Known Issues

symptom: GSA Private Access and/or Application Proxy stops working after Entra ID P1/P2 license expires and a new license is purchased. Accessing apps results in HTTP 404. Portal shows Private Network is currently disabled for your tenant.
rootCause: When a P1/P2 license expires and a new one is purchased, GSA Private Access and Application Proxy do not resume automatically. The private network feature needs to be manually re-enabled.
solution: Step 1: Ensure tenant has valid Entra ID P1/P2 license. Step 2: Navigate to Entra Portal > Global Secure Access > Connectors and Sensors, click Enable private network connectors. Alternative: Entra Portal > Enterprise Applications > Private Network Connectors. For first-time activation, register the first private network connector.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/Microsoft Entra Global Secure Access (ZTNA)/Internal Docs/Microsoft Entra Private Access: Known Issues

symptom: GSA Private Access and Private DNS stop working or become intermittent. Connector trace log: RustSslCertificateValidator: Failed to validate chain of certificate with subject CN=*.msappproxy.net and Initializing the tunnel failed with [InvalidProxyCertificate] InvalidChain.
rootCause: New certificates for *.msappproxy.net use a different certificate chain/CA configuration. The connector server cannot validate the new chain because it lacks access to OCSP/CRL endpoints or is missing intermediate/root certificates.
solution: Option 1 (Recommended): Ensure connector server has outbound access to OCSP and CRL endpoints per docs. Option 2: Manually install Microsoft TLS G2 RSA CA OCSP XX intermediates (XX=02-16) and Microsoft TLS RSA Root G2 root cert into Local Computer store (certlm.msc). Then restart the connector service.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/Microsoft Entra Global Secure Access (ZTNA)/Internal Docs/Microsoft Entra Private Access: Known Issues

symptom: GSA client shows high memory usage, Event ID 425 error requesting new forwarding profile with curl timeout. ForwardingProfile.json shows garbled characters in networkName. Trace: Cannot convert wstring to string, found non printable character.
rootCause: Special (non-ASCII) characters in the Private Network name (Intelligent Local Access) cause a string conversion failure during the GSA Client policy processing, resulting in forwarding profile corruption.
