symptom: Cross-tenant sync fails with WebExceptionProtocolError during user provisioning to target tenant
rootCause: B2B invitation to target tenant is blocked. Target tenant has a restricted allow/deny list for B2B domains and the source user domain is not in the allowed list.
solution: Query Kusto GlobalIfxAllTraces with correlationId filtering for Microsoft Graph invitations endpoint to identify the specific error. Update target tenant B2B allow/deny domain list to include the source domain per https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/active-directory/external-identities/allow-deny-list
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/Sync Provisioning/Cross tenant synchronization/Troubleshooting/TSG Cross-tenant sync (Azure2Azure scenarios)

symptom: Unable to complete due to service connection error when creating first Cross-Tenant Synchronization configuration in Azure portal, error persists on every attempt
rootCause: The Sync Fabric service principal (appId 00000014-0000-0000-c000-000000000000) has appRoleAssignmentRequired (explicitAccessGrantRequired) set to true, blocking the provisioning process
solution: Set appRoleAssignmentRequired to false on Sync Fabric service principal: 1) GET https://graph.microsoft.com/v1.0/servicePrincipals?filter=appId eq 00000014-0000-0000-c000-000000000000 to get ObjectId 2) PATCH the service principal with appRoleAssignmentRequired=false. Then retry CTS configuration creation.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/Sync Provisioning/Cross tenant synchronization/Troubleshooting/TSG Cross-tenant sync (Azure2Azure scenarios)

symptom: During MIM 2016 SP2 to SP3 upgrade, 'Insert Disk' error appears when applying MSP patch via msiexec, installer requests a specific volume label
rootCause: Windows Installer registry contains a volume label reference (e.g. MIM-X22-20157) from original installation media that no longer exists or doesn't match current source
solution: Remove the volume label from registry using PowerShell: Get-ChildItem HKLM:\SOFTWARE\Classes\Installer\Products -Recurse | Get-ItemProperty | Where-Object {$_.PSObject.Properties.Value -match 'MIM-X22-20157'} | ForEach-Object { Remove-ItemProperty matching entries }. Then run the patch command again.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/Sync Provisioning/Microsoft Identity Manager/MIM 2016 Overview/MIM 2016 SP3 Update

symptom: During MIM 2016 SP2 to SP3 upgrade, 'Source file not found' error when applying MSP patch, installer cannot locate required source files
rootCause: SP2 installation ISO is still mounted during patching, causing Windows Installer to reference files from the incorrect (SP2) source path instead of the prepared InstallSource folder with SP2 MSI + SP3 MSP
solution: Unmount the SP2 ISO before running the patch command. Ensure the InstallSource folder contains both the SP2 baseline MSI and the SP3 MSP file, then re-run the msiexec patch command.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/Sync Provisioning/Microsoft Identity Manager/MIM 2016 Overview/MIM 2016 SP3 Update

symptom: FIMService database ExportLog table grows very large, causing performance degradation in MIM Service operations. Database size increases significantly over time.
rootCause: MIM Reporting Logging is enabled (Reporting Logging Enabled flag checked in System Configuration Settings > Extended Attributes) but MIM reporting is not installed or not functioning, causing ExportLog table to accumulate records indefinitely without cleanup.
solution: Check MIM Portal > Administration > All Resources > System Configuration Settings > Extended Attributes. If MIM reporting is not installed or working, uncheck Reporting Logging Enabled flag. Check ExportLog table size via SQL query (SELECT tbl.NAME, SUM(au.total_pages)*8 AS TotalSpaceKB FROM sys.tables tbl JOIN sys.partitions prt ON tbl.object_id=prt.OBJECT_ID JOIN sys.allocation_units au ON prt.partition_id=au.container_id GROUP BY tbl.Name ORDER BY TotalSpaceKB DESC). If ExportLog is the largest table, back up the FIMService database, then run TRUNCATE TABLE ExportLog to clear it.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/Sync Provisioning/Microsoft Identity Manager/MIM 2016 Overview/Sync Service/Troubleshooting Guides/MIM ClearExportLog Table

symptom: FIM Service Management Agent export returns 'stopped-server' run status. Full and Delta Imports succeed but export always fails. SQL Server error: 'Cannot execute as the database principal because the principal "dbo" does not exist, this type of principal cannot be impersonated, or you do not have permission.'
rootCause: The database owner (dbo) mapping in FIMService database is invalid - the owner SID in sys.databases does not match any valid server principal, or there is a mismatch between sys.database_principals dbo SID and sys.databases owner_sid.
solution: 1. Check sys.databases for databases owned by invalid logins: SELECT d.name, s.name FROM sys.databases d LEFT JOIN sys.server_principals s ON d.owner_sid = s.sid. 2. Verify sys.database_principals and sys.databases SIDs match (no NULL results, dbo_login = sysdb_login). 3. Fix with ALTER AUTHORIZATION ON DATABASE::FIMService TO sa; (must coordinate with SQL and PG engineers, do not attempt alone as MIM engineer).
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/Sync Provisioning/Microsoft Identity Manager/MIM 2016 Overview/Sync Service/Troubleshooting Guides/Stopped-Server Errors

symptom: FIM Service Management Agent export shows 'stopped-server' run status. Event log contains 'System.InvalidOperationException: The export session has timed out waiting for responses.' Delta Import and Full Import succeed but Export fails. Large number of Pending Deletes (e.g., 1000 ERE objects).
rootCause: FIM Service MA Exports are processed in batches. A large volume of Pending Deletes (especially ERE object types) causes the batch to be extremely large. The FIM Service times out waiting for SQL Server to process this large batch within the default 600-second (10-minute) exportActivityTimeoutInSeconds window.
solution: Modify miiserver.exe.config (Synchronization Service configuration file) to: 1. Disable Aggregate Processing. 2. Increase exportActivityTimeoutInSeconds from 600 to 900 (15 minutes). Set: <resourceSynchronizationClient aggregate="false" exportActivityTimeoutInSeconds="900"/>. Restart the Synchronization Service after the change.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/Sync Provisioning/Microsoft Identity Manager/MIM 2016 Overview/Sync Service/Troubleshooting Guides/Stopped-Server Errors

symptom: FIM Service MA Export fails with stopped-server error at startup. Event log shows: 'Reraised Error 1088, Level 16, State 7, Procedure ReseedExportMessage - Cannot find the object "ExportMessage" because it does not exist or you do not have permissions.' The error occurs in the sync.ReseedExportMessage stored procedure.
rootCause: The 'FIMServiceTruncateUser' internal database user in the FIMService database has lost all its permissions (Securables tab empty). This user is required by the sync.ReseedExportMessage stored procedure (called via Sync.ClearExport -> Sync.DoClearExport -> sync.ReseedExportMessage) which runs as EXECUTE AS USER='FIMServiceTruncateUser' at the start of each FIM MA Export.
solution: Compare the FIMServiceTruncateUser permissions with a working FIMService database. Recreate all missing permissions in the Securables tab for the FIMServiceTruncateUser account. After restoring permissions, recycle (restart) the SQL Server hosting the FIMService database so permissions are reloaded. The FIM MA Export should then complete successfully.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/Sync Provisioning/Microsoft Identity Manager/MIM 2016 Overview/Sync Service/Troubleshooting Guides/Stopped-Server Errors

symptom: ECMA Connector Host provisioning placed into quarantine with error SystemForCrossDomainIdentityManagementCredentialValidationFailure: While attempting to validate our authorization to access your application, we received this unexpected response: An error occurred while sending the request. Email notification sent to admins about sync failure.
rootCause: ECMA Connector Host is not accessible from Microsoft Entra ID. Common causes: server offline, Microsoft ECMA2Host service not running, TCP port 8585 not listening or not reachable from the Internet.
solution: Verify ECMA Host server is online. Check ECMA2Host service status (Get-Service "Microsoft ECMA2Host") and port 8585 is listening (netstat -aonb | select-string "8585"). Verify inbound TCP 8585 connectivity from Internet using Test-NetConnection or the Test Connection button in Entra provisioning portal. If connectivity fails, check firewall rules and run TestECMA2HostConnection.ps1 script under C:\Program Files\Microsoft ECMA2Host\Troubleshooting.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/Sync Provisioning/SyncFabric/On-premises provisioning/On premises app provisioning EMCA/ECMA Connector

symptom: ECMA Connector Host Test Connection fails with error "You appear to have entered invalid credentials" (SystemForCrossDomainIdentityManagementCredentialValidationUnavailable). Response Status Code: NotFound. Error includes x-ms-proxy-error-details: ResourceNotFound even though correct secret and URL are used.
rootCause: No active provisioning agent assigned to the enterprise application. Customer did not select the correct active agent and click Assign agents in the On-Premises Connectivity section of the provisioning configuration.
solution: In the Entra provisioning configuration, go to On-Premises Connectivity section after setting Provisioning Mode to Automatic. Select the correct active agent from the list and click Assign agents (Steps 2 and 3 of On-Premises Connectivity) before running Test Connection.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/Sync Provisioning/SyncFabric/On-premises provisioning/On premises app provisioning EMCA/ECMA Connector

symptom: Group Writeback V2 export fails during AD export run profile. Export Error is displayed and the group is not created in on-premises AD. EventID 2866 in Directory Service event log on the Domain Controller states: maximum number of audit events that could be cached in memory reached (default 17000), operation was aborted.
rootCause: The Domain Controller reached the maximum audit queue size (default 17000) when auditing attribute changes during group writeback. This typically occurs in large environments with many group membership changes where the member attribute is being audited.
solution: 1. On all Domain Controllers, check registry HKLM\System\CurrentControlSet\services\NTDS\Parameters\Maximum Audit Queue Size (default 17000, DWORD 32bit key may need to be created). 2. Increase the value as needed. 3. Restart the Domain Controller for the change to take effect. 4. Run 2 sync cycles on AAD Connect to confirm successful writeback provisioning. Consider collaborating with Directory Services team before increasing the limit in large environments to avoid excessive DC load.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/Sync Provisioning/SyncFabric/API-Driven Provisioning/placeholder

symptom: After enabling ForcePasswordChangeOnLogon feature for PHS, checking the 'User must change password at next logon' box in AD without resetting the user password does not trigger the force change behavior in Azure AD.
rootCause: The ForcePasswordChangeOnLogon feature only works when combined with an actual password reset/change in AD. Checking the box alone without a password update means PHS has no password update to sync to AAD, so the ForcePasswordChangeOnLogOn flag cannot be sent to AAD.
solution: When using ForcePasswordChangeOnLogon with PHS, always perform a password reset for the user while checking the 'User must change password at next logon' option. Alternatively, use PowerShell: Update-MgUser -UserId <user-object-ID> -PasswordProfile @{forceChangePasswordNextSignIn=$true} (requires setting a new password), or Update-MgUser -UserId <user-object-ID> -PasswordProfile @{forceChangePasswordNextSignIn=$true; password=$null} to force change of current password without resetting it.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/Sync Provisioning/SyncFabric/Inbound provisioning (HR)/Discover Identities Report for SCIM Provisioned Applications

symptom: Users with expired passwords (EnforceCloudPasswordPolicyForPasswordSyncedUsers enabled) are not being prompted to change their password when signing in.
rootCause: Password expiration is only evaluated during interactive password-based sign-ins. If the user signed in via session cookie (Session Token credential type), password expiration is not evaluated and the user is not prompted to change the expired password.
solution: 1. Check sign-in logs (ASC Auth Troubleshooter) for Credential Type - if it says 'Session Token' this is not an interactive password authentication. 2. Admin should revoke all refresh tokens for the user using Revoke-MgUserSignInSession to force interactive re-authentication. 3. Verify user's PasswordPolicies is None/blank (not DisablePasswordExpiration) and LastPasswordChangeTimestamp is older than the domain's PasswordValidityPeriodDays. 4. Test with incognito browser at portal.azure.com for interactive sign-in.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/Sync Provisioning/SyncFabric/Inbound provisioning (HR)/Discover Identities Report for SCIM Provisioned Applications

symptom: User unable to sign in to Entra ID. Sign-in logs show error code 50173 with failure reason: The provided grant has expired due to it being revoked, a fresh auth token is needed. The user's LastPasswordChangeDateTime attribute is set to a time in the future.
rootCause: Due to customer error, the LastPasswordChangeDateTime attribute value for the user was set to a future time and then synchronized to Entra ID, causing all tokens to be considered expired.
solution: If PHS is enabled: reset the user's on-premises password, the value will update with the next sync cycle. If PHS is disabled: 1. Reset user password in Entra ID using MgGraph (Install-Module Microsoft.Graph.Users; Connect-MgGraph -Scopes 'User.ReadWrite'; Update-MgUser with passwordProfile params). 2. If domain is federated, temporarily switch UPN suffix to tenantName.onmicrosoft.com before reset, then switch back. 3. Then reset on-premises password to update LastPasswordChangeDateTime. Note: This solution does not work if the user has any administrative role (Global Admin, User Admin etc.) or is Owner/Member of any Role Assigned group.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/Sync Provisioning/SyncFabric/Kusto Web Ux - Log Analysis

symptom: Password reset fails with error SSPR_0029 for a synchronized AD administrator account. Application event log shows EventID 33004 (PasswordResetService) and EventID 6329 (ADSync) with error hr=80230626: The password could not be updated because the management agent credentials were denied access.
rootCause: The AD account belongs to a protected group in on-premises AD (AdminCount=1). The SDProp background task sets inheritance disabled and overwrites ACL with AdminSDHolder permissions, removing Password Writeback's ability to change/reset passwords on such accounts.
solution: This is by design. AD administrator accounts in protected groups can use Password Writeback to change their password (providing current password) but cannot use SSPR to reset a forgotten password. Options: 1. Remove the user from the protected group if appropriate. 2. Manually grant the AD DS Connector account (MSOL_) Reset Password permission on the specific user object. 3. Use a non-protected account for daily operations.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/Sync Provisioning/SyncFabric/On-premises provisioning/On premises app provisioning EMCA/Configure ECMA Connector for SQL

symptom: Password reset fails with SSPR_0029. Application EventID 6329 shows 'Failed to acquire user information: Contoso\MSOL_xxx. Error Code: ERROR_ACCESS_DENIED' in admaexport.cpp.
rootCause: The DC or AAD Connect server has the 'Network access: Restrict clients allowed to make remote calls to SAM' policy enabled, blocking the MSOL connector account from making SAM queries. Even after disabling the Group Policy, the registry key HKLM\System\CurrentControlSet\Control\Lsa\RestrictRemoteSam persists and continues to block access.
solution: 1. Check if RestrictRemoteSam registry exists: Get-ItemProperty -Path HKLM:\System\CurrentControlSet\Control\Lsa | select restrictremotesam. 2. Option A: Keep the policy but add the AD DS Connector Account (MSOL_) to the allowed users list. 3. Option B: Run GPupdate /force, verify the policy is 'Not Defined' in GPresult, then manually delete the registry: Remove-ItemProperty -Path HKLM:\System\CurrentControlSet\Control\Lsa -Name restrictremotesam. 4. Repeat on ALL Domain Controllers.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/Sync Provisioning/SyncFabric/On-premises provisioning/On premises app provisioning EMCA/Configure ECMA Connector for SQL

symptom: Password reset fails with SSPR_0029. Application EventID 6329 shows error hr=80005000 (unable to get error text) in admaexport.cpp. EventID 33001 shows PasswordResetService error. The affected user has a reserved character (e.g. forward slash /) in their AD Distinguished Name.
rootCause: The user's on-premises AD Distinguished Name contains a reserved LDAP character (forward slash /, comma, plus sign, double quote, backslash, angle brackets, semicolon, equals sign, etc.) which causes the new Password Writeback implementation to fail.
solution: Remove the reserved character from the affected user's AD Distinguished Name. Workaround (not recommended): Enable legacy password writeback implementation by creating registry key HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Services\ADSync\Parameters\PerMAInstance\domainName.com with DWORD PasswordChangeAccessCheckLegacy=1. Warning: Legacy implementation uses NetUserGetInfo API which is prone to Access Denied errors especially when AAD Connect is on a Domain Controller.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/Sync Provisioning/SyncFabric/On-premises provisioning/On premises app provisioning EMCA/Configure ECMA Connector for SQL

symptom: User with Azure AD Administrator role tries SSPR but gets error SSPR_009: Your organization hasn't turned on password reset. Works fine for normal (non-admin) accounts.
rootCause: SSPR for Administrators (SSPR-A) is a separate configuration from SSPR for Users (SSPR-U). The Azure Portal SSPR management page only configures SSPR-U. When an account has an admin role, it uses SSPR-A which may not be enabled on the tenant.
solution: Enable Password Writeback for SSPR: 1. Sign in to Microsoft Entra admin center as Global Administrator. 2. Browse to Protection > Password reset > On-premises integration. 3. Check 'Write back passwords to your on-premises directory'. 4. Optionally check 'Write back passwords with Microsoft Entra Connect cloud sync'. 5. Check 'Allow users to unlock accounts without resetting their password' to Yes. 6. Select Save. Note: Updating PasswordWritebackEnabled from OnPremDirectorySynchronization service features is not supported.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/Sync Provisioning/SyncFabric/On-premises provisioning/On premises app provisioning EMCA/Configure ECMA Connector for SQL

symptom: Password reset or change fails with message: Unfortunately, you cannot reset this user's password because your on-premises policy does not allow it. DC Security event log shows EventID 4724 (Audit Failure) for the MSOL account attempting to reset the password.
rootCause: The new password does not comply with the on-premises AD password policy (password length, complexity, age, history, or fine-grained password policies). Password Writeback enforces on-premises AD DS password policies before committing the change.
solution: 1. Verify current password policy on DC: run NET ACCOUNTS or Get-ADDefaultDomainPasswordPolicy. 2. Check for Fine-Grained Password Policies: Get-ADFineGrainedPasswordPolicy -Filter *. 3. Check resultant policy for specific user: NET USER <username> /DOMAIN. 4. Ensure the new password meets all requirements (length, complexity, minimum age, history). 5. If minimum password age is set, temporarily set it to 0 for testing: allows multiple password changes in a row.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/Sync Provisioning/SyncFabric/On-premises provisioning/On premises app provisioning EMCA/Configure ECMA Connector for SQL

symptom: Password change/reset fails. Application EventID 33009 and 6329 show error hr=8023062C: The password could not be set because the server is not configured for insecure setting of passwords, or a 128 bit TLS or SSL connection is required.
rootCause: AAD Connect has 'Sign and Encrypt LDAP Traffic' disabled in one or more of the three possible configuration locations in the Synchronization Service Manager.
solution: Enable 'Sign and Encrypt LDAP Traffic' in all 3 locations in Synchronization Service Manager: 1. Connectors > AD Connector > Properties > Connect to Active Directory Forest > Options. 2. Connectors > AD Connector > Properties > Configure Directory Partitions > Select partition > Options (under DC connection settings). 3. Connectors > AD Connector > Properties > Configure Directory Partitions > Select partition > Set Credentials (if Alternate credentials) > Options. After enabling in all 3 locations, restart ADSync service.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/Sync Provisioning/SyncFabric/On-premises provisioning/On premises app provisioning EMCA/Configure ECMA Connector for SQL

symptom: Admin resets a user's password from Office Admin Portal (M365 admin center). User can sign in with the new password in the cloud, but the new password is not synchronized back to on-premises AD.
rootCause: The Office Admin Portal does not use SSPR / Password Writeback libraries. It resets the password only in Azure AD without triggering writeback to on-premises AD. This is by design.
solution: Use Azure Portal (Entra admin center) instead of Office Admin Portal for password resets to ensure Password Writeback works. To verify which portal was used: check ASC > Tenant Explorer > Audit Logs. Activity 'Reset password (by admin)' = Azure Portal; Activity 'Reset user password' = Office Admin Portal.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/Sync Provisioning/SyncFabric/On-premises provisioning/On premises app provisioning EMCA/Configure ECMA Connector for SQL

symptom: Password reset fails with connectivity error SSPR_0030 for hybrid users. All Password Writeback connectivity tests from Connect Sync server are successful. Customer previously had a Cloud Sync configuration with password writeback that was deleted.
rootCause: When a domain is added in Cloud Sync configuration, it creates a published resource. Deleting the Cloud Sync configuration does not remove this published resource, causing HIS to route password writeback requests to the non-existent Cloud Sync agent instead of Connect Sync.
solution: 1. Use ASC Graph Explorer query: onPremisesPublishingProfiles/provisioning/publishedResources to list all published resources. 2. Verify if the affected domain has a lingering published resource with no active Cloud Sync configuration. 3. Delete the lingering published resource (CAUTION: only if not in use by active Cloud Sync, WorkDay to AD, or SuccessFactors to AD applications). 4. If the scenario does not match, review standard connectivity requirements (servicebus endpoints, TLS 1.2, certificates, firewall rules).
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/Sync Provisioning/SyncFabric/On-premises provisioning/On premises app provisioning EMCA/Configure ECMA Connector for SQL

symptom: User forced to change password on first sign-in sees a warning message 'our servers take a little time to catch up' after successfully setting the new password. SSPR logs show GraphCurrentPasswordValidationFailed / ChangePasswordOnPremisesSuccessCloudFailure.
rootCause: Timing issue when PTA (Pass-through Authentication) is used. After an admin resets the on-premises password with 'User must change password at next logon', PHS needs ~2 minutes to sync the temporary password to Azure AD. If the user signs in via PTA before PHS completes, the password change succeeds on-premises via writeback but fails cloud-side because Azure AD still has the old password hash.
solution: After resetting a user's on-premises password with 'User must change password at next logon', wait at least 2 minutes for PHS to sync the temporary password to Azure AD before the user attempts to sign in and update the password. This is a timing issue with PTA + PHS coexistence.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/Sync Provisioning/SyncFabric/On-premises provisioning/On premises app provisioning EMCA/Configure ECMA Connector for SQL

symptom: Password Writeback onboarding or operation fails. Application EventID 31034 shows: Listener for Namespace offline, CommunicationException: The client and server cannot communicate because they do not possess a common algorithm.
rootCause: The AAD Connect server does not support TLS 1.2 or the required ciphers for connecting to the SSPR servicebus relay (ssprdedicatedsbprodscu/ncu.servicebus.windows.net).
solution: 1. Verify connectivity: browse to https://ssprdedicatedsbprodscu.servicebus.windows.net and https://ssprdedicatedsbprodncu.servicebus.windows.net from AAD Connect server. 2. Test-NetConnection on port 443 to both endpoints. 3. Enable TLS 1.2 using the PowerShell script from Microsoft docs. 4. Verify required certificates exist: DigiCert Global Root G2 (df3c24f9bfd666761b268073fe06d1cc8d4f82a4) and DigiCert Global Root CA (a8985d3a65e5e5c4b2d7d66d40c6dd2fb19c5436). 5. Check firewall allows outbound to *.servicebus.windows.net and *.cloudapp.net on port 443.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/Sync Provisioning/SyncFabric/On-premises provisioning/On premises app provisioning EMCA/Configure ECMA Connector for SQL

symptom: SCIM Validator tool shows error "Please enter a URL" even after entering a valid URL into the endpoint field.
rootCause: The entered URL is missing the protocol prefix (https:// or http://). The SCIM Validator requires URLs to begin with HTTPS or HTTP.
solution: Ensure the SCIM endpoint URL begins with https:// or http:// (e.g., https://myapp.example.com/scim). Add the protocol prefix and retry.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/Sync Provisioning/SyncFabric/Outbound provisioning/Deployment/SCIM Validator Tool

symptom: SCIM Validator tool shows "SCIM Validation failed. Internal Server Error" after reviewing attributes and initiating the test.
rootCause: The entered SCIM endpoint URL is not valid or the endpoint does not exist. The server returns a 500 error because it cannot reach the specified endpoint.
solution: Verify the SCIM endpoint URL is correct and the endpoint is accessible. Ensure the SCIM server is running and reachable from the internet. Correct the URL and retry the validation.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/Sync Provisioning/SyncFabric/Outbound provisioning/Deployment/SCIM Validator Tool

symptom: SCIM Validator tool shows "Invalid credentials!" after reviewing attributes and initiating the test.
rootCause: The entered bearer token or credentials are incorrect, or the SCIM endpoint URL is invalid/does not exist.
solution: Review and correct the bearer token and SCIM endpoint URL. Ensure the token has not expired and matches what the SCIM server expects. Re-enter credentials and retry.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/Sync Provisioning/SyncFabric/Outbound provisioning/Deployment/SCIM Validator Tool

symptom: SCIM Validator tool shows "Could not discover the schema during processing" after submitting the endpoint and credentials.
rootCause: The SCIM endpoint URL entered is invalid. The validator cannot reach the /Schemas endpoint to discover the schema definition.
solution: Verify the SCIM endpoint URL is correct and accessible. Ensure the SCIM server implements the /Schemas endpoint per SCIM 2.0 spec (RFC 7643). Correct the URL and retry.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/Sync Provisioning/SyncFabric/Outbound provisioning/Deployment/SCIM Validator Tool

symptom: SCIM Validator tool shows "/schemas endpoint not found" after submitting the endpoint and credentials.
rootCause: The SCIM server does not implement the /Schemas endpoint for schema discovery. This is required by the SCIM 2.0 specification for the validator to discover supported attributes.
solution: Implement the /Schemas endpoint on the SCIM server per SCIM 2.0 spec. Refer to Microsoft docs on schema discovery: https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/active-directory/app-provisioning/use-scim-to-provision-users-and-groups#schemas-schema-discovery
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/Sync Provisioning/SyncFabric/Outbound provisioning/Deployment/SCIM Validator Tool

symptom: Custom attribute not provisioned outbound to enterprise application. Provisioning schema shows FlowBehavior set to FlowWhenChanged, causing attribute to only sync when value changes in source, not on every cycle.
rootCause: FlowBehavior is set to FlowWhenChanged in the provisioning schema JSON. Some target apps (e.g. Workday Writeback) require specific attributes to be included in every sync cycle even when unchanged.
solution: Navigate to Enterprise App > Provisioning > Edit attribute mappings > Show advanced options > Review your schema here. Search for FlowWhenChanged, locate the target attribute, change FlowBehavior from FlowWhenChanged to FlowAlways. Save and test with provisioning on demand. Verify attribute flows in incremental sync cycles.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/Sync Provisioning/SyncFabric/Outbound provisioning/Schema and Mappings/Changing Schema FlowWhenChanged FlowBehavior

symptom: User export to AWS IAM Identity Center fails with StatusCode 400 BadRequest: Request is unparsable, syntactically incorrect, or violates schema.
rootCause: User object in Entra ID is missing one or more required attributes for AWS SCIM provisioning: firstName, lastName, displayName, or userName.
solution: Ensure all users in provisioning scope have firstName, lastName, displayName, and userName attributes populated in Entra ID before provisioning to AWS IAM Identity Center. Use the gallery app (AWS IAM Identity Center) instead of legacy non-gallery or BYOA versions.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/Sync Provisioning/SyncFabric/Outbound provisioning/Troubleshooting Identity Provisioning issues/AWS - Troubleshooting

symptom: User export to AWS IAM Identity Center fails with 400 BadRequest when provisioning email or phone number attributes. Error: Request is unparsable, syntactically incorrect, or violates schema.
rootCause: AWS IAM Identity Center does not support multi-valued attributes for email and phone numbers. Having two source attributes both mapped to the same target field (e.g. two attributes mapping to phoneNumber) or a user with multiple values in these fields triggers the error.
solution: Option 1: Ensure user has only one value for phoneNumber/email in source. Option 2: Remove duplicate attribute mappings - keep only one source attribute mapped to each target phone/email field in the provisioning attribute mappings.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/Sync Provisioning/SyncFabric/Outbound provisioning/Troubleshooting Identity Provisioning issues/AWS - Troubleshooting

symptom: Provisioning to AWS IAM Identity Center fails due to invalid/unsupported characters in user attribute values.
rootCause: AWS IAM Identity Center does not allow certain characters that Entra ID supports: tab, newline, carriage return, and special characters including < > ; : %.
solution: Clean attribute values in Entra ID (source) to remove unsupported characters before provisioning to AWS IAM Identity Center. Use expression-based attribute mappings or fix source data to strip these characters.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/Sync Provisioning/SyncFabric/Outbound provisioning/Troubleshooting Identity Provisioning issues/AWS - Troubleshooting

symptom: Group membership update fails during outbound provisioning. User cannot be added to group in target application. Error: unable to resolve reference.
rootCause: The member user is not being successfully provisioned from Entra ID to the target system. The Sync Fabric engine needs to resolve the user sourceAnchor (Entra ID ObjectId) to targetAnchor (target system primary key) via the Connector Data Store. Users out of scope or failing due to sync/export errors cannot be resolved.
solution: Ensure the member user is in provisioning scope and successfully provisioning to the target before attempting group membership sync. Fix any user-level synchronization or export errors first. Verify user appears in provisioning logs with successful export before troubleshooting group membership.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/Sync Provisioning/SyncFabric/Outbound provisioning/Troubleshooting Identity Provisioning issues/Group Membership Updates - Troubleshooting

symptom: After performing clear state/restart of provisioning job, group members are orphaned in target system - members remain in target group even after being removed from source group in Entra ID.
rootCause: When clear state is performed while a read/write gap (backlog) exists and group membership removal changes have been ingested but not yet digested (processed) by Sync Fabric engine, the engine loses confidence in its authority over those members and takes conservative action (does nothing). The membership removal is lost and never re-evaluated.
solution: Check for read/write gap before performing clear state. If orphaning has occurred, manually clean up orphaned members in target system. This issue only impacts apps where group objects are provisioned source-to-target, NOT apps using Sync assigned users and groups mode. Product group has fix on roadmap (no public ETA).
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/Sync Provisioning/SyncFabric/Outbound provisioning/Troubleshooting Identity Provisioning issues/Group Membership Updates - Troubleshooting

symptom: Directory extension attributes are missing from the source attribute dropdown in provisioning attribute mappings; customer cannot find extension attributes when adding new mappings or scoping filters
rootCause: The getAvailableExtensionProperties API used by the attribute mapping UX has a limit of 1000 Service Principals. If tenant has >1000 SPs, extensions from apps beyond the limit are not discovered. Also possible: extensions do not exist in tenant, multi-valued extensions are not supported in mappings, or the appId referenced in schema editor is invalid
solution: Verify extensions exist via Graph API (POST directoryObject/getAvailableExtensionProperties) or PowerShell (Get-MgApplication). If >1000 SPs in tenant, use schema editor to manually add extension: Enterprise Apps > App > Provisioning > Edit attribute mappings > Show advanced options > Edit attribute list for Microsoft Entra ID. Add full extension name (extension_appId_attributeName). Access schema editor via URL: portal.azure.com/?Microsoft_AAD_Connect_Provisioning_forceSchemaEditorEnabled=true. Check if extension is multi-valued (not supported in mappings/filters)
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/Sync Provisioning/SyncFabric/Outbound provisioning/Troubleshooting Identity Provisioning issues/Missing directory extension attributes in the attribute mappings - Troubleshooting

symptom: All group members removed from Oracle IDCS (Identity Cloud Service) when only one user is removed from the Azure AD group; provisioning to Oracle IDC via non-gallery/BYOA SCIM app causes full group membership wipe on single member removal
rootCause: Non-gallery app with Oracle connector is not SCIM compliant. Custom/BYOA SCIM application is not supported for Oracle Cloud Infrastructure Console or Oracle IDCS integration; the gallery app has been customized to work with the Oracle SCIM server
solution: Switch from the non-gallery/BYOA SCIM app to the official gallery app for Oracle Cloud Infrastructure Console. Follow tutorial: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/entra/identity/saas-apps/oracle-cloud-infrastructure-console-provisioning-tutorial. Integrating with Oracle IDCS using a custom/BYOA application is not supported
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/Sync Provisioning/SyncFabric/Outbound provisioning/Troubleshooting Identity Provisioning issues/Oracle IDC group members removed when one is deleted in Entra ID
Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/AD Changes in Windows Server 2025 and Windows 11 24H2/Windows LAPS - 24H2 new features/LAPS: 24H2: Image Rollback Detection Feature
symptom: After VM image rollback (Hyper-V snapshot reversion or image re-deployment), Windows LAPS password stored in Active Directory no longer matches the local device password, resulting in a torn state where IT administrators cannot sign in using the persisted LAPS password
rootCause: Image rollback reverts the local registry GUID (LastPasswordVersion at HKLM\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\LAPS\State) to an old value while AD retains the newer msLAPS-CurrentPasswordVersion GUID. The mismatch means the AD-stored password was updated after the snapshot but the local device still has the pre-snapshot password
solution: Deploy Windows 11 24H2 or Windows Server 2025 and run the latest Update-LapsADSchema cmdlet to add the msLAPS-CurrentPasswordVersion attribute. Once enabled, LAPS automatically detects GUID mismatch during each processing cycle (Event ID 10015 with reason 0x4000) and immediately rotates the password (Events 10018, 10020). No additional policy settings required. If password history is enabled, old passwords in msLAPS-EncryptedPasswordHistory may still work as a workaround before upgrading

symptom: New-ADServiceAccount -CreateDelegatedServiceAccount fails with error "Key does not exist" when creating a new dMSA account
rootCause: KDC Root Key (KdsRootKey) has not been created in the Active Directory environment. This key is required for both gMSA and dMSA password management.
solution: Create a KDC Root Key by running: Add-KdsRootKey -EffectiveTime ((Get-Date).AddHours(-10)). The -10 hours allows immediate use in lab; in production, use without the time offset and wait 10 hours for replication.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/AD Changes in Windows Server 2025 and Windows 11 24H2/dMSA - Delegated Managed Service Account/Known issues of dMSA

symptom: Service configured with dMSA account fails to start with Error 1297: A privilege that the service requires to function properly does not exist in the service account configuration
rootCause: The dMSA account is missing the "Log on as a service" user right in the local security policy on the machine where the service runs.
solution: Open secpol.msc > Local Policies > User Rights Assignment > Log on as a service. Add the dMSA account to this privilege. Alternatively use Group Policy to assign the right.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/AD Changes in Windows Server 2025 and Windows 11 24H2/dMSA - Delegated Managed Service Account/Known issues of dMSA

symptom: Service with dMSA fails to start with Error 1069: The service did not start due to a logon failure. Applies to both new dMSA and migrated service accounts.
rootCause: One or more of: (1) DelegatedMSAEnabled registry key not set under HKLM\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Policies\System\Kerberos\Parameters, (2) msDS-DelegatedMSAState attribute not set to 3 on the dMSA object, (3) machine account not listed in PrincipalsAllowedToRetrieveManagedPassword on the dMSA, (4) machine not rebooted after registry change.
solution: 1) Set registry: reg add "HKLM\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Policies\System\Kerberos\Parameters" /v DelegatedMSAEnabled /t REG_DWORD /d 1 /f, then reboot. 2) Verify msDS-DelegatedMSAState is 3: Get-ADServiceAccount -Identity <dMSA> -Properties msDS-DelegatedMSAState. 3) Verify machine permission: Get-ADServiceAccount -Identity <dMSA> -Properties PrincipalsAllowedToRetrieveManagedPassword.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/AD Changes in Windows Server 2025 and Windows 11 24H2/dMSA - Delegated Managed Service Account/Known issues of dMSA

symptom: LDAP clients fail to connect or authenticate after deploying/upgrading to Windows Server 2025 domain controller; Event ID 2887 logged on DC showing unsigned LDAP bind attempts rejected
rootCause: Windows Server 2025 enforces LDAP Signing by default for all new AD deployments via new LDAP server signing requirements Enforcement Group Policy; if not configured, DC enforces signing and rejects unsigned binds
solution: Check GPO LDAP server signing requirements Enforcement (Default Domain Controller Policy > Security Options). If Not Configured, DC enforces signing. Update all LDAP clients to use signed connections. If legacy clients cannot be updated, temporarily set policy to Disabled to use legacy LDAP Signing policy setting. Monitor Event 2887 to identify offending clients.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/AD Changes in Windows Server 2025 and Windows 11 24H2/LDAP Signing & Channel Binding Changes

symptom: LDAP over SSL/TLS authentication fails; Event ID 3039 (client does not support CBT) or Event ID 3040 (channel binding failure/token mismatch) logged on domain controller
rootCause: Client application or device does not support LDAP Channel Binding Tokens (CBT), or a TLS-terminating intermediary device (load balancer, reverse proxy) breaks the channel binding by establishing a new TLS session to the DC
solution: 1) Identify affected clients from Event 3039/3040 details. 2) Update clients/applications to support CBT. 3) Check for TLS-terminating network devices between client and DC; configure them for TLS pass-through. 4) If client cannot be updated, temporarily set Domain controller: LDAP server channel binding token requirements to When Supported instead of Always. 5) Mac/Linux clients: verify CBT support per vendor docs.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/AD Changes in Windows Server 2025 and Windows 11 24H2/LDAP Signing & Channel Binding Changes

symptom: Domain controller restore fails after enabling Database 32k pages optional feature in Active Directory; 8K-page backup media is rejected and unusable for restore
rootCause: Enabling the Database 32K pages optional feature is irreversible; once enabled, only 32K page format backup media can be used to restore Windows Server 2025 domain controllers. Pre-feature 8K backup images become permanently incompatible.
solution: Before enabling 32K pages: 1) Ensure ALL DCs in forest run Windows Server 2025 with 32K-capable database. 2) Create and verify 32K-capable backups of all DCs, especially GC and FSMO role holders. 3) Validate backup software compatibility with 32K page format in test environment. 4) After enabling, maintain only 32K-format backups. If 8K backup is the only available restore point, a full authoritative forest recovery is required.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/AD Changes in Windows Server 2025 and Windows 11 24H2/Jet 32K Feature

symptom: AD object creation or modification fails because multi-valued attribute (e.g., servicePrincipalName) exceeds maximum value count (~1200) on domain controllers using JET 8K page database
rootCause: JET 8K page database architecture limits the number of multi-valued attribute values per AD object to approximately 1200, due to the 8KB main object page size constraint on storing Long Value IDs (LVIDs)
solution: Upgrade all domain controllers to Windows Server 2025 and enable JET 32K pages feature, which increases multi-valued attribute capacity to approximately 3200 values per object. Requirements: 1) All DCs must run WS2025 2) Raise DFL and FFL to Windows Server 2025 3) Enable Database 32k pages optional feature (irreversible). Verify with esentutl /mh ntds.dit /vss to confirm page size.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/AD Changes in Windows Server 2025 and Windows 11 24H2/Jet 32K Feature

symptom: Application slowness and high CPU/memory on domain controller (LSASS.exe spikes) when resolving user/group names; performance degrades in environments with many external or forest trusts
rootCause: LSALookupNames called with isolated name format (e.g., johnb instead of contoso\johnb) triggers serial RPC calls to domain controllers on every trusted domain/forest to resolve the ambiguous name. Many trusts or slow/offline trusted domains amplify the delay.
solution: 1) Use fully qualified names (domain\user, user@domain, or DNS\user) in applications and scripts instead of isolated names. 2) Disable isolated name lookups via registry: see https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/troubleshoot/windows-server/active-directory/poor-performance-calling-lookup-functions 3) Enable LSP.log (LspDbgInfoLevel=0x40000800, LspDbgTraceOptions=0x1) to trace which processes send isolated lookups. 4) Use PsGetSID for ad-hoc troubleshooting.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/AD Changes in Windows Server 2025 and Windows 11 24H2/Netlogonless Lookups/LSA LookupNames and LSALookupSids

symptom: Name/SID resolution fails for domain accounts; unable to add domain users to file share ACL permissions or search users in Object Picker; applications that use LSALookupNames/LSALookupSids fail to translate names to SIDs
rootCause: LSARPC network ports (RPC endpoint mapper port 135 and ephemeral RPC ports 49152-65535) are blocked between the client machine and the domain controller by firewall or network policy, preventing the LSA RPC communication needed for name/SID translation
solution: 1) Verify connectivity: Test-NetConnection -ComputerName <DC> -Port 135. 2) Ensure firewall allows RPC port 135 and ephemeral ports (49152-65535) between client and DC. 3) Collect network trace (Wireshark) to confirm TCP handshake to RPC port and LSARPC ephemeral port. 4) Check LSP.log for request details. 5) For cross-forest lookups, also ensure trust ports are open between DCs in different forests.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/AD Changes in Windows Server 2025 and Windows 11 24H2/Netlogonless Lookups/LSA LookupNames and LSALookupSids

symptom: Domain Controller fails to boot with stop code 0xc00002e1 or 0xc00002e2. AD database (ntds.dit) is in dirty/inconsistent state after crash or unclean shutdown, preventing LSASS from starting.
rootCause: ESE/JET database is dirty - transactions remain in transaction log files and have not been committed to the database. The database is inconsistent and cannot be loaded by LSASS on boot.
solution: Boot into DS Restore Mode. Dump database header with esentutl /mh ntds.dit to verify state is DIRTY and identify required log range (Log Required Low/High). Verify all required log files are present on disk. Copy all DB + logs to a safe directory, then run soft recovery: esentutl /r edb /d (MUST use /d flag). After recovery, verify state is Clean with esentutl /mh. Restart normally.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/AD Database and DC Boot Failures/Workflow: AD Database: DC no boot/DC No Boot: Resolution & Known issues/DB and log file relationships & How to recover a DB without corrupting it

symptom: AD database becomes corrupted after running esentutl /r recovery. Logs become mismatched to the database file. Original production database gets unexpectedly modified during recovery of a copy.
rootCause: Running esentutl /r without the /d flag causes recovery to read the database path embedded in the transaction log files. If DB+logs were copied to a different folder, recovery follows the path in the logs and replays old/copied logs against the ORIGINAL production database, causing corruption and log mismatch.
solution: ALWAYS use esentutl /r edb /d (with /d flag) which forces recovery to use the current working directory. Correct procedure: (1) Copy ALL DB + Logs to a working directory like C:\data\YourDb, (2) cd to that directory, (3) Run esentutl /r edb /d. Never move or delete log files before recovery. Never run recovery without /d.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/AD Database and DC Boot Failures/Workflow: AD Database: DC no boot/DC No Boot: Resolution & Known issues/DB and log file relationships & How to recover a DB without corrupting it

symptom: AD replication fails or is blocked by a corrupted/bad object. Events may include NTDS ISAM errors, Event ID 2108/1084 during inbound replication, or replication error 8545 (replication update could not be applied). Dumpdatabase analysis shows phantomized children/parents (OBJ=FALSE), differing PDNT chains between DCs, or inconsistent attribute data.
rootCause: A corrupted or inconsistent object in the Active Directory database (ntds.dit) blocks replication. Common causes include hardware/storage failures, unexpected shutdowns, failed schema extensions, snapshot restores on virtual DCs, or third-party recovery tool interference.
solution: Delete the bad object to unblock replication. Use online dumpdatabase feature (KB315098 via LDP.exe) to identify the problematic object by DN or GUID. Check for: phantomized objects (OBJ=FALSE), different PDNT chains across DCs, inconsistent attribute data. If DSID is found in Directory Services event log, collaborate with ADS Pod SEE/EE for investigation. For large dump files, use NOREPAD from CSS tool collection.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/AD Database and DC Boot Failures/Workflow: AD Database: Corruption/DB Corruption: Resolution & Known issues

symptom: Domain controller fails to boot with blue screen error 0xc00002e1 or 0xc00002e2. NTDS.DIT is placed on SAN, NAS, or iSCSI storage device. Error message Directory Services cannot start may appear.
rootCause: NTDS.DIT database file is placed on storage drives (SAN/NAS/iSCSI) that are not available at Windows boot time. The directory service cannot access the database during the boot sequence, causing a fatal boot failure.
solution: Ensure NTDS.DIT is placed on drives available at boot time. See KB2004725 for detailed remediation. For general DC no-boot scenarios with Jet database errors, follow KB4042791 (Troubleshoot Jet database errors and recovery steps) which covers: DB path/settings checklist, integrity checks (NTDSUTIL FILES INTEGRITY), and non-destructive repairs.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/AD Database and DC Boot Failures/Workflow: AD Database: Corruption/KB Articles for Database Corruption and no DC Boot

symptom: AD database corruption detected via integrity checks or semantic analysis. Secondary index corruption in ntds.dit. May present as replication errors (error 8451 - database error), NTDS ISAM errors in Directory Services event log, or LSASS/DNS/DFSR application crashes in system event log.
rootCause: Secondary index corruption in the ntds.dit ESE database, commonly caused by hardware/storage subsystem issues, file system errors, or unplanned reboots. Lost page flush events (KB2518779) may indicate underlying hardware problems.
solution: 1) Collect data: event logs (Application, System, Directory Services, DFS Replication, DNS), dumpdatabase export (KB315098), check for storage/filesystem errors. 2) Run non-destructive checks: NTDSUTIL FILES INTEGRITY, semantic database analysis (KB315136). 3) For secondary index corruption: run offline defragmentation (KB232122) - this leaves original DB intact, copy compacted DB to replace. 4) WARNING: Do NOT run destructive esentutl /r or /p. 5) Check DB paths/settings per KB4042791.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/AD Database and DC Boot Failures/Workflow: AD Database: Corruption/DB Corruption: Data Collection

symptom: AD replication error 8614: The Active Directory cannot replicate with this server because the time since the last replication with this server has exceeded the tombstone lifetime. Replication between domain controllers has been broken for longer than the tombstone lifetime.
rootCause: Replication between domain controllers has not occurred for a period exceeding the tombstone lifetime (default 60 or 180 days). This can happen due to prolonged network isolation, suspended virtual DC, or unresolved replication errors left unattended.
solution: Follow KB2020053 for remediation. Key considerations: verify if enough recent backups exist for forest recovery, check if multiple DCs/domains are affected, determine if virtual DCs had snapshot restores. If DC was isolated beyond tombstone lifetime, it must be forcefully demoted and re-promoted rather than allowed to replicate stale data.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/AD Database and DC Boot Failures/Workflow: AD Database: Corruption/KB Articles for Database Corruption and no DC Boot

symptom: AD replication error 1127: While accessing the hard disk, a disk operation failed even after retries. May also see lost page flush events in Directory Services event log. Hardware-related database access failures on domain controller.
rootCause: Hardware or storage subsystem problems causing disk I/O failures when accessing the AD database. Lost page flush detection (KB2518779) confirms hardware-level write failures where pages were not properly persisted to disk.
solution: 1) Check hardware and storage subsystem health. 2) Review system event log for storage subsystem and file system errors. 3) Check for unplanned reboots before first corruption report. 4) Use lost page flush detection (KB2518779/KB4016886) to identify hardware write failures. 5) If hardware confirmed faulty, migrate DC to healthy storage. 6) Run offline defragmentation (KB232122) and JET Flush Map file verification (KB4016886) after hardware remediation.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/AD Database and DC Boot Failures/Workflow: AD Database: Corruption/KB Articles for Database Corruption and no DC Boot

symptom: Active Directory database (NTDS.dit) grows abnormally large on Windows Server 2008 R2 / Windows 7 environments, link table is bloated
rootCause: Credential Roaming feature enabled causes massive link table growth in the DIT as roaming credentials (certificates, keys) are replicated across DCs and stored as linked attributes
solution: Disable the Credential Roaming Group Policy setting, then follow KB guidance to clean up existing roaming credential data and perform offline defrag to reclaim space. See public KB: AD DS database size increases significantly when the Credential Roaming feature is enabled.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/AD Database and DC Boot Failures/Workflow: AD Database: Growth/DB Growth: Known Issues

symptom: AD database grows due to excessive deleted DNS record objects; large collection of deleted DNS objects visible in DIT analysis
rootCause: Dynamic DNS (DDNS) updates delete existing DNS records and create new ones instead of updating the registration timestamp on the existing record, or DNS scavenging is configured with arbitrarily short values causing premature deletion
solution: Apply preventative DNS fixes on all DNS-enabled Domain Controllers. Review and correct DNS scavenging interval configuration. Check if AD-integrated zones were converted to zone files. Verify all DCs have the fix to avoid record deletion during DDNS updates.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/AD Database and DC Boot Failures/Workflow: AD Database: Growth/DB Growth: Known Issues

symptom: AD database (NTDS.dit) bloats significantly due to frequent creation and deletion of large group objects; link table grows disproportionately
rootCause: Scripts or software regularly create and delete large group objects, causing link table bloat. With AD Recycle Bin enabled, deleted objects are retained longer doubling retention period. RODC excessive password caching also contributes via linked attributes.
solution: Reduce Tombstone Lifetime (TSL) to accelerate garbage collection of deleted objects (balance against lingering object risk). Run regular offline defrags to reclaim space. Review and optimize scripts/software that manage large groups. Check RODC password caching policies.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/AD Database and DC Boot Failures/Workflow: AD Database: Growth/DB Growth: Known Issues

symptom: NTDS.dit file grows continuously and never reclaims free space even after object deletions or domain removals; white space accumulates in the DIT
rootCause: Online defragmentation is disabled or insufficient to reclaim all white space. Large cleanup operations or domain removal leave substantial free space inside the DIT that cannot be reclaimed without offline defrag. Online defrag only reorganizes pages but does not shrink the file.
solution: Enable online defragmentation (runs 1 hour every 12 hours by default). Perform offline defragmentation (ntdsutil compact) to shrink the DIT file and remove root-level white space. Set registry key '6 Garbage Collection' to 1 for event log visibility into white space status.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/AD Database and DC Boot Failures/Workflow: AD Database: Growth/DB Growth: Known Issues

symptom: AD replication is slow or delayed, replication fails with error 8477 (The replication request has been posted; waiting for reply)
rootCause: Overloaded domain controller (CPU/memory/disk) or network issues causing replication latency and timeouts
solution: Monitor DC performance counters (CPU, disk queue, network). Check replication traffic patterns. Use repadmin /replsummary to identify slow links. Review AD replication traffic article and DC performance tuning guidance. For excessive replication, use metadata analysis to determine which objects/attributes are changing frequently.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/AD Replication/Workflow: AD Replication appears slow or is delayed/Troubleshooting Slow or Excessive Replication

symptom: AD replication fails with error 8464 (Synchronization attempt failed). Global Catalog DCs have stale Partial Attribute Set (PAS) that does not match schema isMemberOfPartialAttributeSet count. Applications like Exchange fail when communicating with GC missing expected attributes.
rootCause: The partialAttributeSet on read-only partitions became out of sync with the schema due to prolonged replication failures, neglected replication health monitoring, or operational practices like lag sites, offline sites, and firewalls blocking replication.
solution: Use Get-PAS.ps1 script or repadmin /showattr to compare each GC partition cAttrs count against schema PAS count. For mismatched partitions: 1) repadmin /options DC +DISABLE_NTDSCONN_XLATE, 2) repadmin /unhost DC partition, 3) repadmin /add partition DC knownGoodSource, 4) verify PAS matches schema, 5) repadmin /options DC -DISABLE_NTDSCONN_XLATE. Note: fixing gateway DCs may expose additional DCs behind them with bad data.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/AD Replication/Workflow: Common Solutions to Replication Failures/Active Directory replication error 8464: Synchronization attempt failed

symptom: AD replication fails across firewall boundaries. RPC endpoint mapper on port 135 dynamically assigns high TCP ports (1024-50000 or 49152-65535) that may be blocked by firewall rules, causing replication failures between domain controllers in different network segments.
rootCause: RPC-based Active Directory replication uses dynamic port mapping by default. The client contacts the RPC endpoint mapper on port 135, which returns a dynamically assigned port for AD replication. Firewalls that do not allow the full dynamic RPC port range will block replication traffic.
solution: Restrict AD replication RPC traffic to a specific static port by following https://learn.microsoft.com/troubleshoot/windows-server/active-directory/restrict-ad-rpc-traffic-to-specific-port. In addition to the static RPC port, ensure the following ports are open between DCs: 135/TCP (RPC endpoint mapper), 88/TCP (Kerberos), 53/UDP+TCP (DNS), 389/UDP (LDAP). For SYSVOL replication via DFSR, configure a static port per https://techcommunity.microsoft.com/blog/askds/configuring-dfsr-to-a-static-port---the-rest-of-the-story/396746.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/AD Replication/Additional Information about Replication/Network Ports Used By Replication

symptom: AD replication error 1256 (Remote Server is Unavailable) logged in repadmin /showrepl output alongside other errors like 1722 or 1726
rootCause: Error 1256 is a secondary/cascading error. It occurs when a prior partition replication already failed, so the system skips attempting replication for this partition knowing it will fail. The real issue is the underlying error (e.g., 1722, 1726).
solution: Ignore error 1256 and focus on resolving the other replication errors (commonly 1722 or 1726). Once the primary error is resolved, 1256 will stop appearing. Reference: https://learn.microsoft.com/troubleshoot/windows-server/active-directory/active-directory-replication-error-1256
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/AD Replication/Workflow: Common Solutions to Replication Failures/Error 1256 - Remote Server is Unavailable

symptom: AD replication fails with error 1726 (RPC call cancelled) or error 1727 (RPC call failed). The two DCs can communicate over RPC initially but some RPC traffic fails during replication.
rootCause: Network-related failure where an intermediate network device (firewall, IDS/IPS, WAN optimizer) is performing packet inspection or dropping large-size packets during RPC communication between domain controllers.
solution: Capture two-sided simultaneous network traces on both domain controllers, then force replication while tracing. Examine traces for dropped, inspected, or modified packets. Engage the network team to identify the intermediate device causing the issue. Reference: https://learn.microsoft.com/troubleshoot/windows-server/active-directory/remote-procedure-call-failed-and-did-not-execute-error
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/AD Replication/Workflow: Common Solutions to Replication Failures/Error 1726 | 1727 RPC call is cancelled or failed

symptom: AD replication error 8451: the domain controller encounters a problem with the Active Directory database during replication. Event logs may show ESE/ISAM database errors.
rootCause: Active Directory database (ntds.dit) corruption or inconsistency on the affected domain controller. May be caused by hardware/storage failures, unexpected shutdowns, or file system errors.
solution: 1) Try semantic database analysis (ntdsutil) to check database integrity. 2) Run offline defragmentation (ntdsutil) which creates a new clean database file. 3) If analysis/defrag fails or issue recurs, demote the affected DC and re-promote it. This is often the quickest resolution for one-time occurrences.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/AD Replication/Workflow: Common Solutions to Replication Failures/Error 8451 replication operation encountered a database error

symptom: Promotion of a new Windows Server 2016 or 2019 domain controller into a new child domain fails with AD replication error 8451 during the DCPROMO replication phase.
rootCause: Known issue affecting Server 2016 and 2019 when promoting a DC into a new child domain. The AD database encounters an error during the initial replication to the new DC.
solution: Refer to internal Evergreen article a7889045-b81a-60e1-5458-f970b6e81cf2 for the specific fix. May require updated OS build or registry workaround before retrying promotion.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/AD Replication/Workflow: Common Solutions to Replication Failures/Error 8451 replication operation encountered a database error

symptom: AD replication error 8606: Insufficient attributes were given to create an object. Source DC sends update for an object that does not exist on destination DC. Indicates lingering objects present on source DC.
rootCause: Lingering objects exist on the source domain controller. An object was deleted and garbage collected on the destination DC, but the source DC still has a live copy (did not replicate the deletion within tombstone lifetime). Destination DC configured for strict replication consistency blocks the update.
solution: 1) Use Lingering Object Liquidator (LOL) tool to identify and remove lingering objects across the forest. 2) Alternatively use repadmin /removelingeringobjects <LingeringDC> <ReferenceDC_DSA_GUID> <Partition> to remove them manually. 3) Use repldiag /removelingeringobjects for automated removal. 4) Verify strict replication consistency is enabled (repadmin /regkey * +strict).
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/AD Replication/Workflow: Common Solutions to Replication Failures/Error 8606 - Lingering Objects

symptom: AD replication error 1908: Could not find the domain controller for this domain. Netlogon cannot locate a DC advertising the KDC flag. nltest /dsgetdc returns ERROR_NO_SUCH_DOMAIN (1355).
rootCause: Broken DNS delegation for the child domain. DNS queries for _kerberos._tcp.dc._msdcs.child.domain SRV records fail because the parent zone has a broken or stale delegation to a non-functional DNS server for the child zone.
solution: 1) Enable verbose Netlogon logging: nltest /dbflag:2080ffff. 2) Review Netlogon.log for DsGetDcName failures. 3) Run dcdiag /test:dns /dnsdelegation to identify broken delegations. 4) Fix DNS delegation in the parent zone to point to a valid, functional DNS server hosting the child zone. 5) Verify with nltest /dsgetdc:<child> /kdc.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/AD Replication/Workflow: Common Solutions to Replication Failures/Scenario walkthrough: ADREPL status 1908

symptom: AD replication error 8453: Replication access was denied. RODC fails to advertise as Global Catalog. Event 1926 logged in Directory Service event log. dcdiag /test:checksecurityerror reports missing permission for Enterprise Read-Only Domain Controllers.
rootCause: Enterprise Read-Only Domain Controllers security group is missing the Replicating Directory Changes permission on the cross-domain partition. This ACE is normally added during RODCPREP but may be missing if the infrastructure master was unavailable during that process.
solution: 1) Open ADSIEDIT.msc on a writable DC hosting the affected partition. 2) Right-click the partition DN > Properties > Security. 3) Add Enterprise Read-Only Domain Controllers group. 4) Clear default Read permissions, grant only Replicating Directory Changes. 5) Run repadmin kcc <RODC> to trigger topology recalculation. 6) Verify RODC can now replicate the partition and advertise as GC.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/AD Replication/Workflow: Common Solutions to Replication Failures/Scenario walkthrough: ADREPL status 8453

symptom: USN rollback detected on domain controller. Event ID 2095 logged in Directory Service event log. Netlogon service is paused. Partner DCs refuse to replicate with the affected DC (access denied). Registry key Dsa Not Writable set to 0x4.
rootCause: Domain controller was restored using an unsupported method (e.g., VM snapshot restore on pre-2012 Server or unsupported hypervisor). The restored DC has USN values that conflict with what replication partners have already acknowledged, causing replication inconsistency.
solution: The supported recovery method is to forcefully demote the affected DC (dcpromo /forceremoval) and re-promote it, or restore from a supported backup (Windows Server Backup with AD-aware VSS writer). Starting with Server 2012 on supported hypervisors, snapshot restores are supported via VM-GenerationID mechanism. Check registry NTDS Parameters Dsa Not Writable = 0x4 to confirm USN rollback.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/AD Replication/Workflow: Common Solutions to Replication Failures/USN Rollback - Event ID 2095
Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/AD SQL: Local Security Authority cannot be contacted
symptom: SQL application fails with error "Local Security Authority cannot be contacted" (SSPI handshake failed error code 0x80090304 state 14) when establishing connection with integrated security.
rootCause: FIPS mode is enabled on the server. SQL Server still reads the FIPS registry key despite Crypto PG guidance that it should not, causing authentication failures in SQL 2016 and later.
solution: Disable FIPS mode on the server. Gather auth data from client and server (CAPI2 event log, Kerberos ETL, NTLM ETL, Netlogon log) to identify the specific authentication mechanism that failed. If SQL team cannot determine the failing action, escalate via ICM.

sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/ADLDS/ADAMSYNC
symptom: ADAMSYNC fails with "An internal error occurred: DnManip::DnManip" during synchronization from AD to AD LDS.
rootCause: A CNF (conflict) object exists in Active Directory. The GUID shown in the ADAMSYNC log matches a CNF object created during AD replication conflict resolution.
solution: Take the GUID from the ADAMSYNC log, convert it to a searchable format, and use LDP to find the object by ObjectGUID. Verify it is a CNF object. Compare the CNF object with the original object before deciding whether to delete the CNF object or rename it.

sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/ADLDS/ADAMSYNC
symptom: ADAMSYNC fails with "A constraint violation occurred when attempting to add the target object" (ATT_OR_VALUE_EXISTS), possibly due to schema mismatch.
rootCause: White space exists in the display name of the user account, or in the values of the authOrig attribute (used by Exchange Server) of the user account.
solution: Check and remove trailing/leading white spaces from the display name and authOrig attribute values of the user account listed in the error message.

sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/ADLDS/ADAMSYNC
symptom: ADAMSYNC fails with CONSTRAINT_ATT_TYPE error for UserPrincipalName attribute during AD to AD LDS synchronization.
rootCause: Duplicate UserPrincipalName values exist across user accounts in the domain. AD does not enforce UPN uniqueness, causing sync failures when AD LDS applies the constraint.
solution: Run ldifde to export all UPNs: "ldifde -f UserPrincipalName.txt -t 3268 -d <DN of Domain> -l userPrincipalname". Find and correct duplicates. Also check the AD LDS app partition; if the user is already synchronized, delete it and re-run adamsync.

sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/ADLDS/ADAMSYNC
symptom: ADAMSYNC install command fails with "Error: Unable to find specified configuration file (dc=contoso,dc=com)" when running adamsync /install.
rootCause: The ADAMSync XML configuration file contains errors or the specified configuration partition DN does not match the actual AD LDS partition name.
solution: Verify the ADAMSync configuration XML file for errors. Ensure partition DN matches exactly. Default config file location: C:\Windows\ADAM. After fixing, re-run: adamsync /install localhost:389 CustomAdamsync.xml, then adamsync /sync localhost:389 "DC=fabrikam,DC=com" /log adamsync.log.

symptom: Domain Controller becomes unresponsive or enters a hung state. Users cannot authenticate, applications report connectivity errors to the DC. LSASS process may show high resource usage.
rootCause: Third-party components hooking into the LSASS I/O path or security subsystem cause DC hangs. Common culprits include Dell Quest Change Auditor, Powerbroker Blackbird, Beyond Trust, Centrify Direct Audit, virus/malware scanners, old virtualization/disk/NIC drivers, and event forwarding applications like Splunk.
solution: 1) Check if any third-party security/audit software was recently installed or updated on the DC. 2) Temporarily disable or uninstall suspected third-party components (Quest, Powerbroker, Centrify, etc.). 3) Update virtualization drivers, disk adapter drivers, and NIC drivers to latest versions. 4) Exclude LSASS and AD-related processes from antivirus real-time scanning. 5) Refer to KB4468450 (Centrify SSP causing LSASRV Event 5000) and KB4089038 (DC stops responding to Kerberos).
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/ADPerf/Workflow: ADPERF: DC Unresponsive | Hang/Known Issues

symptom: DC becomes unresponsive with LSASRV Event 5000 logged in System event log. Kerberos authentication stops working on the affected Domain Controller.
rootCause: Third-party Security Support Provider (SSP) such as Centrify is installed on the DC and interferes with LSASS, causing it to become unresponsive and log LSASRV Event 5000.
solution: Uninstall or disable the third-party SSP (e.g., Centrify Direct Audit agent) from the Domain Controller. Refer to internal KB4468450 for detailed steps. After removal, monitor DC stability and LSASS resource usage.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/ADPerf/Workflow: ADPERF: DC Unresponsive | Hang/Known Issues

symptom: LSASS.exe exhibits a handle and memory leak on a Domain Controller or member server. Handle count grows continuously (e.g., 60,000+ unreleased handles daily). High Token handle count observed in WinDbg (!handle 0 0). Semaphore handles also grow proportionally.
rootCause: Logon session leak caused by an application or service repeatedly creating new logon sessions (via LsaLogonUser or AcceptSecurityContext/InitializeSecurityContext) without properly closing them. The leaking application runs under a specific service account, creating thousands of Kerberos logon sessions that are never destroyed.
solution: 1) Collect ADPerf Scenario 5 data (WPR Handle trace + perfmon). 2) Open LSASS dump in WinDbg, run '!handle 0 0' to confirm Token handle leak. 3) Use MEX extension (!mex.lsass > Logon Session List) to identify which user account has excessive sessions. 4) Run '!ul !grep Username !exec -x "!kerbsess"' to count sessions per user. 5) Identify the application running under that account and fix its session cleanup logic. 6) If needed, collect TTD trace of LSASS + tasklist during 2-3 min window to trace the calling process.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/ADPerf/Workflow: ADPERF: LSASS high handles/Data Analysis- Walkthrough of troubleshooting a logon session leak in LSASS

symptom: AD LDS administrator account lost or cannot manage ADLDS instance, permission denied when accessing configuration or application partition via ADSI Edit or LDP
rootCause: ADLDS administrator account/group lost access or was removed from the Administrators role in the configuration partition, leaving no account with full control
solution: Use psexec -i -s cmd.exe to open SYSTEM context CMD. Identify instance port via dsdbutil "list instance". Take ownership of configuration partition: dsacls \localhost:port\CN=Configuration,CN={GUID} /takeownership. Grant GA to new admin: dsacls ... /G domaindmin:GA. Repeat for application partition. Then use LDP to add ACLs with Inherit ACE flag on all containers.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/ADLDS/ADLDS Permission Issue/ADLDS Permission Troubleshooter

symptom: ADLDS replication fails between instances with Kerberos authentication errors during replication
rootCause: Required Service Principal Names (SPNs) for ADLDS replication are missing or incorrectly registered on the service account. ADLDS replication uses SPNs E3514235-4B06-11D1-AB04-00C04FC2DCD2-ADAM/{hostname}:{port} and Ldap/{hostname}:{port}
solution: Register required SPNs on the ADLDS service account: E3514235-4B06-11D1-AB04-00C04FC2DCD2-ADAM/netbiosname:port, E3514235-4B06-11D1-AB04-00C04FC2DCD2-ADAM/dnshostname:port, Ldap/netbiosname:port, Ldap/dnshostname:port. Verify with setspn -L <serviceAccount>.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/ADLDS/ADLDS replication/ADLDS replication troubleshooter

symptom: ADLDS replication fails, instances cannot authenticate to each other for replication despite correct network connectivity
rootCause: msDS-ReplAuthenticationMode values differ between ADAM/ADLDS instances in the configuration set, causing authentication mode mismatch during replication
solution: Use ADSIEDIT to connect to each ADLDS configuration partition and verify all instances have matching msDS-ReplAuthenticationMode values. Ensure the value is consistent across all replication partners.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/ADLDS/ADLDS replication/ADLDS replication troubleshooter

symptom: ADLDS replication fails with access denied or permission errors between replication partners
rootCause: Computer accounts or service accounts for LDS instances are not members of CN=Instances,CN=Roles in the configuration partition, missing required permissions: Replicating Directory Changes, Replication Synchronization, Manage Replication Topology, Replicating Directory Changes All
solution: Add the computer account SID or service account of the problem LDS instance to CN=Instances,CN=Roles,CN=Configuration,CN={GUID} using ADSI Edit or LDP. For NetworkService accounts add S-1-5-20, for machine accounts add the machine SID. Verify membership includes all replication partner machine/service accounts.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/ADLDS/ADLDS replication/ADLDS replication troubleshooter

symptom: After installing June 2024 (6B.2024) cumulative update on Active Directory domain controllers, LSASS memory grows continuously due to kdcsvc memory leak
rootCause: Memory leak bug in kdcsvc service introduced in the June 2024 cumulative update for Windows Server
solution: Install a later cumulative update that contains the fix for the kdcsvc memory leak
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/ADPerf/Workflow: ADPERF: LSASS high memory/Known Issues - LSASS High Memory

symptom: Small memory leak in LSASS on Windows Server 2016 and older domain controllers after installing April 9, 2024 update, occurring in cross-forest runas scenarios with KDC
rootCause: PAC hardening protections in April 2024 (4B.24) Windows Update cause memory leak on pre-1809 operating systems in cross-forest authentication paths
solution: Install the fix from May 2024 (5B.24) cumulative update which resolves the PAC hardening memory leak on older OS versions
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/ADPerf/Workflow: ADPERF: LSASS high memory/Known Issues - LSASS High Memory
