symptom: LDAP Arena Heap and SAM RSO queue memory leak in LSASS on April 9, 2024 patched domain controllers servicing authentication requests at hyper-scale
rootCause: RSO worker thread fails to clear SAM RSO queue, exposed by NTLM auth forwarding bug in the Kerberos PAC Validation security update (4B.24)
solution: Install the fix that addresses the RSO queue memory leak on DCs with hyper-scale authentication workloads
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/ADPerf/Workflow: ADPERF: LSASS high memory/Known Issues - LSASS High Memory

symptom: LSASS memory leak on client machines growing up to 1GB per minute, triggered by opening Office files (Excel, OneNote); reproduced by opening an Office file in an Office app
rootCause: Bad proxy SPN being passed to InitializeSecurityContextA causes an authentication storm flooding LSASS with requests
solution: Correct the proxy SPN configuration or install the relevant Office/Windows update that addresses the InitializeSecurityContextA authentication storm
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/ADPerf/Workflow: ADPERF: LSASS high memory/Known Issues - LSASS High Memory

symptom: LSASS memory and handle count continuously increase on Windows Server 2019 or Vibranium Windows 10 with IIS role installed; up to 16 million handles observed; system becomes unresponsive
rootCause: Enabling TLS 1.3 via registry or group policy on OS versions that do not officially support TLS 1.3 (WS2019, Vibranium Win10) causes LSASS memory and handle leak
solution: Disable TLS 1.3 on unsupported OS versions as workaround; install March/June 2024 Windows Updates that prevent TLS 1.3 from being enabled on unsupported OS
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/ADPerf/Workflow: ADPERF: LSASS high memory/Known Issues - LSASS High Memory

symptom: LSASS on Windows Server 2012 R2 and 2016 domain controllers consumes all available memory, becomes unresponsive to LDAP and other requests while down-level DCs remain responsive
rootCause: Multiple arena heap bugs in LDAP processing on WS2012R2/2016 cause memory growth with long-running LDAP transactions and high LDAP workloads
solution: Identify LDAP clients generating load via 1644 events using Event1644Reader.ps1, optimize or eliminate expensive queries; apply all available cumulative patches for WS2012R2/2016
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/ADPerf/Workflow: ADPERF: LSASS high memory/Known Issues - LSASS High Memory

symptom: Domain Controllers enter reboot loop after installing January 2022 Windows updates (January B/12B). LSASS crashes with error c0000374 / error code 255 during DC startup.
rootCause: January 2022 security updates (KB5009557 for Server 2019, etc.) introduced a regression causing LSASS crash on Domain Controllers, leading to continuous reboot cycles.
solution: Install the out-of-band (OOB) release from January 17, 2022. KB by OS: Server 2008 SP2=KB5010799, 2008 R2=KB5010798, 2012=KB5010797, 2012 R2=KB5010794, 2016=KB5010790, 2019=KB5010791, 20H2=KB5010793, 2022=KB5010796. OOB is superset of Jan B; can deploy on top of existing Jan updates. Check Windows Release Health dashboard for latest status.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/ADPerf/Workflow: ADPERF: Other common scenarios/DC Reboot regressions caused by January 2022 updates | ICM 282863898

symptom: LDAP paged search fails with error '00000057: LdapErr: DSID-xxxxxxxx, comment: Error processing control, data 0'. Applications cannot continue paged LDAP queries against Domain Controllers.
rootCause: LDAP server cookie pool exceeded administrator limits (MaxResultSetSize default 262144 bytes or MaxResultSetsPerConn default 10), causing stored cookies to be evicted from server cache. Client continuation cookie becomes invalid when server discards it.
solution: Enable NTDS diagnostics category 16 (LDAP Interface) at level 2 to get events 2898/2899. If event 2898 (per-connection limit): increase MaxResultSetsPerConn to 25+ in LDAP Policy. If event 2899 (total pool size): increase MaxResultSetSize to >=250MB on DCs with sufficient RAM. Review application design for excessive/repetitive paged queries. Consider adding more replicated DC instances to distribute load.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/ADPerf/Workflow: ADPERF: Other common scenarios/How LDAP Server Cookies are Handled

symptom: Domain Controllers experience random high CPU (90%+) in LSASS process; end-users and application owners report LDAP queries taking long time to complete or timing out
rootCause: Multiple clients simultaneously running costly LDAP queries using LDAP_MATCHING_RULE_IN_CHAIN operator, starting at domain root with deep recursive searches across group memberships, repeated frequently from various clients
solution: 1) Collect AD Data Collector Set (TSS) during high CPU state. 2) Analyze HTML report: check CPU breakdown under Directory Services tab -> Unique Searches section for LDAP_MATCHING_RULE_IN_CHAIN filter patterns. 3) Identify top callers by IP/client using AD ETL 1644 events or Event1644Reader.PS1 parser. 4) Contact service account owner to determine application usage. 5) Remediation: optimize LDAP queries on client/application side (local caching, stagger query schedules, use more efficient filters instead of LDAP_MATCHING_RULE_IN_CHAIN)
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/ADPerf/Workflow: ADPERF: Lsass High CPU/Data Analysis and Walkthroughs/LDAP Workload (LDAP_MATCHING_RULE_IN_CHAIN)

symptom: Domain Controller experiences high CPU (75%+) in LSASS during peak logon times (e.g. 9-10AM); LDAP queries for contact attributes (telephoneNumber, etc.) visiting 100K+ entries but returning 0 results
rootCause: Applications (e.g. Cisco Jabber VoIP) performing LDAP queries against non-indexed AD attributes (telephoneNumber, otherTelephone etc.) with deep searches starting at domain root, causing full directory scans during peak usage
solution: 1) Collect AD Data Collector Set during high CPU window. 2) Analyze HTML report: check Directory Services > LDAP success code CPU %, then 'Clients with Most CPU when Searching' to identify query filters with unindexed attributes. 3) Enable 1644 event logging (Field Engineering=5 + Inefficient Search Results Threshold=50ms) to capture expensive queries. 4) Identify the application making these queries (e.g. Cisco Jabber). 5) Index the required AD attributes per application needs: https://techcommunity.microsoft.com/t5/microsoft-entra-blog/indexing-in-active-directory/ba-p/243119
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/ADPerf/Workflow: ADPERF: Lsass High CPU/Data Analysis and Walkthroughs/LDAP Workload (Unindexed Attribute)

symptom: Domain Controllers experience random high CPU (80%+) in LSASS process; LSA LookupSids consuming 69%+ of LSASS CPU with SAM workload from SID lookups at 10K+ requests/second
rootCause: Application(s) on one or more clients sending excessive LsarLookupSids2 (opnum-57) requests with arrays of 500 SIDs per request in rapid succession, generating massive SAM lookup workload on the DC
solution: 1) Collect AD Data Collector Set during high CPU. 2) Analyze HTML report: check LSA Lookup Sids section for CPU % and request rates. 3) Process LSP.log with ADPERF LSP WPA Profile to identify source IPs and SID array sizes per request. 4) Use network trace with filter (lsarpc.opnum == 57 || dcerpc.opnum == 57) on client side to identify the process ID making requests. 5) Cross-reference PID with tasklist.txt to identify the application. 6) Engage app owner to optimize: implement SID caching, reduce query frequency, stagger queries across DCs
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/ADPerf/Workflow: ADPERF: Lsass High CPU/Data Analysis and Walkthroughs/LSA Workload (Excessive SID Lookups)

symptom: Version store errors (Event ID 623, 1519) on domain controllers after deleting large numbers of group membership links in Active Directory, causing AD replication failures
rootCause: Arena Heap memory issue triggered by ADREPL processing large-scale link deletions, exhausting version store buckets
solution: Apply relevant hotfix per KB e52d4cdc. As best practice, increase Version Store size to 850MB on domain controllers. For Server 2016 and older, size is based on CPU count; for Server 2019+, it is based on RAM.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/ADPerf/Workflow: ADPERF: Version Store issues/Known Issues

symptom: Version store outages and bucket exhaustion on domain controllers running DeviceLock DLP software, leading to AD database operation failures
rootCause: DeviceLock DLP software interferes with ESE database operations on domain controllers, causing version store bucket exhaustion
solution: Remove or update DeviceLock to a compatible version on affected DCs. See KB c1352901. Increase Version Store size to 850MB as preventive measure.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/ADPerf/Workflow: ADPERF: Version Store issues/Known Issues

symptom: High CPU utilization in LSASS process combined with version store exhaustion on domain controllers running StealthIntercept AD monitoring tool
rootCause: StealthIntercept AD monitoring tool causes excessive version store consumption and sustained LSASS high CPU on domain controllers
solution: Remove or reconfigure StealthIntercept AD monitoring tool on affected domain controllers. See KB 3a17ef01. Increase Version Store size to 850MB as interim mitigation.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/ADPerf/Workflow: ADPERF: Version Store issues/Known Issues

symptom: LiveKD fails with error 'Error resolving symbol KdVersionBlock: 126 / Failed to resolve KdVersionBlock - 126 / Failed to load guest symbols - 126 / Failed to prepare hypervisor session for debugger - error 126' when collecting memory dump from Hyper-V VM
rootCause: Symbol path (_NT_SYMBOL_PATH) environment variable is not configured on the machine running LiveKD
solution: Set the _NT_SYMBOL_PATH environment variable to point to a symbol server before running LiveKD. Example: SET _NT_SYMBOL_PATH=srv*c:\\symbols*http://msdl.microsoft.com/download/symbols
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/ADPerf/Workflow: ADPERF: Tools/ADPerf: Diagnostic Collection Tools/Memory Dumps/Full Dump: Hyper-V

symptom: LSASS.exe high CPU on domain controller caused by excessive SAM EnumUsersInDomain calls originating from the DC itself. WMI queries for Win32_UserAccount class (SELECT SID,Domain,Name FROM Win32_UserAccount WHERE SID=...) flood the DC, consuming CPU in SAM workload. Xperf shows OSF_SCALL threads in LSASS processing SamrEnumerateUsersInDomain RPC calls.
rootCause: Third-party product CheckPoint firewall with Identity Awareness feature enabled issues massive WMI queries targeting Win32_UserAccount class on the domain controller. Each query triggers SamEnumerateUsersInDomain API call which is expensive in large domains. Queries come from CheckPoint appliance IP via RPC/DCOM to WmiPrvSE on the DC.
solution: Identify source IP from Xperf call stacks (OSF_SCALL frame shows Client Endpoint IP) or network traces (MSRPC Auth3 frame reveals authenticating user, ExecQuery frame shows WMI query). Disable or disconnect CheckPoint Identity Awareness feature. Contact CheckPoint support for proper configuration.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/ADPerf/Workflow: ADPERF: Lsass High CPU/Data Analysis and Walkthroughs/SAM Workload (using Process Dumps)

symptom: Cisco Jabber LDAP queries execute slowly and cause high CPU on domain controllers. LDAP queries issued by Jabber use non-indexed attributes, resulting in expensive full table scans in the AD database.
rootCause: Cisco Jabber issues LDAP queries against Active Directory using non-indexed attributes. Without proper indexing, each query requires scanning the entire directory, causing high CPU utilization on the targeted domain controller.
solution: Identify unindexed attributes used by Cisco Jabber LDAP queries and create appropriate indexes in AD schema. Work with Cisco support to optimize Jabber LDAP query patterns. Reference KB 3155721.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/ADPerf/Workflow: ADPERF: Lsass High CPU/Top | Known Issues

symptom: LSASS high CPU on domain controller caused by LookupNames calls from nessusd.exe (Nessus vulnerability scanner) via WMIPRVSE.exe. Issue appears after updating Nessus to latest plugin version 71246.
rootCause: Nessus vulnerability scanner plugin 71246 issues excessive LsaLookupNames calls through WMI provider host (WMIPRVSE.exe), causing high CPU utilization in LSASS on the targeted domain controller.
solution: Identify nessusd.exe as source process through WMIPRVSE.exe call stacks. Contact Tenable (Nessus vendor) to address excessive LookupNames behavior in plugin 71246. Temporarily disable or reconfigure offending plugin. Reference KB 4337685.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/ADPerf/Workflow: ADPERF: Lsass High CPU/Top | Known Issues

symptom: LSASS high CPU on domain controller caused by high LDAP load from Outlook or Exchange AutoDiscover process. Domain controllers experience sustained high CPU with LDAP workload dominating LSASS threads.
rootCause: Outlook or Exchange AutoDiscover process generates excessive LDAP queries against the domain controller, causing high CPU utilization in LSASS. The LDAP load from AutoDiscover overwhelms the DC capacity.
solution: Identify source of AutoDiscover LDAP queries via AD Performance diagnostics. Optimize Exchange AutoDiscover configuration or distribute LDAP load across multiple domain controllers. Reference KB 4295099.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/ADPerf/Workflow: ADPERF: Lsass High CPU/Top | Known Issues

symptom: LSASS high CPU and AD version store exhaustion on domain controller. StealthIntercept AD monitoring tool causes sustained high CPU in LSASS and depletes the ESE version store, potentially leading to LDAP query failures.
rootCause: StealthIntercept (Stealthbits/Netwrix) AD monitoring tool hooks into LSASS and generates excessive internal operations that consume CPU and exhaust the Active Directory database version store (JET Blue ESE version store buckets).
solution: Identify StealthIntercept as offending process via AD Performance diagnostics. Disable or reconfigure StealthIntercept monitoring. Contact Stealthbits/Netwrix support. Reference KB 4024857.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/ADPerf/Workflow: ADPERF: Lsass High CPU/Top | Known Issues

symptom: Excessive SAM traffic causes SampLock contention on domain controller, resulting in slow AD replication and degraded authentication performance. SAM operations queue up behind the global SAM lock.
rootCause: High volume of SAM API calls from applications or scripts create contention on SampLock (SAM global lock), blocking AD replication and other SAM operations. Common sources include WMI queries for Win32_UserAccount, bulk user enumeration, or third-party identity tools.
solution: Identify source of excessive SAM traffic using AD Performance diagnostics (Xperf/WPR, network traces). Reduce or throttle SAM queries from offending application. Consider distributing load across multiple DCs. Reference KB 4136738.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/ADPerf/Workflow: ADPERF: Lsass High CPU/Top | Known Issues

symptom: LogRhythm application makes excessive IDL_DRSCrackNames calls to domain controller, exhausting LSASS Database Sessions In Use resource. DC may become unresponsive to authentication requests as available database sessions are depleted.
rootCause: LogRhythm SIEM application issues high volume of DRSCrackNames RPC calls to translate security principals, consuming all available LSASS database sessions (ESE sessions). When sessions exhausted, DC cannot service new LDAP or authentication requests.
solution: Identify LogRhythm as source via AD Performance diagnostics and network traces showing DRSCrackNames traffic. Reconfigure LogRhythm to reduce volume of name resolution queries or distribute across multiple DCs. Contact LogRhythm support. Reference KB 4039445.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/ADPerf/Workflow: ADPERF: Lsass High CPU/Top | Known Issues

symptom: SCCM (System Center Configuration Manager) job causes high LSASS CPU on domain controller by calling LsalookupAccountNames API repeatedly. DC CPU spikes correlate with SCCM collection evaluation or deployment schedules.
rootCause: SCCM executes bulk LsaLookupAccountNames calls during collection evaluation, software deployment, or compliance scanning. Each call triggers SID-to-name translation via LSARPC to the domain controller, causing high CPU when done at scale.
solution: Identify SCCM as source via AD Performance diagnostics (look for LsaLookupAccountNames in call stacks). Adjust SCCM collection evaluation intervals, stagger deployment schedules, or configure SCCM to use a dedicated DC/site. Reference KB 4090541.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/ADPerf/Workflow: ADPERF: Lsass High CPU/Top | Known Issues

symptom: Domain Controller fails to start due to hardware failure, BugCheck (BSOD), ransomware attack, or other critical issues preventing normal boot
rootCause: Physical hardware damage, OS-level crash (BugCheck/BSOD), ransomware encryption, or catastrophic system failure rendering the Domain Controller unbootable
solution: Option A (preferred, when multiple DCs exist and no unique data/roles on failed DC): Perform metadata cleanup of the failed DC using ntdsutil, seize any FSMO roles held by the broken DC, then promote a new DC. Option B (non-authoritative restore): Install same OS on same hardware, restore from BMR or System State backup, boot into DSRM (bcdedit /set safeboot dsrepair), perform system state restore, clear safeboot flag (bcdedit /deletevalue safeboot), reboot to allow AD replication to bring the DC current. Note: System State backups can only be restored to same or identical hardware.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Active Directory Disaster Recovery/DC Recovery

symptom: DNS records deleted from AD-integrated zone cannot be restored by setting dnsTombstoned attribute back to False or restoring the dnsNode object from AD Recycle Bin
rootCause: When a DNS record is deleted via DNS tools, both dnsTombstoned is set to TRUE and the dnsRecord attribute value is modified/cleared. The dnsRecord attribute stores the actual DNS resource record definition, so restoring only dnsTombstoned does not restore the record data.
solution: Use authoritative restore to recover DNS records or the entire DNS zone. Alternatively, within the 7-day window (before dnsNode AD tombstone cleanup at 2AM daily), restore both dnsRecord and dnsTombstoned attributes using LDIFDE from a previously exported LDF file. Note: if record was deleted via ADSIEdit (not DNS tools), it follows normal AD object deletion lifecycle instead.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Active Directory Disaster Recovery/DNS records Removal and Recovery (Microsoft AD-integrated DNS zones)

symptom: Active Directory Administrative Center (ADAC) cannot display all deleted objects in large enterprise environments - Deleted Objects container appears incomplete or shows fewer than expected objects
rootCause: ADAC uses client-side filtering which cannot handle displaying more than approximately 20,000 objects in the Deleted Objects container
solution: Right-click the Deleted Objects container > select 'Search under this node' > click the chevron to expose +Add criteria > add 'Last modified between given dates' (whenChanged attribute approximates deletion time). This performs a server-side search bypassing the display limitation. Use further filtering/sorting on the results.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Active Directory Disaster Recovery/Recovering AD Objects

symptom: Cannot restore deleted AD objects from the Configuration partition using Active Directory Administrative Center (ADAC) - objects deleted from Configuration partition not visible in ADAC Deleted Objects
rootCause: ADAC only manages domain partitions and cannot restore deleted objects from other partitions such as the Configuration partition
solution: Use PowerShell to restore objects from non-domain partitions: Get-ADObject -Filter {isDeleted -eq $true -and name -like 'ObjectName*'} -SearchBase 'CN=Configuration,DC=CONTOSO,DC=COM' -IncludeDeletedObjects | Restore-ADObject. For DNS zones/records in Configuration partition, refer to the AD Recycle Bin DNS recovery guidance.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Active Directory Disaster Recovery/Recovering AD Objects

symptom: Authoritative restore of AD objects fails - objects are not recovered after system state backup restoration and the entire restore process needs to be repeated
rootCause: Domain controller was rebooted after system state restore but before marking objects as authoritative using ntdsutil. Without the authoritative flag (version bump of +100,000), restored objects have lower version numbers and get overwritten during replication from other DCs.
solution: After system state restore completes in DSRM, do NOT restart. Open elevated command prompt > ntdsutil > activate instance NTDS > authoritative restore > 'restore object "DN"' or 'restore subtree "DN"'. Only reboot after marking all required objects as authoritative. Also keep 'Perform authoritative restore of Active Directory Files' checkbox UNCHECKED during WSB restore, and uncheck 'Automatically reboot' as well.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Active Directory Disaster Recovery/Recovering AD Objects

symptom: Certificate-based authentication fails on Domain Controller with KDC System Event 39 (Kerberos-Key-Distribution-Center). Certificate is valid but Kerberos authentication is denied.
rootCause: Certificate could not be mapped to a user account in a strong way per KB5014754 enforcement. Weak certificate mappings (e.g., UPN-only mapping without SID extension) are no longer accepted after strong mapping enforcement date.
solution: Implement strong certificate mapping per KB5014754: 1) Add the SID extension (OID 1.3.6.1.4.1.311.25.2) to certificate templates via CA, or 2) Create explicit strong mapping via altSecurityIdentities attribute (X509:<SHA1-PUKEY>hash or X509:<I>issuer<SR>serial). Reference: https://support.microsoft.com/topic/kb5014754
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/AI SME Agent JSON Rules/Strong Cert Mapping Rules

symptom: Certificate-based authentication denied on Domain Controller with KDC System Event 40 (Kerberos-Key-Distribution-Center). User certificate is valid but authentication fails.
rootCause: The certificate was issued (NotBefore date) before the user account was created in Active Directory. KB5014754 strong mapping enforcement rejects certificates that predate the mapped account as a security measure.
solution: Re-enroll/re-issue the certificate after the user account exists, so the certificate NotBefore date is after the account creation date. Alternatively, create an explicit strong mapping via altSecurityIdentities (X509:<SHA1-PUKEY> or X509:<I><SR>) which bypasses the date check. Reference: https://support.microsoft.com/topic/kb5014754
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/AI SME Agent JSON Rules/Strong Cert Mapping Rules

symptom: Certificate-based authentication denied on Domain Controller with KDC System Event 41 (Kerberos-Key-Distribution-Center). Certificate appears valid but Kerberos rejects it.
rootCause: The SID embedded in the certificate new SID extension (OID 1.3.6.1.4.1.311.25.2) does not match the target user account SID in Active Directory. This implies the certificate was issued to a different user account than the one being authenticated.
solution: Verify the certificate ownership: check which user the SID in the certificate extension belongs to. If cert was issued to wrong user, re-issue to correct user. If template misconfiguration, fix the certificate template to include correct SID. Reference: https://support.microsoft.com/topic/kb5014754
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/AI SME Agent JSON Rules/Strong Cert Mapping Rules

symptom: Certificate-based authentication (smartcard/PKINIT) fails on Domain Controller. CAPI2 Operational Event 30 (Error level) logged with CERT_CHAIN_POLICY_NT_AUTH chain policy failure.
rootCause: The Issuing CA certificate is not published in the NTAuth store in Active Directory. Windows requires the issuing CA to be trusted in the NTAuth store for smartcard/certificate-based logon.
solution: Publish the Issuing CA certificate to the NTAuth store in AD: certutil -dspublish -f <CACert.cer> NTAuthCA. Then wait for AD replication and Group Policy refresh (or run certutil -pulse on the DC). Verify with: certutil -viewstore -enterprise NTAuth.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/AI SME Agent JSON Rules/Strong Cert Mapping Rules

symptom: Certificate mapping fails on Domain Controller with KDC Operational Event 313. Strong certificate mapping enforcement rejects authentication attempts.
rootCause: Invalid tuple configuration in the certificate mapping rules. The faulting line contains typos or extra characters that prevent the KDC from parsing the mapping tuples correctly.
solution: Examine KDC Operational Event 313 details to identify the faulting tuple line. Check for typos, extra characters, or incorrect syntax in the certificate mapping configuration. Fix the tuple and verify with a test authentication.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/AI SME Agent JSON Rules/Strong Cert Mapping Rules

symptom: Certificate-based (smartcard) authentication fails on Domain Controller after KB5014754 enforcement. Existing weak altSecurityIdentities mappings stop working.
rootCause: Registry key UseSubjectAltName (HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Services\Kdc) is set to 0, which disables Subject Alternative Name (SAN)-based certificate mapping. After KB5014754 strong mapping enforcement, weak altSec mappings that relied on SAN are rejected.
solution: Remove or set UseSubjectAltName to 1 (default) at HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Services\Kdc\UseSubjectAltName. Value 0 disables SAN-based mapping; value 1 (default) enables strong binding. After change, no reboot needed but KDC service restart may be required.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/AI SME Agent JSON Rules/Strong Cert Mapping Rules

symptom: KDC System Event 39 logged on Domain Controller AND Group Policy configured with Process even if the Group Policy objects have not changed (NoGPOListChanges=1). Certificate authentication intermittently fails after GPO refresh.
rootCause: KDC policy settings (UseStrongNameMatches=1 at HKLM\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Policies\System\KDC\Parameters) may be incompatible with GPO registry policy processing setting NoGPOListChanges=1. GPO reprocessing resets registry-based KDC settings on each policy refresh cycle.
solution: Review the interaction between KDC strong mapping policy and GPO processing: either exclude KDC Parameters registry from GPO processing, or configure strong mapping settings via Group Policy instead of direct registry edits to prevent GPO refresh from overwriting manual settings.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/AI SME Agent JSON Rules/Strong Cert Mapping Rules

symptom: Account lockout for a user where security events (4740, 4776) show NPS server as the source of bad credentials, and event 4625 shows svchost hosting the eaphost process. No direct authentication traffic correlates with events, only RADIUS traffic visible in network trace.
rootCause: EAP RADIUS authentication through a wireless access point (802.11) sends bad credentials via RADIUS Access-Request packets to the NPS server, which validates against AD domain controller, causing failed logons and eventual account lockout.
solution: Collect a network trace during lockout events filtered for RADIUS traffic. Inspect RADIUS Access-Request packets to identify: Calling-Station-Id (end user device MAC) and Called-Station-Id (wireless access point MAC). Engage the network team to identify the device by searching the MAC address in the ARP table on the corresponding access point.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Account Lockouts/Workflow: Account Lockout: Known Issues and Solutions/Workflow: Account Lockout: NPS, Radius, eaphost

symptom: Group Policy editing fails with Access is denied or sharing violations on SYSVOL files (gpt.ini, registry.pol, gpttmpl.inf). Clients log Event ID 7016 ErrorCode 1252 in Group Policy Operational Log. Security Extension processing completes with errors.
rootCause: Concurrent file handle locks from large numbers of GP clients downloading SYSVOL files with ShareMode Read/Write. Latency or packet loss between clients and DC extends lock duration, causing conflicts when GP editor attempts to acquire write handle.
solution: Edit Group Policy only from PDC (or single designated DC). Place PDC in a separate AD Site not covering high-latency client subnets. Use machines with low network latency to the editing DC. Configure AV exclusions per KB822158 to prevent security product file locks on SYSVOL.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/AskDS Blog Content/20240126 Stop Worrying and Love the Outage, Vol I: Group Policy and Sharing Violations

symptom: AD replication failures and lingering objects after implementing firewall rules or RPC port restrictions between Domain Controllers. Replication monitoring shows persistent errors and alert fatigue.
rootCause: Overzealous firewall/ACL configuration restricting RPC dynamic port ranges (49152-65535) between DCs. DC isolation via separate sites, lag sites, or Bridge All Site Links misconfiguration gives KCC incorrect topology information for replication path calculation.
solution: Ensure all DCs in the forest can communicate on all required AD ports including full RPC dynamic range. Do not restrict RPC port ranges on DCs. Do not permanently isolate DCs or create lag sites. Let KCC manage replication topology. Verify replication monitoring alerts are active and not fatigued.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/AskDS Blog Content/20240226 Stop Worrying and Love the Outage, Vol II: DCs, custom ports, and Firewalls ACLs

symptom: Certificate Authority audit fails due to ESC1 vulnerability. Certificate template with Subject Name set to Supply in the request is configured for automatic issuance without requiring CA Manager approval, allowing unvalidated SAN values.
rootCause: Certificate template allows submitters to specify arbitrary Subject Alternative Names without validation. Auto-issuance bypasses the CA Manager review step that should verify SAN values match legitimate ownership.
solution: Configure CA Manager approval for all certificate templates with Supply in the request Subject Name. For NDES, use Enrollment Agent (Certificate Request Agent EKU) cross-signing. Lock down enrollment permissions to specific security groups. For automated renewals post first issuance, set Issuance Requirements to Valid existing certificate under reenrollment section.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/AskDS Blog Content/20240513 First Issuance manual, with automated renewals

symptom: After running a program as administrator with Administrator Protection (AP) enabled, user cannot find saved files in their Documents folder
rootCause: Administrator Protection creates a separate shadow admin profile (ADMIN_<Username>). When a program runs elevated, it operates under this shadow profile, so files are saved to C:\Users\ADMIN_<Username>\Documents instead of the users regular profile
solution: Navigate to C:\Users\ADMIN_<Username>\Documents to find the file. Alternatively, run the program unelevated if admin rights are not truly needed. Educate user about APs profile isolation design
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Administrator Protection - Adminless/Customer Scenarios

symptom: Application shows cannot write to user config error or behaves abnormally when running elevated with Administrator Protection enabled. Visual Studio 2022 extensions and settings missing when launched elevated
rootCause: App expects user profile settings/extensions but runs under shadow admin context with a different user name and profile path. Extensions installed under standard user profile are not visible in elevated session due to profile isolation
solution: Run the application without elevation if possible. Check vendor guidance for AP compatibility. For VS, run without elevation when possible. As a workaround, temporarily disable AP for that user or use a dedicated local admin account
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Administrator Protection - Adminless/Customer Scenarios

symptom: User receives frequent PIN/password prompts for every admin action after Administrator Protection is enabled, compared to UAC which only showed a Yes click
rootCause: By design, Administrator Protection prompts for credentials on every elevation. Unlike traditional UAC consent mode, AP defaults to credential prompt mode for enhanced security
solution: Set up Windows Hello (fingerprint/face) for faster authentication. If allowed by policy, switch to Prompt for consent mode instead of Prompt for credentials. Note: there is no remember or whitelist option - every elevation is interactive by design
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Administrator Protection - Adminless/Customer Scenarios

symptom: Network shares (e.g. winbuilds) cannot be accessed from an elevated PowerShell/CMD session with Administrator Protection enabled - fails with incorrect username or password
rootCause: Network shares are mapped to the standard user account, not the shadow admin account created by AP. The elevated session runs under a different security context that does not have the share credentials
solution: Access shares from an unelevated PowerShell/CMD session. Alternatively, use net use to map the network share to a local drive using the users credentials from within the elevated session
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Administrator Protection - Adminless/Known issues and Workaround

symptom: Scheduled tasks or automation scripts that require elevation fail to run properly after Administrator Protection is enabled. Tasks configured to Run with highest privileges under user context fail silently
rootCause: Administrator Protection requires interactive credential approval for every elevation. Scheduled tasks running under user context cannot trigger the interactive secure desktop prompt, so tasks requiring admin privileges fail
solution: Reconfigure tasks to run under SYSTEM account or a service account instead of user context. For scripts, split into standard and elevated parts using Start-Process -Verb RunAs. For enterprise, leverage Intune/ConfigMgr for unattended admin operations
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Administrator Protection - Adminless/Customer Scenarios

symptom: Remote support tools cannot perform elevated operations on machines with Administrator Protection enabled - helpdesk technicians find every admin action requires end user to approve the prompt locally
rootCause: AP requires interactive user approval for all elevations on the secure desktop. Remote sessions cannot pass the secure desktop prompt to the remote helper, and AP does not allow silent elevation
solution: Use management tools that operate as SYSTEM or with their own agent (e.g., RMM tools with a system-level service). Guide user over phone/chat to approve prompts. For RDP, use Ctrl+Alt+End to trigger security screen. Schedule admin changes when user can be present
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Administrator Protection - Adminless/Customer Scenarios

symptom: Apps installed before Administrator Protection was enabled do not function correctly - app references wrong registry paths or user profile paths after AP is enabled
rootCause: Apps installed before AP stored references to original user profile/registry paths. After AP is enabled, elevated operations use the shadow admin profile with different paths, causing mismatches
solution: Reinstall the application after AP is enabled so it configures paths correctly for the new profile structure. If not possible, manually adjust registry/config entries to point to correct paths
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Administrator Protection - Adminless/Known issues and Workaround

symptom: Hyper-V programs including WSL and Visual Studio have issues when Administrator Protection is enabled. Server and AVD (ServerRDSH) editions are not fully supported with AP
rootCause: APs elevation model and profile isolation are not fully compatible with Hyper-V virtualization stack, WSL subsystem, and Server/AVD editions. These components require persistent elevated context conflicting with APs per-action elevation design
solution: Avoid enabling AP on Server and AVD editions until full support is available. For Hyper-V/WSL issues, team is actively investigating. Monitor Windows Insider builds for fixes
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Administrator Protection - Adminless/Known issues and Workaround

symptom: Cross-domain authentication fails with KRB_AP_ERR_BAD_INTEGRITY error. Users get repeated password prompts, logon failures, or trust validation failures when accessing resources in a different AD domain across a forest trust
rootCause: The Trusted Domain Object (TDO) secret (trustAuthIncoming/trustAuthOutgoing) is out of sync between domains. Occurs when DCs are replaced/upgraded, authoritative restores from old backups, or AD replication failures cause some DCs to have different TDO password versions
solution: 1) Run repadmin /showobjmeta * on TDO DN to check trustAuthIncoming/trustAuthOutgoing version consistency across all DCs in both domains. 2) If versions differ, fix AD replication first. 3) Reset trust secret using netdom (run on both sides with same /passwordT) or Domains and Trusts snap-in. 4) Run the reset at least twice with time for replication between changes. 5) Clear client Kerberos cache with klist purge
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/AskDS Blog Content/20240112 KRB_AP_ERR_BAD_INTEGRITY

symptom: Certificate enrollment via CAWE (Certification Authority Web Enrollment) pages fails or is limited - Active-X controls not supported in modern browsers, only V1/V2 templates shown, KSP-based templates unavailable
rootCause: CAWE web pages rely on Active-X controls supported only by Internet Explorer (or Edge IE-mode). Pages have not been updated since Server 2003 era and are deprecated. Version 3+ templates, KSP templates, and Key Archival templates on Win10+ are not displayed.
solution: Use alternative enrollment methods: CertReq.exe for CSR submission (certreq -submit -config CA\Name CSR.csr OUT.cer), MMC snap-in (CertLM.msc/CertMgr.msc) for GUI-based enrollment, or PowerShell Get-Certificate cmdlet. CAWE is deprecated and no longer maintained by the product team.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/AskDS Blog Content/20240514 We need to discuss the Microsoft Certification Authority Web Enrollment (CAWE) Role

symptom: Users unable to log on remotely with errors: 'There are currently no logon servers available' or 'your domain is not available' or 'The domain specified is not available' when domain controller is unreachable
rootCause: Interactive logon cached logon count (GPO setting) set too low (0 or 1). Smart card (per issuer), password, Windows Hello, and service accounts each consume separate cache entries. New cache entries cannot be created without line of sight to a DC. Setting to 0 disables cached logon entirely.
solution: Keep 'Interactive logon: Number of previous logons to cache' at default value of 10 or higher. Windows security baselines do not recommend lowering this value. Account for multiple credential types (smart card, password, WHfB, service accounts) each needing separate entries per user.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/AskDS Blog Content/20240620 Stop Worrying and Love the Outage, Vol III: Cached Logons

symptom: Domain controller fails to enroll for Kerberos Authentication certificate template from Enterprise CA - enrollment request denied or hangs
rootCause: CA must make RPC callback to the requesting DC over ports 135/445 to validate dNSHostName and DNS domain name (LsarOpenPolicy/LsarQueryInformationPolicy via SMB). Firewall blocking these ports or NTLMv2 not enabled on the DC causes validation failure.
solution: Ensure network ports 135 and 445 are open from CA to DC. Enable NTLMv2 on the domain controller for SMB authentication. The Kerberos Authentication template has CT_FLAG_SUBJECT_ALT_REQUIRE_DOMAIN_DNS and CT_FLAG_SUBJECT_ALT_REQUIRE_DNS flags requiring this callback.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/AskDS Blog Content/20240701 Consolidating Windows Active Directory Domain Controller Certificates

symptom: Virtualized domain controller or domain member VM time jumping/drifting, not following GPO-configured time settings in AD DS environment. w32tm /query /source shows VM IC Time Synchronization Provider.
rootCause: VMICTimeProvider enabled by default on Azure/Hyper-V VMs, causes VMs to sync time with hypervisor host instead of AD DS time hierarchy. W32time prefers host time due to stratum level, root delay, root dispersion, and time offset evaluation.
solution: Disable VMICTimeProvider via registry: HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Services\W32Time\TimeProviders\VMICTimeProvider, set Enabled DWORD to 0. Restart W32Time service (net stop w32time && net start w32time). Apply to all virtualized DCs including Forest Root PDC to maintain traditional AD DS time hierarchy.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/AskDS Blog Content/20240812 Synchronizing Time on a Forest Root PDC housed within an Entra VM

symptom: Remote Desktop Services TLS connection fails after certificate renewal period - users unable to connect to RDS server, SSLCertificateSHA1Hash in WMI points to archived/missing certificate
rootCause: RDS Authentication certificate template configured for autoenrollment. Autoenrollment archives existing certificate, causing SessionEnv service to lose its configured certificate thumbprint (SSLCertificateSHA1Hash mismatch). Network Service account may also lack private key Read permission when certificate obtained via autoenrollment.
solution: Configure RDS certificate via Group Policy: Computer Configuration > Administrative Templates > Windows Components > Remote Desktop Services > Remote Desktop Session Host > Security > Server authentication certificate template. Use Template Name (not Display Name). Set template permission to Allow Enroll only - DO NOT enable Autoenroll. Template EKU should be Remote Desktop Authentication (OID 1.3.6.1.4.1.311.54.1.2) only, not Server Authentication.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/AskDS Blog Content/20240902 Remote Desktop Services enrolling for TLS certificate from an Enterprise CA

symptom: RDS uses wrong certificate or displays self-signed certificate despite Enterprise CA certificate being available. Certificate in WMI SSLCertificateSHA1Hash does not match expected certificate.
rootCause: WMI SSLCertificateSHA1Hash value references incorrect or missing certificate. May occur when multiple certificates with Server Authentication EKU exist in personal store, or when certificate was manually imported without proper private key association.
solution: Check current RDS certificate: Get-WmiObject -Class Win32_TSGeneralSetting -Namespace Root\cimv2\Terminalservices. To fix: either (1) delete wrong cert and restart SessionEnv service to trigger re-enrollment, or (2) update WMI thumbprint via Set-WmiInstance. Verify Network Service has Allow Read on private key via CertLM.msc > Manage Private Keys. Use CertUtil -RepairStore My THUMBPRINT if private key association is broken.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/AskDS Blog Content/20240902 Remote Desktop Services enrolling for TLS certificate from an Enterprise CA

symptom: Domain Controllers experience unexpected time jumps (often into the future), causing Kerberos authentication failures and AD replication issues. No w32time events indicate the cause.
rootCause: Secure Time Seeding (STS) enabled by default on Windows Server 2016+. STS uses time data from TLS/SSL connections which can come from misconfigured network devices. STS does NOT honor MaxPosPhaseCorrection or MaxNegPhaseCorrection settings, and no Event ID is logged when STS changes the time.
solution: Disable Secure Time Seeding on all DCs: set registry HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Services\W32Time\Config\UtilizeSslTimeData (REG_DWORD) to 0, then reboot. Audit all DCs with: For /f %i IN ('dsquery server -o rdn') do REG QUERY \\%i\HKLM\...\W32Time\Config /v UtilizeSslTimeData. Also consider disabling on non-DC servers.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/AskDS Blog Content/20240909 Secure Time Seeding on DCs: A Note from the Field

symptom: TLS connections intermittently fail due to cipher suite configuration randomly resetting. The SSL Cipher Suite Order registry value under HKLM\SOFTWARE\Policies\Microsoft\Cryptography fluctuates between a single cipher and the full configured list across Group Policy refreshes.
rootCause: A Group Policy Preference item targets the same registry key (Functions under SSL\00010002) as an Administrative Template. The Registry CSE processes first (setting Admin Template value), then the Group Policy Registry CSE may or may not overwrite it depending on the NoGPOListChanges flag. When no GPO changes are detected and NoGPOListChanges=1, the Preference item skips processing, leaving the correct Admin Template value. On forced refreshes, the Preference item overwrites it.
solution: Never use GP Preference items to target group policy registry locations (including Windows Settings\Local Policies\Security Options). Remove the conflicting Preference item and use only Administrative Templates for cipher suite ordering. Enable GP debug logging (gpsvc.log) to diagnose CSE processing order conflicts.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/AskDS Blog Content/20250128 Stop Worrying and Love the Outage, Vol IV: Preference items

symptom: Domain join fails with error 'The specified domain either does not exist or could not be contacted'. nltest /dsgetdc returns ERROR_NO_SUCH_DOMAIN (status 1355/0x54b).
rootCause: Required ports between the client and Domain Controller are blocked. Most commonly UDP 389 (LDAP Ping for DC locator) is overlooked - customers verify TCP 389 but miss UDP 389. Other required ports include TCP/UDP 53 (DNS), TCP 88 (Kerberos), TCP 135 (RPC), TCP 445 (SMB), and TCP 1024-65535 (RPC ephemeral).
solution: Verify all required ports are open using Test-NetConnection for TCP and PortQryUI (portqry -n DC_FQDN -p UDP -e 389) for UDP. Ensure firewall rules and antivirus/Windows Defender are not blocking domain join traffic. Check both TCP and UDP 389 connectivity.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/AskDS Blog Content/20250422 Domain Join and Basic troubleshooting

symptom: Domain join fails with error 'The operation failed because SPN value provided for addition/modification is not unique forest wide.'
rootCause: Another computer object with the same hostname exists in a different domain within the same AD forest. During domain join, Netlogon registers HOST/ComputerName SPN which must be forest-unique. The existing SPN in the other domain prevents registration.
solution: Delete the existing computer object in the other domain if no longer needed, or rename the new workstation to a unique name. Before joining, search the global catalog for the planned computer name to verify uniqueness across the forest.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/AskDS Blog Content/20250422 Domain Join and Basic troubleshooting

symptom: Domain join fails with error 'An account with the same name exists in Active Directory. Re-using the account was blocked by security policy.'
rootCause: KB5020276 introduced domain join hardening that enforces additional validation when reusing existing computer accounts. Only users with proper permissions (Administrators, Domain Administrators, or users with delegated permissions) can reset/reuse an existing computer account.
solution: Either (1) delete the existing computer object in AD, (2) ensure the joining user has proper permissions per KB5020276 requirements (owner of the computer object or delegated permissions), or (3) use a different unique name for the new machine. Review C:\Windows\Debug\netsetup.log for detailed error information.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/AskDS Blog Content/20250422 Domain Join and Basic troubleshooting

symptom: After upgrading to Windows Server 2025 / Windows 11 24H2, Kerberos authentication fails or uses unexpected encryption types. Previously working Kerberos configurations stop functioning. Attempts to eliminate RC4 usage show unexpected RC4 still being used.
rootCause: The new Kerb3961 library (named after RFC3961) refactors the Kerberos cryptography engine, removing hard-coded etype usage and enforcing strict adherence to configured encryption types. The legacy registry key HKLM\CurrentControlSet\Control\Lsa\Kerberos\Parameters\SupportedEncryptionTypes is no longer honored. Misconfigurations previously masked by loose etype enforcement are now exposed.
solution: Use the Group Policy 'Network security: Configure encryption types allowed for Kerberos' instead of the legacy registry key. Use the Kerberos EType Calculator (https://microsoft.github.io/Kerberos-Crypto/pages/etype-calc.html) to verify configuration. Audit etype usage with KDC events 4768/4769 and PowerShell scripts from the Microsoft Kerberos-Crypto GitHub repository.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/AskDS Blog Content/20250603 What's the deal with Kerb3961?

symptom: Windows 10 client machines with Credential Guard enabled cannot access file shares on IBM file servers. Error: 0xc0020023 RPC_NT_INVALID_BOUND (The array bounds are invalid)
rootCause: IBM file server NTLM implementation sends parameters incompatible with Credential Guard protected NTLMv2 authentication. Third-party server-side issue.
solution: Contact IBM for a fix. This is a third-party issue where IBM file server NTLMv2 handling is incompatible with Credential Guard. Reference: https://internal.evergreen.microsoft.com/topic/4012080
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Credential Guard Troubleshooting/Authentication Failures when Credential Guard is enabled/Known Issues

symptom: Windows 10 client machines with Credential Guard enabled cannot access Internet through Cisco Connector. Error: 0xc0020023 RPC_NT_INVALID_BOUND (The array bounds are invalid)
rootCause: Cisco Connector sends empty ServerName string inside NTLM Challenge message. Dual defect: NTLM code fails to generate challenge response when no target info is provided, and Cisco side sends empty string which is insecure.
solution: Bug 11205373 filed for Windows RS1 fix. Server-side: Cisco needs to fix NTLM Challenge message to include proper ServerName. Client-side: Windows NTLM should handle missing target info gracefully. Reference: https://internal.evergreen.microsoft.com/topic/4020927
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Credential Guard Troubleshooting/Authentication Failures when Credential Guard is enabled/Known Issues

symptom: When Credential Guard is enabled on Windows 10 and user enters wrong password, BadPwdCount is incremented twice in Active Directory instead of once
rootCause: Kerberos code defect causes double password attempt counting when Credential Guard is enabled. Bug 10280633.
solution: Fixed in April 2017 Windows update. Ensure Windows 10 is fully patched. Reference: https://internal.evergreen.microsoft.com/topic/3213955, Bug 10280633
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Credential Guard Troubleshooting/Authentication Failures when Credential Guard is enabled/Known Issues

symptom: Azure Files Kerberos authentication fails on Entra ID joined or hybrid joined Windows devices; users cannot mount Azure file shares via SMB using Kerberos SSO, falling back to NTLM or receiving access denied
rootCause: Missing Kerberos domain realm registry configuration on client devices. The registry keys under HKLM\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Policies\System\Kerberos\domain_realm are not configured to map storage account endpoints (.file.core.windows.net) to the correct Kerberos realm
solution: Configure registry: (1) Set HKLM\...\Kerberos\domain_realm_Enabled=1 (REG_DWORD). (2) Under domain_realm, add on-prem domain mapping: Onprem_domain_name.com=storageaccount.file.core.windows.net. (3) For Entra Kerberos auth, add KERBEROS.MICROSOFTONLINE.COM=storageaccount.file.core.windows.net. Deploy via GPO or Intune for scale.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Azure Active Directory Topics/Azure Files in DS/Azure Files: Troubleshooting

symptom: Azure Files access with Microsoft Entra Kerberos for hybrid identities fails; klist cloud_debug shows CloudKerberosTicketRetrievalEnabled=0 and Cloud Referral TGT is not present in cache; dsregcmd shows CloudTgt=No
rootCause: CloudKerberosTicketRetrievalEnabled registry key is not set or set to 0. This key is required when using Microsoft Entra Kerberos authentication method for Azure Files to enable cloud Kerberos ticket retrieval on the client
solution: Set registry key HKLM\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Policies\System\Kerberos\Parameters\CloudKerberosTicketRetrievalEnabled (REG_DWORD) to 1. Verify with klist cloud_debug that Cloud Referral TGT present in cache=1 and with dsregcmd /status that CloudTgt=Yes.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Azure Active Directory Topics/Azure Files in DS/Azure Files: Troubleshooting

symptom: Azure Files AD DS authentication fails with Kerberos encryption type mismatch; storage account SPN ticket cannot be obtained; Get-ADUser shows msDS-SupportedEncryptionTypes inconsistent with configured AES256
rootCause: AES 256 encryption type was configured on the AD DS storage account object after initial creation, but the storage account password was not rotated to generate new keys aligned with the updated encryption type
solution: Follow MS docs to (1) enable AES 256 encryption on the AD DS storage account and (2) update/rotate the password for the AD DS storage account identity. Verify with Get-ADUser that msDS-SupportedEncryptionTypes=16 (AES256). Ref: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/storage/files/storage-files-identity-ad-ds-enable#enable-aes-256-encryption-recommended
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Azure Active Directory Topics/Azure Files in DS/Azure Files: Troubleshooting

symptom: Microsoft Entra Connect installation wizard fails during authentication step; sign-in page does not load or returns authentication error when entering Entra ID global admin credentials
rootCause: The URLs login.microsoftonline.com and aadcdn.msauth.net are not added to the Trusted Sites zone in Internet Explorer/Edge on the server running Entra Connect, causing the embedded browser to block authentication requests
solution: Add https://login.microsoftonline.com and https://aadcdn.msauth.net to Trusted Sites in Internet Options > Security > Trusted Sites > Sites. Then retry the Entra Connect wizard authentication step.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Azure Active Directory Topics/How to setup your Azure tenant/How to sync your on-premise lab with Entra ID

symptom: Microsoft Entra Connect setup wizard shows JavaScript is required to sign in error during the authentication step on Windows Server
rootCause: JavaScript (Active Scripting) is disabled in Internet Explorer/Edge browser settings on the Windows Server running Entra Connect, preventing the embedded sign-in page from functioning
solution: Enable JavaScript in Windows by following the Microsoft support article: https://support.microsoft.com/en-us/topic/how-to-enable-javascript-in-windows. Then retry the Entra Connect authentication step.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Azure Active Directory Topics/How to setup your Azure tenant/How to sync your on-premise lab with Entra ID

symptom: NTLM authentication fails to generate a challenge response when Credential Guard is enabled, NTLMv2 is used, and no target information is provided by the server (Bug 11205373)
rootCause: When Credential Guard is active and NTLMv2 is negotiated, the isolated LSA process (LsaIso) cannot compute the challenge response if no target information (server SPN/domain info) is included in the NTLM challenge message. This is a known Windows bug.
solution: Apply the fix for Bug 11205373. As a workaround, ensure the target server provides target information in the NTLM challenge (Type 2) message, or consider switching to Kerberos authentication which is not affected.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Credential Guard Troubleshooting/EE Debug Information/Known Bugs

symptom: Credential Guard doubles bad password counts, causing premature account lockouts even when only one incorrect password attempt was made (Bug 10280633)
rootCause: A Windows security bug (Bug 10280633) in Credential Guard causes each failed password attempt to be counted twice by the domain controller, effectively halving the account lockout threshold.
solution: Apply the fix for Bug 10280633. As a temporary workaround, consider increasing the account lockout threshold to compensate for the doubled count, or disable Credential Guard if lockouts are critical.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Credential Guard Troubleshooting/EE Debug Information/Known Bugs

symptom: LsaIso.exe process consumes high CPU when Credential Guard is enabled on Windows, potentially caused by third-party applications interacting with the isolated LSA process
rootCause: Third-party applications (e.g., Citrix, McAfee) trigger excessive authentication calls to the LsaIso.exe process when Credential Guard is running, causing sustained high CPU usage. The isolated nature of LsaIso makes debugging extremely difficult.
solution: Identify the third-party application causing excessive auth calls. Check MS article 'High CPU usage in the LSAISO process on Windows'. Test with Credential Guard in Debug mode to collect ETL traces and LsaIso process dumps. If not reproducible in debug mode, escalate to the Windows security team.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Credential Guard Troubleshooting/HIGH CPU with CG ON/Known Issues

symptom: VMware VMs running on ESXi 6.5 do not support Credential Guard, causing Credential Guard to fail to enable or function incorrectly on virtualized Windows guests
rootCause: VMware ESXi 6.5 does not provide the required virtualization-based security (VBS) features that Credential Guard depends on. The hypervisor lacks necessary support for nested virtualization / Virtual Secure Mode.
solution: Upgrade VMware ESXi to a version that supports VBS and Credential Guard, or disable Credential Guard on VMs running on ESXi 6.5. Refer to VMware documentation for VBS-compatible ESXi versions.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Credential Guard Troubleshooting/HIGH CPU with CG ON/Known Issues

symptom: ADMT (Active Directory Migration Tool) user migration fails with error 'No credentials are available in the security package' when Credential Guard is enabled on the ADMT server
rootCause: Credential Guard blocks ADMT from accessing the NTLM credential material needed for the migration process. ADMT relies on legacy credential delegation that Credential Guard is designed to prevent.
solution: Disable Credential Guard on the ADMT server during migration operations. Credential Guard is incompatible with ADMT user migration workflows that require NTLM credential delegation.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Credential Guard Troubleshooting/HIGH CPU with CG ON/Known Issues

symptom: Cannot enable Credential Guard on Windows 10/Windows Server 2016 VMs running on VMware ESXi 6.5. The DG_CG_Hardware_Readiness_tool_v3.2 shows the server is capable of running Credential Guard, but it fails to enable.
rootCause: VMware ESXi 6.5 does not support Credential Guard for virtual machines. The hypervisor version lacks the required virtualization extensions for VBS (Virtualization-Based Security).
solution: Upgrade the VMware host to ESXi v6.7 or later and ensure VMs are using hardware version 14 or above. These versions provide the necessary virtualization support for Credential Guard.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Credential Guard Troubleshooting/Other scenarios & known issues

symptom: LSASS leaks memory on servers where Kerberos FAST (Flexible Authentication Secure Tunneling) is enabled and there is heavy authentication load. LSASS process memory consumption grows continuously.
rootCause: Memory leak in LSASS when FAST (Kerberos armoring) is enabled under heavy authentication load conditions. The leak occurs in credential handling paths within the LSA process.
solution: Apply the relevant Windows update/hotfix that addresses the LSASS memory leak with FAST enabled. As a workaround, consider disabling FAST if not required, or schedule periodic LSASS process restarts (requires DC reboot).
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Credential Guard Troubleshooting/Other scenarios & known issues

symptom: Domain controller promotion fails with error 'Could not logon to the domain with the specified credential. Supply a valid credential and try again'. dcpromoui.log shows LDAP connect/bind operation failed with error 1326.
rootCause: Multiple possible causes: wrong username/password, user is not an Enterprise Admin, stale metadata for a computer with the same name or IP, unhealthy secure channel, or communication issue due to blocked ports or security software.
solution: Verify credentials are correct and user has Enterprise Admin membership. Check for stale computer objects with the same name. Verify secure channel health with 'nltest /sc_verify'. Ensure required ports (LDAP 389, RPC 135, etc.) are not blocked by firewall or security software.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/DC Promotion/Common and Known Issues

symptom: Domain controller promotion using Server Manager fails with error 'The server is not operational'.
rootCause: NTLM authentication is disabled either in the domain or on the domain controllers. Server Manager relies on NTLM authentication to browse the AD DS forest when promoting a non-domain-joined server.
solution: Join the server to the domain first, then configure it to be a domain controller. Server Manager will use Kerberos instead of NTLM authentication to browse the AD DS forest.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/DC Promotion/Common and Known Issues

symptom: Domain controller promotion fails with error 'The filename, directory name, or volume label syntax is incorrect'. dcpromoui.log shows 8007007B = ERROR_INVALID_NAME. Directory Services event 1168 logged.
rootCause: The source DC name does not have a Fully Qualified Domain Name (FQDN). The promotion process requires FQDN format for the source domain controller name.
solution: Change the source DC name so that it has a Fully Qualified Domain Name (FQDN). Ensure DNS resolution is working correctly for the source DC.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/DC Promotion/Common and Known Issues

symptom: Domain controller promotion fails with error 'An attempt was made to add an object with a name that is already in use'. dcpromo.log shows error 8305 ERROR_DS_OBJ_STRING_NAME_EXISTS.
rootCause: The NetBIOS name of the promoted DC is identical to the CN (Common Name) of another object already existing in the Active Directory database.
solution: Rename the server to use a unique NetBIOS name that does not conflict with any existing CN in Active Directory, or remove/rename the conflicting object.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/DC Promotion/Common and Known Issues

symptom: Domain controller promotion fails with 'Verification of prerequisites for Domain Controller promotion failed. The TCP/IP networking protocol must be properly configured.' dcpromoui.log shows 'TCP/IP is NOT functioning'.
rootCause: The interface metric of the physical NIC is larger than the value for the Loopback Pseudo-Interface. DC promotion requires the physical adapter to have a lower metric than the loopback interface.
solution: Set the metric of the physical NIC lower than the Loopback Interface using: netsh int ipv4 set interface <InterfaceIndex> metric=<LowerValue>
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/DC Promotion/Common and Known Issues

symptom: Domain controller promotion fails with 'Failed to find a DC for domain contoso: 5' (Access is denied). dcpromo.log shows 'Failed to get domain controller for account DC01$ (5)'.
rootCause: 'NT AUTHORITY\Authenticated Users' is missing from the 'Access this computer from the network' user right within the Default Domain Controller Policy.
solution: Add 'NT AUTHORITY\Authenticated Users' back to the 'Access this computer from the network' user right in the Default Domain Controller Policy GPO.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/DC Promotion/Common and Known Issues

symptom: Domain controller promotion running Install-ADDSDomainController fails with 'verification of user credential Permissions failed'. dcpromoui.log shows 'VerifyUserCredentialPermissions returns exit code: 12'. Using a delegated administrator account.
rootCause: Install-ADDSDomainController requires domain or enterprise admin security group membership. Delegated permissions alone are insufficient for this cmdlet.
solution: For pre-Windows Server 2012 R2, delegate the DC promotion operation to the desired groups. For newer OS versions, use DCPROMO.exe instead of Install-ADDSDomainController, which supports delegated credentials.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/DC Promotion/Common and Known Issues

symptom: Domain controller promotion hangs when using short (flat/NetBIOS) credential names in a NetBIOS-less/WINS-less environment. dcpromoui.log shows RPC exception 1962/1963 and error 1908: Could not find the domain controller for this domain.
rootCause: Flat credential names were specified in an environment without NetBIOS or WINS name resolution. The promotion process cannot resolve the domain controller using short names.
solution: Use fully qualified (long) credentials (e.g., domain.com\admin instead of DOMAIN\admin). Alternatively, join the computer as a domain member prior to promotion, or enable NetBIOS over TCP/IP (note: this will not help in Azure as it does not support multicast).
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/DC Promotion/Common and Known Issues

symptom: Domain controller promotion gets stuck at 'Creating the NTDS Settings object for this Active Directory Domain Controller on the remote AD DC Server'. dcpromoui.log shows error 1962 and Directory Service event 1962 'The RPC server is unavailable (1722)'.
rootCause: Timeout due to communication issues on port 135 (RPC endpoint mapper). The 6to4 adapter may be interfering with network communication between the server being promoted and the remote DC.
solution: Disable the 6to4 adapter by running: netsh int 6to4 set state disabled. Also verify that RPC port 135 and dynamic RPC ports are not blocked by firewall.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/DC Promotion/Common and Known Issues

symptom: Promotion of a member server to domain controller causes the existing DC to restart/crash. Application event 1015 shows 'wininit.exe has initiated the restart of computer' due to lsass.exe crash (event 1000).
rootCause: A third-party notification package registered under the LSA Notification Packages registry value causes lsass.exe to crash when processing the DC promotion replication.
solution: On the DC: open regedit, locate HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Control\Lsa, and remove the third-party entry from the 'Notification Packages' value. Reboot the DC, then retry the promotion.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/DC Promotion/Common and Known Issues

symptom: Read-Only Domain Controller (RODC) promotion fails with error 'The expected state objects could not be found. (87)'.
rootCause: The 'authenticated users' security group lacks permission to read properties on the RODC computer account in Active Directory.
solution: Grant adequate permissions to read all properties on the RODC computer account for the machine account performing the promotion.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/DC Promotion/Common and Known Issues

symptom: Domain controller demotion fails with error 'The directory service is missing mandatory configuration information, and is unable to determine the ownership of floating single-master operation roles'. dcpromo.log shows error 2091 when transferring operations master roles.
rootCause: Unable to contact the DNS infrastructure master. The fsMORoleOwner attribute points to a non-existent or unreachable domain controller.
solution: Assign the fsMORoleOwner attribute to a live, reachable DC. Ensure DNS infrastructure master is accessible. Run the provided script to fix the FSMO role assignment, then retry the demotion.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/DC Promotion/Common and Known Issues

symptom: DC promotion fails with error 'An Active Directory domain controller for domain contoso.com could not be contacted' when promoting a server to DC in an existing domain (HRESULT 0x8007054B, DCPROMOUI exit code 25)
rootCause: UDP port 389 (LDAP/NETLOGON) is blocked to existing Domain Controllers, DNS SRV record queries fail for the domain, or RPC port range is blocked between the member server and target DC
solution: Collect network packet capture (netsh trace start capture=yes) while reproducing the failure. Review DCPROMO.LOG and dcpromoui.log in %windir%\debug for HRESULT and exit codes. Use 'nltest /dsgetdc:<domain>' to verify DC reachability. Check DNS SRV records resolution and UDP 389 NETLOGON PING responses. Look for TCP retransmits (Property.TCPRetransmit==1) in network trace. Resolve the network-level blocking (firewall/port rules) identified in the capture.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/DC Promotion/Scenario: An Active Directory domain controller for domain "contoso.com" could not be contacted

symptom: DC promotion Prerequisites Check fails with error 'The RPC server is unavailable' (HRESULT 0x800706BA) when promoting a server to Domain Controller in an existing domain
rootCause: RPC and Dynamic RPC port range is blocked between the server and existing Domain Controllers. The prerequisite check uses WMI calls that require RPC connectivity, which fails when blocked by firewall rules.
solution: Collect simultaneous network packet captures from both source and target to identify where TCP retransmits to the Endpoint Mapper (port 135) or dynamic RPC ports occur. Review DCPromoUI.log for 0x800706BA errors. Check if RPC port range has been modified (see MS KB on configuring RPC dynamic port allocation with firewalls). Verify all AD-required ports are open per Microsoft service overview and network port requirements. Consider restricting AD RPC traffic to specific port if needed.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/DC Promotion/Scenario: Prerequisites Check fails with RPC Server is unavailable

symptom: DC promotion or demotion fails with error 'The directory service is missing mandatory configuration information, and is unable to determine the ownership of floating single master operation roles'
rootCause: Previous FSMO role holders were demoted improperly or stale data was restored to the partition. The fSMORoleOwner attribute on infrastructureUpdate objects (e.g. CN=Infrastructure,DC=DomainDnsZones,DC=contoso,DC=com) references an NTDS Settings DN of a DC that no longer holds the infrastructure master role.
solution: Use ADSIEdit.msc to manually update the fSMORoleOwner attribute on the infrastructure object to reflect the current Infrastructure Master's NTDS Settings DN. This change must be made against the DC holding the Infrastructure Master FSMO role. Alternatively, run the script from Microsoft KB 949257 to automatically restore the object.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/DC Promotion/Scenario: Unable to determine the ownership of floating single-master operation roles

symptom: Robocopy preseeding for DFSR stalls indefinitely or takes extremely long time to complete
rootCause: Robocopy executed without specifying /r and /w parameters. The default values are 1 million retries and 30-second waits per retry, causing the process to stall on any file access error.
solution: Always specify /r and /w parameters with Robocopy for DFSR preseeding. Use /r:1 /w:1 for efficiency. Example: robocopy "<src>" "<dst>" /e /b /copyall /r:1 /w:1 /xd DfsrPrivate /tee /log:<logpath>
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/DFSR/DFSR: Preseeding

symptom: Robocopy preseeding with /MT switch appears stalled with no progress output for extended periods
rootCause: The /MT (multi-threaded) switch causes Robocopy to buffer output internally. It processes files in batches and dumps log information in bursts followed by apparent hangs.
solution: This is expected behavior with /MT switch. Robocopy will dump information in the log periodically then appear to hang, then repeat. Monitor the log file for actual progress rather than relying on console output.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/DFSR/DFSR: Preseeding

symptom: Conflicts appear on domain controllers after reinitializing SYSVOL replication with authoritative flag on one DC
rootCause: Setting the authoritative flag (msDFSR-options=1) on one DC without setting non-authoritative sync on all other DCs causes conflicts from DCs that were not reconfigured and still have their original DFSR state.
solution: When setting authoritative flag on one DC, you MUST set non-authoritative sync (msDFSR-Enabled=FALSE then TRUE) on ALL other DCs in the domain. Preferably use PDC Emulator as the authoritative DC since its Sysvol replication contents are most up-to-date.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/DFSR/DFSR: SYSVOL/DFSR: Reinitialize Sysvol on a DC

symptom: DFSR replication fails or is blocked between domain controllers through firewalls or restricted network environments
rootCause: Firewall or network ACL blocking required RPC ports. DFSR requires TCP port 135 and dynamic RPC ports (49152-65535). Legacy Windows Server 2008/2008R2 DCs also used port 5722.
solution: Ensure TCP port 135 and dynamic RPC ports (49152-65535) are open between DFSR partners. For restricted environments, limit DFSR to a specific static port using dfsrdiag or Set-DfsrServiceConfiguration PowerShell cmdlet. Port 5722 is only needed on Server 2008/2008R2.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/DFSR/Workflow: DFSR: Dependency information

symptom: DFSR database corruption or frequent auto-recovery events on a volume hosting replicated folders
rootCause: Underlying disk subsystem instability causing ESE database issues. DFSR stores one monolithic ESE database per volume at %volumeLetter%\System Volume Information\DFSR\database_*. Disk I/O failures corrupt this database.
solution: Check Application event log for ESENT warnings (508, 510, 533, 902, 906, 482, 428) and errors (454, 455). Investigate disk subsystem stability. Engage storage team or Windows High Availability team if disk issues confirmed. DFSR cannot succeed without stable disk infrastructure.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/DFSR/Workflow: DFSR: Dependency information

symptom: Teams interactive logon failures after installing March 12, 2024 Windows Updates. WAM-dependent Teams sign-in breaks after applying monthly security patch.
rootCause: March 12, 2024 Windows security update (KB) introduced a regression affecting WAM/Token Broker flows used by Teams for interactive logon. ICM 482538640.
solution: Check if uninstalling the March 2024 cumulative update or applying the follow-up fix resolves the issue. See linked internal article for specific KB remediation.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Azure Active Directory Topics/Web Accounts Manager (WAM) - TokenBroker Service/Known issues

symptom: Dell Optimizer driver ExpCo10X64.sys causes sign-in issues in Outlook and Microsoft Teams on Dell devices. Repeated authentication prompts or failures.
rootCause: Dell Optimizer driver (ExpCo10X64.sys) interferes with WAM/Token Broker authentication flows, blocking or corrupting token acquisition for AAD-integrated applications.
