solution: Uninstall or disable Dell Optimizer driver (ExpCo10X64.sys). Verify sign-in works after driver removal. Contact Dell for updated driver that does not conflict with WAM.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Azure Active Directory Topics/Web Accounts Manager (WAM) - TokenBroker Service/Known issues

symptom: WebAuthn via WAM does not support Windows Hello-based authentication. Users attempting WHFB/passkey auth through WAM-brokered WebAuthn flow find it unsupported.
rootCause: WAM WebAuthn implementation does not support Windows Hello for Business (WHFB) as an authentication method. This is a known platform limitation.
solution: Use FIDO2 security keys instead of WHFB for WebAuthn scenarios via WAM. Or use browser-native WebAuthn (non-brokered) which supports WHFB directly.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Azure Active Directory Topics/Web Accounts Manager (WAM) - TokenBroker Service/Known issues

symptom: Windows RDClient published as Citrix app fails to do feed discovery for WVD/AVD. Immersive Shell/WAM dependency causes feed discovery failure in Citrix environment.
rootCause: RDClient relies on WAM (Immersive Shell) for feed discovery but Citrix published app environment does not properly support WAM broker plugin, breaking token acquisition.
solution: Use RDClient natively instead of as Citrix published app, or use web client as alternative for AVD feed discovery. Verify Citrix environment WAM broker support.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Azure Active Directory Topics/Web Accounts Manager (WAM) - TokenBroker Service/Known issues

symptom: WAM-dependent operations including M365 apps (OneDrive, Outlook, Teams) and VPN fail on June 2022 (6B.22) patched AAD-joined ARM64 devices. ICM 314710957.
rootCause: June 2022 Windows security update introduced a regression specifically affecting ARM64 architecture AAD-joined devices, breaking WAM token broker operations.
solution: Check for follow-up patch fixing ARM64 WAM regression. Workaround: uninstall 6B.22 cumulative update on affected ARM64 devices. Upgrade to newer Windows build.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Azure Active Directory Topics/Web Accounts Manager (WAM) - TokenBroker Service/Known issues

symptom: Conditional access fails when IWamProviderRegistration::GetFromSsoUri() does not find a provider with URL as ID when WAM cache is disabled.
rootCause: When WAM cache is disabled, provider registration lookup by SSO URI fails because cached provider mappings are unavailable, breaking conditional access evaluation.
solution: Re-enable WAM cache. Check registry for WAM cache disable settings and remove them. Clear and rebuild WAM token cache.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Azure Active Directory Topics/Web Accounts Manager (WAM) - TokenBroker Service/Known issues

symptom: Always On VPN (AoVPN) with Conditional Access generates Can't connect error. VPN connection fails when conditional access policy is enforced alongside AoVPN.
rootCause: WAM token acquisition for VPN conditional access compliance check fails due to interaction between Always On VPN automatic connection and WAM broker token flow.
solution: Review VPN profile XML for correct device tunnel and user tunnel separation. Ensure WAM can acquire tokens before VPN connection. Check CA policy exclusions for VPN.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Azure Active Directory Topics/Web Accounts Manager (WAM) - TokenBroker Service/Known issues

symptom: Enterprise State Roaming (ESR) settings do not sync when MFA is enabled on Edge or Internet Explorer. Settings synchronization stops after MFA enforcement.
rootCause: MFA enforcement on browser sessions interferes with WAM-based token flow used by ESR for settings sync. WAM silent token acquisition fails when MFA challenge is required.
solution: Ensure user has valid PRT with MFA claim. Consider excluding ESR sync from MFA conditional access policy. Verify WAM can complete MFA interactively for ESR.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Azure Active Directory Topics/Web Accounts Manager (WAM) - TokenBroker Service/Known issues

symptom: Teams, OneDrive for Business, and Outlook experience sign-in and connection issues with error 80080300 after installing KB5003169 on Windows 10 1909. ICM 241493159.
rootCause: Windows cumulative update KB5003169 for version 1909 introduced regression in WAM/Token Broker service causing M365 app authentication failures with error 80080300.
solution: Uninstall KB5003169 as workaround. Apply superseding cumulative update with fix. Upgrade Windows 10 from 1909 to newer feature update.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Azure Active Directory Topics/Web Accounts Manager (WAM) - TokenBroker Service/Known issues

symptom: Outlook fails to sign in when user checks allow my organization to manage my device in Gallatin/Mooncake (China sovereign cloud) environment.
rootCause: WAM device registration flow in Gallatin/Mooncake sovereign cloud has different endpoint configuration. Device join consent triggers flow that fails in sovereign cloud.
solution: Verify tenant is correctly configured for Gallatin/Mooncake endpoints. Check device registration in sovereign cloud AAD. Review WAM logs for specific error.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Azure Active Directory Topics/Web Accounts Manager (WAM) - TokenBroker Service/Known issues

symptom: DFSR server offline for more than 60 days causes stale data to replicate and overwrite live files, or previously deleted files reappear after the server rejoins replication. Event ID 4012 logged if content freshness protection is enabled.
rootCause: DFSR tombstone garbage collection is hard-coded to 60 days. If a member is offline beyond this period, tombstones are purged and the member cannot reliably reconcile deletions, similar to Active Directory lingering objects.
solution: Enable content freshness protection on all DFSR servers (Windows Server 2008 R2+). When triggered, Event ID 4012 blocks replication and requires admin intervention to restore. See blog: Implementing Content Freshness protection in DFSR. Plan re-seeding for members offline >60 days.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/DFSR/Workflow: DFSR: FAQ || Customer education (in-depth)/Best Practices

symptom: DFSR replication fails with large backlog on downstream servers. FSRM (File Server Resource Manager) quotas or file screens prevent DFSR from writing to replicated folders.
rootCause: FSRM quotas enforced on replicated folders block DFSR internal write operations. FSRM file screens can also interfere with DFSR staging and file installation.
solution: Review and adjust FSRM quotas and file screens on volumes hosting DFSR replicated folders. Ensure DFSR staging and database paths are excluded from FSRM enforcement. Reference KB959210 for DFSR/FSRM interaction issues.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/DFSR/Workflow: DFSR: FAQ || Customer education (in-depth)/Performance expectations, tuning and optimizations

symptom: DFSR health check topology errors, staging issues, and database problems after configuring one-way replication connections. Disabled connections for >60 days cause additional failures.
rootCause: One-way replication connections are unsupported in DFSR. Replication connections must exist in both directions between partners. Disabling connections beyond the 60-day tombstone lifetime is also unsupported.
solution: Use read-only replicated folders instead of one-way connections to achieve effective one-way replication. Follow recovery steps in AskDS blog: Recovering from unsupported one-way replication in DFSR.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/DFSR/Workflow: DFSR: FAQ || Customer education (in-depth)/Performance expectations, tuning and optimizations

symptom: DFSR replication halts for entire server when staging quota is exceeded with more than 15 concurrent inbound files or 16 outbound RPC requests. All inbound/outbound replication stops until staging cleanup completes.
rootCause: Staging area quota is too small for the volume of replicated data. When exceeded with >=15 inbound tasks, staging cleanup uses 1 task and all 15 remaining wait, blocking further replication. With >=16 outbound, all RPC slots are consumed waiting for cleanup.
solution: Increase staging quota size to match or exceed the size of commonly modified/created data. Monitor staging usage. Larger staging reduces CPU/disk overhead from constant purge/reload cycles. Use DFS Management snap-in or PowerShell to adjust staging quota per replicated folder.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/DFSR/Workflow: DFSR: FAQ || Customer education (in-depth)/Performance expectations, tuning and optimizations

symptom: DFSR files not replicating, Event ID 4302 logged repeatedly for sharing violations. Upstream server wastes resources staging and compressing files that fail to replicate downstream.
rootCause: A process on the downstream server holds a file open without shared access, preventing DFSR from installing the update. The staged data may be purged if other files push staging over the 90% high watermark.
solution: Identify the process holding the file lock (e.g., using Process Monitor or handle.exe). Ensure applications use compatible file sharing modes. Address the locking process to allow DFSR to replicate the file.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/DFSR/Workflow: DFSR: FAQ || Customer education (in-depth)/Performance expectations, tuning and optimizations

symptom: DFSR performance degradation or replication failures caused by antivirus software scanning DFSR database and working folders under System Volume Information\DFSR.
rootCause: Antivirus real-time scanning of DFSR JET database files, staging files, and working folders interferes with DFSR service I/O operations, causing locks, timeouts, or corruption.
solution: Configure antivirus exclusions for DFSR database path (\System Volume Information\DFSR per volume). Exclude specific files: $db_normal$, FileIDTable_*, SimilarityTable_*, *.xml, $db_dirty$, $db_clean$, $db_lost$, Dfsr.db, Fsr.chk, *.frx, *.log, Fsr*.jrs, Tmp.edb. Also exclude staging and ConflictAndDeleted folders. Reference KB822158.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/DFSR/Workflow: DFSR: FAQ || Customer education (in-depth)/Performance expectations, tuning and optimizations

symptom: DFSR replication fails between partners with Event ID 5008 (error 1722: RPC server unavailable) and Event ID 5014 (error 1726: remote procedure call failed). Files updated on one server do not replicate to partner.
rootCause: TCP/IP connectivity failure between DFSR replication partners. The downstream server cannot establish RPC connection to the upstream server through the endpoint mapper (port 135) or the dynamic RPC port. Causes include firewall blocking, network misconfiguration, or partner server being unreachable.
solution: Restore TCP/IP connectivity between systems. Use RPCDump to verify endpoint mapper registration and dynamic port availability. Capture network traces filtering on partner IP and port 135 (filter: ipv4.address==<partnerIP> && tcp.port==135), then filter on the dynamic port returned. Check for TCP SYN retransmits (Property.TCPSynRetransmit==1). Provide captures to networking team as proof of the connectivity issue.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/DFSR/Workflow: DFSR: Looking for Known Solutions/Common Solutions/No Replication/Connectivity failures

symptom: DFSR events 2102/2104 with error 9014/9203 'Database failure (-1206)' or 'The database is corrupt (-1206)', replication stops on the affected volume
rootCause: DFSR JET database corruption from unexpected system restart/crash, dfsrs.exe process termination, disk failures, or high disk I/O latencies causing the JET dirty bit to remain set upon next service start
solution: Update DFSR to latest version (KB2951262 for Server 2012/R2, KB968429 for Server 2008/R2). Check System event log for STOP errors (event 6008/1001) indicating system crashes. Check ESENT event 508 and Avg Disk sec/read perfmon counter for disk latency issues (>0.030s = Poor). If dfsrs.exe crashes, capture dump via 'procdump -e -ma dfsrs.exe'.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/DFSR/Workflow: DFSR: Looking for Known Solutions/Common Solutions/No Replication/DFSR service failures (database)

symptom: DFSR error 9098 'A tombstoned content set deletion has been scheduled', DFSR stops replicating for the affected replicated folder and does not recover even after non-authoritative restore
rootCause: DFSR database unable to clean up an old (tombstoned) replica set entry, the corrupted state persists across service restarts and non-authoritative restores
solution: 1) Stop DFSR service. 2) Delete all contents of <Drive>:\System Volume Information\DFSR directory (files are System/Hidden, use PowerShell: Get-ChildItem -Force -Recurse | Remove-Item -Force). 3) Restart DFSR service - it will perform non-authoritative restore and rebuild the database.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/DFSR/Workflow: DFSR: Looking for Known Solutions/Common Solutions/No Replication/DFSR service failures (database)

symptom: DFSR events 2212/2214 logged on most server/service restarts, event 2213 (AutoRecovery) triggered repeatedly, database failures occur after every service restart due to dirty shutdown
rootCause: Service Control Manager (SCM) default 20-second WaitToKillServiceTimeout is too short for complex DFSR implementations, causing forcible dfsrs.exe termination before the JET database is properly closed
solution: Set registry WaitToKillServiceTimeout (HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Control, String type) to 300000 (5 min). Monitor events 1006 (stopping) and 1008 (stopped) to determine actual shutdown time, then tune value accordingly. Install hotfix KB2549760 on Server 2008 R2. Max value is 1 hour (3600000). See KB2846759 for details.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/DFSR/Workflow: DFSR: Looking for Known Solutions/Common Solutions/No Replication/DFSR service failures (database)

symptom: DFSR event 5016 'connection removed or disabled', event 5012 error 9026 'The connection is invalid', event 5014 'stopping communication with partner', DfsrDiag syncnow returns 'Cannot find inbound DfsrConnection Info object to the given partner'
rootCause: DFSR connection between replication partners has been disabled (ConnEnabled=No visible in SDP output or DFS Management Connections tab)
solution: Re-enable the disabled connection in DFS Management snap-in (Connections tab). CRITICAL: Before re-enabling, verify last successful replication was within tombstone lifetime (60 days). If exceeded, follow recovery procedure for one-way replication to avoid replicating stale deletions/overwriting newer files.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/DFSR/Workflow: DFSR: Looking for Known Solutions/Common Solutions/No Replication/Disabled connections

symptom: Large DFSR replication backlogs on Windows Server 2012/2012 R2, unexpected backlog growth not correlated with user file changes, performance data shows excessive updates at predictable times
rootCause: Garbage collection activities (GCTask) in unpatched DFSR versions cause unexpected replication backlogs. Alternatively, an external process (antivirus, backup, scripts) is modifying files in the replicated folder excessively
solution: Apply KB2931973 for GCTask-related backlogs on Server 2012/2012 R2. For external process issues: use Process Monitor with filters on replicated folder path and write operations to identify the responsible process. For shared files (System process writes), use network captures with SMB2 write filter (SMB2.SMB2Header.Command==0x9).
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/DFSR/Workflow: DFSR: Looking for Known Solutions/Common Solutions/Slow Replication/Excessive content changes

symptom: DFSR slow replication between partners, files take abnormally long to replicate, performance counters show very low Bytes Received Per Second (e.g. 22 KB/s), debug logs show large time gaps (17+ seconds) between file send and data pipe thread exit
rootCause: High network latency or low bandwidth between DFSR partner servers causing slow RPC data transfer
solution: Increase network capabilities (bandwidth/reduce latency). If bandwidth is limited, adjust DFSR replication schedules to allow replication only during high-bandwidth windows. Diagnose with: 1) Network captures filtered on partner IP or DFSR port 2) Check Bytes Received/Sec perfmon counter 3) Enable DFSR debug level 5 temporarily to observe time gaps in data pipe operations.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/DFSR/Workflow: DFSR: Looking for Known Solutions/Common Solutions/Slow Replication/Network latencies
Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/DFSR/Workflow: DFSR: Looking for Known Solutions/Common Solutions/Slow Replication/Poor disk performance
symptom: DFSR replication is slow between two partners. Debug logs (level 4) show large delays between entries. Level 5 logs show long durations between RDC generate begin and RDC generate end for StreamToIndex operations. Performance counters show high Avg. Disk sec/Read and Avg. Disk sec/Write values (e.g., 0.375 read, 0.203 write).
rootCause: The disk subsystem on the DFSR server has poor I/O performance (high latency reads/writes). Disk response times exceeding 0.030 sec/read or write are classified as Poor. Other processes (e.g., svchost) may also compete with DFSR for disk I/O resources.
solution: 1. Enable DFSR debug logging level 5 temporarily and search for StreamToIndex RDC generate begin/end time deltas. 2. Collect performance data (LogicalDisk Avg. Disk sec/Read, Avg. Disk sec/Write) and correlate with replication slowdowns. 3. Check IO Read/Write Bytes/sec per process to identify competing processes. 4. Ensure disk subsystem meets DFSR demands. Disk performance benchmarks: Excellent=0.008, Good=0.012, Fair=0.020, Poor=0.030. 5. Engage HA support team if disk performance is unexpectedly degraded.

sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/DFSR/Workflow: DFSR: Looking for Known Solutions/Common Solutions/Slow Replication/Sharing violations
symptom: DFSR replication is slow or files never replicate. DFSR Event ID 4302 is logged repeatedly stating sharing violations. Debug logs show Meet::Install -> WAIT Error code:32 (ERROR_SHARING_VIOLATION). Backlog shows specific files stuck. The file cannot be installed because it is being used by another process.
rootCause: A process or driver on the downstream server holds an open handle (sharing violation) on the file being replicated. This prevents DFSR from completing the file install (rename) operation. The lock may come from a user-mode process, a client accessing the file through a file share (PID 4 System process), or a kernel-mode driver.
solution: 1. Use SysInternals handle.exe from elevated command prompt: handle.exe <filename> to find the process holding the file lock. 2. If the handle owner is PID 4 (System), use net file and net session commands to identify the remote client holding the lock. 3. For driver-held handles, a memory dump analysis may be required (consult Serviceability team). 4. Restart the service holding the handle to release it. 5. Note: sharing violations can also block outbound replication staging (Event ID 4304 variant).

sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/DFSR/Workflow: DFSR: Looking for Known Solutions/Emerging issues
symptom: Excessive SYSVOL replication traffic occurs on domain controllers patched with 1B.24 (January 2024) updates. Large environments with Starter Group Policy Objects (GPOs) folder experience performance issues caused by excessive DFSR conflicts and backlogs.
rootCause: The 1B.24 patch causes permissions for Starter Group Policy Objects (GPOs) in SYSVOL to be restamped, generating DFSR conflicts. In large environments where the Starter GPOs folder exists, this leads to excessive replication traffic and growing backlogs. (ICM 498037568)
solution: Engage Escalation Engineer (EE) for this known issue. See internal Evergreen article for detailed mitigation. Monitor SYSVOL replication backlogs after applying January 2024 updates.

sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/DFSR/Workflow: DFSR: Looking for Known Solutions/Emerging issues
symptom: On Windows Server 2016, DFSR stops inbound replication on downstream, backlog increases. DFSR Event 5014 is logged with RPC error 1726 The remote procedure call failed. Anti-event 5004 does not appear for a long time (up to 24 hours). Stopping and starting DFSR service hangs at stopping state, requiring reboot.
rootCause: The DFSR shutdown thread is blocked by a busy thread waiting on RPC, which also blocks the semaphore. The RPC runtime enters an error state without notifying the DFSR service, so DFSR waits until the AsyncRpcDownloadMaxWaitInSeconds timeout expires. This is an RPC runtime issue. (KB4092565)
solution: 1. Check Event Viewer: filter for events 5014, 5004, and 1011 and check timing. If Event 5004 appears right after 5014, this is NOT the hang condition. 2. If confirmed, reboot the affected server to restore DFSR service. 3. Apply latest Windows Server 2016 cumulative updates. 4. See KB4092565 for updates on the fix.

sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/DFSR/Workflow: DFSR: Looking for Known Solutions/Emerging issues
symptom: DFSR SYSVOL replication stops on downstream DC. Group Policy changes not replicated, backlog increasing. No DFSR events logged. Restarting DFSR or non-authoritative (D2) restore does not help. New DC promotions show empty SYSVOL domain folder. Initial replication stays in State 2 indefinitely. Debug logs show Meet::Install -> WAIT Error code 87 (ERROR_INVALID_PARAMETER) and Meet::GetParent -> BLOCKED.
rootCause: A parent folder object in the DFSR database cannot be installed due to error 87 (ERROR_INVALID_PARAMETER) at NtfsFileSystem::MakeBackupCreateWriter. Subordinate objects fail with Could not find parent, creating a cascading blockage of the entire SYSVOL content set replication. (KB4466692)
solution: 1. Check debug logs for Meet::Install -> WAIT Error with code 87 and Meet::GetParent -> BLOCKED. 2. Verify replication state via PowerShell: Get-WmiObject -Namespace root\MicrosoftDFS -Class DfsrReplicatedFolderInfo (State 2 = stuck). 3. AVOID needlessly resetting SYSVOL authoritatively unless SME/TA approves. 4. AVOID database deletions/recreations unless approved by DFSR SME. 5. Contact DFSR SME or TA. See KB4466692.

symptom: DFSR replication fails or exhibits anomalous behavior with no obvious errors in event/debug logs, network connectivity and configuration appear correct
rootCause: Third-party filter drivers (CrowdStrike, Symantec, McAfee, Carbon Black, FireEye, Kaspersky, Trend Micro) interfere with DFSR file operations on replicated volumes
solution: Run fltmc or fltmc instances to identify loaded filter drivers on replicated volumes. Set AV exclusions, temporarily uninstall suspect applications to test. If uninstalling resolves the issue and reinstalling reproduces it, direct customer to the third-party vendor.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/DFSR/Workflow: DFSR: Scoping/DFSR: Known to cause issues

symptom: DFSR replication issues potentially caused by antivirus scanning the DFSR working directory under System Volume Information
rootCause: Antivirus software scanning the hidden %drive%\System Volume Information\DFSR folder and subfolders, interfering with DFSR service local configuration data
solution: Exclude %drive%\System Volume Information\DFSR and all subfolders from antivirus scanning. Also exclude DFS files in SYSVOL replica sets. Educate customer that this folder contains local configuration data accessed only by Local System account and DFSR service.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/DFSR/Workflow: DFSR: Scoping/DFSR: Known to cause issues

symptom: DFSR performance degradation on volumes with Data Deduplication enabled; significant latency during initial sync or large backlog processing
rootCause: Data Deduplication filter driver on DFSR replicated volumes causes performance overhead; highly optimized and active volumes with large changes or backlogs see significant replication impact
solution: Evaluate necessity of Data Deduplication on DFSR volumes. Be aware that even having the dedup feature enabled and filter driver loaded may cause slight latency. Consider disabling dedup on high-activity replicated volumes during initial sync or large change processing.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/DFSR/Workflow: DFSR: Scoping/DFSR: Known to cause issues

symptom: Access Denied errors in DFSR debug logs; DFSR replication failures when hard file quotas are configured on replicated folders
rootCause: Aggressive or hard file quotas cause replication to exceed quota when files from multiple members converge (e.g., two members each adding files that together exceed quota limit)
solution: Ensure DfsrPrivate hidden folder is not subject to quotas or file screens. Use hard quotas with caution on replicated folders. Ensure FSRM file screening settings match on both ends of the replication.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/DFSR/Workflow: DFSR: Scoping/DFSR: Known to cause issues

symptom: DFSR performance degradation or self-inflicted denial of service when backlog is checked too frequently via PowerShell or DFSRDIAG
rootCause: Aggressive backlog monitoring (Get-DfsrBacklog or DFSRDIAG Backlog run too frequently) consumes DFSR service resources and degrades replication performance
solution: Educate customers not to check backlogs more often than every 4 hours. Once or twice daily is sufficient for normal monitoring. Use caution when running backlogs during active troubleshooting.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/DFSR/Workflow: DFSR: Scoping/DFSR: Known to cause issues

symptom: DFS Namespace share targets or replication group members accidentally deleted/disabled when modifying DFSR or DFSN configuration through DFS Management console
rootCause: DFS Management console creates hidden links between DFSR replication groups and DFSN namespace shares (indicated by green arrow icon); changes to one service cascade to the other without clear warning
solution: Check for linked DFSR/DFSN configurations in the console (green arrow icon). To break the link: open ADSIedit > Domain NC > navigate to msDFSR-ContentSet object > clear the msDFSR-DfsPath attribute. Document the path before clearing. Re-add link after troubleshooting if needed.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/DFSR/Workflow: DFSR: Useful Tools and Commands/DFS Management console and DFSN interoperability
Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/ESR/Workflow: ESR: Known Issues/Workflow: Do not Sync defaults to "enabled"
symptom: Enterprise State Roaming (ESR) "Do not sync" policy incorrectly defaults to sync settings being ON when admin configures "Allow users to turn syncing on" option. Customers expect the default to be OFF or disabled, but the setting is enabled by default.
rootCause: ESR "Do not sync" group policy has an incorrect default value — when the policy is set to allow users to control syncing, the default state is incorrectly set to enabled (sync ON) instead of disabled (sync OFF).
solution: Known issue documented in internal KB (topic/4464980). Review the ESR policy configuration and manually set the "Do not sync" policy to the desired default state. Advise customers to explicitly configure the policy value rather than relying on the default.

symptom: Domain join fails with error 0x32 (ERROR_NOT_SUPPORTED, decimal 50). Netsetup.log shows "NetUseAdd to \DC\IPC$ returned 50" and "NetpJoinDomainOnDs: status of connecting to dc: 0x32". Client fails to establish SMB session to the Domain Controller. Network trace shows DC returns STATUS_NOT_SUPPORTED to the client NTLM Session Setup request.
rootCause: The security policy "Network security: Restrict NTLM: Incoming NTLM traffic" on the Domain Controller is incorrectly set to "Deny all accounts", blocking NTLM authentication required for domain join SMB session establishment. When NTLM is restricted on the DC, non-domain-joined clients cannot authenticate via Kerberos and the SMB IPC$ connection fails.
solution: Change the "Network security: Restrict NTLM: Incoming NTLM traffic" policy on the affected DC to "Allow All" (or add appropriate exceptions), then refresh group policy (gpupdate /force). Note: there are 7 NTLM-related security policies controlling client outgoing, server incoming, and DC validation roles. Review all three restriction policies and the two exception lists to ensure domain join traffic is permitted.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Domain Join/Workflow: Domain Join: Troubleshooting Examples/0x32: NetpJoinDomainOnDs exits with status code 0x32

symptom: Netlogon Event ID 5719 or Group Policy Event 1129 logged during domain member boot indicating no domain controllers are available; domain-joined machine reports no DC contact at startup
rootCause: Netlogon service starts before the network stack is fully initialized during system boot, so DCLocator DNS SRV queries fail or time out before network connectivity is established
solution: This is typically transient and self-recovers once the network is ready. For SQL Server or other services that start early with domain service accounts or GMSA, configure delayed automatic start with network trigger: sc.exe triggerinfo SQLServiceName start/networkon and sc.exe config SQLServiceName start= delayed-auto
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Domain Controller Locator (DCLocator)/WorkFlow: DcLocator: FAQ

symptom: DNS SRV records for demoted domain controllers reappear in DNS after manual deletion; systems continue attempting to contact old/demoted DCs during DCLocator operations
rootCause: AD-integrated DNS zones store SRV records as multi-valued attributes on DNS objects. Active Directory replication conflict resolution (last-write-wins) can reanimate previously removed SRV records for demoted DCs when conflicting updates from other DCs replicate
solution: Manually clean up the conflicting DNS objects on all DCs holding the AD-integrated zone. Follow detailed guidance at https://techcommunity.microsoft.com/t5/microsoft-entra-azure-ad-blog/a-complicated-scenario-regarding-dns-and-the-dc-locator-srvs/ba-p/243139. Use DcDiag to verify DC advertising status after cleanup
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Domain Controller Locator (DCLocator)/WorkFlow: DcLocator: FAQ

symptom: DCLocator responses from domain controllers are slow or time out, causing delays in LDAP, Kerberos, and DC locator operations
rootCause: Domain controller fails to map the client IP address to an AD site via DsAddressToSite API, triggering an exhaustive search through all known IP addresses before responding
solution: Apply Windows update KB https://support.microsoft.com/en-us/topic/5a9a62a5-348d-50ce-5e0b-019f42142b3c. Verify client subnet definitions exist in Active Directory Sites and Services. Test IP-to-site mapping with NLTEST /DSADDRESSTOSITE: /ADDRESSES:x.x.x.x on the DC
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Domain Controller Locator (DCLocator)/WorkFlow: DcLocator: FAQ

symptom: Application fails with error 1355 ERROR_NO_SUCH_DOMAIN or 1311 ERROR_NO_LOGON_SERVERS when attempting to locate a domain controller via DCLocator
rootCause: No domain controllers match the DCLocator flags specified by the calling application (e.g., DS_GC_SERVER_REQUIRED, DS_PDC_REQUIRED, DS_DIRECTORY_SERVICE_6_REQUIRED), either because DCs lack required capabilities, FSMO role holders are offline, or DCs are not advertising their roles
solution: Check Netlogon.log or Netlogon ETL trace to identify the flags passed by the application. Verify DC capabilities match the requested flags. Run DcDiag to check DC advertising status. Review C:\Windows\System32\config\Netlogon.DNS to confirm SRV record registration. Test specific SRV records with NSLookup (set type=SRV)
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Domain Controller Locator (DCLocator)/WorkFlow: DcLocator: Dependency Information

symptom: Application or service communicates with domain controller in a remote/unoptimized site instead of the closest one; Netlogon log shows NO_CLIENT_SITE for the client IP address
rootCause: Client IP address is not mapped to any AD site because subnet definitions are missing or incorrect in Active Directory Sites and Services, so DCLocator returns a random DC instead of the nearest one
solution: Run NLTEST /DSADDRESSTOSITE: /ADDRESSES:x.x.x.x on a DC to test IP-to-site mapping. Add missing subnet definitions in Active Directory Sites and Services. Verify DC server objects are in the correct sites. For cross-forest scenarios, ensure matching site names exist in both forests
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Domain Controller Locator (DCLocator)/WorkFlow: DcLocator: Looking for Known Solutions

symptom: Domain member discovers domain controllers from an unexpected site despite correct subnet-to-site mapping in AD; nltest /dsgetsite returns wrong site name
rootCause: Static SiteName registry value at HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Services\Netlogon\Parameters overrides the DynamicSiteName (auto-detected from subnet mapping), forcing DCLocator to discover DCs in the statically configured site
solution: Check registry at HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Services\Netlogon\Parameters for SiteName value. Remove or correct the static SiteName to allow DynamicSiteName (auto-detected) to take effect. Verify with nltest /dsgetsite after change
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Domain Controller Locator (DCLocator)/WorkFlow: DcLocator: Looking for Known Solutions

symptom: DNS SRV records not registered by certain domain controllers; DCLocator unable to discover specific DCs that should be available
rootCause: Domain controller does not host the required FSMO role or feature for certain SRV records, or administrator configured DNS registration mnemonics (DnsAvoidRegisterRecords) to prevent registration of specific record types
solution: Run DcDiag /test:advertising to verify DC is advertising correctly. Review DnsAvoidRegisterRecords registry setting on the DC (see https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/troubleshoot/windows-server/identity/optimize-dc-location-global-catalog). Check C:\Windows\System32\config\Netlogon.DNS to verify which SRV records the DC is attempting to register
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Domain Controller Locator (DCLocator)/WorkFlow: DcLocator: FAQ

symptom: Enterprise State Roaming (ESR) settings do not sync. GPO policies for Activity Feed, PublishUserActivities, or UploadUserActivities are set to 0 (disabled) under Computer Configuration > Administrative Templates > System > OS Policies.
rootCause: GPO settings EnableActivityFeed, PublishUserActivities, or UploadUserActivities under HKLM\SOFTWARE\Policies\Microsoft\Windows\System are configured to 0, disabling the Activity Feed and cloud sync pipeline required by ESR.
solution: Check registry keys under HKLM\SOFTWARE\Policies\Microsoft\Windows\System for EnableActivityFeed, PublishUserActivities, UploadUserActivities. Ensure all three are set to 1 (enabled). Update the corresponding GPO and run gpupdate /force on the device.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/ESR/Workflow: ESR: Troubleshooting/Workflow: ESR: Client Troubleshooting

symptom: Enterprise State Roaming (ESR) settings do not sync despite GPO being correctly configured. MDM policy AllowSyncMySettings is set to 0 (disabled).
rootCause: MDM policy AllowSyncMySettings (Experience area CSP) is configured to 0/Disabled, blocking ESR sync at the MDM policy level. This overrides GPO settings when MDMWinsOverGP is enabled.
solution: Check MDMDiagReport.html (from TSSv2 BasicLog_Mini) for AllowSyncMySettings policy. If value is 0, collaborate with MDM/Intune team to set AllowSyncMySettings to 1 (enabled). Also check EnableActivityFeed, PublishUserActivities, UploadUserActivities MDM policies under HKLM\Software\Microsoft\PolicyManager.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/ESR/Workflow: ESR: Troubleshooting/Workflow: ESR: Client Troubleshooting

symptom: Enterprise State Roaming (ESR) settings do not sync despite correct GPO settings. MDMWinsOverGP is enabled causing MDM policies to override GPO settings for ESR-related policies.
rootCause: Registry key HKLM\Software\Microsoft\PolicyManager\current\device\ControlPolicyConflict\MDMWinsOverGP is set to DWORD 1, causing MDM policy CSP settings to take precedence over GPO. MDM-side ESR policies (EnableActivityFeed, PublishUserActivities, UploadUserActivities) may be disabled.
solution: Check MDMWinsOverGP registry key. If set to 1, verify MDM-side policies for ESR are enabled. Either update MDM policies to enable ESR settings, or if GPO should take precedence, set MDMWinsOverGP to 0. Reference: Policy CSP - ControlPolicyConflict.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/ESR/Workflow: ESR: Troubleshooting/Workflow: ESR: Client Troubleshooting

symptom: Settings page controls are unavailable/greyed out with message "Some Windows features are only available if you are using a Microsoft account or work account" on domain-joined Azure AD registered device.
rootCause: Device is set up to be domain-joined and registered to Azure AD, but has not yet successfully authenticated to Azure AD. Device policy application happens asynchronously after join.
solution: Verify device registration status: 1) Check Event Viewer > Applications and Services > Microsoft > Windows > User Device Registration for Event IDs 104 and 105. 2) Run PowerShell: Get-ChildItem -Path Cert:\LocalMachine\My | Where-Object {$_.Issuer -like "*MS-Organization-Access*"} to confirm certificate. 3) Run dsregcmd /status and verify AzureAdJoined=YES, WamDefaultSet=YES, WamDefaultGUID has (AzureAd), Settings URL is populated. If not, follow https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/active-directory/devices/enterprise-state-roaming-troubleshooting
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/ESR/Workflow: ESR: Troubleshooting/Workflow: ESR: Device Registration Troubleshooting

symptom: Domain join fails with error 0x534 (ERROR_NONE_MAPPED / No mapping between account names and security IDs was done). Netsetup.log shows NetpCreateComputerObjectInDs or NetpGetComputerObjectDn failed with 0x534.
rootCause: Transient error when domain join UI calls NetDomainJoin API without create flag first to locate a pre-created computer account. If no account found, 0x534 is logged before retrying with create flag. Also occurs when machine password change cannot find the computer account on the targeted DC (replication latency or failure).
solution: 0x534 is usually transient - check if a second NetDomainJoin call with create flag succeeds. Focus on the options flag bits (25, 27, 425, 427) to determine if join relies on pre-existing accounts or creates new ones. If persistent, check AD replication health to the targeted DC.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Domain Join/Workflow: Domain Join: Troubleshooting Examples/0x534: No mapping between account names and security IDs was done

symptom: Domain join fails with error 0x569 (ERROR_LOGON_TYPE_NOT_GRANTED / Logon failure: the user has not been granted the requested logon type at this computer). NetUseAdd to \\DC\IPC$ returned 1385.
rootCause: The user account performing the domain join lacks the Access this computer from network user right on the domain controller servicing the join operation.
solution: 1) Verify the domain join user (or their security group) has Access this computer from network right in Default Domain Controllers Policy. 2) Ensure the policy is linked to the OU hosting the DC computer account. 3) Confirm the DC is successfully applying the user rights settings from the policy.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Domain Join/Workflow: Domain Join: Troubleshooting Examples/0x569: Logon failure: the user has not been granted the requested logon type at this computer

symptom: Domain join fails with error 0x6d9 (EPT_S_NOT_REGISTERED / There are no more endpoints available from the endpoint mapper). Netsetup.log shows NetpGetComputerObjectDn Unable to bind to DS on DC: 0x6d9, join is rolled back.
rootCause: Network connectivity blocked between joining client and helper DC over TCP port 135 or ephemeral port range (1025-5000 for pre-Vista, 49152-65535 for Vista+). Other causes: Symantec Endpoint Protection on the DC blocking ports, or port exhaustion.
solution: 1) Identify the helper DC from NETSETUP.LOG. 2) Verify network connectivity on TCP 135 and ephemeral port range between client and DC. 3) Check for port exhaustion with netstat. 4) Review Symantec Endpoint Protection settings on the DC if installed. 5) Check firewall/router rules.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Domain Join/Workflow: Domain Join: Troubleshooting Examples/0x6d9: There are no more endpoints available from the endpoint mapper

symptom: Domain join fails with error 0xa8b (NERR_SetupCheckDNSConfig / An attempt to resolve the DNS name of a DC in the domain being joined has failed). Netsetup.log shows DNS A record name resolution verification failed: 0x2746.
rootCause: Workgroup computer points to invalid DNS server (ISP DNS, stale DNS), DNS server missing required zones (_msdcs, target domain forward lookup zone), missing or incorrect host A records for DC, problematic DNS names (single-label, disjoint namespace, numeric TLD), or network connectivity issues.
solution: 1) Verify client DNS server IPs are valid internal DNS servers (not ISP). 2) Check DNS servers host forward lookup zone for target domain and _msdcs zone. 3) Verify LDAP SRV records exist in _msdcs zone. 4) Verify DC host A record exists with correct IP. 5) Check for special domain names. 6) Test network connectivity.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Domain Join/Workflow: Domain Join: Troubleshooting Examples/0xa8b: An attempt to resolve the DNS name of a DC in the domain being joined has failed

symptom: Domain join fails with error 0xaac (NERR_AccountReuseBlockedByPolicy) when trying to re-use an existing computer account. Netsetup.log shows Account exists and re-use is blocked by policy, NetpCheckIfAccountShouldBeReused returns fReuseAllowed: FALSE.
rootCause: Windows updates from October 2022+ (CVE-2022-38042) block computer account reuse unless: joiner is original creator (ms-DS-creatorSID match), account was created by domain/enterprise/builtin admins, or account owner is in Allow computer account re-use during domain join GPO. NetJoinLegacyAccountReuse registry removed August 2024.
solution: 1) Use same account that created the computer account. 2) Delete stale computer account and rejoin. 3) Rename computer to use new account name. 4) Configure Domain controller: Allow computer account re-use during domain join GPO. WARNING: Do NOT use NetJoinLegacyAccountReuse registry - removed August 2024.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Domain Join/Workflow: Domain Join: Troubleshooting Examples/Domain Join fails when trying to re-use an existing computer account

symptom: Domain join fails with error 0x40 (ERROR_NETNAME_DELETED / The specified network name is no longer available). Netsetup.log shows NetUseAdd to \\DC\IPC$ returned 64. DC acknowledges TGS request for cifs/DC SPN but never sends TGS response.
rootCause: A firewall device between the client and the DC intercepted and blocked the KDC (Kerberos) request packet. The DC receives the TGS request (TCP ACK sent) but the response is dropped or blocked by the intermediate device.
solution: 1) Reproduce with net use \\DC\IPC$ to confirm SMB/Kerberos failure. 2) Collect network trace on both client and DC sides. 3) Check for firewall/IDS devices between client and DC intercepting Kerberos TCP 88 traffic. 4) Compare traces from both endpoints to verify TGS response.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Domain Join/Workflow: Domain Join: Troubleshooting Examples/NetUseAdd returned 0x40
Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Domain Join/Workflow: Domain Join: Troubleshooting Examples/NetUserSetInfo 0x5 can't rename computer
symptom: Access Denied error (0x5) when renaming computer name targeting Windows Server 2016 Domain Controller using NetUserSetInfo. Renaming succeeds when targeting Windows Server 2012 R2 DCs. Netsetup.log shows NetpManageMachineAccountWithSid: status of NetUserSetInfo on DC: 0x5 Access Denied. SAMSRV ETL shows SampCheckRpcRemoteCallerAccess() - Remote SAM Access is denied.
rootCause: Windows Server 2016 introduced the RestrictRemoteSam security policy (Network access: Restrict clients allowed to make remote calls to SAM) under Security Settings > Local Policies > Security Options. This policy restricts remote SAM RPC access via registry HKLM\system\CurrentControlSet\Control\Lsa\RestrictRemoteSam, and the calling account security group is not in the allowed SDDL.
solution: Remove the RestrictRemoteSam registry value on target Server 2016+ DCs: reg delete "HKLM\system\currentControlSet\control\lsa" /v restrictRemoteSam /f. This restores the default SDDL granting everyone read permissions. No reboot required. Also check Server 2012 R2 DCs with KB4012219 as the policy was backported. Note: customized SDDL may cause failures in admin tools, RDP to RDS servers, and AuthZ-based applications.

sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/ESR/Workflow: ESR: Additional Resources/Workflow: ESR: Internal Contents
symptom: Enterprise State Roaming (ESR) settings do not sync between Windows devices despite being enabled in the Azure AD tenant. CDP ETL shows either error 0x80070533 (ERROR_ACCOUNT_DISABLED) with Creating a cloud key failed in clouddataencryptionkeymanager.cpp, or activity blocked by source EnterpriseStateRoaming with empty permission scope and blockedOperationFlags PublishAndUpdate Download Delete.
rootCause: The AAD user is missing the RMSBASIC subscription required for ESR data encryption. Without this subscription, CDS cannot create encryption keys (causing 0x80070533) or the EnterpriseStateRoaming source blocks sync operations due to empty permission scopes.
solution: Ensure the RMSBASIC subscription is assigned to the affected user in the Azure AD tenant. Verify ESR configuration in Azure Active Directory > Enterprise State Roaming blade. After assigning the subscription, the user may need to sign out and sign back in for changes to take effect.

sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/ESR/Workflow: ESR: Additional Resources/Workflow: ESR: Internal Contents
symptom: Enterprise State Roaming Do not Sync group policy configured with Allow users to turn syncing on option causes sync settings to default to ON instead of the expected OFF state. Policy path: Computer Configuration > Administrative Templates > Windows Components > Sync your settings > Do not Sync.
rootCause: Known issue with the Windows Do not Sync policy implementation where the Allow users to turn syncing on override option incorrectly sets the default sync state to enabled instead of disabled, contrary to the policy description.
solution: Apply the workaround from the Microsoft article (ADDS: ESR: Do not Sync policy incorrectly defaults to sync settings being ON). As a temporary fix, use alternative policy configuration or manually configure the sync setting to disabled on affected devices via Intune CSP ./Vendor/MSFT/Policy/Config/Experience/AllowSyncMySettings set to 0.

sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/ESR/Workflow: ESR: Additional Resources/Workflow: ESR: Internal Contents
symptom: Enterprise State Roaming (ESR) sync is enabled in Azure AD tenant but settings do not sync. CDP ETL shows: Activity blocked by source AppUserSettings with empty permission scope and blockedOperationFlags PublishAndUpdate Download Delete for sync operations like Microsoft.Language.CultureSettings.
rootCause: The AppUserSettings source is blocking CDS sync operations due to misconfigured permission scopes. The empty permission scope prevents the sync framework from publishing, downloading, or deleting settings data through the Activity Feed Service.
solution: Review and correct the AppUserSettings configuration. Check if the user has manually toggled sync settings off in Windows Settings > Accounts > Sync your settings. Ensure ESR is enabled at the tenant level and the device is properly AAD-joined. Re-enable sync settings on the device if they were manually disabled.

symptom: Folder Redirection set via Group Policy not working. GPRESULT /v shows folder redirection setting as N/A. Event ID 1012 in Folder Redirection Operational log: Folder Redirection configuration is being controlled by WMI configuration class Win32_FolderRedirectionUserConfiguration
rootCause: MECM/SCCM (or another management tool) is controlling Folder Redirection via WMI Win32_UserStateConfigurationControls class. Registry value FolderRedirection is set to 1 under HKLM:\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\UserState\UserStateTechnologies\ConfigurationControls, causing Group Policy folder redirection to be ignored
solution: Check WMI class: gwmi Win32_UserStateConfigurationControls. If FolderRedirection=1, set registry to 0: Set-ItemProperty -path hklm:\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\UserState\UserStateTechnologies\ConfigurationControls -name FolderRedirection -value 0. Run gpupdate /force and verify.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Folder Redirection/Folder Redirection failed with Event ID 1012 under Folder Redirection Event Log

symptom: Service fails to start with gMSA account. Error 1069: The service did not start due to a logon failure. Security Event ID 4625 with Status 0xC0000022. Netlogon logs show Supported encryption types did not match 0xc0000719. Security-Netlogon Event 9001: the account cannot be used as a managed service account because not all the supported encryption types are supported by the local machine
rootCause: gMSA KerberosEncryptionType is configured with RC4 only, but the client machine does not support RC4 (only supports AES128/AES256). The encryption type mismatch prevents the machine from fetching the gMSA password from AD
solution: Update gMSA Kerberos encryption type: Set-ADServiceAccount -Identity <gMSA> -KerberosEncryptionType AES128,AES256. Verify: Get-ADServiceAccount <gMSA> -Properties KerberosEncryptionType. Restart the service. To diagnose: enable netlogon debug logging, filter for MSA entries; check Security-Netlogon/Operational for Event 9001; collect Kerberos ETL traces.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/GMSA/Data analysis: Demo troubleshooting

symptom: gMSA creation or password retrieval fails shortly after KDS root key creation. gMSA host cannot retrieve password. Service using gMSA fails to start with logon failure.
rootCause: KDS root key has not reached its effective time. By default DCs wait 10 days after root key creation to allow AD replication convergence. Even with Add-KdsRootKey -EffectiveImmediately, there is up to 10 hours delay due to time-slice algorithm (EffectiveTime in NTTE format divided into 10-hour slices).
solution: For production: wait for KDS root key effective time (up to 10 days default). Verify Event 4004 in KDS event log. For test/lab only: Add-KdsRootKey -EffectiveTime ((get-date).addhours(-10)). Do NOT recommend for production. Check root key replication across all DCs.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/GMSA/KDS RootKey

symptom: gMSA password generation or Windows LAPS operations suddenly fail/disrupted after customer performed gMSA setup or AD cleanup activities
rootCause: Customer mistakenly deleted KDS root keys from AD Configuration partition during gMSA setup. KDS root keys are required for both gMSA password generation and Windows LAPS operations.
solution: Verify KDS root keys exist: Get-KdsRootKey. If keys were deleted, recreate with Add-KdsRootKey. Check if customer recently worked on gMSA setup or performed AD object cleanup. Note: deleted KDS keys affect all gMSAs and Windows LAPS in the domain.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/GMSA/KDS RootKey

symptom: Service configured with gMSA fails to start with Error 1069 (logon failure). Event ID 7000 in System log, Event 4625 in Security log with status 0xC000006D/0xC000006A. Netlogon shows NlGetOutgoingPassword error 0xc0000034 and NlChangePassword error C000018A.
rootCause: Service Control Manager (SCM) does not properly initiate the service with IsManagedAccount flag set to true. SC QmanagedAccount shows ACCOUNT MANAGED: FALSE.
solution: Run 'SC QmanagedAccount <ServiceName>' to verify managed account status. If FALSE, reconfigure the service with gMSA by removing and re-adding the gMSA account in the service Log On properties. This will set ServiceAccountManaged registry to true.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/GMSA/Workflow: gMSA : Looking for known solutions/ADDS: Security: Services fail to start with error "Error 1069" due to username and password error

symptom: After creating a new KDS root key via PowerShell (Add-KdsRootKey), gMSA password operations on Domain Controllers still use the old root key.
rootCause: KDS service maintains an MRK (Master Root Key) cache ordered by recency, created upon service start. When a new key is created via PowerShell/LDAP (kdscli), kdssvc is not notified and the cache is not updated until the service restarts.
solution: Restart the KDS service (kdssvc) on the Domain Controller to refresh the MRK cache with the newly created root key. Verify the active root key by following the internal KB for identifying the RootKey in use for gMSA.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/GMSA/Workflow: gMSA : Looking for known solutions/How to find which Root Key which was used to generate the current password of the gMSA

symptom: After configuring a service with gMSA, the Log On tab in services.msc becomes greyed out and administrators cannot modify the service logon properties.
rootCause: When a service is configured with gMSA, Windows sets the service as a managed service (SC QmanagedAccount shows TRUE), which locks the Log On properties to prevent manual password changes.
solution: Run 'SC ManagedAccount <ServiceName> False' to unlock the Log On properties. After making modifications, reconfiguring the service with gMSA will automatically set managedAccount back to TRUE.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/GMSA/Workflow: gMSA : Looking for known solutions/How to reconfigure a Service to run with gMSA account when it is greyed out

symptom: Service fails to start under gMSA with Error 1069 (logon failure). Computer hosting the service cannot retrieve gMSA password from DC.
rootCause: The computer account hosting the service is not added to the msDS-GroupMSAMembership (PrincipalsAllowedToRetrieveManagedPassword) attribute of the gMSA object, so it lacks permission to retrieve the managed password.
solution: Run 'Get-ADServiceAccount -Identity gMSA1$ -Properties PrincipalsAllowedToRetrieveManagedPassword' to verify. Add the computer account (or a group containing it) to the PrincipalsAllowedToRetrieveManagedPassword attribute of the gMSA object.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/GMSA/Workflow: gMSA : Looking for known solutions/How to troubleshoot service logon failures with error code 1069

symptom: Service fails to start under gMSA with Error 1069. PrincipalsAllowedToRetrieveManagedPassword is set to a security group, but the computer still cannot retrieve the gMSA password.
rootCause: The computer account is either not a member of the security group specified in PrincipalsAllowedToRetrieveManagedPassword, or the group membership has not been replicated to domain controllers, or the membership change has not taken effect on the client system (stale Kerberos ticket).
solution: 1) Verify group membership with Get-ADGroupMember. 2) Run 'GPResult /v' on the affected computer to confirm updated group membership. 3) Collect AD object metadata for both the gMSA and the security group to verify replication. 4) Restart the computer or run 'klist purge' to refresh Kerberos tickets.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/GMSA/Workflow: gMSA : Looking for known solutions/How to troubleshoot service logon failures with error code 1069

symptom: Service fails to start under gMSA with Error 1069. Test-KdsRootKey returns 'Value does not fall within the expected range' instead of True.
rootCause: The KDS root key is either not created properly or has not yet reached its effective time (msKds-UseStartTime). By default, a new KDS root key has a 10-day wait period before it becomes active.
solution: 1) Run 'Get-KdsRootKey' and copy the KeyID. 2) Run 'Test-KdsRootKey -KeyID <KeyID>' to verify. 3) If it fails, open ADSIEDIT > CN=Master Root Keys,CN=Group Key Distribution Service,CN=Services,CN=Configuration and check the msKds-UseStartTime attribute. 4) Run 'w32tm /ntte <value>' to see the effective date. 5) If needed, create a new root key with 'Add-KdsRootKey -EffectiveTime ((get-date).addhours(-10))' for immediate use (test only).
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/GMSA/Workflow: gMSA : Looking for known solutions/How to troubleshoot service logon failures with error code 1069

symptom: New-ADServiceAccount fails with 'Key does not exist' error when creating a Group Managed Service Account (gMSA)
rootCause: KDS Root Key does not exist or has not reached its effective time yet. Without a valid KDS Root Key, the domain cannot generate gMSA passwords.
solution: Run Get-KdsRootKey to check if a root key exists. If not, create one with Add-KdsRootKey. If it exists, verify the effective time has passed. Wait for the key to become effective before creating gMSAs.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/GMSA/Workflow: gMSA : Looking for known solutions/New-ADServiceAccount fails with Key does not exist

symptom: New-ADServiceAccount fails with 'Parameter: Path is required for this operation' when creating a gMSA
rootCause: The otherWellKnownObjects attribute on the domain DN contains a deleted/stale reference to the Managed Service Accounts container (pointing to CN=Deleted Objects). This occurs after the original MSA container is deleted and recreated - the attribute does not auto-update.
solution: Update the otherWellKnownObjects attribute on the domain DN to point to the correct Managed Service Accounts container path. Alternatively, use the -Path parameter with the full DN of the MSA container as a workaround.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/GMSA/Workflow: gMSA : Looking for known solutions/gMSA creation failure due to 'Parameter: 'Path' is required for this operation'

symptom: Service using gMSA fails to start with 'Error 1069: The service did not start due to logon failure', or Install-ADServiceAccount fails with 'An unspecified error has occurred'
rootCause: Domain Controllers experienced a time skew (often caused by Secure Time Seeding / UtilizeSslTimeData) which caused the gMSA password to roll to a future date. UtilizeSslTimeData does not adhere to W32Time large time skew protections. Commonly seen with Microsoft Defender for Identity (MDI) installations.
solution: Create a new gMSA account for the service (there is no documented method to force password change on a broken gMSA). Disable Secure Time Seeding on Domain Controllers via Group Policy or registry to prevent future time jumps.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/GMSA/Workflow: gMSA : Unknown Solution Investigation/Unable to install GMSA or Services fail to start due to gMSA password set at a future date

symptom: Cannot configure gMSA as the run-as account for a Windows Scheduled Task via the Task Scheduler GUI
rootCause: Windows Task Scheduler GUI does not support selecting gMSA accounts directly. This is a known platform limitation.
solution: Create the scheduled task with required parameters via GUI first, then use schtasks to switch to gMSA: schtasks /change /TN <taskname> /RU domain\gmsaaccount$ /RP (leave password blank as gMSA manages its own password).
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/GMSA/Workflow: gMSA : Useful information about gMSA

symptom: AGPM-controlled GPO settings appear missing when editing after checkout; settings changed do not appear in policy settings report from archive. Issue occurs when AGPM client and server are in different AD sites.
rootCause: AGPM adds complexity to DC selection when machines are in multiple AD sites. AGPM client may connect to a different Domain Controller than the one where changes were committed, causing stale reads from replication lag.
solution: Ensure AGPM client and AGPM server communicate with the same DC. See internal KB 3144314 (GPHELP: AGPM adds complexity to DC selection when machines are in multiple sites). Verify AD replication health between sites.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Group Policy/Workflow: GPO: AGPM workflow

symptom: AGPM GPO check-out fails with 'Naming Violation' error when attempting to check out a GPO for editing in GPMC
rootCause: CNF (conflict/duplicate) objects exist for the GPO in Active Directory, causing AGPM to encounter naming conflicts during checkout operations
solution: Identify and clean up CNF objects for the affected GPO in Active Directory. Use ADSIEdit or repadmin to locate CNF objects (objects with �ACNF: in DN). See internal KB 4462720.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Group Policy/Workflow: GPO: AGPM workflow

symptom: Changes to 'Wired settings' (802.3) in a GPO disappear after AGPM check-in/check-out cycle. The 802.3 wired network policy settings are lost.
rootCause: AGPM does not properly preserve 802.3 wired network settings during the check-in/check-out archive operations. The settings are dropped during GPO serialization in the AGPM archive.
solution: Known AGPM limitation (KB 4343998). Workaround: apply 802.3 wired settings directly in GPMC outside of AGPM control, or verify settings after each AGPM check-in/check-out cycle.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Group Policy/Workflow: GPO: AGPM workflow

symptom: GPO edit fails with error 0x80071128: 'The data present in the reparse point buffer is invalid' after installing monthly rollup KBs (1B/1C patches)
rootCause: A servicing update (monthly rollup KB) introduces a regression that corrupts reparse point data used by GPO editing operations in AGPM
solution: Apply the fix from KB 4077654 (Servicing: 1B.18). If fix is not available, uninstall the offending monthly rollup as a temporary workaround.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Group Policy/Workflow: GPO: AGPM workflow

symptom: Group Policy settings (registry keys, file permissions, configurations) are applied correctly as verified by gpresult, but the application functionality does not work as expected (e.g., wallpaper not visible despite correct registry values, USB drive still accessible despite disable policy applied, Edge not treating site as trusted despite GP applied)
rootCause: The application that reads the GPO-configured settings has a timing issue or does not re-read the updated configuration. Group Policy successfully writes the configuration (registry, files, permissions) but the consuming application fails to pick up the changes. This is an application-side issue, not a Group Policy deployment issue.
solution: 1. Use Process Monitor (Procmon) while running gpupdate /force to verify GP writes the correct configuration values. 2. Restart the application to force it to re-read the registry/configuration. 3. If the issue persists after restart, filter Procmon on the specific registry key/file to confirm the application is reading the correct path. 4. Engage the specific application support team (Edge, USB/Storage, etc.) rather than Directory Services, as the configuration delivery via GP is working correctly.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Group Policy/TSG: Group Policy Troubleshooting/GPO applied, Settings appear but functionality not working

symptom: GPO is in the Applied list (gpresult /h) but settings are missing or outdated. GPO version number on client machine differs from the version on the PDC Emulator (e.g., client shows version 2 while PDC shows version 3).
rootCause: Active Directory or file replication (DFSR/FRS) latency or failure between the PDC Emulator and the domain controller serving the client machine. GPO changes are created/updated on PDC first and must replicate to other DCs. The client is reading an older version of the GPO from a DC that has not received the latest replication.
solution: 1. Identify the GPO version on the PDC Emulator via GPMC. 2. Run gpresult /h on client and compare the version number in the Applied GPOs section. 3. If version mismatch: force replicate from PDC to the client local DC site, or wait for replication cycle. 4. If replication is broken: run repadmin /showrepl * /csv to diagnose replication issues. 5. Engage Directory Services team for persistent replication failures.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Group Policy/TSG: Group Policy Troubleshooting/GPO in Applied list but settings missing

symptom: GPO is in the Applied list but the actual setting value on the client differs from the configured value (e.g., a setting should be Enabled but appears Disabled). The setting appears to be overwritten by another source.
rootCause: Another GPO with higher precedence is applying the opposite setting and winning the conflict, or a non-GPO tool (SCCM, Intune, custom script) is modifying the same registry key/setting after Group Policy applies it.
solution: 1. Run gpresult /h and identify the winning GPO for the conflicting setting. 2. If another GPO is winning: re-order GPO link precedence in GPMC or adjust security filtering. 3. If a non-GPO process is suspected: run gpupdate /force and verify the value is correct immediately after. Then run Procmon filtering on the specific registry key to capture which process changes it. 4. Alternatively, configure auditing on the registry key or file object to identify the modifying process. 5. Engage the appropriate team based on what is overwriting the setting.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Group Policy/TSG: Group Policy Troubleshooting/GPO in Applied list but settings missing

symptom: GPO appears in the Denied list in gpresult with Reason Denied: WMI Filter. The GPO is in scope for the OU but not being applied to the target machine.
rootCause: The WMI Filter linked to the GPO does not match the client machine attributes (e.g., OS version, hardware properties). WMI Filters allow targeting GPOs to specific machines based on WMI queries, and a non-matching filter causes the GPO to be denied.
solution: 1. Open Group Policy Management Console (GPMC) and identify the WMI Filter linked to the denied GPO. 2. Review the WMI query and test it against the target machine using wmic or PowerShell Get-WmiObject. 3. If the filter is incorrect, update the WMI query to match the intended target machines. 4. As a temporary workaround, create a separate GPO without a WMI Filter targeting the specific machine/user. 5. Engage the appropriate application team if the WMI Filter is for a specific application (Edge, SQL, etc.).
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Group Policy/TSG: Group Policy Troubleshooting/GPO in Denied List of GPO's

symptom: GPO appears in the Denied list in gpresult with Reason Denied: Inaccessible, Empty or Disabled. This may occur especially in Loopback Processing scenarios.
rootCause: The user/computer does not have Read/Apply permissions on the GPO due to security filtering configuration. In Loopback Processing scenarios, the Authenticated Users group must have READ permission (not Apply) on the GPO for it to be accessible; without this, the GPO is treated as inaccessible.
solution: 1. Check the GPO Security Filtering in GPMC - verify the target user/computer or their group is listed with Read and Apply permissions. 2. For Loopback Processing: ensure Authenticated Users has READ permission (not Read+Apply) on the GPO. Important: it is only READ, not READ and Apply. 3. Reference: https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/archive/blogs/askds/back-to-the-loopback-troubleshooting-group-policy-loopback-processing-part-2 4. If the GPO is Disabled, enable the appropriate section (User/Computer Configuration) in GPMC.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Group Policy/TSG: Group Policy Troubleshooting/GPO in Denied List of GPO's

symptom: GPO appears in the Denied list in gpresult with Reason Denied: Access denied (Security Filtering). User or computer is explicitly denied access to the GPO.
rootCause: The user or computer (or a group they belong to, including nested groups) has a Deny permission set on the GPO, or the user/computer lacks both Read and Apply permissions. Deny permissions override Allow permissions in GPO security filtering.
solution: 1. Open GPMC, navigate to the GPO, and check the Delegation/Security tab for Deny entries. 2. Check the user group membership including nested groups - gpresult also shows group membership in the Security section. 3. Remove the Deny permission if it was set incorrectly, or add the user/computer to an allowed security group. 4. Verify both Read and Apply Group Policy permissions are granted for the target user/computer.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Group Policy/TSG: Group Policy Troubleshooting/GPO in Denied List of GPO's

symptom: GPO is not in the Applied list nor in the Denied list when running gpresult /h. The intended Group Policy is completely absent from the results.
rootCause: Active Directory replication is broken between Domain Controllers, so the GPO and its link have not replicated to the DC that the client machine contacted. Unlike version mismatch (where GPO appears but is stale), here the GPO is entirely missing from the client DC.
solution: 1. Identify which DC the client is contacting (check gpresult output for DC name). 2. Force replicate from the PDC Emulator to the client local DC site. 3. Run repadmin /showrepl * /csv to check replication health across all DCs. 4. If DFSR/FRS file replication is also broken, the SYSVOL share may be missing the GPO files. 5. Engage Directory Services team for replication troubleshooting.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Group Policy/TSG: Group Policy Troubleshooting/GPO is not in the Applied or Denied list of GPO's

symptom: GPO is not in the Applied or Denied list in gpresult. AD replication is healthy. The GPO exists on all DCs but does not appear in the client policy results at all.
rootCause: The user or computer object is not located in the OU (or parent OU) where the GPO is linked. GPOs follow the LSDOU model (Local, Site, Domain, OU) and only apply to objects within the linked scope. Additionally, Group Policy Loopback Processing in Replace mode can change the effective scope, causing expected user GPOs to not apply.
solution: 1. Verify the user/computer object OU location in Active Directory Users and Computers. 2. Check that the GPO is linked to the correct OU (or a parent OU) containing the target object. 3. Check if Group Policy Loopback Processing is configured in Replace mode - this causes user policies to come from the computer OU instead of the user OU. 4. If the object was recently moved between OUs, run gpupdate /force and verify the new OU policies apply.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Group Policy/TSG: Group Policy Troubleshooting/GPO is not in the Applied or Denied list of GPO's
Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Kerberos/Kerberos: Looking for known solutions/KDC_ERR_BADOPTION (0xD) [Typically associated with Kerberos delegation issues]
symptom: Kerberos TGS request fails with KDC_ERR_BADOPTION (0xD), typically during Kerberos constrained delegation scenarios. The KDC rejects the ticket request with error code 13.
rootCause: Service account is not trusted for delegation, not trusted for delegation to the requested SPN (msDS-AllowedToDelegateTo mismatch), the target user account is marked as 'sensitive and cannot be delegated', or the service account needs Protocol Transition (S4U2self) but is configured for 'Kerberos only' constrained delegation without the TRUSTED_TO_AUTHENTICATE_FOR_DELEGATION flag.
solution: Check delegation settings on the service/computer account properties in AD. Verify msDS-AllowedToDelegateTo includes the target SPN. If S4U2self is needed, enable 'Use any authentication protocol' (sets userAccountControl flag 0x1000000 TRUSTED_TO_AUTHENTICATE_FOR_DELEGATION). Verify target user is not marked 'Account is sensitive and cannot be delegated'. Use ldifde to dump account and verify msDS-AllowedToDelegateTo values.

sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Kerberos/Kerberos: Looking for known solutions/KDC_ERR_CLIENT_REVOKED (0x12) [Clients credentials have been revoked]
symptom: Kerberos authentication fails with KDC_ERR_CLIENT_REVOKED (0x12) - Client credentials have been revoked. Occurs when TGT is older than 20 minutes and KDC re-validates account standing.
rootCause: The user account is disabled, expired, locked out, or the current time is outside the user logon hours. The KDC validates user rights policy (TicketValidateClient) when Enforce user logon restrictions Kerberos policy is enabled and TGT age exceeds 20 minutes (per MS-KILE section 59).
solution: Check user account status in Active Directory: verify account is not disabled, not expired, not locked out, and current time is within allowed logon hours. Review Enforce user logon restrictions setting in Default Domain GPO under Computer Configuration > Windows Settings > Security Settings > Account Policies > Kerberos Policy.

sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Kerberos/Kerberos: Looking for known solutions/KDC_ERR_C_PRINCIPAL_UNKNOWN (0x6) [Client Principal Unknown]
symptom: Kerberos AS-REQ or TGS-REQ fails with KDC_ERR_C_PRINCIPAL_UNKNOWN (0x6) - KDC cannot find the client principal. Visible in Kerberos.etl as KerbGetAuthenticationTicketEx() - KerbCallKdc failed: error 0x6.
rootCause: The User Principal Name (UPN) supplied in the Kerberos request does not match any account in Active Directory. This may be a UPN routing issue when using a generic/custom UPN suffix that is not properly configured for routing in the forest.
solution: Verify the UPN is correctly set on the target account in AD. Try using the explicit UPN registered on the account. If using a generic/custom UPN suffix, test with the implicit UPN (sAMAccountName@AD-FQDN) to isolate UPN routing issues. Check UPN suffix routing in AD Domains and Trusts.

sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Kerberos/Kerberos: Looking for known solutions/KDC_ERR_C_PRINCIPAL_UNKNOWN (0x6) [Client Principal Unknown]
symptom: S4U2Self request fails with KDC_ERR_C_PRINCIPAL_UNKNOWN (0x6) when an application performs access checks via group memberships using the TokenGroupsGlobalAndUniversal constructed attribute.
rootCause: The service account performing the S4U2Self request lacks permissions to read the TokenGroupsGlobalAndUniversal constructed attribute on the user object in Active Directory.
solution: Grant the service account read access to the TokenGroupsGlobalAndUniversal attribute on user objects in AD. Verify permissions using dsacls or ADSIEdit on the user object.

sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Kerberos/Kerberos: Looking for known solutions/KDC_ERR_POLICY (0xC) [KDC policy rejects request]
symptom: Kerberos constrained delegation (S4U2Proxy) fails with KDC_ERR_POLICY (0xC) and substatus STATUS_CROSSREALM_DELEGATION_FAILURE (0xC000040B) when attempting delegation to a target in another domain.
rootCause: Traditional Kerberos Constrained Delegation (KCD) does not support cross-domain delegation. The msDS-AllowedToDelegateTo list contains SPNs from another domain, which the KDC rejects.
solution: Use Resource-Based Constrained Delegation (RBCD) instead of traditional KCD for cross-domain scenarios. Configure msDS-AllowedToActOnBehalfOfOtherIdentity on the target service account. Ensure service accounts for S4U2Proxy are from the same realm/domain as the KDC.

sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Kerberos/Kerberos: Looking for known solutions/KDC_ERR_POLICY (0xC) [KDC policy rejects request]
symptom: Kerberos TGS request fails with KDC_ERR_POLICY (0xC) and substatus STATUS_AUTHENTICATION_FIREWALL_FAILED (0xC0000413) when authenticating across a forest trust with Selective Authentication enabled.
rootCause: Selective Authentication is configured on the forest trust and the requesting user does not have the Allowed to Authenticate permission on the target computer or service account. May also occur for Krbtgt requests if client realm is passed in flat (NetBIOS) name format.
