solution: Grant Allowed to Authenticate permission to the user or group on the target computer/service when Selective Authentication is enabled. If the issue involves Krbtgt requests with flat name format, investigate client realm name resolution (see KB 2959395). Verify trust configuration in AD Domains and Trusts.

sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Kerberos/Kerberos: Looking for known solutions/KDC_ERR_TGT_REVOKED (0x14)  [TGT has been revoked]
symptom: Kerberos TGS request fails with KDC_ERR_TGT_REVOKED (0x14) after domain controllers are patched for CVE-2021-42287 (KB5008380). Client previously obtained TGT from an unpatched DC.
rootCause: Inconsistent patch levels across domain controllers for CVE-2021-42287. Client obtained TGT from an unpatched DC (missing PAC_REQUESTOR buffer). When presented to a patched DC for TGS request, the patched DC rejects the TGT because it lacks required PAC_REQUESTOR validation data.
solution: Update all domain controllers to the same patch level (KB5008380 or later). After patching, purge Kerberos ticket cache on affected clients using klist purge or PowerShell: Get-WmiObject Win32_LogonSession | Where-Object {$_.AuthenticationPackage -ne 'NTLM'} | ForEach-Object {klist.exe purge -li ([Convert]::ToString($_.LogonId, 16))}.

symptom: Kerberos authentication fails after April/July 2026 Patch Tuesday for accounts with msDS-SupportedEncryptionTypes attribute NULL/Not Set. KDC Event 203/204 (KDCSVC) logged on DC. Security Event 4768/4769 shows Ticket Encryption Type 0x17 (RC4). Error KDC_ERR_ETYPE_NOSUPP (0x12) in network trace.
rootCause: CVE-2026-20833 RC4 deprecation enforcement phases changed KDC default behavior. April 2026 Soft Enforcement makes AES the default for accounts with NULL msDS-SupportedEncryptionTypes. Accounts that never had a password reset since pre-Server 2008 era lack AES hashes in AD, so KDC cannot issue AES tickets to them.
solution: Reset the affected service/computer account password to regenerate AES keys in AD. Alternatively, explicitly set msDS-SupportedEncryptionTypes to include AES (0x18 = AES128+AES256). Verify with Get-ADUser/Get-ADComputer -Properties msDS-SupportedEncryptionTypes. Use List-AccountKeys.ps1 and Get-KerbEncryptionUsage.ps1 from Microsoft Kerberos-Crypto GitHub repo to audit at-risk accounts. Check registry RC4DefaultDisablementPhase (1=Audit, 2=Enforcement) on DCs.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Kerberos/Kerberos RC4 Deprecation/Scoping and Log collections

symptom: Non-Windows device (NAS, Linux server, printer, legacy Java app) fails Kerberos authentication after RC4 deprecation enforcement. AS_REQ only advertises EType 23 (RC4). KDC Event 201/203 (KDCSVC) logged indicating requesting client is RC4-only.
rootCause: Non-Windows devices often use hard-coded keytabs or legacy Kerberos libraries (e.g., old Java versions, MIT krb5) that only support RC4-HMAC. After April 2026 enforcement, KDC rejects RC4-only requests from these clients.
solution: Regenerate the keytab with AES encryption support (ktpass or ktutil with AES256). Update firmware/OS/Kerberos library on the device to support AES. For Java apps, ensure JCE Unlimited Strength policy is installed and krb5.conf includes AES enctypes. Verify client AS_REQ advertises AES ETypes in network trace.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Kerberos/Kerberos RC4 Deprecation/Scoping and Log collections

symptom: User gets 'Your account is configured to prevent you from using this PC' when logging on interactively. Security Event 4768 on DC shows Result Code 0xC (KDC_ERR_POLICY). Event 4625 on client shows Status 0xC000006E SubStatus 0xC0000070 (STATUS_INVALID_WORKSTATION). Network trace shows KRB_ERROR KDC_ERR_POLICY (12) in AS_REP phase.
rootCause: User account has Log On To workstation restrictions configured (userWorkstations attribute in AD). The user is attempting to log on from a workstation not listed in the allowed workstations. KDC performs logon restriction check during AS_REQ processing and denies the TGT.
solution: Check user object Log On To settings in ADUC (Account tab). Verify userWorkstations attribute via Get-ADUser -Properties userWorkstations. Either add the target workstation to the allowed list or remove the restriction. Note: uses NetBIOS computer names. Key differentiator: KDC_ERR_POLICY (0xC) at AS_REQ phase with SubStatus 0xC0000070.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Kerberos/Kerberos: Additional features/Kerberos: Authentication Policies and Silos/Verifications and Troubleshooting/Troubleshooting/Basics verifications

symptom: User gets 'The sign in method you are trying to use is not allowed' when logging on interactively. TGT request succeeds (AS_REP successful, Event 4768 shows no error). Event 4625 on client shows Status 0xC000015B (logon type not granted) SubStatus 0x0. Kerberos authentication itself succeeds but local logon is denied.
rootCause: Group Policy Deny log on locally (Computer Configuration > Windows Settings > Security Settings > Local Policies > User Rights Assignment) is applied on the target workstation for the user account. Unlike Logon Restrictions which fail at KDC (AS_REQ phase), this policy is enforced locally after successful Kerberos authentication.
solution: Run gpresult /v or rsop.msc on target machine to identify which GPO is denying logon. Review Deny log on locally and Allow log on locally user rights assignments. Key differentiator from Logon Restrictions: TGT succeeds at KDC, failure is local with Status 0xC000015B (not 0xC0000070).
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Kerberos/Kerberos: Additional features/Kerberos: Authentication Policies and Silos/Verifications and Troubleshooting/Troubleshooting/Basics verifications

symptom: User in Authentication Policy/Silo gets 'Your account is configured to prevent you from using this PC' when logging on to non-allowed device. DC Event 105 (AuthenticationPolicyFailures-DomainController): TGT denied because device does not meet access control restrictions. KDC_ERR_POLICY (0xC) in FAST armored AS response. Kerberos.etl shows ExtendedStatus 0xc0000070.
rootCause: Authentication Policy linked to a Silo enforces SDDL-based access control conditions on TGT request. The device TGT used for FAST armoring does not satisfy the conditional expression in the Authentication Policy. Requires Kerberos armoring (FAST) to be enabled on domain. Failure at AS_REQ phase on DC side.
solution: Check Event 105 on DC (Microsoft-Windows-Authentication/AuthenticationPolicyFailures-DomainController) with XML filter for the user. Review Auth Policy in ADAC (Get-ADAuthenticationPolicy, Get-ADAuthenticationPolicySilo). Verify SDDL conditions under User sign on tab. Use Kerberos.etl (client) and KDC.etl (DC) for detailed trace. Key differentiator from plain Logon Restrictions: FAST armoring is used, Event 105 logged, and Silo/Policy name shown in event.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Kerberos/Kerberos: Additional features/Kerberos: Authentication Policies and Silos/Verifications and Troubleshooting/Troubleshooting/Deep-dive/Logon Denied

symptom: TGS request for specific SPN fails with Authentication Policy restriction. DC Event 105: Kerberos service ticket denied because user/device does not meet access control restrictions. Kerberos.etl shows ExtendedStatus 0xc0000413 on TGS response. KDC.etl shows I_GetTGSTicket leaving with 0xc. User gets prompted for credentials or access denied when accessing Kerberos-protected resource (e.g., ADFS SSO).
rootCause: Authentication Policy (without Silo) enforces service ticket issuance restrictions via SDDL conditions on the service account. The policy specifies which users/devices can obtain TGS tickets for the service. User not matching the SDDL condition is denied the service ticket. ExtendedStatus 0xc0000413 is specific to TGS-level Auth Policy denial.
solution: Check Event 105 on DC for policy name and denied account. Review Authentication Policy service ticket issuance tab for the service account. Verify SDDL condition. Key differentiator: TGT succeeds but TGS fails = service-level Auth Policy (not Silo). Use XML filter: *[System[(EventID=105)]]and*[EventData[Data[@Name='TargetUserName']='username']]. 0xc0000413 in Kerberos.etl confirms TGS-level Auth Policy denial vs 0xc0000070 for TGT-level denial.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Kerberos/Kerberos: Additional features/Kerberos: Authentication Policies and Silos/Verifications and Troubleshooting/Troubleshooting/Deep-dive/TGS Request fail
Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Kerberos/Kerberos: Looking for known solutions/KRB_AP_ERR_SKEW (0x25) [Clock skew between client and server-KDC is too great]
symptom: Kerberos authentication fails with KRB_AP_ERR_SKEW (0x25) error indicating clock skew between client and server/KDC is too great
rootCause: Time difference between the client and server or Key Distribution Center (KDC) exceeds the acceptable Kerberos threshold (default 5 minutes). The issue is often self-healing on client side as the client will resync using the KDC/server time, but persistent skew between KDC and server requires investigation.
solution: Verify time synchronization between the server and the KDC. Check Windows Time service (w32time) configuration. If the KDC and server clocks are skewed, investigate time sync issues between systems and domain controllers.

sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Kerberos/Kerberos: Looking for known solutions/Kerberos: Other Known solutions/Kerberos: Client problems: MaxPacketSize, MaxTokenSize
symptom: Kerberos authentication via UDP fails silently with no response from KDC when UDP port 88 is closed by network topology
rootCause: Client is configured to use UDP for Kerberos (non-default on modern Windows) but the network does not allow UDP port 88 traffic to the KDC. When the KDC response is too large for UDP, it instructs the client to retry via TCP, but if UDP is blocked entirely, no response is received.
solution: Set the registry value MaxPacketSize to 0 or 1 to force Kerberos to use TCP instead of UDP. Refer to KB 244474. Note: Java clients have an equivalent setting udp_preference_limit=1 in krb5.conf.

sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Kerberos/Kerberos: Looking for known solutions/Kerberos: Other Known solutions/Kerberos: Client problems: MaxPacketSize, MaxTokenSize
symptom: Kerberos ticket too large for client token cache causing partial authentication failures when user belongs to many groups
rootCause: The KDC response ticket exceeds the client MaxTokenSize limit, typically when a user is a member of many groups resulting in too many SIDs in the PAC. The hardcoded LSASRV limit is 1015 SIDs maximum.
solution: Adjust MaxTokenSize registry setting per KB 327825. Be aware of the hardcoded 1015 SIDs limit per KB 328889 - users who are members of more than 1015 groups may fail logon authentication regardless of MaxTokenSize.

sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Kerberos/Kerberos: Looking for known solutions/Kerberos: Other Known solutions/Kerberos: Credential Manager prevents Kerberos ticketing
symptom: Client does not request a Kerberos ticket and only performs NTLM authentication despite Kerberos being available and properly configured
rootCause: An existing entry in Windows Credential Manager for the target resource causes the client to use stored credentials and bypass Kerberos ticket request, falling back to NTLM authentication.
solution: Remove or correct the related Credential Manager entries on the affected client. Optionally set registry HKLM\System\CurrentControlSet\Control\Lsa\DisableDomainCreds=1 (REG_DWORD). For a broader solution, use GPO: Computer Configuration > Security Settings > Local Policies > Security Options > Network access: Do not allow storage of credentials or .NET Passports.

sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Kerberos/Kerberos: Looking for known solutions/Kerberos: Other Known solutions/Kerberos: Fallback to NTLM - only when allowed
symptom: Kerberos authentication fails with KDC_ERR_S_PRINCIPAL_UNKNOWN and falls back to NTLM. In computer context (Local System), fallback may result in a NULL session. After Jan 2022 hardening (CVE-2022-21920), authentication with 3-part SPNs may also be affected.
rootCause: Default NTLM fallback occurs when KDC returns KDC_ERR_S_PRINCIPAL_UNKNOWN. For Local System accounts, NTLM fallback may produce NULL sessions by default. The January 2022 hardening update changed Kerberos-to-NTLM fallback behavior for 3-part SPNs.
solution: Configure GPO Network security: Allow Local System to use computer identity for NTLM for computer context fallback. Adjust NTLM authentication restriction level via GPO Network Security: Restrict NTLM: NTLM authentication in this domain. Review hardening changes from CVE-2022-21920 for 3-part SPN scenarios.

sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Kerberos/Kerberos: Looking for known solutions/Kerberos: Other Known solutions/Kerberos: LDAP and Kerberos Server may not respond
symptom: TCP sessions to AD DS/AD LDS server ports 88, 464, 389, 3268 (and SSL/TLS ports 636, 3269) are reset immediately after creation. UDP requests on ports 88 and 464 may not receive responses.
rootCause: The KDC session scavenging thread monitors idle sessions every 30 seconds. The default NewConnectionTimeout of 10 seconds combined with the 30-second scavenging interval means newly created sessions can be disconnected immediately (effective interval 0-30 seconds).
solution: Set the NewConnectionTimeout registry value to 40 or higher for a 30-90 second timeout window, or 70+ for 60-120 seconds. Refer to Kerberos protocol registry entries and KDC configuration keys in Windows documentation. Original KB number: 2000061.

sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Kerberos/Kerberos: Looking for known solutions/Kerberos: Other Known solutions/Kerberos: LDAP and Kerberos Server may not respond
symptom: KDC does not respond to UDP Kerberos authentication or password change requests sent from source port 22528/UDP or 53249/UDP, typically from third-party Kerberos clients
rootCause: The KDC has a built-in anti-loop protection that blocks Kerberos requests on source ports 88/UDP and 464/UDP. A byte-ordering bug in the implementation causes it to also block source ports 22528/UDP (byte-swapped 88) and 53249/UDP (byte-swapped 464).
solution: Exclude source ports 22528/UDP and 53249/UDP from the ephemeral port range of UDP on the client. Note: This primarily affects third-party products using UDP for Kerberos, as the Microsoft Kerberos client uses TCP by default since Windows Vista.

symptom: Kerberos authentication fails over external trust with KDC_ERR_S_PRINCIPAL_UNKNOWN when client uses a two-part SPN (service/servername) to access resources in the trusting domain
rootCause: External trusts have no name routing information for Kerberos. SPNs and UPNs are only looked up in the local forest KDC/GC, so the trusted domain KDC cannot resolve the trusting domain SPN and returns KDC_ERR_S_PRINCIPAL_UNKNOWN. Only the AS exchange (logon) works over external trust; subsequent TGS requests with two-part SPNs fail and fall back to NTLM.
solution: Use forest trust instead of external trust for Kerberos authentication. If external trust is required, ensure: (1) trust created using FQDN, (2) UPN suffix is DNS-resolvable (implicit UPN), (3) ports UDP 389, UDP/TCP 88, UDP/TCP 464 open to trusted domain DCs, (4) server names use FQDN matching AD DS DNS domain, (5) applications build three-part SPNs with service/server@realm syntax to provide explicit name routing. Microsoft recommends forest trusts for better Kerberos security and scalability.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Kerberos/Kerberos: Protocol Flow/Kerberos: Example Reference/Kerberos: Trusts/Kerberos: over External Trust

symptom: Kerberos Forest Search Order (KFSO) policy does not work with external trust, event ID 17 is returned when attempting cross-domain Kerberos authentication
rootCause: KFSO requires a forest trust to function. Forest search order policies are not supported with external trusts because external trusts lack the name routing infrastructure needed for cross-forest SPN resolution.
solution: Implement a forest trust instead of external trust for KFSO to work. External trusts do not support forest search order policies. Refer to KB: 'Kerberos Forest Search Order may not work in an external trust and event ID 17 is returned'.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Kerberos/Kerberos: Protocol Flow/Kerberos: Example Reference/Kerberos: Trusts/Kerberos: over External Trust

symptom: Kerberos network trace shows KRB_ERROR KDC_ERR_PREAUTH_REQUIRED (error code 25) after initial AS_REQ, causing alarm during troubleshooting
rootCause: Expected Kerberos v5 behavior. First AS_REQ is sent without pre-authentication data. KDC replies with KDC_ERR_PREAUTH_REQUIRED to request PA-ENC-TIMESTAMP. Client automatically retries with encrypted timestamp in second AS_REQ. This is a false positive error.
solution: This error is normal Kerberos behavior and not a real failure. Only investigate further if the second AS_REQ (with PA-ENC-TIMESTAMP) also fails. If pre-authentication should be disabled for specific accounts, check account property 'Do not require Kerberos preauthentication' in AD Users and Computers.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Kerberos/Kerberos: Protocol Flow/Kerberos: Example Reference/Kerberos: Single Hop No Kerberos Delegation/Kerberos: Single Hop: KRB_AS_REQ KDC_ERR_PREAUTH_REQUIRED

symptom: Slow Kerberos authentication or intermittent authentication failures to services running under a domain service account (not LocalSystem/NetworkService). Netlogon debug log shows SamLogon Generic logon Package Kerberos
rootCause: PAC (Privilege Attribute Certificate) verification requires the service host to contact a DC via Netlogon RPC to validate PAC SIDs before generating the access token. Services not running as LocalSystem/NetworkService/LocalService and without TCB privilege trigger this validation. If DC is slow/unreachable or MaxConcurrentApi limit is exhausted, authentication stalls.
solution: 1) Ensure DC connectivity from service host. 2) Tune MaxConcurrentApi (default 150 on WS2008+, 10 on WS2003). 3) Since WS2008/Vista, PAC verification is off by default. For WS2003 SP2, disable via ValidateKdcPacSignature=0 (note: does not apply to IIS). 4) Monitor using Netlogon debug logging (KB 109626) and performance counters.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Kerberos/Kerberos: Protocol Flow/Kerberos: Example Reference/Kerberos: Single Hop No Kerberos Delegation/Kerberos: Single Hop: PAC Verification

symptom: Setting ValidateKdcPacSignature=0 registry value does not disable PAC validation for IIS-hosted applications. Authentication to IIS remains slow due to PAC verification round-trips to DC.
rootCause: ValidateKdcPacSignature registry key only disables PAC validation for services running as a Windows service accepting security contexts with default credentials. IIS is a worker process, not a service, so this registry key does not apply to IIS application pools.
solution: For IIS, PAC validation cannot be disabled via ValidateKdcPacSignature. Consider running the IIS application pool identity as LocalSystem or NetworkService (which has TCB privilege and skips PAC validation). Alternatively, ensure DC connectivity is fast to minimize PAC verification latency.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Kerberos/Kerberos: Protocol Flow/Kerberos: Example Reference/Kerberos: Single Hop No Kerberos Delegation/Kerberos: Single Hop: PAC Verification

symptom: Kerberos authentication failures after installing Windows security updates: KB5020805 (11b.22, KrbtgtFullPacSignature) or KB5037754 (4b.24, PacSignatureValidationLevel) for CVE-2022-37967, CVE-2024-26248, CVE-2024-29056
rootCause: Security updates introduced new PAC signature validation mechanisms. KrbtgtFullPacSignature (since 11b.22) and PacSignatureValidationLevel (since April 2024) enforce stricter PAC validation. If not all DCs are updated before enforcement mode is enabled, Kerberos authentication breaks due to PAC signature mismatch.
solution: Follow phased deployment: 1) Install updates on ALL DCs before enabling enforcement. 2) For KrbtgtFullPacSignature, see KB5020805 guidance. 3) For PacSignatureValidationLevel, see KB5037754 guidance. 4) Monitor Kerberos event logs during transition period. 5) Ensure all DCs are at the same patch level before moving to enforcement.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Kerberos/Kerberos: Protocol Flow/Kerberos: Example Reference/Kerberos: Single Hop No Kerberos Delegation/Kerberos: Single Hop: PAC Verification

symptom: Kerberos authentication fails with access denied and Event 40970 / error 0xc00002fd across forest trust after installing January 2022 (1B.22) or later security updates
rootCause: Security updates enforce AES encryption for Kerberos cross-trust authentication. If AES is not configured on the trust (trust keys are RC4 only), authentication fails because the KDC cannot process AES-only requests from updated clients.
solution: Ensure AES encryption support is enabled on the trust. Rotate trust passwords after both sides are updated to regenerate trust keys with AES support. See internal KB for detailed remediation steps on enabling AES on existing trusts.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Kerberos/Kerberos: Protocol Flow/Kerberos: Example Reference/Kerberos: Trusts/Kerberos: Forest Trust

symptom: Kerberos authentication fails with KDC_ERR_POLICY (error 12) when accessing resources across forest trust with selective authentication. NTLM fallback returns 0xC0000413 STATUS_AUTHENTICATION_FIREWALL_FAILED. Application shows generic Access denied.
rootCause: Selective authentication on forest trust requires explicit Allowed to Authenticate permission on the trusting server/service account objects for trusted accounts. Without this permission, KDC rejects TGS requests with KDC_ERR_POLICY (Kerberos) or Netlogon returns 0xC0000413 (NTLM).
solution: Grant Allowed to Authenticate permission on the target computer/service account objects in the trusting domain for the trusted domain accounts that need access. This can be done via AD Users and Computers > target object Properties > Security tab.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Kerberos/Kerberos: Protocol Flow/Kerberos: Example Reference/Kerberos: Trusts/Kerberos: Forest Trust

symptom: Kerberos Constrained Delegation (KCD) fails across forest trust when KERB_S4U_LOGON struct uses NetBios domain name as ClientRealm for a TLN subordinate domain. S4U2Self or S4U2Proxy cannot locate the user realm.
rootCause: NetBios names of TLN subordinate domains are not populated in trusted forest name suffix routing information by default. Only DNS-based Top Level Names (TLNs) are routed, so KCD using NetBios realm names cannot resolve the target domain across the forest trust.
solution: 1) In the trusted forest root domain, add the NetBios domain names to attribute msDS-SPNSuffixes at DN CN=Partitions,CN=Configuration,DC=DomainNamingContext. 2) Restart NETLOGON for immediate update. 3) In the trusting forest, update trust info via Domain.msc (tab General > Validate or restart Netlogon), then in tab Name Suffix Routing press Refresh and Enable the new NetBios names.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Kerberos/Kerberos: Protocol Flow/Kerberos: Example Reference/Kerberos: Trusts/Kerberos: Forest Trust

symptom: Windows 11 22H2 breaks Kerberos authentication interoperability with legacy MIT Kerberos realms. Users cannot authenticate to MIT KDC-based services from Windows 11 22H2 clients.
rootCause: Windows 11 22H2 introduced changes to Kerberos protocol handling that are incompatible with legacy MIT Kerberos implementations, particularly around encryption type negotiation and protocol extensions.
solution: Refer to the known issue article for Win11 22H2 MIT Kerberos interop breakage for specific workarounds. Consider upgrading the MIT Kerberos implementation to a version that supports TCP and current encryption types. Check /etc/krb5.conf for proper realm and encryption type configuration.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Kerberos/Kerberos: Protocol Flow/Kerberos: Example Reference/Kerberos: Trusts/Kerberos: MIT Kerberos Interoperability

symptom: Kerberos authentication fails for services using S4u2self protocol transition (KCD) after installing November 2021 (11B.21) updates on domain controllers (W2K8 through WS2019). Affected: Azure AD Application Proxy IWA/KCD SSO, WAP IWA SSO, ADFS, CEP/CES Web Enrollment Services, IIS IWA, SQL Server, Remote EFS.
rootCause: 11B.21 PAC hardening adds new PAC Requestor buffer and PAC Attributes to TGTs. Kerberos tickets acquired via S4u2self before the patch lack the new PAC attributes, causing signature validation failure when used as evidence tickets for protocol transition to delegate to backend services.
solution: Install hotfix KB5008611 on affected domain controllers. For CEP/CES failures (error 0x303d0013), see KB5008693. For Remote EFS encryption failures, see KB5008695. Ensure all DCs in the environment are patched to avoid inconsistent PAC validation behavior.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Kerberos/Kerberos: Security Enhancements & Changes/Kerberos: 11B.21 - Kerberos PAC hardening changes

symptom: Cluster CNO/VCO password change operations fail after installing 11B.21 PAC hardening updates on domain controllers. ICM 283154839.
rootCause: 11B.21 Kerberos PAC hardening changes break certain password change operations for cluster Computer Name Objects (CNO) and Virtual Computer Objects (VCO) due to new PAC validation requirements during password change flows.
solution: Apply the fix referenced in the internal KB article for ICM 283154839. See: Servicing: 11B.21: PAC hardening changes affect certain password change operations such as cluster CNO/VCO password changes.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Kerberos/Kerberos: Security Enhancements & Changes/Kerberos: 11B.21 - Kerberos PAC hardening changes

symptom: Kerberos authentication fails between domain controllers after October 2022 enforcement update when some DCs have not installed November 2021 (11B.21) update, or have PacRequestorEnforcement registry key set to 0.
rootCause: October 2022 update transitions all DCs into Enforcement phase, deprecating PacRequestorEnforcement registry key. DCs without the November 2021 update or with PacRequestorEnforcement=0 become incompatible, as enforcement requires all DCs to support new PAC attributes.
solution: Ensure all domain controllers have at least the November 2021 update installed AND the July 2022 update. Remove PacRequestorEnforcement=0 setting if present. Compatible states: DCs with Oct 2022+ update, or DCs with Nov 2021+ and Jul 2022+ updates with PacRequestorEnforcement=1 or 2.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Kerberos/Kerberos: Security Enhancements & Changes/Kerberos: 11B.21 - Kerberos PAC hardening changes

symptom: Authentication outage for Exchange, TMG, and other services after resetting KRBTGT account password twice within a short interval (less than 10-12 hours). Server services fail to authenticate, users see Kerberos authentication errors.
rootCause: Resetting KRBTGT password twice too quickly causes TGT validation failures because the new password hash has not replicated to all domain controllers. The KRBTGT password history stores only 2 entries, and a double reset before full replication clears all valid password hashes from some DCs perspective.
solution: Wait at least 10-12 hours between two KRBTGT password resets to allow full replication. If outage has occurred: purge Local System Kerberos cache with 'klist -li 0x3e7 purge', or restart affected services. For all logon sessions use PowerShell: Get-WmiObject Win32_LogonSession | Where-Object {$_.AuthenticationPackage -ne 'NTLM'} | ForEach-Object {klist.exe purge -li ([Convert]::ToString($_.LogonId, 16))}.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Kerberos/Kerberos: References/Kerberos: KrbTgt - Reset

symptom: Authentication errors (KDC_ERR_ETYPE_NOTSUPP) on domain controller after raising Domain Functional Level (DFL) from Windows Server 2003 to 2008 or higher. KRBTGT password automatically changes during DFL raise and DCs may fail to authenticate.
rootCause: Raising DFL from 2003 triggers automatic KRBTGT password change. DCs that have replicated the DFL change but have not refreshed the new krbtgt password in memory will fail with encryption type mismatch. Post-11B.22, KDCs assume AES support without DFL 2008 check since Server 2003 is EOL, and will generate audit events if AES keys are missing.
solution: Restart KDC service on the affected domain controller to trigger in-memory refresh of the new krbtgt password. Before raising DFL: review Exchange team guidance. Ensure KRBTGT password has been rotated to generate AES keys. Check for legacy devices that do not support AES signing.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Kerberos/Kerberos: Security Enhancements & Changes/Kerberos: 11B.22 - KrbtgtFullPacSignature DefaultDomainSupportedEncTypes/Tips from Kerberos and Netlogon/Domain Functional level at 2003

symptom: LDAP over SSL (LDAPS) connection fails with Schannel event 36884 on the domain controller
rootCause: An entry in the hosts file causes the SSL/TLS certificate validation to fail during the LDAPS handshake
solution: Check the hosts file on the DC for incorrect entries that may interfere with certificate validation. Remove or correct the problematic hosts file entry. Ref: KB 4299022
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/LDAP/LDAP Client/LDAP Client: Troubleshooting

symptom: LDAPS connection intermittently fails between LDAP client and domain controller
rootCause: TLS/SSL certificate or negotiation issue causes intermittent LDAPS connectivity failures between client and DC
solution: Review DC certificate chain, TLS version compatibility, and network stability. Check for certificate renewal timing issues. Ref: KB 5008929
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/LDAP/LDAP Client/LDAP Client: Troubleshooting

symptom: LDAP simple bind fails with error 80090308 immediately after raising Domain Functional Level (DFL) to Windows Server 2012 R2
rootCause: DFL upgrade to 2012R2 changes authentication requirements; simple bind over non-SSL connections may be blocked by new security policies
solution: Enable LDAPS or use SASL bind instead of simple bind. Alternatively, review and adjust LDAP authentication policies after DFL upgrade. Ref: KB 5022801
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/LDAP/LDAP Client/LDAP Client: Troubleshooting

symptom: Third-party LDAP client (e.g., Linux/Java app) fails LDAPS connection when using domain FQDN instead of DC hostname; certificate SAN only contains DC name
rootCause: The domain controller certificate Subject Alternative Name (SAN) contains only the DC FQDN, not the domain FQDN. Third-party clients validate hostname against SAN and reject the cert
solution: Either configure the client to connect using the DC hostname instead of domain FQDN, or issue a new certificate that includes the domain FQDN in the SAN field
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/LDAP/LDAP Client/LDAP Client: Troubleshooting

symptom: LDAP client receives error code 0x65 (timeout) when connecting to Active Directory domain controller
rootCause: TCP-level connectivity issue prevents the LDAP client from reaching the server within the timeout period; common causes include firewall rules, network latency, or port blocking (389/636/3268/3269)
solution: Check network connectivity between client and DC: verify firewall rules allow LDAP ports (TCP/UDP 389, TCP 636, TCP 3268/3269), test with telnet/portqry, capture network trace to identify where connection drops
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/LDAP/LDAP Client/LDAP Client: Troubleshooting

symptom: LDAP Bind fails with Error 82 (0x52) - Local Error
rootCause: Client-side LDAP library encounters a local error during bind, often due to SASL/authentication mechanism mismatch or missing Kerberos ticket
solution: Verify the client authentication mechanism matches what the DC supports. Check Kerberos ticket availability (klist). If using SASL, ensure matching encryption algorithms. Ref: KB 4131706
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/LDAP/LDAP Client/LDAP Client: Troubleshooting

symptom: LDAPS connection fails when the domain controller certificate is issued from a Certificate Authority (CA) in a different forest
rootCause: Cross-forest CA trust is not automatically established; the LDAP client trust store does not contain the root CA certificate from the other forest
solution: Import the root CA certificate from the other forest into the client trusted root certificate store. Ensure the full certificate chain is available. Ref: KB 4316681
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/LDAP/LDAP Client/LDAP Client: Troubleshooting

symptom: Java-based application fails LDAP operations due to LDAP referrals being returned by Active Directory
rootCause: AD returns LDAP referrals for cross-domain or cross-partition queries; Java LDAP client (JNDI) follows referrals by default and may fail if it cannot resolve or authenticate to the referred server
solution: Configure the Java application to ignore LDAP referrals (set Context.REFERRAL to 'ignore') or ensure the application can authenticate to all referred DCs. Use specific DC targeting instead of domain-level queries. Ref: KB 4078361
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/LDAP/LDAP Client/LDAP Client: Troubleshooting

symptom: LDAP DirSync synchronization client never completes when using a linked value attribute (e.g., member) in the LDAP search filter
rootCause: DirSync control has a known limitation where linked value attributes in the search filter cause the sync operation to hang or never return results
solution: Remove linked value attributes from the DirSync search filter. Use a post-filter approach: retrieve all objects via DirSync without the linked attribute filter, then filter client-side. Ref: KB 4294771
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/LDAP/LDAP Client/LDAP Client: Troubleshooting

symptom: LDAP search for memberOf attribute incorrectly terminates with DIR_ERROR on Windows Server 2016 or later domain controllers
rootCause: Bug in Windows Server 2016+ LDAP implementation causes incorrect DIR_ERROR when querying memberOf attribute under certain conditions
solution: Apply the latest cumulative update for Windows Server. Check for KB 4494478 hotfix. As workaround, use alternative query approach (e.g., query group membership from the group object instead)
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/LDAP/LDAP Client/LDAP Client: Troubleshooting

symptom: LDAP Undelete operation or adding/removing objectclass inetOrgPerson fails with error 0x207d ERROR_DS_ATT_NOT_DEF_FOR_CLASS
rootCause: The inetOrgPerson objectclass has attribute constraints that conflict with the undelete or class change operation in Active Directory
solution: Verify the target object schema compatibility. For undelete, ensure all required attributes are present for the restored object class. Ref: KB 4494710
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/LDAP/LDAP Client/LDAP Client: Troubleshooting

symptom: Third-party LDAP client fails to connect to AD because it does not support any compatible authentication method (no Kerberos, no NTLM, no SASL encryption match)
rootCause: Authentication protocol mismatch between client and AD: client may not support Kerberos ticket acquisition, NTLM, or SASL encryption algorithms required by the DC
solution: Verify client capabilities: check if it supports Kerberos (kinit), NTLM, or LDAPS with simple bind. Configure the client to use a supported auth method. If using simple bind, ensure LDAPS is configured (simple bind over non-SSL may be disabled)
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/LDAP/LDAP Client/LDAP Client: Troubleshooting

symptom: Kerberos authentication fails after configuring a load balancer in front of Active Directory domain controllers for LDAP traffic
rootCause: Load balancers are not supported for Active Directory domain controllers. Kerberos relies on server-specific SPNs and tickets tied to individual DCs; load balancing breaks this relationship
solution: Remove the load balancer from the DC LDAP path. Use the built-in DNS-based DC locator mechanism for client-side failover instead of hardware load balancing
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/LDAP/LDAP General/LDAP : FAQ

symptom: LSASS process crashes with exception code 0xc00000fd (STATUS_STACK_OVERFLOW) in ntdsai.dll on domain controllers processing LDAP queries
rootCause: Excessively large or deeply nested LDAP filters cause stack overflow in the NTDS Active Directory LDAP processing code path
solution: Identify the client sending the problematic LDAP query using LDAP server diagnostics logging (Event 1644). Fix or simplify the LDAP filter. Apply latest Windows Server cumulative updates
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/LDAP/LDAP Server/LDAP Server: Looking for known solutions

symptom: High LSASS memory utilization on domain controller, memory grows continuously during LDAP operations
rootCause: LDAP queries that visit a large number of database records cause the dbevalint code path to allocate excessive arena heap memory that is not promptly released
solution: Identify expensive LDAP queries using Event 1644 (enable Field Engineering diagnostics). Optimize queries to reduce result set size, use paging, or add indexes for frequently queried attributes
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/LDAP/LDAP Server/LDAP Server: Looking for known solutions

symptom: Kerberos Event ID 38 logged on domain controller - SID of user/computer that originally requested TGT differs from SID of user/computer using TGT to get a service ticket, authentication is denied
rootCause: Account was re-created while TGT was still alive for the original account (SID mismatch between old TGT and new account SID), or network attack exploiting CVE associated with PAC signature enforcement
solution: Reboot affected machine, run 'klist purge' to clear Kerberos ticket cache, or wait for TGT to expire. New TGT will have correct SID after account recreation.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Kerberos/Kerberos: Security Enhancements & Changes/Kerberos: 11B.22 - KrbtgtFullPacSignature DefaultDomainSupportedEncTypes/Tips from Kerberos and Netlogon/Event ID 38

symptom: Security Audit Event 4768 displays incorrect value in Ticket Encryption Type field - shows session key encryption type instead of actual ticket encryption type
rootCause: Known Windows bug in event 4768 logging - the Ticket Encryption Type field incorrectly displays the session key encryption type value instead of the ticket encryption type
solution: This is a known logging/display issue only. Actual Kerberos encryption type negotiation is correct. Do not rely on this field for encryption type auditing.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Kerberos/Kerberos: Security Enhancements & Changes/Kerberos: 11B.22 - KrbtgtFullPacSignature DefaultDomainSupportedEncTypes/Tips from Kerberos and Netlogon/Id 4768 event shows incorrect values

symptom: Event 4768 shows chain of Asian symbols/garbled characters in Account Name field when x509 certificate pre-authentication fails (e.g., password expired)
rootCause: When AS_REQUEST using x509 certificate fails, the KDC logs the raw binary KeyID as Account Name without conversion, resulting in binary data interpreted as Unicode string
solution: This is a known display issue in the event log. Use error/result codes rather than Account Name field to identify failed x509 authentication events.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Kerberos/Kerberos: Security Enhancements & Changes/Kerberos: 11B.22 - KrbtgtFullPacSignature DefaultDomainSupportedEncTypes/Tips from Kerberos and Netlogon/Id 4768 event shows incorrect values

symptom: KDC warning 35 logged on domain controllers - TGT from login.microsoftonline.com does not contain PAC_ATTRIBUTES_INFO, even though all DCs are fully patched
rootCause: Azure AD adds PAC Requestor to Kerberos tickets but does not add PAC_ATTRIBUTES_INFO. Internal feature request exists but not yet implemented by Azure AD engineering.
solution: This warning is expected behavior and can be safely acknowledged. No customer action required. Azure AD cloud-issued Kerberos tickets are missing PAC Attributes by design pending engineering update.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Kerberos/Kerberos: Security Enhancements & Changes/Kerberos: 11B.22 - KrbtgtFullPacSignature DefaultDomainSupportedEncTypes/Tips from Kerberos and Netlogon/KDC warning 35 - PAC without attributes from login.microsoftonline.com

symptom: Kerberos authentication failures for legacy applications after installing November 2022 (11B.22) updates - apps relying on RC4 session keys can no longer authenticate
rootCause: RC4 session key usage deprecated and moved to unsupported in 11B.22. RC4 ticket encryption still supported, but session keys now require AES. Platforms not supporting AES (pre-Windows Server 2008) fail.
solution: 1) RC4 ticket encryption still works if msds-SupportedEncryptionTypes configured correctly. 2) For legacy apps unable to use AES, contact stillneedrc4@microsoft.com. 3) Long-term: upgrade legacy applications to support AES.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Kerberos/Kerberos: Security Enhancements & Changes/Kerberos: 11B.22 - KrbtgtFullPacSignature DefaultDomainSupportedEncTypes/Tips from Kerberos and Netlogon/RC4 depreciation & Impact for CVE-2022-37967

symptom: Unexpected Kerberos encryption behavior when msds-SupportedEncryptionTypes is 0x0 vs not set (NULL) - confusion about default encryption type selection after November 2022 updates
rootCause: When msds-SupportedEncryptionTypes is NULL or 0, DC uses default 0x27 (39) or DefaultDomainSupportedEncTypes registry. After Nov 2022, NULL/0 defaults to RC4_HMAC_MD5 ticket with AES256_CTS_HMAC_SHA1_96 session keys.
solution: Explicitly set msds-SupportedEncryptionTypes to desired value. Review KB5021131 for CVE-2022-37966 guidance. Both 0x0 and NULL have same behavior but changed after November 2022 updates.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Kerberos/Kerberos: Security Enhancements & Changes/Kerberos: 11B.22 - KrbtgtFullPacSignature DefaultDomainSupportedEncTypes/Tips from Kerberos and Netlogon/msds-SupportedEncryptionTypes

symptom: Kerberos authentication failures on domain controllers after mid-2026 update - RC4 disabled by default on KDC role for Windows Server 2008+, services relying on RC4 fail
rootCause: CVE-2026-20833 changes DC KDC defaults to only allow AES-SHA1. RC4 disabled by default via RC4DefaultDisablementPhase registry key. Legacy RC4-dependent authentication breaks unless explicitly configured.
solution: 1) Before update: detect RC4 usage via 'Beyond RC4 for Windows authentication' blog. 2) Migrate service accounts/apps to AES-SHA1. 3) If needed, explicitly configure RC4 via RC4DefaultDisablementPhase registry. Refer to KB for CVE-2026-20833.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Kerberos/Kerberos: Security Enhancements & Changes/Kerberos: 1B.26 RC4 deprecation on DC role CVE-2026-20833

symptom: Kerberos certificate-based authentication fails after installing April 2025 (4B.25) update - DCs reject certificates not chained to an issuing CA in the NTAuth store
rootCause: CVE-2025-26647 protections require DCs to verify certificates for Kerberos auth MUST chain to an issuing CA in the NTAuth store. Certificates from CAs not in NTAuth store are rejected.
solution: 1) Add required CA certificates to NTAuth store. 2) Audit Mode by default in April 2025 - watch events. 3) July 2025 enforced by default (revert via registry setting 1). 4) October 2025 full enforcement, no registry override. Refer to KB KISP-103787.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Kerberos/Kerberos: Security Enhancements & Changes/Kerberos: 4B.25 Protections for CVE-2025-26647

symptom: Windows Server 2016+ cluster fails to come online with error 'Bind to domain controller. More data is available' during CNO/VCO creation, especially after migrating to third-party DNS
rootCause: Server 2016 LDAP code scans all active application partitions for LDAP _srv records. Third-party DNS missing LDAP SRV records for application partitions (e.g., DomainDnsZones) causes cluster LDAP bind failure via referral.
solution: Test: 'nltest /dsgetdc:partitionname.contoso.com /LDAPONLY /DNS'. Fixes: 1) Create _LDAP SRV records in third-party DNS (static). 2) Create DNS delegation pointing to DCs (dynamic). 3) Disable unused partition. 4) Delete partition (cleanest but hard to restore).
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/LDAP/LDAP Client/LDAP Client: Cluster fails to come Online - "Error Bind to domain controller. More data is available."

symptom: Certificate enrollment fails with Event ID 13 'The RPC server is unavailable (0x800706ba)' when requesting certificates via MMC console or autoenrollment
rootCause: Lack of network connectivity to the CA server, or DCOM permissions not properly configured on the CA. The client cannot reach the CA via RPC.
solution: 1) Capture network trace to verify connectivity to CA. 2) Restart CA service (net stop certsvc && net start certsvc). 3) Check DCOM permissions per KB5004442. 4) Verify firewall rules allow RPC traffic to CA.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/PKI - AD Certificate Services/Workflow: PKI Client/Workflow: PKI Client: Certificate Enrollment

symptom: Certificate enrollment denied: 'The EMail name is unavailable and cannot be added to the Subject or Subject Alternate name. 0x80094812 CERTSRV_E_SUBJECT_EMAIL_REQUIRED'
rootCause: User requesting the certificate does not have an email address configured in AD, but the certificate template requires email name in Subject or Subject Alternative Name.
solution: Either configure the user with an email address in Active Directory, or modify the certificate template to remove the 'include e-mail name' requirement from Subject name and/or Subject Alternative Name settings.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/PKI - AD Certificate Services/Workflow: PKI Client/Workflow: PKI Client: Certificate Enrollment

symptom: Certificate enrollment fails with 'The revocation function was unable to check revocation because the revocation server was offline. 0x80092013 CRYPT_E_REVOCATION_OFFLINE'
rootCause: The Issuing CA certificate failed revocation check on the CA itself. CRL Distribution Point (CDP) or OCSP paths are unavailable or contain expired CRLs.
solution: 1) Enable CAPI2 logging on the CA. 2) Identify which CDP/OCSP paths fail from CAPI2 logs. 3) Make CDP/OCSP paths available with latest CRL files. 4) Restart CA service after fixing CRL availability.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/PKI - AD Certificate Services/Workflow: PKI Client/Workflow: PKI Client: Certificate Enrollment

symptom: Certificate enrollment denied: 'Configuration information could not be read from the domain controller, either because the machine is unavailable, or access has been denied. 0x80070547'
rootCause: User trying to enroll is in a different domain/forest from the CA, and LDAP referrals are not enabled on the CA.
solution: Enable LDAP referrals on the CA: certutil -SetReg Policy\EditFlags +EDITF_EnableLDAPReferrals. See AD CS cross-forest certificate enrollment documentation for full configuration requirements.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/PKI - AD Certificate Services/Workflow: PKI Client/Workflow: PKI Client: Certificate Enrollment

symptom: Smartcard logon fails - KDC Event ID 19 or Event ID 29 on domain controller indicating KDC cannot find a valid certificate for smartcard logon
rootCause: KDC certificate has expired, been superseded by another not valid for smartcard logon, or the CA certificate that issued the KDC/client smartcard certificate is not in the NTAuth store in AD.
solution: 1) Verify KDC cert: certutil -verify -urlfetch KDC_CERT.cer. 2) Check NTAuth store: certutil -store -enterprise NTAuth. 3) Ensure KDC cert has OID 1.3.6.1.5.2.3.5 (KDC Auth) or 1.3.6.1.4.1.311.20.2.2 (SmartcardLogon). 4) Renew expired KDC certificate. 5) Add issuing CA cert to NTAuth if missing.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/PKI - AD Certificate Services/Workflow: PKI Client/Workflow: PKI Client: Smartcard logon

symptom: Smartcard logon fails with 'Signing in with a smart card isnt supported for your account' after domain controller reboot
rootCause: Third-party certificate validation software (Tumbleweed/AXWAY Desktop Validator) service does not start before the KDC service after DC reboot, causing a race condition where KDC cannot validate smartcard certificates.
solution: Configure the third-party certificate validation service to start before the KDC service, or restart the KDC service after the validation service has fully started.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/PKI - AD Certificate Services/Workflow: PKI Client/Workflow: PKI Client: Smartcard logon

symptom: CA service fails to start with Event ID 48 'Revocation status for CA certificate could not be verified' and Event ID 7024 error 0x80092013
rootCause: During startup the CA service verifies its own certificate chain including revocation. If a CRL from a higher-level CA is expired or unavailable, the CA service refuses to start.
solution: Temporary workaround: certutil -setreg ca\CRLFlags +CRLF_REVCHECK_IGNORE_OFFLINE then restart CA. Fix root cause: publish fresh CRLs from parent CAs. Use CAPI2 logging to identify failing CDP. Re-enable: certutil -setreg ca\CRLFlags -CRLF_REVCHECK_IGNORE_OFFLINE.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/PKI - AD Certificate Services/Workflow: PKI Server/Workflow: PKI Server: Certification Authority

symptom: CA service fails to start with 'Keyset does not exist 0x80090016 NTE_BAD_KEYSET'
rootCause: The CA private keys are missing or inaccessible. The link between the CA certificate and its corresponding private key may be broken, or the key container file is missing.
solution: 1) Run certutil -v -store my and certutil -v -verifykeys to confirm. 2) For software keys, locate and restore key container files from C:\ProgramData\Microsoft\Crypto\Keys. 3) Use certutil -repairstore to re-link cert and key. 4) For HSM keys, engage HSM vendor. 5) If old CA cert key is missing, replace its thumbprint with '-' in CACertHash registry.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/PKI - AD Certificate Services/Workflow: PKI Server/Workflow: PKI Server: Certification Authority

symptom: ADCS cluster CA fails to start with Event ID 17 CertificationAuthority
rootCause: The ADCS database is marked for restore operations. A RestoreInProgress registry key exists at HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Services\CertSvc\Configuration that prevents the CA from starting.
solution: 1) Verify RestoreInProgress key exists in HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Services\CertSvc\Configuration. 2) Note the cluster node owning the ADCS resource. 3) Remove the RestoreInProgress registry key on that node. 4) Restart the cluster ADCS resource.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/PKI - AD Certificate Services/Workflow: PKI Server/Workflow: PKI Server: Certification Authority

symptom: NTLM relay attack vulnerability on ADCS - attacker can trick AD into providing NTLM credentials and self-elevate to Domain/Enterprise Admins via ADCS Web Enrollment or CES/CEP endpoints
rootCause: ADCS web enrollment and CES/CEP endpoints accept NTLM authentication without Extended Protection for Authentication (EPA), enabling NTLM relay attacks in environments where NTLM is enabled.
solution: Mitigate per KB5005413: 1) Preferred: Disable NTLM on domain controllers via GPO. 2) Alternative: Disable NTLM on ADCS servers. 3) Enable Extended Protection for Authentication on ADCS IIS endpoints. 4) Disable NTLM on specific IIS application pools.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/PKI - AD Certificate Services/Workflow: PKI Server/Workflow: PKI Server: NTLM ADCS RELAY ATTACK INFORMATION

symptom: NTLM authentication fails across a forest/domain trust with Event ID 4625 Status 0xC000005E. User cannot access file share on trusting domain server. Netlogon log shows NlpUserValidateHigher returning 0xC000005E, secure channel setup failure to trusted DC.
rootCause: Name resolution failure prevents the trusting domain DC from locating the trusted domain DC. Netlogon cannot establish a secure channel because it cannot resolve the trusted domain controller name (DNS or WINS resolution broken for the trust).
solution: 1) Check DNS/WINS name resolution from trusting DC to trusted domain DC. 2) Verify trust with nltest /sc_verify:<trustedDomain>. 3) Fix DNS records (conditional forwarders, stub zones, WINS). 4) Enable Netlogon debug logging (Nltest /DBFlag:2080FFFF). 5) Verify with nltest /sc_reset.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/NTLM/Workflow: NTLM: Troubleshooting/Workflow: NTLM: Failed authentication request accross a Trust

symptom: Intermittent NTLM auth failures and credential prompts under load. Netlogon log shows NlAllocateClientApi timed out and Cant allocate Client API slot with SamLogon returning 0xC000005E. Semaphore timeouts in Netlogon perf counters.
rootCause: MaxConcurrentApi value too low for NTLM auth load. Concurrent NTLM/Digest or Kerberos PAC validation requests exceed available Netlogon semaphore threads per secure channel causing 45-second queue timeouts.
solution: 1) Enable Netlogon debug logging and search for NlAllocateClientApi timeouts. 2) Monitor Netlogon perf counters (semaphore holders/waiters/timeouts). 3) Increase MaxConcurrentApi at HKLM SYSTEM CurrentControlSet Services Netlogon Parameters (default 1 workstations, 10 servers; max 150 on 2008R2 SP1+, 9999 on 2012+). 4) Test in non-prod first.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/NTLM/Workflow: NTLM: Troubleshooting/Workflow: NTLM: MaxConcurrentAPI for NTLM Authentication

symptom: Root certificates published in AD not deployed on client machines. Certificates missing from Trusted Root CA store despite being correctly published in AD. gpupdate /force and certutil -pulse do not restore them.
rootCause: Certificate auto-enrollment not enabled via GPO AND certificates were previously manually deleted from local store. Once deleted autoenrollment will not re-download until USN change detected in AD tracked in AEDirectoryCache registry key.
solution: 1) Enable autoenrollment GPO in Computer Config > Public Key Policies > Auto-Enrollment > Enabled with both checkboxes. 2) Export then delete AEDirectoryCache registry key to force re-download. 3) Run gpupdate /force. 4) Ensure Network List Services and Network Location Awareness running.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/PKI - AD Certificate Services/Step by Step Guides/A "classical" autoenrollment problem

symptom: Warning in Microsoft Office apps: digital signature is valid but from publisher not yet chosen to trust, when opening Office with COM add-ins enabled.
rootCause: Office Trust Center Require Application Add-ins to be signed by Trusted Publisher is enabled but the signing certificate of the COM add-in not in Trusted Publishers certificate store.
solution: 1) Select Trust all code from this publisher in warning dialog. 2) Or manually export add-in signing cert and import into Trusted Publishers store via certmgr.msc.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/PKI - AD Certificate Services/Step by Step Guides/Trusted Publishers Certificate warning in Office Apps

symptom: NTFS 'Access denied' error when user tries to delete files or folders. User can access parent folder but not delete child items. Administrator can delete successfully.
rootCause: User account lacks the necessary ACL (Access Control List) permission for delete operation on the target file/folder. The ACEs may have deny entries before allow entries, or the user's group memberships don't include any group with delete permission.
solution: Take ownership of the file (administrators have implicit ability), then modify ACL to grant delete permissions. Check for deny ACEs that override allow ACEs. Verify user group memberships with 'whoami /all' and compare against NTFS permissions via 'get-acl' or icacls.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Permissions, access control, and auditing/Workflow: NTFS Permissions/Workflow: NTFS Permissions: Scenarios (samples)/Scenario 2: "Unable to Delete files | folders"

symptom: Cannot delete file or folder, 'Access denied' error. File is currently open or locked by another process.
rootCause: Another process holds an open handle to the file (exclusive access or shared lock), preventing deletion. The file may be opened by a service, application, or background process.
solution: Use Process Monitor (procmon) trace or handle.exe to identify the process holding the open handle. Close the offending process or wait for it to release the handle. Check if the file is opened for exclusive access vs shared access.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Permissions, access control, and auditing/Workflow: NTFS Permissions/Workflow: NTFS Permissions: Scenarios (samples)/Scenario 2: "Unable to Delete files | folders"

symptom: Cannot open, edit, or delete files because file path exceeds MAX_PATH (260 characters). Error occurs with deeply nested folder structures.
rootCause: File path length exceeds the Windows MAX_PATH limit of 260 characters due to deeply nested folders or long folder/file names. NTFS supports paths up to ~32,767 characters but Win32 API has 260-char limit.
solution: Resolution 1: Use auto-generated 8.3 short names to access the file. Resolution 2: Rename deep folders to shorter names starting from root. Resolution 3: Map a drive letter to a deep folder using 'subst' command to shorten the virtual path. Check if 8.3 name creation is disabled on the volume (KB 121007).
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Permissions, access control, and auditing/Workflow: NTFS Permissions/Workflow: NTFS Permissions: Scenarios (samples)/Scenario 2: "Unable to Delete files | folders"

symptom: ACL Editor displays a warning about non-canonicalized ACE ordering when viewing security descriptor of a file or folder.
rootCause: ACEs (Access Control Entries) in the ACL are in non-canonical order. The preferred sequence has deny ACEs before allow ACEs, but a program wrote ACEs in arbitrary order. Nothing prevents programs from writing ACLs with arbitrary ACE sequences.
solution: Reorder ACEs to canonical order (deny before allow) using the ACL Editor or programmatically. Alternatively, reset permissions to defaults: 'cd /d <drive>: && icacls * /T /Q /C /RESET'. Reference: https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/windows/win32/secauthz/order-of-aces-in-a-dacl
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Permissions, access control, and auditing/Workflow: NTFS Permissions/Workflow: NTFS Permissions: FAQ

symptom: After clicking 'Continue' button to get access to a folder in Explorer, the user account is unexpectedly added directly to the folder's ACL.
rootCause: Explorer.exe always runs with a restricted token when UAC is enabled. The non-elevated admin user is added directly into the ACLs to grant access in elevated mode. This is by-design behavior in Windows.
solution: This is by design. When UAC is enabled, Explorer runs with restricted token. To access protected folders, Explorer adds the user directly to ACLs. No fix needed. Reference: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-US/troubleshoot/windows-server/windows-security/dont-have-permission-access-folder
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Permissions, access control, and auditing/Workflow: NTFS Permissions/Workflow: NTFS Permissions: FAQ

symptom: Robocopy fails to copy files when path size is greater than 256 characters (KB 2839617). Or Robocopy unable to copy NTFS permissions to NAS volume (KB 2026146).
rootCause: Path length exceeds 256 characters causing Robocopy failure. For NAS volumes, the target file system may not support NTFS ACL semantics, preventing permission copy with /SEC or /COPYALL flags.
solution: For long paths: shorten folder names or use 'subst' to map a drive to reduce path length. For NAS: verify NAS supports NTFS ACLs. Use /COPY:DAT instead of /SEC or /COPYALL if ACL copy is not needed. See KB 2839617 and KB 2026146.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Permissions, access control, and auditing/Workflow: NTFS Permissions/Workflow: NTFS Permissions: Known Issues

symptom: NTFS permissions not reflecting correctly on mount points when viewed via Explorer and Disk Management (diskmgmt.msc). SIDs may resolve to incorrect friendly names in the NTFS permissions tab (KB 2632907).
rootCause: Mount point permission display is a known UI issue in Explorer and Disk Management (KB 4504930). SID-to-name resolution may return incorrect names due to cache or cross-domain name conflicts (KB 2632907).
solution: For mount point display: use command-line tools (icacls, Get-Acl) to verify actual permissions instead of relying on Explorer UI. For SID resolution: clear SID-to-name cache, verify cross-domain trust. See KB 4504930 and KB 2632907.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Permissions, access control, and auditing/Workflow: NTFS Permissions/Workflow: NTFS Permissions: Known Issues

symptom: Files moved using tools other than Explorer.exe (e.g. robocopy, xcopy, scripts) do not inherit or apply the target folder's NTFS permissions after the move (KB 4487863). Permissions from the source folder persist after move (KB 2560088).
rootCause: Non-Explorer file move operations may not trigger NTFS permission inheritance. When files are moved within the same volume, NTFS performs a metadata-only move preserving original permissions. Cross-volume moves via non-Explorer tools may skip inheritance application.
solution: Use Explorer.exe for moves that require permission inheritance. After moving with other tools, manually reset inheritance: 'icacls <path> /reset /T /C'. Or use 'icacls <path> /inheritance:e' to force inheritance. See KB 4487863 and KB 2560088.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Permissions, access control, and auditing/Workflow: NTFS Permissions/Workflow: NTFS Permissions: Known Issues
Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Secure Channel/Workflow: Secure Channel: Looking for Known Solutions/Workflow: Secure Channel: Broken SC - VDI Desktops
symptom: Non-persistent VDI desktops (golden image) report 'The trust relationship between this workstation and the primary domain failed' after approximately 32 days of operation
rootCause: Non-persistent VDI reverts to golden image on logoff, losing machine account password changes. After 30 days the computer changes its password, but revert discards it. By day 32, neither current nor old LSA Secret password matches AD unicodepwd or lmpwdHistory, breaking the secure channel.
solution: Set 'Domain member: Maximum machine account password age' GPO to 120 days (ensure golden image refresh cycle is shorter). When refreshing the image, reset password using Reset-ComputerMachinePassword or rejoin VDI desktops to the domain.

sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Secure Channel/Workflow: Secure Channel: Looking for Known Solutions/Workflow: Secure Channel: Broken SC - VDI Desktops
symptom: Citrix PVS non-persistent VDI machines lose trust relationship with domain after reboot once machine account password age threshold is reached. Netlogon.log confirms password changed successfully but old password is restored after reboot.
rootCause: The Workstation (LanmanWorkstation) service lacks dependency on PvsVMAgent service. When Netlogon changes the machine password, PvsVMAgent is not notified of the registry change and does not update its ini file. On next VDA reboot, old password values are written back to the registry.
solution: Add PvsVMAgent as dependency of LanmanWorkstation in the base image: 'sc config LanmanWorkstation depend= Bowser/MRxSmb20/NSI/pvsVMAgent'. Also add 'PvsVmAgent' to DependOnService REG_MULTI_SZ at HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\LanmanWorkstation. Citrix articles CTX249833 and CTX251326 provide additional details.

sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Secure Channel/Workflow: Secure Channel: Looking for Known Solutions/Workflow: Secure Channel: Restored machine
symptom: 'The trust relationship between this workstation and the primary domain failed' on a VM or physical machine. GetSecret_CupdTime script shows the computer's secret (LSA Secret) is older than the AD computer object's pwdLastSet date.
rootCause: The machine was reverted from an old VM snapshot, deliberately restored from a bare metal backup, or System Restore was used. The restored machine has an older machine account password in HKLM\SECURITY\Policy\Secrets\$machine.ACC that no longer matches the current AD unicodepwd.
solution: Reset the computer machine password using Reset-ComputerMachinePassword or 'netdom resetpwd', or rejoin the computer to the domain. Verify using GetADComputer_PwdLastSet.ps1 and GetSecret_CupdTime_from_stale_comp.ps1 scripts.

sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Secure Channel/Workflow: Secure Channel: Looking for Known Solutions/Workflow: Secure Channel: known issues for older unicodepwd
symptom: 'The trust relationship between this workstation and the primary domain failed' on clients in a particular AD site. AD computer's unicodepwd date is older than the client's Secrets registry key date. repadmin /replsum shows replication failures (error 1722: RPC server unavailable) with large deltas.
rootCause: The computer is authenticating against a Domain Controller that has not replicated recently. The DC has a stale unicodepwd value that does not match the client's current machine account password because inbound replication failed.
solution: Fix AD replication issues between Domain Controllers. Use 'repadmin /replsum' to identify the unreplicated DC and resolve RPC connectivity issues. Once replication is restored, the password values will synchronize.

sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Secure Channel/Workflow: Secure Channel: Looking for Known Solutions/Workflow: Secure Channel: known issues for older unicodepwd
symptom: 'The trust relationship between this workstation and the primary domain failed'. repadmin /showobjmeta shows AD computer object attributes with version numbers starting at 100001, indicating an authoritative restore of the object.
rootCause: The AD computer object was authoritatively restored (version numbers 100001+), which reset the unicodepwd to an older value. The restored AD password no longer matches the client's current Secrets key.
solution: Rejoin the computer to the domain to re-establish the secure channel with matching machine account passwords. Investigate why the AD object was authoritatively restored to prevent recurrence.

sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Secure Channel/Workflow: Secure Channel: Looking for Known Solutions/Workflow: Secure Channel: other reasons for older Secrets Key
symptom: 'The trust relationship between this workstation and the primary domain failed' after an unexpected shutdown or power outage. User chose Startup Repair which restored the system to an old restore point.
rootCause: System Restore reverted the machine account password in HKLM\SECURITY\Policy\Secrets\$machine.ACC to an older value from the restore point, creating a mismatch with the current AD unicodepwd.
solution: Prevent recurrence: (1) Set 'bcdedit /set {default} bootstatuspolicy ignoreshutdownfailures' to skip recovery on shutdown failures, (2) Create frequent restore points via GPO scheduled task using Checkpoint-Computer, (3) Educate users to try normal boot before Startup Repair.

sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Secure Channel/Workflow: Secure Channel: Looking for Known Solutions/Workflow: Secure Channel: other reasons for older Secrets Key
symptom: 'The trust relationship between this workstation and the primary domain failed' on computers using state-reverting software (Deep Freeze, Sandboxie, Shadow Defender, VMware ThinApp, GesWall, Clean Slate) after the machine account password age threshold (~30 days).
rootCause: State-reverting PCI hardware or software reverts the machine to its saved state on each reboot. After 30 days the machine password changes, but the next reboot reverts the password in the registry, causing mismatch with AD (same mechanism as non-persistent VDI).
solution: Rejoin the machine to the domain and contact the state-reverting software vendor for proper configuration. Some vendors suggest disabling machine account password changes (not a Microsoft recommendation). Adjust image refresh schedule to be within password age window.

sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Secure Channel/Workflow: Secure Channel: Looking for Known Solutions/Workflow: Secure Channel: other reasons for older Secrets Key
symptom: 'The trust relationship between this workstation and the primary domain failed' on Windows Embedded clients after an unexpected shutdown or power loss, occurring shortly after a machine password change.
rootCause: Windows Embedded uses a RegFilter driver that writes protected registry changes to a Regfdata volume. Changes to HKLM\SECURITY\Policy\Secrets\$machine.ACC are only committed on clean shutdown. Unclean shutdown loses the new machine account password, reverting to the old value.
solution: Either (1) disable RegFilter driver: reg add HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Services\RegFilter /v Start /t REG_DWORD /d 0x4, or (2) add registry filter bypass for domain secret key by adding MonitoredKeys entry with RelativeKeyName=Security\Policy\Secrets under HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Services\RegFilter\Parameters.

symptom: Black screen after logon to Windows. User sees blank desktop with no taskbar, start menu, or icons. Event ID 1000 in Application log for Explorer.exe.
rootCause: Explorer.exe crashes during shell initialization. On Server OS, WinZip installation can cause explorer crash (KB 4052140).
solution: 1) Check Application log for Event ID 1000 for Explorer.exe at logon time. 2) On Server OS, check if WinZip is installed (KB 4052140). 3) Setup procdump as postmortem debugger: download procdump, run 'procdump -accepteula -ma -i', reproduce logon, collect explorer dumps. 4) Engage Windows EE team with dump files.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/SBSL - Slow Logon/Workflow: SBSL: Scenario Based Troubleshooting/Slow Blank Desktop | Black Screen

symptom: Black screen after logon to Windows. Explorer.exe is running (visible in Task Manager Details tab) but desktop remains blank/unresponsive.
rootCause: Explorer.exe is hung or stalled before completing shell initialization (background display, taskbar, start menu).
solution: 1) Open Task Manager (Ctrl+Shift+Esc) > Details tab, confirm explorer.exe is in the list. 2) Collect user-mode memory dump: 'procdump -ma -mk explorer.exe'. 3) Engage Windows EE team with the dump for analysis.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/SBSL - Slow Logon/Workflow: SBSL: Scenario Based Troubleshooting/Slow Blank Desktop | Black Screen

symptom: Black screen after logon to Windows. Explorer.exe is NOT in Task Manager process list, and no crash event (Event ID 1000) found.
rootCause: Explorer.exe started, ran, and exited without crashing. Possible causes include Access Denied errors or silent exit with non-zero status code.
solution: 1) Collect Process Monitor log of the logon (use boot log if single-session scenario). 2) Review procmon for Access Denied or other errors by explorer.exe that don't occur in working scenario. 3) Check if explorer exited with non-zero status code. 4) Verify Shell registry key: HKLM\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows NT\CurrentVersion\Winlogon\Shell should be 'explorer.exe'.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/SBSL - Slow Logon/Workflow: SBSL: Scenario Based Troubleshooting/Slow Blank Desktop | Black Screen

symptom: 10+ minute logon hang or black screen after waking from sleep on Windows 10.
rootCause: Critical section deadlock in GPAPI (Group Policy API) during logon or wake from sleep (KB 4339081).
solution: Apply KB 4339081 hotfix. Root cause analysis requires memory dump analysis. See http://aka.ms/dbgtrain for logon hang debugging training.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/SBSL - Slow Logon/Workflow: SBSL: Scenario Based Troubleshooting/Logon Hang

symptom: Logon stuck at 'Please wait...' or 'Welcome' screen indefinitely.
rootCause: WMI (Windows Management Instrumentation) verification process blocks logon (KB 2464244).
solution: Apply KB 2464244 fix. Check WMI repository health with 'winmgmt /verifyrepository'. If corrupt, rebuild WMI repository.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/SBSL - Slow Logon/Workflow: SBSL: Scenario Based Troubleshooting/Logon Hang

symptom: Logon hangs at 'Applying Computer Settings' on Windows 10 clients.
rootCause: Symantec Endpoint Protection (SEP) 14.0 or higher interferes with logon process (KB 4038374).
solution: Update or temporarily disable Symantec Endpoint Protection. Apply KB 4038374 fix. Test logon with SEP disabled to confirm.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/SBSL - Slow Logon/Workflow: SBSL: Scenario Based Troubleshooting/Logon Hang

symptom: Windows 10 RS1 hangs at Welcome screen during logon.
rootCause: Secondary Authentication Factor Credential Provider is disabled on Windows 10 RS1, causing Welcome screen hang (KB 4046086).
solution: Re-enable the Secondary Authentication Factor Credential Provider or apply KB 4046086 fix.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/SBSL - Slow Logon/Workflow: SBSL: Scenario Based Troubleshooting/Logon Hang

symptom: Logon hang and other system hangs on Windows 10 RS4.
rootCause: Windows Event Service deadlocks due to re-entrancy deadlock bug in RS4 (KB 4462425).
solution: Apply KB 4462425 hotfix for the Windows Event Service re-entrancy fix on RS4.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/SBSL - Slow Logon/Workflow: SBSL: Scenario Based Troubleshooting/Logon Hang

symptom: Slow logon on Windows 10 clients connected to Azure AD (Entra ID). PRT token refresh failures seen in AAD Operational event log.
rootCause: Firewall or proxy dropping packets to login.microsoftonline.com, preventing Azure AD authentication and PRT refresh (KB 3218254).
solution: 1) Check dsregcmd /status output for device registration state. 2) Check AAD Operational event log for PRT token refresh failures. 3) Run network trace to identify dropped packets. 4) Ensure firewall/proxy allows traffic to login.microsoftonline.com and related AAD endpoints.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/SBSL - Slow Logon/Workflow: SBSL: Scenario Based Troubleshooting/Slow Logon | Slow cached cred logon

symptom: Slow logon with extended delay during 'Applying Group Policy' phase.
rootCause: Group Policy Client service (gpsvc) processing takes excessive time during logon (KB 4077712).
solution: 1) Collect xperf/WPR boot trace to identify GP processing delays. 2) Enable gpsvc debug log via registry GPSvcDebugLevel = 0x00030002. 3) Review gpresult /H output. See KB 4077712 for GP troubleshooting.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/SBSL - Slow Logon/Workflow: SBSL: Scenario Based Troubleshooting/Slow Logon | Slow cached cred logon

symptom: Slow logon with extended delay during user profile loading phase.
rootCause: User Profile Service takes excessive time loading or creating user profile during logon (KB 4077717).
solution: 1) Check User Profile Service Operational event log for delays. 2) Collect xperf/WPR boot trace to measure profile load time. 3) Check for large profile sizes, network profile issues, or profile corruption. See KB 4077717.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/SBSL - Slow Logon/Workflow: SBSL: Scenario Based Troubleshooting/Slow Logon | Slow cached cred logon

symptom: Slow logon or logoff on Windows 10 version 1803. Significant delay during user session teardown.
rootCause: Local and LocalLow folders being synchronized at logoff, causing delays in profile unload on Windows 10 1803 (KB 4339060/4339282).
solution: Apply KB 4339060 / KB 4339282 hotfix. Check if roaming profile or folder redirection policies are syncing Local/LocalLow folders at logoff.
