sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/SBSL - Slow Logon/Workflow: SBSL: Scenario Based Troubleshooting/Slow Logoff

symptom: Slow logoff. Delay occurs during user session logoff phase.
rootCause: Windows Search indexing of cached profiles in registry, combined with profile deletion policy, delays logoff (KB 2576017).
solution: Review and adjust profile deletion Group Policy settings. Apply KB 2576017 fix.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/SBSL - Slow Logon/Workflow: SBSL: Scenario Based Troubleshooting/Slow Logoff

symptom: Slow Secure Attention Sequence (SAS) - delay in presenting credential dialog after pressing Ctrl+Alt+Del.
rootCause: Third-party credential providers interfering with credential dialog display. McAfee Endpoint Encryption (EEPC) is a known cause (KB 2584922).
solution: 1) Check for third-party credential providers in registry. 2) Disable third-party credential providers one by one to isolate. 3) If McAfee EEPC installed, update or uninstall per KB 2584922.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/SBSL - Slow Logon/Workflow: SBSL: Scenario Based Troubleshooting/Slow SAS

symptom: 20+ second delay when unlocking Windows workstation. Credential dialog slow to appear after pressing Ctrl+Alt+Del on locked screen.
rootCause: VPN connection is disconnected while screen is locked, and ForceUnlockLogon registry key is not configured (KB 4077627).
solution: Set ForceUnlockLogon registry key to prevent unlock delays when VPN is disconnected. See KB 4077627. Also check for third-party credential providers.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/SBSL - Slow Logon/Workflow: SBSL: Scenario Based Troubleshooting/Slow Unlock

symptom: 60 second boot delay on Windows 10 RS4 systems that were system-prepped (sysprep).
rootCause: System-prepped RS4 systems without a smart card reader attached experience 60-second boot delay (KB 4465111).
solution: Apply KB 4465111 hotfix. Alternatively, attach a smart card reader to eliminate the delay.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/SBSL - Slow Logon/Workflow: SBSL: Scenario Based Troubleshooting/Slow Boot

symptom: OS startup hangs or is extremely slow. System appears to deadlock during boot.
rootCause: Group Policy 'Delete profiles older than N days' triggers profile deletion during profile load, causing deadlock during OS startup (KB 2703143).
solution: Modify or disable the profile deletion Group Policy. Apply KB 2703143 fix.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/SBSL - Slow Logon/Workflow: SBSL: Scenario Based Troubleshooting/Slow Boot

symptom: Slow logon to Remote Desktop Services (RDS) servers. Black screen for 40 to 60 seconds after entering credentials.
rootCause: RDS-specific logon delay causing extended black screen period before desktop appears (KB 4040065).
solution: Apply KB 4040065 hotfix. Check RDS session host configuration and user profile settings.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/SBSL - Slow Logon/Workflow: SBSL: Scenario Based Troubleshooting/Slow Blank Desktop | Black Screen

symptom: Blank screen or slow logon during shell startup on first logon.
rootCause: AppResolverCache refresh during shell startup delays explorer.exe initialization (KB 3159433).
solution: Apply KB 3159433 hotfix. Issue is specific to first-time logon or after cache invalidation.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/SBSL - Slow Logon/Workflow: SBSL: Scenario Based Troubleshooting/Slow Blank Desktop | Black Screen

symptom: Blank screen or slow first-time logon on Windows 8/8.1/10.
rootCause: Modern app firewall rules being generated during first logon causes shell startup delay (KB 2863679).
solution: Apply KB 2863679 hotfix. Pre-provision modern app firewall rules or disable Windows Firewall temporarily to test.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/SBSL - Slow Logon/Workflow: SBSL: Scenario Based Troubleshooting/Slow Blank Desktop | Black Screen

symptom: Running gpresult returns error 'The user does not have RSOP data' when attempting to retrieve Resultant Set of Policy information for a specific user
rootCause: The target user has never logged into the computer. RSoP data is only available for users who have previously signed in to that machine.
solution: Skip user-specific Group Policy settings and retrieve computer settings only by using the /SCOPE COMPUTER parameter: 'gpresult /SCOPE COMPUTER /H GPReport.html'. This prevents the error and still provides the computer-level policy results needed for troubleshooting.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Tools/RsoP - GPresult

symptom: Cannot paste credentials (copy/paste not working) into the UAC elevation prompt when User Account Control setting is set to 'Always Notify' on Windows 10 version 1709 or later
rootCause: Copy and paste into UAC credential fields was intentionally disabled starting with Windows 10 version 1709 as a security hardening measure. This is by design to prevent credential theft via clipboard interception.
solution: This behavior is by design since Windows 10 1709. Users must manually type their credentials into the UAC prompt. There is no supported workaround to re-enable clipboard paste in UAC credential prompts.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/User Account Control/Workflow: User Account Control: KB References

symptom: UAC elevation prompt shows 'To continue, enter an admin user name and password' but the 'Yes' button is missing, preventing elevation approval
rootCause: The built-in Administrator account is disabled on the machine. When no other local admin account credentials are readily available and the built-in Administrator is disabled, the UAC prompt cannot display the consent (Yes) button and instead shows only the credential prompt without an approval option.
solution: Enable the built-in Administrator account or ensure a valid local administrator account is available. Use 'net user Administrator /active:yes' from an elevated command prompt or Safe Mode to enable the built-in Administrator. Alternatively, provide credentials for another account that is a member of the local Administrators group.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/User Account Control/Workflow: User Account Control: KB References

symptom: FIDO2 security key authentication works on websites (portal.office.com, MyApps) but fails for Windows login with error 'Sorry, try again. A problem occurred with the server' - key is detected and fingerprint accepted, but login fails with status -2146893039 in CredProvFramework logs
rootCause: Enforce attestation option set to YES in FIDO2 security key policy. The security key (e.g. ATKeyPro) metadata is not published/verified with FIDO Alliance Metadata Service or does not pass Microsoft additional validation testing. Microsoft requires resident key, client pin, hmac-secret, and multiple accounts per RP extensions.
solution: Set enforce attestation option to NO and re-register the FIDO2 security key. Once enrolled with attestation=NO, future changes to the setting only affect new registrations. Yubikey keys work with attestation=YES. Verify key compatibility at https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/windows/security/identity-protection/hello-for-business/microsoft-compatible-security-key
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Windows Hello and Modern Credential Providers/FIDO2/FIDO2: Looking for known solutions | tips/"Sorry, try again. A problem occurred with the server" during signin

symptom: Authentication Mechanism Assurance (AMA) does not work when using FIDO2/passkey or Windows Hello for Business on Entra ID Joined devices - users are not placed into expected security groups based on certificate authentication policy despite successful FIDO2 sign-in
rootCause: Partial TGTs from Entra ID Kerberos service bypass the AS-REQ flow entirely. AMA relies on certificates presented during AS-REQ to map OIDs to security group membership. FIDO2/passkeys do not use certificates so AMA cannot determine group placement. Only the username is trusted from partial TGTs; group membership and authorization metadata are ignored. Kerberos FAST is also incompatible - machine must be domain member with primary TGT from on-prem DC.
solution: Choose between FIDO2+passkeys OR Authentication Mechanism Assurance - cannot have both. For AMA support, use WHFB key trust or cert trust instead of FIDO2, as those perform the AS leg regardless of hybrid or Entra join. If customer requires both passwordless and AMA, WHFB cert trust is the only viable option.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Windows Hello and Modern Credential Providers/FIDO2/FIDO2: Looking for known solutions | tips/Authentication Mechanism Assurance and Partial TGT

symptom: FIDO2 cached credential logon fails on Windows 24H2 when device is Hybrid Domain Joined and AD User Home Folder is configured
rootCause: Regression in Windows 24H2 (Bug 54160686) causes FIDO cached credential logon to fail when both Hybrid Domain Join and AD User Home Folder are configured
solution: Wait for the Windows fix via servicing update. Track Bug 54160686 for status.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Windows Hello and Modern Credential Providers/FIDO2/FIDO2: Looking for known solutions | tips/Emerging issues

symptom: FIDO2 PIN entry not displayed when laptop lid is closed while using external monitor and docking station; key appears unresponsive after touch
rootCause: LogonUI handles lid close/open events from power manager, hiding the PIN entry UI when the laptop lid is fully closed even with external monitor connected
solution: Workaround 1: Reinserting the FIDO2 key takes another code path not impacted by display state. Workaround 2: Set registry value LidNotifyReliable to 0 to prevent LogonUI from handling lid close/open events. Or open the laptop lid.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Windows Hello and Modern Credential Providers/FIDO2/FIDO2: Looking for known solutions | tips/Emerging issues

symptom: FIDO2 PIN entry not displayed during Windows sign-in (LogonUI) when GPO security option Interactive logon Message title/text for users attempting to log on is enabled
rootCause: The GPO security option for logon message title/text conflicts with the FIDO2 credential provider PIN entry UI, preventing PIN input display
solution: Workaround 1: Unplug and replug the FIDO2 key. Workaround 2: Disable GPO settings Interactive logon: Message text for users attempting to log on and Interactive logon: Message title for users attempting to log on.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Windows Hello and Modern Credential Providers/FIDO2/FIDO2: Looking for known solutions | tips/Emerging issues

symptom: First cached FIDO2 logon fails, second logon works after installing 9C DCR update
rootCause: Bug in cached FIDO2 authentication data handling introduced by 9C DCR update causes first logon attempt to fail
solution: Install January 2023 update KB5019275 (OS Builds 19042.2546, 19044.2546, 19045.2546) which addresses the cached FIDO2 authentication data issue.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Windows Hello and Modern Credential Providers/FIDO2/FIDO2: Looking for known solutions | tips/Emerging issues

symptom: FIDO2 cached logon fails with Sorry try that again There was an issue with the server on Hybrid AADJ device when DC connectivity is unavailable
rootCause: Since February 2022, cached FIDO2 credentials are prematurely removed from hybrid domain-joined devices, preventing offline cached logon (Bug 37629228)
solution: Install October 2022 cumulative updates: Windows 10 KB5017383 (preview) or later. Fix shipped in 9c preview updates for Windows 11 and Windows 10.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Windows Hello and Modern Credential Providers/FIDO2/FIDO2: Looking for known solutions | tips/Emerging issues

symptom: PacRequestorEnforcement=2 breaks FIDO2 and Kerberos on-premises sign-in scenarios
rootCause: Azure AD is not inserting the PAC (Privilege Attribute Certificate) data structure yet, so enforcement mode 2 rejects FIDO2/Kerberos authentication
solution: Set PacRequestorEnforcement=1 if using FIDO2 or Cloud Trust until Azure AD supports PAC insertion.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Windows Hello and Modern Credential Providers/FIDO2/FIDO2: Looking for known solutions | tips/Emerging issues

symptom: FIDO2/Cloud Trust sign-in intermittently fails with STATUS_BAD_LOGON_SESSION_STATE (0xC000006D, 0xC0000104) after resume from sleep
rootCause: Race condition between backend PRT refresh and user authentication attempt after resume from sleep causes logon session state error
solution: Workaround: Click Other user, input UPN manually, then enter PIN to sign in. Issue is intermittent and resolved by waiting ~10 seconds post resume from sleep.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Windows Hello and Modern Credential Providers/FIDO2/FIDO2: Looking for known solutions | tips/Emerging issues

symptom: FIDO2 key registration fails with invalidcanary error on MySignins page
rootCause: Regression caused by syntax error in FIDO controller KO ifnot statement caused the canary token to be included twice in the redirect response from ESTS to MySignins, making the canary token invalid
solution: Fixed server-side (ICM 346504098). No customer action needed if issue recurs, escalate to ESTS team.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Windows Hello and Modern Credential Providers/FIDO2/FIDO2: Looking for known solutions | tips/Emerging issues

symptom: FIDO2 browser logon fails with 0x5B4 timeout error; unable to enter PIN on security key in browser scenarios; WebAuthN event 2226 shows Ctap USB timeout
rootCause: Incomplete Citrix client auto-update (lacking admin rights) leaves broken USB redirection components that interfere with FIDO2 security key communication in browsers
solution: Reinstall the Citrix client in admin mode. Verify Citrix components via msinfo32.exe modules section. Disable Citrix auto-install to prevent recurrence.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Windows Hello and Modern Credential Providers/FIDO2/FIDO2: Looking for known solutions | tips/Unable to enter pin in browser logon scenarios (0x5B4)

symptom: FIDO2 Windows sign-in fails with Your credentials could not be verified (0xc000006d STATUS_LOGON_FAILURE); KDC.etl shows TGT not cacheable by branch krbtgt and error 0x14
rootCause: User is a member of a protected group (directly or via nested group membership) listed in the msDS-NeverRevealGroup attribute of AzureADKerberos object, preventing TGT redemption through the RODC-like Azure AD Kerberos Server
solution: Remove the user from the protected group, or modify the msDS-NeverRevealGroup property on CN=AzureADKerberos,OU=Domain Controllers to allow the group. Use PowerShell to check nested groups: foreach($sid in [Security.Principal.WindowsIdentity]::new('user@domain.com').Groups){$sid.Translate([System.Security.Principal.NTAccount])}
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Windows Hello and Modern Credential Providers/FIDO2/FIDO2: Looking for known solutions | tips/Your credentials couldn't be verified

symptom: WHfB PIN reset triggers old PIN reset experience instead of new Web Sign-In experience when user signs out, uses Other User tile with local admin, then clicks Forgot my PIN for another AAD user
rootCause: Bug 46530811: When switching users via Other User tile, the system does not associate the AAD user correctly and falls back to the old PIN reset flow instead of the new Web Sign-In-based flow
solution: Known bug (Bug 46530811). Workaround: User should sign in from their own tile directly (not via Other User tile) before clicking Forgot my PIN.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Windows Hello and Modern Credential Providers/New PIN Reset behavoir/Collaboration, Scoping and Log Collection

symptom: Set-AzureADKerberosServer fails with Failed to read secrets from domain; on-premises AzureADKerberos object created but cloud attributes missing in Azure AD
rootCause: The user executing Set-AzureADKerberosServer lacks Replicate Directory Changes and Replicate Directory Changes All permissions on the AD directory, preventing secret replication
solution: Grant the executing user Replicate Directory Changes and Replicate Directory Changes All permissions on the domain directory partition.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Windows Hello and Modern Credential Providers/WHfB/WHFB: Cloud Kerberos Trust/Looking for known solutions & tips/AzureADKerberos creation error "Failed to read secrets from domain"

symptom: Set-AzureADKerberosServer fails with Failed to read secrets from domain when FIPS compliance mode is enabled on the server
rootCause: FIPS compliance policy interferes with the AzureADHybridAuthenticationManagement module ability to create/read the Kerberos Server encryption keys
solution: Temporarily disable FIPS compliance (local or domain GPO), run gpupdate /force, close all PowerShell sessions, then re-run Set-AzureADKerberosServer. Re-enable FIPS after successful creation. FIPS must be disabled again only for key rotation or object re-creation.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Windows Hello and Modern Credential Providers/WHfB/WHFB: Cloud Kerberos Trust/Looking for known solutions & tips/AzureADKerberos creation error "Failed to read secrets from domain"

symptom: Set-AzureADKerberosServer fails with Failed to read secrets from domain and trace shows RPC Error 1726 The remote procedure call failed when connecting to PDCe
rootCause: The cmdlet targets the PDC Emulator (PDCe) for creating the Kerberos Server object, but network infrastructure blocks DsGetNCChanges RPC traffic between the executing machine and the PDCe (which may be in a different AD site)
solution: Ensure network settings allow DsGetNCChanges RPC communication between the machine running Set-AzureADKerberosServer and the PDC Emulator. Run the cmdlet from the same AD site as the PDCe if possible.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Windows Hello and Modern Credential Providers/WHfB/WHFB: Cloud Kerberos Trust/Looking for known solutions & tips/AzureADKerberos creation error "Failed to read secrets from domain"

symptom: Cannot access on-premises resources (file shares, NAS, DFS-N) using WHfB Cloud Kerberos Trust despite CloudTgt and OnpremTgt showing yes; Kerberos token has public key value 0
rootCause: NETBIOSNAME and ONPREMISESSAMACCOUNTNAME attributes are not synced to Azure AD because custom AD Connect sync rules disabled the default connector space rules for these attributes
solution: In AD Connect Synchronization Service Manager, enable the sync rules for ONPREMISESSAMACCOUNTNAME, NETBIOSNAME, onPremisesDistinguishedName, and onPremisesDomainName in the metaverse. Run full sync: Start-ADSyncSyncCycle -PolicyType Initial. Verify attributes via MS Graph Explorer.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Windows Hello and Modern Credential Providers/WHfB/WHFB: Cloud Kerberos Trust/Looking for known solutions & tips/Cannot access on-premises resources

symptom: WHfB Cloud Kerberos Trust login fails with That option is temporarily unavailable For now please use a different method to sign in; KDC.etl shows TGT not cacheable by branch krbtgt and KDC_ERR_TGT_REVOKED (0x14)
rootCause: User is a member of protected groups (Administrators, Domain Admins, Enterprise Admins, etc.) listed in the msDS-NeverRevealGroup attribute of AzureADKerberos RODC object, preventing TGT validation
solution: Option 1: Remove the user from the protected groups. Option 2: Modify the msDS-NeverRevealGroup property on CN=AzureADKerberos,OU=Domain Controllers to allow the specific group/user. See: https://docs.microsoft.com/azure/active-directory/authentication/howto-authentication-passwordless-faqs
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Windows Hello and Modern Credential Providers/WHfB/WHFB: Cloud Kerberos Trust/Looking for known solutions & tips/That option is temporarily unavailable

symptom: WHfB Cloud Kerberos Trust login fails with That option is temporarily unavailable and KDC_ERR_TGT_REVOKED, but user is NOT in any protected group listed in msDS-NeverRevealGroup
rootCause: User is not a member of the Domain Users group. The msDS-RevealOnDemandGroup attribute on AzureADKerberos object contains Domain Users by default (allow list), so users not in Domain Users are effectively denied
solution: Add the user to the Domain Users group. The msDS-RevealOnDemandGroup on CN=AzureADKerberos contains Domain Users as an allow-by-default entry.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Windows Hello and Modern Credential Providers/WHfB/WHFB: Cloud Kerberos Trust/Looking for known solutions & tips/That option is temporarily unavailable: no it's not due to msDS-NeverRevealGroup!

symptom: WHfB Cloud Kerberos Trust sign-in fails with Your credentials could not be verified; KDC.etl shows TGT not cacheable by branch krbtgt error 0x14; user in Account Operators group
rootCause: User is a member of a protected group (e.g. Account Operators) which is included in the msDS-NeverRevealGroup attribute of AzureADKerberos object, same behavior as FIDO2 protected group restriction
solution: Remove the user from the protected group, or modify msDS-NeverRevealGroup on CN=AzureADKerberos to allow the group. Use nested group check PowerShell: foreach($sid in [Security.Principal.WindowsIdentity]::new('user@domain.com').Groups){$sid.Translate([System.Security.Principal.NTAccount])}
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Windows Hello and Modern Credential Providers/WHfB/WHFB: Cloud Kerberos Trust/Looking for known solutions & tips/Your credentials couldn't be verified

symptom: After enabling WHFB Passwordless Experience, the Password Credential Provider is still displayed in LogonUI on the client machine
rootCause: MDM/Intune policy for EnablePasswordlessExperience is not being applied to the client machine. The registry key at HKLM\Software\Microsoft\PolicyManager\Current\Device\Authentication is not set.
solution: Verify MDM/Intune EnablePasswordlessExperience policy is applied. Check registry key HKLM\Software\Microsoft\PolicyManager\Current\Device\Authentication. For testing, manually set: reg add HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\PolicyManager\default\Authentication\EnablePasswordlessExperience /v value /t REG_DWORD /d 0x1 /f. If Intune policy not deploying, engage Intune team.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Windows Hello and Modern Credential Providers/WHfB/WHFB: Enhancing Passwordless experience/Scoping & Troubleshooting

symptom: WebSign-In Credential Provider is not visible in LogonUI after enabling WHFB Passwordless Experience
rootCause: WebSign-In is a separate feature that must be enabled independently via Intune-MDM, not automatically enabled with Passwordless Experience
solution: Enable WebSign-In using Intune-MDM. Configure via Authentication CSP: EnableWebSignIn policy. Reference: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/windows/client-management/mdm/policy-csp-authentication#enablewebsignin
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Windows Hello and Modern Credential Providers/WHfB/WHFB: Enhancing Passwordless experience/Scoping & Troubleshooting

symptom: After enabling WHFB Passwordless Experience with Interactive logon: Do not display last signed-in GPO enabled, Password Credential Provider appears in LogonUI instead of being hidden
rootCause: Known conflict between the Do not display last signed-in GPO (Computer Configuration > Windows Settings > Security Settings > Local Policies > Security Options) and the Passwordless Experience feature. The GPO breaks Passwordless behavior in LogonUI.
solution: Disable the Interactive logon: Do not display last signed-in GPO. Note: In-Auth scenarios are not affected by this GPO - only LogonUI shows the Password provider.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Windows Hello and Modern Credential Providers/WHfB/WHFB: Enhancing Passwordless experience/Scoping & Troubleshooting

symptom: When user encounters a TPM error during WHFB Passwordless login and clicks Set up my PIN, WebSign-In only displays Temporary Access Pass (TAP) instead of Password provider
rootCause: Known bug in Passwordless Experience feature (Bug #46437175) - WebSign-In credential provider incorrectly filters to TAP only when TPM errors occur
solution: Known bug tracked at https://microsoft.visualstudio.com/OS/_workitems/edit/46437175. Workaround: User needs to contact admin to reset password and use alternative sign-in method.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Windows Hello and Modern Credential Providers/WHfB/WHFB: Enhancing Passwordless experience/Scoping & Troubleshooting

symptom: Chrome and other non-Windows Credential Provider applications still show password prompts after enabling WHFB Passwordless Experience
rootCause: By design - Passwordless Experience is only implemented for Windows Credential Provider applications (Edge, mapped drives). Applications not using Windows Credential Provider (e.g., Chrome) are unaffected.
solution: This is by design. Application developers need to implement Windows Credential Provider support for passwordless integration. Only applications using Windows Credential Provider API will honor the Passwordless Experience setting.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Windows Hello and Modern Credential Providers/WHfB/WHFB: Enhancing Passwordless experience/Scoping & Troubleshooting

symptom: Device registration fails with DSREG_E_DEVICE_AUTHENTICATION_ERROR (0x801C0002 / -2145648638) - server failed to authenticate the user
rootCause: Token expired, environment mismatch (PPE token sent to PROD ADRS or vice versa), or bad/corrupt authentication token
solution: Retry the operation. If persistent, check ADRS server logs using correlation ID and timestamp from dsreg admin log. Verify environment registry settings are correct (PPE vs PROD). Run dsregcmd /status to check device join state.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Windows Hello and Modern Credential Providers/WHfB/WHFB: Looking at logs/Dsregcmd/DSREG Error codes (CDJ, WPJ, NGC, DJ++)

symptom: Device registration fails with DSREG_E_DEVICE_AUTHORIZATION_ERROR (0x801C0003 / -2145648637) - user not authorized to enroll
rootCause: User does not have permission to perform the device join operation. Azure AD device settings may restrict who can join devices.
solution: For Cloud Domain Join (CDJ): Check Azure portal > Users > Device settings > Users may join devices to Azure AD. Ensure the user has appropriate permissions or is in the allowed group.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Windows Hello and Modern Credential Providers/WHfB/WHFB: Looking at logs/Dsregcmd/DSREG Error codes (CDJ, WPJ, NGC, DJ++)

symptom: Device registration fails with DSREG_E_DEVICE_REGISTRATION_QUOTA_EXCEEDED (0x801C000E / -2145648626) - registration quota reached
rootCause: The user has reached the maximum number of devices that can be registered/joined under their account in Azure AD
solution: Unjoin or remove unused devices currently joined using the same account, then retry registration. Check Azure AD > Devices > Device settings for the maximum device limit per user.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Windows Hello and Modern Credential Providers/WHfB/WHFB: Looking at logs/Dsregcmd/DSREG Error codes (CDJ, WPJ, NGC, DJ++)

symptom: Hybrid Azure AD Join fails with DSREG_AUTOJOIN_ADCONFIG_READ_FAILED (0x801C001D / -2145648611) - failed to read DJ++ configurations from AD
rootCause: Either (1) misconfiguration with both AAD info (tenant name/ID) and on-prem DRS info set in AD, or (2) failed to read configuration from AD due to LDAP connectivity/configuration issues
solution: Verify AD configuration - ensure only one configuration source (AAD or on-prem DRS) is set. Check LDAP connectivity. Review debug log for detailed error information.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Windows Hello and Modern Credential Providers/WHfB/WHFB: Looking at logs/Dsregcmd/DSREG Error codes (CDJ, WPJ, NGC, DJ++)

symptom: Device registration or NGC provisioning fails with DSREG_MFA_REQUIRED (0x801C0023 / -2145648605) - operation requires multi-factor authentication
rootCause: ADRS server does not find the MFA claim in the authentication token. MFA was not completed or the MFA claim was not included in the token.
solution: Troubleshoot CloudAP or WAM plugin log. Ensure MFA is properly completed before device registration. Check Conditional Access policies that may require MFA for device registration.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Windows Hello and Modern Credential Providers/WHfB/WHFB: Looking at logs/Dsregcmd/DSREG Error codes (CDJ, WPJ, NGC, DJ++)

symptom: NGC/WHfB key registration fails with DSREG_E_USER_TOKEN_ERROR (0x801C044D / -2145647539) - unable to obtain user token via Web Account Manager
rootCause: WAM or AAD WAM plugin issue. Possible causes: network connectivity failure, invalid credentials, token URL discovery failure, or AAD WAM plugin crash/timeout.
solution: Retry by relogging on. Check network connectivity. Verify credentials. Check admin log for WAM get token return value. If persistent, collect CloudAP and WAM plugin logs for analysis.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Windows Hello and Modern Credential Providers/WHfB/WHFB: Looking at logs/Dsregcmd/DSREG Error codes (CDJ, WPJ, NGC, DJ++)

symptom: NGC/WHfB provisioning fails with NTE_DEVICE_NOT_FOUND (0x80090035 / -2146893771) - no TPM hardware meeting NGC policy requirements
rootCause: The device does not have TPM hardware that meets NGC policy requirements, or TPM is not properly configured/enabled in BIOS/UEFI
solution: Verify TPM hardware is present and enabled in BIOS/UEFI. Check TPM status via tpm.msc or Device Manager. If hardware TPM not available, consider software-based key storage if policy permits.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Windows Hello and Modern Credential Providers/WHfB/WHFB: Looking at logs/Dsregcmd/DSREG Error codes (CDJ, WPJ, NGC, DJ++)

symptom: Operation fails with DSREG_E_DEVICE_NOT_FOUND (0x801C03F3 / -2145647629) - device object not found in Azure AD directory
rootCause: The device object was deleted from the Azure AD directory
solution: Ask the customer to rejoin the device to Azure AD. Run dsregcmd /leave followed by dsregcmd /join or unjoin and rejoin through Settings > Accounts > Access work or school.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Windows Hello and Modern Credential Providers/WHfB/WHFB: Looking at logs/Dsregcmd/DSREG Error codes (CDJ, WPJ, NGC, DJ++)

symptom: NGC key registers successfully but certificate enrollment fails with DSREG_NGC_KEY_SUCCESS_RETRY_CERT (0x801C03F8 / -2145647624)
rootCause: NGC key registered successfully but not yet ready for certificate enrollment. In hybrid deployments, the NGC key has not yet been back-synced to on-premises AD via Azure AD Connect.
solution: This is expected behavior for hybrid deployments. Certificate auto-enrollment will retry on regular intervals. Do NOT roll back the key registration. Wait for Azure AD Connect sync cycle to complete.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Windows Hello and Modern Credential Providers/WHfB/WHFB: Looking at logs/Dsregcmd/DSREG Error codes (CDJ, WPJ, NGC, DJ++)

symptom: Windows Hello PIN reset from login screen does not work on Windows 10 Professional edition
rootCause: PIN reset from login screen requires Windows 10 Enterprise edition build 1709 or later. Professional edition is not supported for this feature.
solution: Upgrade to Windows 10 1709+ Enterprise. If PIN was set before PIN reset policy was applied, user must first reset PIN from Settings > Accounts > Sign-in options > PIN > Change > I forgot my PIN. After that, PIN reset from login screen will work on next attempt.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Windows Hello and Modern Credential Providers/WHfB/WHFB: Looking at logs/PIN Reset

symptom: Users click I Forgot My PIN on Windows login screen and get error: This feature is not supported in your organization. Please contact your administrator.
rootCause: PIN reset service is not configured in the environment or the client device lacks internet access to connect to the PIN reset service endpoint
solution: Verify PIN reset service configuration in the tenant. Ensure client has internet access to connect to the PIN reset service. Refer to PIN reset documentation for configuration steps.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Windows Hello and Modern Credential Providers/WHfB/WHFB: Looking at logs/PIN Reset

symptom: After WHfB sign-in (fingerprint/PIN), access to on-premises resources fails. User prompted for username/password. Network capture shows KRB5KDC_ERR_PADATA_TYPE_NOSUPP. KDC logs show Unable to find KDC certificate in KDC store and Event ID 19 STATUS_PKINIT_FAILURE
rootCause: Issuing CA certificate that issued the Domain Controller certificate was not published in the NTAuth store. CA certificate was renewed but the new certificate was not published to NTAuth.
solution: Export the Issuing CA certificate and publish to NTAuth store: certutil -dspublish -f Ca.cer NTAuthCA. Run gpupdate /force on the DC and the client. Note: KDC uses only one certificate selected at service start; restart KDC service after obtaining new certificate.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Windows Hello and Modern Credential Providers/WHfB/WHFB: Looking for known solutions | tips/Access to on-premises resources from an AADJ device/Cannot logon with PIN | KDC certificate problem

symptom: Users prompted for credentials when accessing on-premises resources (Integrated Auth websites, DFS shares) via SSO on Azure AD Joined device with Always On VPN. Kerberos event 200: could not locate a domain controller for domain fabrikam.local where fabrikam.local is the Azure tenant name
rootCause: VPN client stores RAS cached credential in Credential Manager using Azure tenant name as the realm. Kerberos then tries to find DC for Azure tenant domain instead of on-premises domain, causing authentication failure.
solution: Option 1: Disable cached credentials via registry HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Control\Lsa\disabledomaincreds=1 (disables all cached creds). Option 2: Set UseRasCredentials=0 in rasphone.pbk via Intune. Option 3: Create logon scheduled task running cmdkey /delete /RAS to delete only RAS credentials after user logon.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Windows Hello and Modern Credential Providers/WHfB/WHFB: Looking for known solutions | tips/Access to on-premises resources from an AADJ device/VPN RAS Cached Credentials

symptom: Windows Hello for Business biometrics (fingerprint, face recognition) not working. Auth-scripts Winbio-key.txt shows Enabled registry key is not set to 1
rootCause: WinBio Credential Provider registry key is not set to 1, disabling biometric authentication for WHfB
solution: Set registry key HKLM\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Winbio\Credential Provider\Enabled to REG_DWORD 0x1. Verify fix by checking auth-scripts Winbio-key.txt file.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Windows Hello and Modern Credential Providers/WHfB/WHFB: Looking for known solutions | tips/Generic solutions/Biometrics/Biometric is not working

symptom: Face recognition stops working after upgrading to Windows 10 version 20H2. Enhanced Sign-in Security UI shows device is not compatible.
rootCause: Enhanced Sign-in Security is only supported on devices configured by the manufacturer for this capability with the Windows 10 October 2020 update. In-market devices upgrading to 20H2 that were not pre-configured by manufacturer are not supported.
solution: Workaround: Clean install (install device from scratch) with Windows 10 version 20H2 instead of in-place upgrade. This ensures Enhanced Sign-in Security is properly initialized.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Windows Hello and Modern Credential Providers/WHfB/WHFB: Looking for known solutions | tips/Generic solutions/Biometrics/Biometrics gesture not working after upgrading to 20h2 - Enhanced Sign-in Security UI

symptom: Intermittent WHfB sign-in failure with error 0xC000006D / 0xC0000104 (STATUS_BAD_LOGON_SESSION_STATE) after resume from sleep on Cloud Trust or FIDO2 deployment. Device prompts to set up PIN again instead of falling back to cached credentials.
rootCause: Race condition between backend PRT refresh and user authentication attempt after resume from sleep. System detects DC availability but network stack is not ready, fails without falling back to cached credentials.
solution: User workaround: Click other user, input UPN then PIN to sign in. Fixed in Copper release; Win10 fix in 2023.07D update.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Windows Hello and Modern Credential Providers/WHfB/WHFB: Looking for known solutions | tips/Emerging issues

symptom: Event ID 21 flooding DC event logs after applying September 2021 updates in WHfB key trust environment: The client certificate for user is not valid, resulted in failed smartcard logon
rootCause: Change in September 2021 (8C) update attempts full cert chain validation on key trust self-signed certificates, fails validation, then falls back to less strict checks. Warning Event ID 21 is not suppressed during the first Key Trust check.
solution: Install fix: Windows SV 21H2: 2022 6C, Windows Server 2022: RTM, Windows Server 2019 (1809): 2022 6C, Windows Server 2016 (1607): 2022 7B.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Windows Hello and Modern Credential Providers/WHfB/WHFB: Looking for known solutions | tips/Emerging issues

symptom: Remote Credential Guard authentication fails with error c0030009 after applying January 2022 (1B.22) update to the client initiating the RDP connection
rootCause: Regression from January 2022 update. WHfB with key credential (including cloud trust) does not support supplied credentials for RDP. RDP does not support authentication with a key or self-signed certificate.
solution: Install fix: Windows Server 2022: 2022 4C, Windows 11: 2022 4C, 2004/20H1/20H2/21H1/21H2: 2022 4C. Note: WHfB with key credential can be used with Windows Defender Remote Credential Guard; certificate-based WHfB deployment supports supplied credentials for RDP.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Windows Hello and Modern Credential Providers/WHfB/WHFB: Looking for known solutions | tips/Emerging issues

symptom: LSASS crashes with error c0000374 / 255 on Domain Controllers causing reboot loop after applying January 2022 Windows Server updates
rootCause: Regression in January 2022 (1B.22) Windows Server cumulative update affecting LSASS stability on Domain Controllers
solution: Install the January 17, 2022 Out-of-Band (OOB) update for Windows Server to resolve the LSASS crash.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Windows Hello and Modern Credential Providers/WHfB/WHFB: Looking for known solutions | tips/Emerging issues

symptom: Slow logon or black screen on Azure AD-joined clients when login.microsoftonline.com is unreachable (firewall drops packets). CloudAP InteractiveLogon hangs holding UserCacheEntry lock.
rootCause: CloudAP InteractiveLogon waits on network while holding UserCacheEntry lock exclusively, blocking logon process when Azure AD endpoints are unreachable
solution: Install fixes: 2004/20H1/20H2: KB5000842 (March 29, 2021), 1909/19H2: KB5000850 (March 25, 2021), 1809/WS2019: KB5000854 (March 25, 2021). Ensure firewall allows access to login.microsoftonline.com.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Windows Hello and Modern Credential Providers/WHfB/WHFB: Looking for known solutions | tips/Emerging issues

symptom: WHfB Hybrid Key Trust sign-in fails with That option is temporarily unavailable. For now, please use a different method to sign in when only Windows Server 2019 DCs are used for authentication
rootCause: Bug in Windows Server 2019 Domain Controllers handling WHfB Hybrid Key Trust authentication
solution: Install KB4487044 on Windows Server 2019 DCs. See Windows Hello for Business deployment issues documentation.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Windows Hello and Modern Credential Providers/WHfB/WHFB: Looking for known solutions | tips/Emerging issues

symptom: PIN complexity Group Policy settings not honored on Windows 11 22H2 workgroup device and convenience PIN
rootCause: Bug in Windows 11 22H2 where PIN complexity policies do not take effect on fresh or upgrade install
solution: Fixed in VNEXT/Active Branch. Windows 11 22H2 and Windows SV 21H2 fix scheduled for 2023 3D release.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Windows Hello and Modern Credential Providers/WHfB/WHFB: Looking for known solutions | tips/Emerging issues

symptom: Credential Roaming roams WHfB certificates into AD and breaks WHfB functionality on Windows 10 2004/20H1/20H2
rootCause: Credential Roaming interoperability issue with WHfB - CR roams WHfB certificates into AD which conflicts with WHfB operation
solution: Install KB5000842 to resolve the Credential Roaming and WHfB interoperability issue.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Windows Hello and Modern Credential Providers/WHfB/WHFB: Looking for known solutions | tips/Emerging issues

Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Windows Hello and Modern Credential Providers/WHfB/WHFB: Looking for known solutions | tips/Generic solutions/Biometrics/Disable the automatic dismiss unlock of the device using face recognition
symptom: Windows Hello face recognition auto-dismiss/unlock is not working even though the customer disabled it. Setting the AutoDismissOn registry key under FaceLogon does not take effect.
rootCause: The NoLockScreen registry key (HKLM\SOFTWARE\Policies\Microsoft\Windows\Personalization) is set to 1, which overrides the face logon auto-dismiss behavior.
solution: Set the NoLockScreen registry key to 0. Note: the device will not auto-sign-in immediately after locking (by design, to prevent unlock when user walks away). Auto-unlock triggers when the screen turns off and back on.

sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Windows Hello and Modern Credential Providers/WHfB/WHFB: Looking for known solutions | tips/Generic solutions/Biometrics/Face Recognition, external camera and lid
symptom: Windows Hello face recognition fails when the laptop lid is closed and an external camera is used. Clicking the Hello icon at the login screen does nothing (no error messages). Face recognition works normally with the lid open.
rootCause: The LidNotifyReliable registry value is set to 1 (True), causing the OS to suppress Windows Hello when it detects the lid is closed, even though an external camera is available.
solution: Set the LidNotifyReliable registry key to 0 at HKLM\System\CurrentControlSet\Control\Power. This tells the OS that lid notifications are not guaranteed reliable, so it will not suppress Windows Hello when the lid is closed.

sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Windows Hello and Modern Credential Providers/WHfB/WHFB: Looking for known solutions | tips/Generic solutions/Biometrics/Face Recognition, external camera and sleep mode
symptom: A new external camera plugged in while the device is locked or in sleep mode is not recognized by Windows Hello. The camera only becomes available after entering PIN first, requiring a second lock/unlock cycle.
rootCause: Security fix for MSRC 64205 (Windows Hello Identity Spoofing by CyberArk) added a sensor allow list. New cameras are blocked during lock screen until the user authenticates via PIN to add the camera to the allow list.
solution: This is by-design security behavior. For the first use of a new external camera, the user must enter PIN once to add it to the allow list. Subsequent lock/unlock cycles will recognize the camera. If peripheral sensors should be blocked entirely, set AllowPeripheralSensorsAtLogon=0 at HKLM\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\WinBio.

sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Windows Hello and Modern Credential Providers/WHfB/WHFB: Looking for known solutions | tips/Generic solutions/Biometrics/Face recognition is not operational after an upgrade (SE401P)
symptom: After upgrading to Windows 10 version 1903, the face recognition sensor (SE401P) is not operational. Settings shows no Windows Hello compatible camera although it worked in previous versions.
rootCause: The machine was not migrated correctly during the OS upgrade. The HelloFace software device driver should have been excluded during migration to be automatically reinstalled on demand.
solution: Reinstall the HelloFace driver: (1) Run pnputil /enum-drivers (2) Locate the HelloFace software driver (3) Delete and reinstall it. If issue persists, check for correct sensor driver versions and consider updating firmware.

sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Windows Hello and Modern Credential Providers/WHfB/WHFB: Looking for known solutions | tips/Generic solutions/Biometrics/Face recognition setup is not working after upgrading to 20h2 (it was working in previous build)
symptom: After upgrading to Windows 10 20H2 on HP EliteBook 840 G7 devices, face recognition setup wizard gets stuck during enrollment. Camera starts and shows the user face but the wizard never completes. WinBio logs show WINBIO_E_INSECURE_SENSOR (0x80098057).
rootCause: HP enabled Enhanced Sign-in Security (VBS/Secure Mode) in BIOS, but the built-in camera does not have the hardware capability to run in secure mode. The sensor fails secure mode validation, causing enrollment to hang.
solution: Contact HP to get camera firmware that supports secure mode, or clarify why Enhanced Security was enabled with incompatible camera. Keep VBS enabled for security but do not use face recognition on devices with incompatible cameras. Do NOT disable VBS as a workaround.

sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Windows Hello and Modern Credential Providers/WHfB/WHFB: Looking for known solutions | tips/Generic solutions/Biometrics/Fingerprint credential provider tile not displayed at logon screen/Conflicting Config
symptom: Biometric/fingerprint tile does not appear at the logon screen on Entra ID joined devices managed by MDM. MDM policies for PassportForWork and UseBiometrics are configured, but biometrics do not work until specific registry keys are manually added.
rootCause: SharedPC configuration (SharedPCCSP) overrides the biometric credential provider settings. SharedPC policies conflict with Windows Hello biometric enrollment, disabling the WinBio credential provider.
solution: Add registry keys: (1) HKLM\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Winbio\Credential Provider: Enabled=1, Domain Accounts=1 (2) Verify HKLM\SOFTWARE\Policies\Microsoft\PassportForWork: Enabled=1. Review SharedPC CSP configuration for conflicts.

sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Windows Hello and Modern Credential Providers/WHfB/WHFB: Looking for known solutions | tips/Generic solutions/Biometrics/Fingerprint credential provider tile not displayed at logon screen/Don't display last signed-in and fingerprint
symptom: Fingerprint login icon/option is missing from the sign-in options after reboot or logoff. However, the fingerprint icon appears after the user logs on and locks the screen. Putting a finger on the scanner at the logon screen still works despite no tile shown.
rootCause: GPO Interactive logon: Do not display last signed-in or Do not display username at sign-in is enabled. When active, Logon UI queries credential providers without a known user context, and WinBio credential provider cannot enumerate tiles without a target user.
solution: Review and adjust GPO settings. If these GPOs must remain enabled, fingerprint will work at lock screen (where user context exists) but tiles will not appear at the initial logon screen (by design). Consider alternative authentication for initial logon.

sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Windows Hello and Modern Credential Providers/WHfB/WHFB: Looking for known solutions | tips/Generic solutions/Biometrics/Fingerprint credential provider tile not displayed at logon screen/Incorrect configuration
symptom: Fingerprint credential provider not displayed at logon on Hybrid Azure AD joined device (Windows 10 20H2). PIN and fingerprint enrollment succeeded. WinBio_oper.evtx shows error 0x80098030 (WINBIO_E_CRED_PROV_DISABLED).
rootCause: Multiple conflicting registry configurations: WinBio Credential Provider Domain Accounts key missing or set to 0, and/or GPO conflict disabling biometric credential provider. Error 0x80098030 indicates system policy has explicitly disabled Windows biometric credential provider.
solution: Set registry keys: (1) HKLM\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Winbio\Credential Provider: Enabled=1 and Domain Accounts=1 (2) Check HKLM\SOFTWARE\Policies\Microsoft\Biometrics\Credential Provider: Enabled=1 (3) Verify no conflicting GPO disables biometric credential provider. Run gpupdate /force.

sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Windows Hello and Modern Credential Providers/WHfB/WHFB: Looking for known solutions | tips/Generic solutions/Effect of renaming the Samaccountname attribute
symptom: On a Hybrid Azure AD joined device, user cannot log in using PIN after samAccountName was changed in AD. New WHfB enrollment also fails after deleting the Hello container. Error: A problem has occurred and your PIN is not available.
rootCause: After samAccountName change, the Kerberos logon request still uses the old samAccountName (cached in NGC credential). The NGC key is bound to the original samAccountName, and the rename breaks the key-to-account mapping. Known bug tracked for fix.
solution: Known bug with fix planned in a future Windows update. Workaround: user can enroll WHfB on a new device where no previous session existed. On the affected device, delete and re-enroll Hello container after the fix is applied.

sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Windows Hello and Modern Credential Providers/WHfB/WHFB: Looking for known solutions | tips/Generic solutions/MSDS-Keycredentiallink/Duplicate keyid
symptom: The msDS-KeyCredentialLink attribute on user/computer objects has duplicate KeyID entries on Windows Server 2016 and 2019 Domain Controllers, causing subsequent key-based authentications (WHfB, device auth) to fail.
rootCause: Bug in SamDsReplaceSamNgcKey that writes duplicate KeyID entries to the msDS-KeyCredentialLink attribute. Fixed in KB4565511 but requires a registry key to activate on Windows Server 2016 DCs.
solution: Install KB4565511 on all Domain Controllers. For Windows Server 2016 DCs, additionally create: HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Services\NTDS\Parameters\AllowLogonWithDuplicateNGCKey (DWORD)=1. Windows Server 2019 DCs do not need the registry key.

sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Windows Hello and Modern Credential Providers/WHfB/WHFB: Looking for known solutions | tips/Generic solutions/MSDS-Keycredentiallink/Msds-keycredentiallink in AD was not updated . Object is an InetOrgperson
symptom: Enterprise PRT is not received when doing Windows Hello for Business logon. AAD Operational log shows authentication error. ADFS server reports could not find the UserKey. The msds-KeyCredentialLink attribute in AD is blank for the user.
rootCause: The user object class is inetOrgPerson instead of user. Azure AD Connect default outbound sync rule for NGC key is scoped to person class and does not match inetOrgPerson objects. Therefore msds-KeyCredentialLink is never written back to AD.
solution: Create a new Azure AD Connect outbound sync rule scoped for inetOrgPerson: New-ADSyncRule with source attribute deviceKey mapped to destination msDS-KeyCredentialLink with FlowType Direct and ValueMergeType Update. Add scope condition for sourceAnchor ISNOTNULL.

symptom: Hybrid WHfB key trust: users can enroll and sign in with WHfB only once, second sign-in fails until delta sync completes. WHfB public key removed from local AD user msds-keycredentiallink attribute after first successful sign-in.
rootCause: Missing Windows Server 2016/2019 Domain Controller updates (KB4593226 for 2016 DC, KB4592440 for 2019 DC) cause the WHfB public key to be removed from the keycredentiallink attribute after first logon.
solution: Install KB4593226 on all Windows Server 2016 DCs and KB4592440 (December 8, 2020 update, OS Build 17763.1637) on all Windows Server 2019 DCs.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Windows Hello and Modern Credential Providers/WHfB/WHFB: Looking for known solutions | tips/Generic solutions/MSDS-Keycredentiallink/Users were able to enroll for WHfB, and they can sign in only once

symptom: msDs-KeyCredentialLink attribute is not being synchronized by Microsoft Entra Connect (Azure AD Connect) to Azure AD. WHfB enrollment may fail or key data not replicated.
rootCause: Entra Connect (AADConnect) was deployed before AD schema was upgraded to Windows Server 2016. The sync service account lacks required permissions on the msDs-KeyCredentialLink attribute.
solution: Upgrade AD schema to Windows Server 2016 level, then grant the Entra Connect sync account read/write permissions on the msDs-KeyCredentialLink attribute for user objects.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Windows Hello and Modern Credential Providers/WHfB/WHFB: Looking for known solutions | tips/Generic solutions/MSDS-Keycredentiallink/msDs-KeyCredentialslLink attribute is not being synchronized

symptom: PIN removal option is greyed out in Windows Settings sign-in options. User cannot remove their Windows Hello PIN.
rootCause: By-design behavior: when WHfB is enabled by policy, PIN removal is blocked in Settings. For convenience PIN (non-WHfB), removal requires disabling Windows Hello through GPO.
solution: For convenience PIN: disable Windows Hello via GPO (AllowDomainPINLogon=0) or registry. For WHfB PIN: use 'certutil -deletehellocontainer' to remove the entire WHfB container including PIN and biometrics. Disable WHfB policy (Computer GPO or User GPO) if the goal is to remove the feature entirely.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Windows Hello and Modern Credential Providers/WHfB/WHFB: Looking for known solutions | tips/Generic solutions/PIN Removal

symptom: WHfB provisioning fails with error ClearTemporaryWebDataAsyncFailed Value Fatal. The CloudExperienceHost (CXH) UI window appears and disappears quickly during PIN setup.
rootCause: Missing capability SID for CloudExperienceHost (CXH) application breaks the OOBE/CXH experience, preventing the provisioning UI from functioning.
solution: Apply the fix for missing capability SID for CXH. Refer to internal article: Missing capability SID for CXH can break OOBE/CXH experience.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Windows Hello and Modern Credential Providers/WHfB/WHFB: Looking for known solutions | tips/Generic solutions/Provisioning/'ClearTemporaryWebDataAsyncFailed', Value: 'Fatal'

symptom: WHfB provisioning fails with error EPT_S_NOT_REGISTERED (0x800706d9 / 1753). Appears as Application Event ID 57 Microsoft Passport Key Storage Provider not loaded every ~8 hours, or PIN provisioning failure 0x800706d9 during NGC key registration.
rootCause: NgcSvc (Microsoft Passport) and NgcCtnrSvc (Microsoft Passport Container) services are disabled on the device. The KSP is registered but the backing services are not running, causing RPC endpoint mapper errors.
solution: Enable and start both NgcSvc and NgcCtnrSvc services. Verify with certutil -csplist that Microsoft Passport Key Storage Provider no longer returns endpoint mapper errors.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Windows Hello and Modern Credential Providers/WHfB/WHFB: Looking for known solutions | tips/Generic solutions/Provisioning/EPT_S_NOT_REGISTERED during provisioning or in event logs

symptom: PIN provisioning fails with error code 0x801c03f2. ASC audit logs show Update user by Device Registration Service with failure and PropertyUpdateNotAllowedException. DRS cannot update ProxyAddresses for DirSync-mastered user objects.
rootCause: Device Registration Service attempts to update ProxyAddresses on a DirSync-mastered user object in Azure AD, but this is blocked because on-premises mastered attributes cannot be updated in the cloud.
solution: Ask the customer to trigger any update to the user ProxyAddresses attribute on-premises (in local AD). This forces a recalculation of the Mail attribute and resolves the sync conflict, unblocking WHfB provisioning.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Windows Hello and Modern Credential Providers/WHfB/WHFB: Looking for known solutions | tips/Generic solutions/Provisioning/Pin provisioning failure error code 0x801c03f2

symptom: Users experience a black screen upon login when WHfB GPO is enabled. CTRL-ALT-DEL still works. Explorer.exe running but stuck launching CloudExperienceHost (CXH). WPR Trace shows ProcessLifetimeManager gets ACCESS DENIED when enabling DebugMode for CXH.
rootCause: Invalid permissions on an undocumented registry key prevent CloudExperienceHost (CXH) from launching. Procmon shows ACCESS DENIED errors from registry permission issues.
solution: Restore correct default registry permissions from a clean/reference machine. If issue persists: capture full memory dump, WPR trace (wpr.exe -start AppLifecycle.wprp!AppLifecycle.Light -filemode), Procmon trace, and registry permission output for further analysis.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Windows Hello and Modern Credential Providers/WHfB/WHFB: Looking for known solutions | tips/Generic solutions/Provisioning/The enrollment application which never launched (SE401P)

symptom: WHfB PIN setup hangs indefinitely with spinning donut during first login after domain join and MFA setup. The PIN dialog exists but cannot be brought into focus. Dump analysis shows PIN prompt window painted in wrong Z-band.
rootCause: User Account Control (UAC) is disabled (EnableLUA=0). When UAC is off, the PIN prompt dialog is rendered in the wrong Z-band (band #0), making it invisible/unreachable behind other windows.
solution: Enable UAC by setting registry key HKLM\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Policies\System\EnableLUA = 1 (REG_DWORD). Reboot the machine.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Windows Hello and Modern Credential Providers/WHfB/WHFB: Looking for known solutions | tips/Generic solutions/Provisioning/The hidden PIN prompt window (SE401P)

symptom: RDP connection with WHfB biometric (face/fingerprint) fails with 'The client certificate does not contain a valid UPN'. PIN-based RDP works fine. Key Trust deployment with additional RDP certificate.
rootCause: In Key Trust deployment, biometric authentication uses the self-signed WHfB certificate (lacks SmartCard Logon EKU) instead of the deployed RDP certificate. Only PIN can select the correct certificate for RDP.
solution: Re-enroll WHfB certificate using Generate-CertificateRequest.ps1 with WHfBCertificateAuthentication template, submit to PKI CA via certreq.exe, accept via certreq -accept. Biometrics should then work for RDP. Alternative: enable Remote Credential Guard (hardware requirements on session host).
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Windows Hello and Modern Credential Providers/WHfB/WHFB: Looking for known solutions | tips/Generic solutions/RDP/RDP fail with WHfB biometric - The client certificate does not contain a valid UPN

symptom: Certificate not displayed in CredUI prompt during RDP on AADJ device. Neither PIN nor biometric shown, no Certificate credential provider visible. certutil shows only key trust key, certificate uses Microsoft Platform Crypto Provider (PCPKSP) instead of Passport KSP.
rootCause: Certificate enrolled using PKCS instead of SCEP/NDES. Certificate template misconfigured: pKIDefaultCSPs missing Microsoft Passport Key Storage Provider, msPKI-Private-Key-Flag missing CTPRIVATEKEY_FLAG_REQUIRE_SAME_KEY_RENEWAL, msPKI-Certificate-Name-Flag missing required UPN and directory path flags.
solution: Fix certificate template per Microsoft docs step 17 of Deploying Certificates to Key Trust Users: set pKIDefaultCSPs to Microsoft Passport Key Storage Provider, add CTPRIVATEKEY_FLAG_REQUIRE_SAME_KEY_RENEWAL, set msPKI-Certificate-Name-Flag with CT_FLAG_SUBJECT_ALT_REQUIRE_UPN and CT_FLAG_SUBJECT_REQUIRE_DIRECTORY_PATH. Use SCEP/NDES enrollment not PKCS.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Windows Hello and Modern Credential Providers/WHfB/WHFB: Looking for known solutions | tips/Generic solutions/RDP/RDP on AADJ device :  credui is not showing the certificate

symptom: WHfB credential provider shows as default for RDP connections. Users prompted for FaceID/PIN instead of password when connecting to RDS environment that only supports password auth.
rootCause: By design: no built-in mechanism to disable a credential provider for specific scenarios only. WHfB credential provider is used globally. DCR 34707977 exists but not implemented.
solution: User can click 'other sign-in options' and select password. Only alternative is disabling NLA to shift credential prompt to server side (NOT recommended). Case should be closed as rejected (by-design limitation).
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Windows Hello and Modern Credential Providers/WHfB/WHFB: Looking for known solutions | tips/Generic solutions/RDP/Rdp and credui selection & configuration

symptom: WHfB with key trust does not work for RDP to WVD/AVD or RDS broker scenarios. enablerdsaadauth RDP property not supported in RDS Broker scenario.
rootCause: WHfB is NOT compatible with RDS SSO and NOT compatible with Credential delegation GPO. enablerdsaadauth only supported for Azure Virtual Desktop direct connections, not RDS Broker.
solution: Follow certificate configuration at learn.microsoft.com/windows/security/identity-protection/hello-for-business/rdp-sign-in for WHfB + RDP. This is the only supported path.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Windows Hello and Modern Credential Providers/WHfB/WHFB: Looking for known solutions | tips/Generic solutions/RDP/key trust + RDP - WVD or AVD

symptom: WHfB PIN provisioning not triggered despite GPO enabled. DC has wrong certificate type, Azure endpoints blocked, enrollment retry counter exhausted.
rootCause: Multiple misconfigurations: (1) DC had client certificate instead of KDC authentication certificate, (2) Required Azure endpoints blocked (login.microsoftonline.com, account.live.com, accountalt.azureedge.net, secure.aadcdn.microsoftonline-p.com, enterpriseregistration.windows.net), (3) NumOfAttRetry exhausted, (4) Credential Manager disabled on client.
solution: Fix all: (1) Install correct KDC auth certificate on DC, run certutil -pulse. (2) Allow all required Azure/O365 URLs. (3) Reset HKCU\Software\Microsoft\Windows NT\CurrentVersion\WorkplaceJoin\AADNGC\NumOfAttRetry to 0. (4) Enable Credential Manager feature on client.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Windows Hello and Modern Credential Providers/WHfB/WHFB: Looking for known solutions | tips/Hybrid Certificated Trust/Example of a total case mess

symptom: WHfB PIN setup completes but user cannot log on with PIN - certificate used is not trusted. CA certificate in NTAuth store does not match CA that issued the ADFS RA certificate after CA renewal.
rootCause: NTAuth store contains outdated CA certificate (pre-renewal). The renewed CA certificate was not published to NTAuth. CRL distribution only published to INETPUB path, not LDAP.
solution: (1) Publish renewed CA certificate to NTAuth. (2) Enable LDAP CDP with publish CRLs and include in CDP extension. (3) Restart ADCS. (4) Renew certificates with same key on client and all DCs. (5) Restart KDC service on DCs. (6) Publish new CRL.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Windows Hello and Modern Credential Providers/WHfB/WHFB: Looking for known solutions | tips/Hybrid Certificated Trust/Example of a total case mess

symptom: WHfB PIN logon fails with 'PIN is not available cannot be verified' even though certificate is trusted. Issue depends on which PKI cluster node issues the certificate.
rootCause: ADCS cluster with two nodes not synced. Node 2 certificates fail. Additionally node 1 CRL expired in Active Directory.
solution: Synchronize both PKI cluster nodes. Ensure CRL is valid and published to AD from all CA nodes. Verify certificate chain via whoami /all shows NT AUTHORITY\This Organization Certificate.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Windows Hello and Modern Credential Providers/WHfB/WHFB: Looking for known solutions | tips/Hybrid Certificated Trust/Example of a total case mess

symptom: WHfB hybrid: user auth sent to wrong Azure AD tenant because UPN suffix domain is verified in different tenant than device tenant. WHfB requires user and device in same tenant.
rootCause: UPN suffix domain verified in tenant A while device registered in tenant B. Windows client uses UPN suffix for user auth first then falls back to device domain. Cross-tenant WHfB not supported.
solution: (1) Add/verify UPN domain in same tenant as device, OR use non-verified non-routable domain in UPN. Also for ADFS: set SupportsMfa=$True, set PromptLoginBehavior to Disabled or NativeSupport, verify ADFS issuance rules include authnmethodsreferences claim.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Windows Hello and Modern Credential Providers/WHfB/WHFB: Looking for known solutions | tips/Hybrid Certificated Trust/Example of a total case mess

symptom: Need to identify which Windows Hello for Business (WHFB) user certificate was issued on a lost or stolen laptop in hybrid certificate trust environment
rootCause: WHFB certificates are tied to NGC keys stored in msDS-KeyCredentialLink attribute; identifying the specific certificate requires correlating device ID with key credential entries in AD and Azure AD
solution: Use the msDS-KeyCredentialLink attribute in Active Directory to identify the key associated with the lost device. Cross-reference the device ID using Azure AD portal or Graph Explorer (GET https://graph.microsoft.com/v1.0/devices?$filter=displayName eq 'deviceName'). Revoke the WHFB certificate via the CA. Note: even after revocation, existing Kerberos tickets remain valid until they expire.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Windows Hello and Modern Credential Providers/WHfB/WHFB: Looking for known solutions | tips/Hybrid Certificated Trust/How to identify which WHFB user certificate was issued on lost or stolen laptop

symptom: WHfB enrolled successfully but missing smartcard logon certificate. Event 365 in User Device Registration log: Unable to enroll for a logon certificate using a Registration Authority
rootCause: Communication issues between client and ADFS or Certificate Authority during initial WHFB enrollment prevented the smartcard logon certificate from being issued. Certificate autoenrollment Group Policy is not enabled, so the certificate is never re-requested after the initial failure.
solution: Enable certificate autoenrollment via Group Policy: Computer Configuration > Policies > Windows Settings > Security Settings > Public Key Policies > Certificate Services Client - Auto-Enrollment. Alternatively, set registry key HKCU\Software\Microsoft\Cryptography\AutoEnrollment\AEPolicy = 7. Enable Update certificates that use certificate templates, Renew expired certificates, and Update and install certificates.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Windows Hello and Modern Credential Providers/WHfB/WHFB: Looking for known solutions | tips/Hybrid Certificated Trust/When is the certificate re-issued after an initial enroll

symptom: Unable to log on to Windows 10 using Windows Hello for Business (WHFB) Key Trust. Error message: Your Credential could not be verified. Kerberos trace shows KDC certificate validation failure.
rootCause: The Issuing CA certificate used to issue the KDC certificate is not published in the NTAuth store in Active Directory. The KDC cannot validate its own certificate chain, causing PKINIT authentication to fail.
solution: Publish the Issuing CA certificate to the NTAuth store: certutil -dspublish <IssuingCA.cer> NTAuth. Verify with: certutil -viewstore ldap:///CN=NTAuthCertificates,CN=Public Key Services,CN=Services,CN=Configuration,DC=domain,DC=com. After publishing, wait for AD replication and run certutil -pulse on the DC to refresh.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Windows Hello and Modern Credential Providers/WHfB/WHFB: Looking for known solutions | tips/Hybrid Key Trust/"Your Credential could not be verified"

symptom: WHFB PIN login shows Feature Unavailable. Biometrics shows Unable to verify Credentials. Use PIN. Only password works. DC Event System/KDC ID 19: KDC unable to use PKINIT. Client Kerberos/Operational ID 104.
rootCause: The Domain Controller does not have a Kerberos Authentication certificate enrolled. Without this certificate, the KDC cannot perform PKINIT (Public Key Cryptography for Initial Authentication) required for WHFB key trust sign-in.
solution: Enroll a Kerberos Authentication certificate on the Domain Controller. Verify prerequisites: 1) Ensure the Kerberos Authentication certificate template is published on the CA. 2) The DC must have autoenrollment enabled. 3) Run certutil -pulse to trigger enrollment. 4) Verify the certificate is installed: certutil -store my Kerberos Authentication.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Windows Hello and Modern Credential Providers/WHfB/WHFB: Looking for known solutions | tips/Hybrid Key Trust/Logging using PIN fails with error "feature unavailable."

symptom: WHFB hybrid key trust authentication fails with STATUS_NO_LOGON_SERVERS (0xc000005e) immediately after provisioning. PIN was set up successfully but login with PIN fails.
rootCause: The user public key (msds-keycredentiallink) has not yet been synced from Azure AD to on-premises Active Directory by Azure AD Connect. The DC cannot find the user NGC key to validate the PKINIT request.
solution: Wait for Azure AD Connect sync cycle to complete (default interval: 30 minutes). To expedite, manually trigger a delta sync: Start-ADSyncSyncCycle -PolicyType Delta. After sync, verify msds-keycredentiallink is populated on the user object in on-prem AD. Ensure DC is Windows Server 2016+ with line-of-sight to the client.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Windows Hello and Modern Credential Providers/WHfB/WHFB: Looking for known solutions | tips/Hybrid Key Trust/STATUS_NO_LOGON_SERVERS 0xc000005e

symptom: WHFB PIN login fails with That option is temporarily unavailable after successful PIN setup on hybrid key trust. KDC returns KDC_ERR_CLIENT_NAME_MISMATCH. Network trace shows PKINIT AS-REQ sent but KDC cannot match user identity.
rootCause: msDS-KeyCredentialLink attribute in on-premises AD does not match Azure AD due to device write-back disabled or misconfigured in Azure AD Connect. KDC retrieves public key from self-signed certificate but cannot find matching key in AD (KdcNormalize returns 0x6 KDC_ERR_C_PRINCIPAL_UNKNOWN).
solution: Re-enable device write-back in Azure AD Connect wizard. Trigger a full sync: Start-ADSyncSyncCycle -PolicyType Initial. Verify msDS-KeyCredentialLink on the user object in AD matches the NGC key in Azure AD. Check that DC is Server 2016+. After fix, user must re-provision WHFB (certutil -deletehellocontainer).
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Windows Hello and Modern Credential Providers/WHfB/WHFB: Looking for known solutions | tips/Hybrid Key Trust/That option is temporarily unavailable/Deep dive troubleshooting steps

symptom: WHFB sign-in fails with We cant sign you in with this credential because your domain isnt available in a multi-forest hybrid key trust deployment.
rootCause: In multi-forest environments, the device forest has Domain Controllers running Windows Server 2012 R2 which do not support WHFB key trust PKINIT authentication. The device sends the AS-REQ to a DC in its local forest, but that DC cannot process the NGC key-based pre-authentication.
solution: Upgrade Domain Controllers in the device forest to Windows Server 2016 or later, which supports WHFB key trust. Alternatively, consider deploying certificate trust model instead of key trust for multi-forest scenarios. Ensure all forests have at least one 2016+ DC accessible by the client devices.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Windows Hello and Modern Credential Providers/WHfB/WHFB: Looking for known solutions | tips/Hybrid Key Trust/We can't sign you in with this credential because your domain isn't available

symptom: WHFB PIN reset does not work over the Windows lock screen. Reset password link not showing or SSPR not functioning from the lock screen on domain-joined devices.
rootCause: The Group Policy Interactive logon: Do not require CTRL+ALT+DEL is disabled (default for domain-joined PCs). On Windows 10 versions before v1909, this setting must be enabled for WHFB PIN reset and SSPR to work on the lock screen.
solution: Enable the policy Interactive logon: Do not require CTRL+ALT+DEL via Group Policy or registry (HKLM\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Policies\System\DisableCAD = 1). Note: For Windows 10 v1909 and later, this policy is no longer required for lock screen PIN reset.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Windows Hello and Modern Credential Providers/WHfB/WHFB: Looking for known solutions | tips/PIN Reset/Ctrl+Alt+Del behavior with Pin reset , Fingerprint at logonui or SSPR

symptom: After setting WHFB PIN recovery policy to Not configured in Intune, AAD-only joined clients still perform non-destructive PIN reset. Clicking I forgot my PIN redirects to cred.microsoft.com even after certutil -deletehellocontainer.
rootCause: The PIN recovery policy is cached on the client and not properly reverted when the Intune policy is set to Not configured. The registry under HKLM\Software\Microsoft\Policies\PassportForWork\{TenantID} still retains the EnablePinRecovery setting.
solution: Check DSREGCMD /Status output for the reset path type in the User State section (should show Destructive Only for default). Verify and manually clear the registry at HKLM\Software\Microsoft\Policies\PassportForWork\{TenantGUID} - set EnablePinRecovery to 0 or delete the key. Then run certutil -deletehellocontainer and re-provision WHFB.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Windows Hello and Modern Credential Providers/WHfB/WHFB: Looking for known solutions | tips/PIN Reset/Disable non-destructive PIN-Reset

symptom: Need to manually trigger destructive PIN reset via command line for testing or troubleshooting purposes.
rootCause: Standard UI-based PIN reset flow may not be accessible or may follow the non-destructive path. A command-line method is needed to directly invoke the destructive PIN reset CXH scenario.
solution: Run the CXH (Customer Experience Hub) scenario command: ms-cxh://NTHAADNGCRESETDESTRUCTIVE. This directly invokes the destructive PIN reset flow, bypassing the normal UI path. Use DsrGetCxhScenarioInfo to verify the scenario ID.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Windows Hello and Modern Credential Providers/WHfB/WHFB: Looking for known solutions | tips/PIN Reset/Execute Pin reset in command line

symptom: WHFB PIN is not triggering or enrolling. tpmprotected shows no despite healthy TPM. RequireSecurityDevice is set to 1 in Group Policy. certutil -deletehellocontainer and TPM clear did not resolve.
rootCause: The Group Policy RequireSecurityDevice is set to 1 (require TPM), but the TPM precheck reports tpmprotected=no. This blocks PIN enrollment even though the TPM hardware is functional. The GPO enforcement prevents WHFB from proceeding without a confirmed TPM protection status.
solution: Set RequireSecurityDevice to 0 (disabled) in Group Policy for Hybrid Azure AD Joined devices with certificate trust. Then run gpupdate /force to apply. Re-attempt WHFB enrollment. If TPM is genuinely healthy (certutil -v -tpminfo shows fIsReady=1), the issue is with the GPO precheck, not the TPM itself.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Windows Hello and Modern Credential Providers/WHfB/WHFB: Looking for known solutions | tips/PIN Reset/Pin is not triggering due to tpm prechecks

symptom: Destructive PIN reset via the Logon UI (lock screen) does not work for key trust on hybrid Azure AD-joined devices.
rootCause: Destructive PIN reset from above the Lock Screen is not supported for key trust on hybrid Azure AD-joined devices. This is a product limitation, not a configuration issue.
