solution: Enable non-destructive PIN reset instead: 1) Configure the PIN recovery policy to Enabled via Intune or GPO. 2) Deploy the Microsoft PIN Reset Service. 3) With non-destructive PIN reset enabled, users can reset their PIN from the lock screen without losing their container.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Windows Hello and Modern Credential Providers/WHfB/WHFB: Looking for known solutions | tips/PIN Reset/destructive PIN Reset via Logon UI

symptom: WHFB hybrid certificate trust synchronous certificate enrollment fails with error 0xcaa20002 / MSIS9711: Unable to create Windows Hello certificate. Unable to find a NGC Key which has the same public key as the certificate request.
rootCause: ADFS cannot match the NGC key from the certificate signing request (CSR) with the key registered in Active Directory. The msDS-KeyCredentialLink has not replicated from Azure AD to on-prem AD before ADFS attempts to validate the key receipt context (krctx).
solution: 1) Ensure Windows Server 2016 KB4088889 (14393.2155) or later is installed on federation servers. 2) Verify ADFS can reach the AD domain controller and query msDS-KeyCredentialLink. 3) Check ADFS Device Registration Service Tracing/Debug logs for ValidateKeyReceiptContext and GetDeviceKeyFromKeyCredLink entries. 4) Ensure Azure AD Connect sync is completing successfully. 5) If issue persists, trigger manual delta sync and re-provision WHFB.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Windows Hello and Modern Credential Providers/WHfB/WHFB: Looking for known solutions | tips/Hybrid Certificated Trust/Issue with Synchronous Certificate Trust deployment

symptom: Windows Hello for Business user can provision PIN successfully but cannot log in using the PIN. KDC event ID 200 is logged on the domain controller indicating no suitable certificate was found for Kerberos authentication.
rootCause: Domain controller does not have a certificate with KDC Authentication and Smart Card Logon Extended Key Usage (EKU), or the issuing CA certificate is not present in the NTAuth store on the DC or client device.
solution: Check DC computer certificate store (Machine-Store.txt) for a certificate with KDC Authentication OID in EKU. Verify the issuing CA is present in the NTAuth store on both DC and client device (NTAuth-store.txt). Check KDC event log for Event ID 302 (success) or 200 (failure) in Applications and Services Logs > Microsoft > Windows > Kerberos-Key-Distribution-Center > Operational.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Windows Hello and Modern Credential Providers/WHfB/WHFB: Quick Start Check List/Quick troubleshooting steps for an authentication problem

symptom: Windows Hello for Business key trust authentication fails. Kerberos trace shows 'User has a self-signed certificate. Looking for DS 10 or above' but the client cannot locate a Windows Server 2016+ domain controller via DC Locator.
rootCause: Key trust deployments require Windows Server 2016+ DCs for PKINIT with the self-signed certificate. DC Locator with WIN10_DC flag cannot find a qualifying DC in the user's AD site. The synced user may also be missing netbiosdomainname and onpremsamaccountname attributes needed for DC locator.
solution: Check netlogon.log for DC locator calls with WIN10_DC flag. Run 'Nltest /dsgetdc:domain /DS_10' to verify 2016+ DC availability. Ensure Windows Server 2016+ DCs are deployed in each AD site where key trust users reside. Verify the user object has netbiosdomainname and onpremsamaccountname attributes populated by Azure AD Connect.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Windows Hello and Modern Credential Providers/WHfB/WHFB: Quick Start Check List/Quick troubleshooting steps for an authentication problem

symptom: Windows Hello for Business authentication fails at client side after receiving KDC response. CAPI2 event log shows certificate chain build or verification errors for the KDC certificate (lsass.exe Build Chain / Verify Chain Policy entries).
rootCause: Client device cannot validate the KDC certificate chain. The KDC certificate does not chain to a trusted root CA on the client, the certificate is expired, or Certificate Revocation List (CRL) check fails.
solution: Check CAPI2 event log for lsass.exe 'Build Chain' and 'Verify Chain Policy' entries. Verify CertVerifyRevocation, CertGetCertificateChain, ChainElement TrustStatus, and CertVerifyCertificateChainPolicy results. Ensure KDC cert root CA is in the client device trusted root store. Check CRL distribution point accessibility from the client.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Windows Hello and Modern Credential Providers/WHfB/WHFB: Quick Start Check List/Quick troubleshooting steps for an authentication problem

symptom: Windows Hello for Business authentication fails after successful PIN provisioning in hybrid key trust deployment. The msDS-KeyCredentialLink attribute on the user's on-premises AD object is empty or not populated.
rootCause: Azure AD Connect has not synchronized the user's public key from Azure AD to on-premises Active Directory. The sync rule maps searchableDeviceKey to DeviceKey (metaverse) then exports to msDS-KeyCredentialLink in AD. If this rule is missing or sync has not run, the attribute remains empty.
solution: Verify msDS-KeyCredentialLink attribute value on the user's AD object. Check Azure AD Connect sync rules for searchableDeviceKey -> DeviceKey -> msDS-KeyCredentialLink mapping. Force a delta sync cycle with 'Start-ADSyncSyncCycle -PolicyType Delta'. Check Source field in the key data: 0x0 = created in AD, 0x1 = created in AAD.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Windows Hello and Modern Credential Providers/WHfB/WHFB: References/Implementation and Advantages/AD

symptom: Windows Hello for Business PIN complexity settings configured in Intune/MDM are not applied to the device. The device enforces unexpected PIN length or complexity requirements different from the Intune policy.
rootCause: Group Policy (GP) takes precedence over Intune/MDM for Windows Hello for Business policies. When a WHFB GP registry key exists under HKLM\SOFTWARE\Policies\Microsoft\PassportForWork and is not empty, GP is treated as the policy source and Intune policy is entirely ignored. All PIN complexity policies are grouped and enforced from a single source following precedence: User GP > Computer GP > User MDM > Device MDM > Device Lock.
solution: Remove Windows Hello for Business Group Policy settings from devices managed by Intune. Do not mix GP and MDM for WHFB policies. Check registry at HKLM\SOFTWARE\Policies\Microsoft\PassportForWork for GP-set values. MDM PIN complexity is stored under HKLM\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Policies\PassportForWork\{tenantGuid}\{userSID}\Policies\PINComplexity. Note: PIN complexity changes are enforced only at next PIN change or creation.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Windows Hello and Modern Credential Providers/WHfB/WHFB: References/Implementation and Advantages/GPO/PIN complexity

symptom: Windows Hello for Business user authentication fails in certificate trust or key trust deployment. The domain controller has a valid KDC certificate but Kerberos PKINIT authentication still fails.
rootCause: The issuing CA certificate for the DC certificate is not present in the NTAuth store. WHfB uses the same PKINIT protocol as smart card logon, which requires the issuing CA to be registered in the NTAuth store. Enterprise CAs are added by default, but third-party CAs require manual configuration.
solution: Verify the DC certificate issuing CA is present in the NTAuth store using NTAuth-store.txt from collected data. For third-party CAs, manually add to NTAuth using 'certutil -dspublish <CACert> NTAuth'. Verify on both DC and client device. This applies to both certificate trust and key trust models since both use PKINIT protocol.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Windows Hello and Modern Credential Providers/WHfB/WHFB: References/Implementation and Advantages/PKI

symptom: Windows Hello for Business provisioning fails in hybrid or on-premises certificate trust deployment. ADFS cannot issue the WHfB authentication certificate for the user during the provisioning flow.
rootCause: ADFS enrollment agent certificate is not configured, has expired, or does not have proper permissions. In certificate trust deployments, ADFS acts as registration authority and must sign certificate requests using its enrollment agent certificate before forwarding to the enterprise CA.
solution: Configure the enrollment agent certificate template on ADFS server. Ensure the certificate template permissions allow ADFS service account to enroll. Verify the enterprise issuing CA trusts the enrollment agent certificate and the WHfB authentication certificate template is properly configured. Check if ADFS service can reach the CA for certificate enrollment.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Windows Hello and Modern Credential Providers/WHfB/WHFB: References/Implementation and Advantages/PKI

symptom: Unable to determine if Windows Hello issue is Convenience PIN or Windows Hello for Business (WHFB) - customer unsure which setup type is in use
rootCause: Convenience PIN and WHFB are different technologies with different support paths. Convenience PIN uses AllowDomainPINLogon policy and can only be used by pure on-premises users (not synced AAD users). WHFB is used on AAD/hybrid joined devices.
solution: Check GPO 'Turn on convenience PIN sign-in' (HKLM\Software\Policies\Microsoft\Windows\System\AllowDomainPINLogon). If registry key 'EncryptedPassword' exists under SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Authentication\LogonUI\NgcPin\Credentials, a convenience PIN has been enrolled. If convenience PIN, change SAP to 'Windows Security Technologies\Biometric, Passwordless Authentication, SSO, and Windows Hello'.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Windows Hello and Modern Credential Providers/WHfB/WHFB: Scoping/WHAT is the product : Convenience PIN or WHFB?

symptom: Customer wants to disable Windows Hello for Business PIN at Windows logon UI while keeping PIN usable for third-party application authentication
rootCause: WHFB PIN cannot be officially disabled - PIN is the required fallback mechanism when biometrics are unavailable. Disabling PIN credential provider also disables biometrics.
solution: Use 'Exclude credential providers' GPO (ExcludedCredentialProviders) to hide PIN from logon UI. The NGC credential provider still appears in app credential prompts (e.g., Edge InPrivate mode), so PIN remains usable for in-app authentication. Reference: admx.help > ExcludedCredentialProviders.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Windows Hello and Modern Credential Providers/WHfB/WHFB: Tips & Hints/Assign a default Credential provider : PIN/Disable PIN at logonui

symptom: Customer needs password credential provider at Windows logon instead of WHFB PIN, even when user last logged in with PIN
rootCause: When WHFB (NGC) is enabled, Windows uses lastloggedonprovider registry to show the last-used credential provider (PIN) at logon, making it the default for returning users
solution: Configure 'Assign a default credential provider' GPO with password credential provider CLSID. This sets password as default for 'other user' tile and first logon. Also enable 'Interactive logon: Do not display last signed-in' policy to enforce password provider on sign-out/other user tile. Lock/unlock still uses lastloggedonprovider (PIN). This combination keeps PIN working while defaulting to password on fresh logon.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Windows Hello and Modern Credential Providers/WHfB/WHFB: Tips & Hints/Assign a default Credential provider : PIN/Force password credential provider when NGC is enabled

symptom: After November 2022 Windows update, users see unexpected biometric consent prompt asking to keep signing in with face or fingerprint when using Windows Hello for Business
rootCause: November 2022 update introduced BIPA (Illinois Biometric Information Privacy Act) compliance. Consent dialog appears if biometric data stored >365 days without use, or user upgraded to Win11 21H2/22H2 without seeing updated privacy text (LearnMoreAvailable keys absent)
solution: By-design behavior (KB5019103, KB5028763). Check BioConsentNoticeShownTime under HKCU\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Authentication\WindowsHello - if present with timestamp, user already consented. If absent along with LearnMoreAvailable/LearnMoreAvailableInOOBE/LearnMoreAvailableInSCOOBE keys, consent prompt is expected. Prompt appears once then every 365 days per BIPA.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Windows Hello and Modern Credential Providers/WHfB/WHFB: Tips & Hints/Biometric consent & BioConsentNoticeShownTime

symptom: Customer wants to programmatically remove user biometric data (face/fingerprint) without affecting Windows Hello for Business PIN
rootCause: No WHfB API exists for selective biometric data removal. certutil -deletehellocontainer removes entire WHfB container including PIN.
solution: Use Windows Settings UI to remove biometric data per sensor (face/fingerprint separately). Or use WinBioRemoveCredential API for biometric-only removal. certutil -deletehellocontainer removes ALL WHfB data including PIN. Disabling biometric feature via GPO does NOT delete existing stored biometric data.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Windows Hello and Modern Credential Providers/WHfB/WHFB: Tips & Hints/Biometric consent & BioConsentNoticeShownTime

symptom: After password change, Windows Hello for Business / smartcard cached logon stops working - user forced to sign in with password before PIN/smartcard logon works again offline
rootCause: By design - smartcard/WHfB cache entries are per-credential and contain password-derived DPAPI key. When password changes, old cache entries are deleted to prevent DPAPI from receiving old password-derived keys which would cause VPN certificate decryption failure and WIP data loss
solution: No workaround - by design to prevent DPAPI corruption. User must sign on with smartcard/PIN while connected to DC to recreate cache entry. DCR filed (Bug 23529534) for per-user cache architecture. Consider WHFB cloud trust deployment to avoid certificate-related issues.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Windows Hello and Modern Credential Providers/WHfB/WHFB: Tips & Hints/Cached logon update

symptom: Confusion about differences between Certificate Trust Mixed Managed and Certificate Trust Modern Managed in WHFB hybrid AADJ deployments
rootCause: Classic certificate trust enrolls certs via ADFS Registration Authority into WHfB KSP. Modern managed is key trust with additional certificates deployed via NDES/Intune into WHfB KSP. GPO and Intune managed deployments cannot be mixed on same device.
solution: If WHfB policy delivered via GPO, must use ADFS RA for cert enrollment (Intune cannot provision). If via Intune, use Intune Certificate Connector + NDES for logon certificate. If no ADFS, must use Intune for both WHfB config and certificate deployment. Cannot mix GPO and Intune on same device.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Windows Hello and Modern Credential Providers/WHfB/WHFB: Tips & Hints/Certificates related topics/Certificate trust Mixed managed and Certificate trust Modern managed in Hybrid Deployments

symptom: WHFB certificate trust deployment may not store user certificate private key in TPM, creating security risk
rootCause: Without 'Use a hardware security device' (GPO) or RequireSecurityDevice (MDM) policy configured, WHFB key provisioning may use software storage instead of TPM
solution: Configure 'Use a hardware security device' in Group Policy or set RequireSecurityDevice via MDM/Intune to enforce TPM usage for WHFB key provisioning. Certificate trust enrolls a certificate for an already-provisioned key, so the key must be in TPM before certificate enrollment.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Windows Hello and Modern Credential Providers/WHfB/WHFB: Tips & Hints/Certificates related topics/Ensure the private key for the user certificate is stored in the TPM

symptom: Customer concerned about WHFB key trust self-signed certificate having very long validity period (30 years) and wants to configure shorter expiration
rootCause: Key trust self-signed certificate validity is non-configurable by design. Unlike PKI where CA-signed cert expiration ensures periodic identity re-verification, key trust uses direct 1:1 key-to-account registration. The x.509 v3 format requires an end date so a high value is used to bypass expiration.
solution: No configuration needed - long validity is by design and expected for key trust. To revoke trust, remove the key registration from the user account. Consider implementing WHFB cloud trust to avoid certificate-related concerns entirely.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Windows Hello and Modern Credential Providers/WHfB/WHFB: Tips & Hints/Certificates related topics/WH4B self-signed cert validity period

symptom: VPN does not accept Windows Hello for Business credential at logon in Key Trust deployment - user prompted for separate credentials after WHFB logon but not after password logon
rootCause: WHfB Key Trust certificate is self-signed with minimal information, lacks Extended Key Usages (EKUs) required by EAP-TLS, and cannot chain to trusted root authority. It exists only as a container for the key pair.
solution: WHfB Key Trust certificates cannot be used for VPN/EAP-TLS authentication. Use Certificate Trust deployment instead which issues proper CA-signed certificates with required EKUs that chain to trusted root. Alternatively use other VPN authentication methods compatible with key trust.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Windows Hello and Modern Credential Providers/WHfB/WHFB: Tips & Hints/Certificates related topics/WHFB compatibility with EAP-TLS ?

symptom: I forgot my PIN option on Windows Hello (Convenience PIN) fails with error 'This device does not meet your organizations policy' when users try to reset their PIN
rootCause: By design - Convenience PIN does not support PIN reset feature. Convenience PIN is not supported for Microsoft Entra user accounts (including synchronized identities), only for on-premises Active Directory users and local account users.
solution: Hide 'I forgot my PIN' via registry HKLM\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Authentication\LogonUI\NgcPin\EnablePinReset. Default 0xf shows for all types. Values: 0x1=Entra ID joined, 0x2=MSA, 0x4=Domain Joined, 0x8=Entra Hybrid joined. Set to exclude target device types. Advise migration to Windows Hello for Business.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Windows Hello and Modern Credential Providers/WHfB/WHFB: Tips & Hints/Convenience PIN (aka Windows Hello) and PIN Reset

symptom: After disabling Convenience PIN policy via GPO ('Turn on convenience PIN sign-in'), users can still sign in with already provisioned PINs on Windows devices
rootCause: Disabling the GPO only prevents new PIN enrollment but does not clean up existing Convenience PIN containers. If WHfB is enabled on a device with existing convenience PIN, it enrolls the user identity key into the existing container with the existing PIN.
solution: Disable convenience PIN GPO, run 'certutil -deleteHelloContainer' on each device (in user context, not elevated), reboot. Then enable WHfB and have users re-enroll. Order: disable policy -> clean up containers -> enable WHfB -> re-enroll.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Windows Hello and Modern Credential Providers/WHfB/WHFB: Tips & Hints/Disable of convenience PIN

symptom: Windows Hello for Business smart card emulation stops working when more than 10 users enroll WHfB on a shared device; users beyond the 10th lose smart card emulation capabilities
rootCause: Windows supports maximum 10 concurrent smart card readers. Each enrolled WHfB credential uses a virtualized smart card reader for smart card emulation. WHfB was not designed for shared computing environments.
solution: For shared workstations, use FIDO2 security keys or physical smart cards instead of WHfB. Do not exceed 10 WHfB enrollments per device. Going above 10 is unsupported and could break. DCR opened (05/2024) to enforce enrollment limit.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Windows Hello and Modern Credential Providers/WHfB/WHFB: Tips & Hints/Enrollment limit per device | maximum number of users using WHfB

symptom: Customer needs to identify Windows Hello for Business login events by authentication method (PIN vs face vs fingerprint) but event logging is inconsistent across biometric types
rootCause: No consistent Windows eventing exists for distinguishing WHfB sign-in methods. Fingerprint logs events 1004/1605/1702 in Biometrics log, face logs only 1605/1702 (no sensor detail), PIN has no specific client-side event.
solution: For fingerprint: check events 1004, 1605, 1702 in 'Applications and Services\Microsoft\Windows\Biometrics\Operational' (event 1004 specifies sensor). For face: events 1605, 1702 only. Check registry HKLM\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Winbio for EnrolledFactors/AvailableFactors.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Windows Hello and Modern Credential Providers/WHfB/WHFB: Tips & Hints/Events about PIN usage like for face or fingerprint

symptom: Hybrid WHfB provisioning fails with error 0x801C0451 (DSREG_E_USER_TOKEN_SWITCH_ACCOUNT) after domain migration using Quest Migration Manager. Office 365 apps also fail.
rootCause: Token broker files become stale/corrupted after domain migration. When the user UPN changes to the target domain, old token broker files conflict with the new identity, causing provisioning and O365 authentication failures.
solution: Exclude these paths during migration: %localappdata%\Packages\Microsoft.AAD.BrokerPlugin_cw5n1h2txyewy\AC\TokenBroker\Accounts, %localappdata%\Microsoft\TokenBroker\Cache, %localappdata%\Packages\Microsoft. If already failed: delete token broker files, reboot, log on with password.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Windows Hello and Modern Credential Providers/WHfB/WHFB: Tips & Hints/Exclude path during user migration

symptom: Users who enrolled WHfB on their laptop are being prompted to enroll WHfB on shared/warehouse machines that should not use WHfB
rootCause: WHfB settings applied via user-based GPO follow the user to all devices they log into, regardless of whether the device is intended for WHfB use.
solution: Apply all WHfB settings in the machine part (HKLM registry keys) instead of user part. Apply the GPO to an AD security group containing specific devices. Devices not in the group will not have WHfB settings, preventing enrollment prompts.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Windows Hello and Modern Credential Providers/WHfB/WHFB: Tips & Hints/Is there a way to exclude the users who are enrolled from being prompted to enroll WHfB on machine which are not supposed to using WHfB?

symptom: WHfB key registration fails repeatedly during provisioning due to TPM attestation failures. After exhausting retry count, client stops attempting provisioning.
rootCause: AIK (Attestation Identity Key) certificate enrollment fails because cloud CA for TPM line is not set up, no network connectivity to fetch EK certificate, or OOBE pre-generated key pool not populated. Registry NumOfAttRetry counts down from 3 to 0.
solution: Check HKCU\Software\Microsoft\Windows NT\CurrentVersion\WorkplaceJoin\AADNGC\NumOfAttRetry (DWORD). Check AIK status: 'certutil -getreg ngc\AIKCertEnroll'. Ensure cloud CA is configured for the TPM line. If retry count exhausted after CA setup, manually trigger AIK enrollment task.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Windows Hello and Modern Credential Providers/WHfB/WHFB: Tips & Hints/NumOfAttRetry

symptom: WHfB PIN expires silently without any notification to the user. Customer wants PIN expiry notification similar to AD password expiry notification.
rootCause: No PIN expiry notification feature exists in Windows. There is no feature in the pipeline for PIN expiry notification (as of Feb 2023).
solution: No workaround available. Inform customer that silent PIN expiry is expected behavior when PIN expiry policy is configured via GPO. Feature not in the pipeline.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Windows Hello and Modern Credential Providers/WHfB/WHFB: Tips & Hints/PIN Expiry Notification

symptom: SSO with Windows Hello for Business does not work with Remote Desktop Services (RDS). Customer expects WHfB to provide seamless SSO to RDS-published applications.
rootCause: By design - WHfB SSO is not supported with RDS.
solution: Inform customer that WHfB SSO with RDS is not supported by design. Users must authenticate separately for RDS sessions.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Windows Hello and Modern Credential Providers/WHfB/WHFB: Tips & Hints/RDS SSO with WHfB - Considerations

symptom: Launching 2nd RemoteApp via WHfB from a different RDS session collection fails with 'authentication error, LSA cannot be contacted due to expired password' while first session is active. Works with username/password.
rootCause: Known bug/limitation affecting both smart cards and WHfB with RDS using mstsc. Concurrent WHfB-authenticated sessions across different RDS session collections are not supported.
solution: Set registry HKCU\Software\Microsoft\Workspaces, create REG_DWORD fWorkspaceSSOEnabled=0. This disables workspace SSO and allows concurrent sessions across different collections to authenticate.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Windows Hello and Modern Credential Providers/WHfB/WHFB: Tips & Hints/RDS SSO with WHfB - Considerations

symptom: Windows system clock resets to dates months or years off from the current time after reboot, especially on machines without outgoing SSL traffic. No events in System/Security/Time-Service logs indicate the cause.
rootCause: Secure Time Seeding (STS) feature in Windows Time service uses stale SSL metadata from the registry. On restart without outgoing SSL traffic, the feature does not clear/update old registry data and sets the clock based on stale SecureTimeEstimated registry values.
solution: Disable STS via registry: net stop w32time; reg add HKLM\System\CurrentControlSet\Services\W32Time\Config /v UtilizeSslTimeData /t REG_DWORD /d 0 /f; net start w32time. Can also disable via GPO (Computer Configuration > Administrative Templates > System > Windows Time Service > Global Configuration Settings). Check SecureTimeEstimated registry under HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Services\W32Time\SecureTimeLimits to confirm STS as cause. Enable w32tm debug log to see 'ClockDispln Discipline: *SET*SECURE*TIME*' entry.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Windows Time/Workflow: Windows Time: Looking for known solutions/Secure Time Seeding (STS) known issue

symptom: VDI (Virtual Desktop Infrastructure) machines running Windows Server 2016 or Windows 10 experience unexpected time jumps despite STS bug being fixed in later OS versions.
rootCause: On VDI machines, the Secure Time Seeding registry data (SecureTimeEstimated under HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Services\W32Time\SecureTimeLimits) is not cleared between sessions, causing stale time data to be applied on reboot even on patched OS versions.
solution: Disable Secure Time Seeding on VDI golden image: reg add HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\System\CurrentControlSet\Services\W32Time\Config /v UtilizeSslTimeData /t REG_DWORD /d 0 /f, then restart w32time service. Apply this to the VDI template/golden image to prevent recurrence across all VDI sessions.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Windows Time/Workflow: Windows Time: WS2016 | Win10 new features

symptom: Virtualized Domain Controller experiences time skew or unexpected time synchronization changes. System Event ID 24, 29, or 38 from Microsoft-Windows-Time-Service may appear.
rootCause: VMICTimeProvider (Hyper-V/VMware time integration service) is enabled on the virtualized DC, causing it to synchronize time from the hypervisor host instead of the AD domain hierarchy. This conflicts with the expected NT5DS time sync and can cause time drift across the domain.
solution: Disable VMICTimeProvider on virtualized DCs: reg add HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Services\W32Time\TimeProviders\VMICTimeProvider /v Enabled /t reg_dword /d 0. Also disable time sync from hypervisor side: Hyper-V VM Settings > Integration Services > uncheck Time synchronization; VMware: disable periodic time sync in VMware Tools. Restart w32time service after changes.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Windows Time/Workflow: Windows Time: Looking for known solutions/W32Time Configuration issues

symptom: Domain-wide time drift observed. PDC Emulator of the forest root domain is not synchronizing with an external NTP source. w32tm /query /source shows Local CMOS Clock or another DC instead of an external time server on the PDCe.
rootCause: The PDC Emulator of the forest root domain has Type set to NT5DS instead of NTP, so it synchronizes from the domain hierarchy (which loops back to itself) instead of an authoritative external time source. All other DCs and domain members inherit this drifting time.
solution: Configure the PDCe with an external NTP source: w32tm /config /manualpeerlist:"time.windows.com,0x9" /syncfromflags:manual /reliable:YES /update. Restart w32time, then force resync: w32tm /resync. Verify with w32tm /query /source. All other domain members should use Type: NT5DS (w32tm /config /syncfromflags:domhier /update).
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Windows Time/Workflow: Windows Time: Looking for known solutions/W32Time Configuration issues

symptom: Windows Time synchronization fails when configured to sync with a non-Windows NTP server. Time source shows as Local CMOS Clock despite NTP server being reachable and UDP port 123 open.
rootCause: Windows Domain Controllers use Symmetric Active mode (flag 0x4) by default for NTP requests. Non-Windows NTP servers may only respond to Client mode (0x8) requests and reject symmetric active queries.
solution: Change the NTP server flag to use Client mode: w32tm /config /manualpeerlist:"<ntp-server>,0x8" /syncfromflags:manual /update. For combined SpecialInterval + Client mode use flag 0x9. Restart w32time and verify with w32tm /resync and w32tm /query /source.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Windows Time/Workflow: Windows Time: Looking for known solutions/W32Time Configuration issues

symptom: Cannot reliably detect if a user has provisioned Windows Hello for Business (WHfB) credential on a device; checking certificate in user personal store is unreliable (fails with bad biometric drivers)
rootCause: No officially documented detection method used; certificate-based detection is inconsistent because certificate population depends on biometric driver behavior
solution: Check registry key HKCU\Software\Microsoft\Windows NT\CurrentVersion\WorkplaceJoin\AADNGC\{NGC-CREDENTIAL-GUID} (exists only when WHfB provisioned, destroyed by certutil -deleteHelloContainer). Better long-term approach: use Windows API KeyCredentialManagerGetInformation from KeyCredMgr.dll via PowerShell/native code.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Windows Hello and Modern Credential Providers/WHfB/WHFB: Tips & Hints/Who is using WHfB?/How to authoritatively, programmatically, determine if a user has provisioned WHfB

symptom: Windows Hello PIN or biometric sign-in fails on Device A after user changed their account password on Device B; sign-in attempt using PIN is rejected
rootCause: When password is changed on a different device, the original device Windows Hello credentials reference the old password and become outdated. This only affects non-WHfB (convenience) Hello setups; WHfB uses key/certificate-based auth unaffected by password changes.
solution: On the affected device, sign in using the new password first, then re-enroll Hello PIN/biometric. For WHfB deployments, password changes do not impact Hello sign-in. To avoid confusion, deploy Windows Hello for Business instead of convenience Hello.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Windows Hello and Modern Credential Providers/WHfB/WHFB: Tips & Hints/Windows Hello and password changes

symptom: Documentation shows Microsoft will deprecate virtual smartcards in the near future warning; customer asks about impact and migration path for virtual smart card (VSC) deployments
rootCause: Microsoft is deprecating virtual smart cards (TPM-based VSCs) in favor of modern authentication methods like WHfB and FIDO2
solution: Plan migration to alternatives: 1) Windows Hello for Business for passwordless auth (recommended), 2) Physical smart cards for certificate-based auth, 3) Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA). Steps: assess current VSC usage, evaluate alternatives, plan migration timeline, communicate to users, execute transition.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Windows Hello and Modern Credential Providers/WHfB/WHFB: Tips & Hints/deprecate virtual smart cards

symptom: Web sign-in option/icon does not appear at Windows logon after deploying Passwordless experience and Web sign-in via Intune policy; only WHfB/PIN option is shown despite EnablePasswordlessExperience and EnableWebSignIn both set to 1
rootCause: GPO ExcludedCredentialProviders policy inadvertently includes the Cloud Experience Credential Provider GUID {C5D7540A-CD51-453B-B22B-05305BA03F07} alongside the Password provider GUID, causing LogonUI to exclude both providers. Visible in credprovauthuitrace.etl: ExcludeCredentialProviderByPolicy for both GUIDs.
solution: Remove Cloud Experience Credential Provider GUID {C5D7540A-CD51-453B-B22B-05305BA03F07} from ExcludedCredentialProviders at HKLM\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Policies\System\ExcludedCredentialProviders. Only exclude Password provider {60b78e88-ead8-445c-9cfd-0b87f74ea6cd}. Verify fix with ProcMon filtering on ExcludedCredentialProviders path.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Windows Hello and Modern Credential Providers/WebSign-in Support for LogonUI/Hands-On lab/Web sign-in troubleshooting scenario

symptom: Legacy LAPS ms-MCS-AdmPwd attribute not available on Read-Only Domain Controller (RODC); administrators in DMZ site cannot query LAPS password via LAPS UI or PowerShell from RODC
rootCause: ms-MCS-AdmPwd is part of the RODC Filtered Attribute Set (FAS) by default, so it is not replicated to RODCs. This is by design to prevent passwords from being stored on RODCs in less-secure locations.
solution: Query LAPS password from a LAN machine with access to a Read-Write Domain Controller (RWDC) instead. If RODC replication is required (not recommended): change the 10th bit of searchFlags attribute for ms-MCS-AdmPwd schema object to 0 (subtract 512 from current searchFlags value). Use ADSIEdit or ldifde to modify the schema attribute.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Windows LAPS/Legacy LAPS or LAPSv1/Workflow: LAPS : RODC

symptom: Legacy LAPS UI shows blank/empty password when administrator tries to view local administrator password for a computer
rootCause: The administrator running LAPS UI lacks extended rights (Read ms-MCS-AdmPwd permission) on the Organizational Unit (OU) where the computer object resides
solution: Use PowerShell to check permissions: Import-module AdmPwd.PS; Find-AdmPwdExtendedrights -identity OU_DistinguishedName | FT -AutoSize. Grant appropriate read permissions on the OU for the admin user/group.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Windows LAPS/Legacy LAPS or LAPSv1/Workflow: LAPS: Looking for Known Solutions

symptom: LAPS attributes (ms-MCS-AdmPwd, ms-MCS-AdmPwdExpirationTime) missing from computer object properties after running Update-AdmPwdADSchema to extend the AD schema
rootCause: Active Directory replication latency after schema extension; the schema changes have not yet replicated to all domain controllers
solution: Confirm Update-AdmPwdADSchema cmdlet completed without errors. Force AD replication using repadmin /syncall or check replication status with repadmin /replsummary. Wait for replication to complete, then verify attributes appear.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Windows LAPS/Legacy LAPS or LAPSv1/Workflow: LAPS: Looking for Known Solutions

symptom: Legacy LAPS fails to set local administrator password; Event ID 7 with error code 0x80070032 in Application event log under AdmPwd source fires during gpupdate
rootCause: SELF principal (computer account SYSTEM identity) lacks write permissions to ms-MCS-AdmPwd and ms-MCS-AdmPwdExpirationTime attributes on the OU where the managed computer account resides
solution: Grant SELF permission on the OU: Import-module AdmPwd.PS; Set-AdmPwdComputerSelfPermission -OrgUnit OU=target,DC=contoso,DC=com. Alternatively, verify SELF ACE is present with write access to ms-Mcs-AdmPwd and ms-Mcs-AdmPwdExpirationTime using LDP or ADSIEdit.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Windows LAPS/Legacy LAPS or LAPSv1/Workflow: LAPS: Looking for Known Solutions

symptom: Customer needs to revoke refresh tokens using Microsoft Graph PowerShell but only knows old MSOnline Set-MsolUser -StsRefreshTokensValidFrom method. StsRefreshTokensValidFrom is now read-only in Graph.
rootCause: MSOnline and AzureAD PowerShell modules are deprecated. StsRefreshTokensValidFrom property renamed to signInSessionsValidFromDateTime/refreshTokensValidFromDateTime and is read-only. Must use dedicated Graph API.
solution: Use Revoke-MgUserSignInSession -UserId user@contoso.com in Microsoft Graph PowerShell to revoke refresh tokens and sessions. This replaces the old Set-MsolUser -StsRefreshTokensValidFrom approach.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/PowerShell Scenarios/MS Graph Powershell/Microsoft Graph PowerShell Common Migration Mappings

symptom: MSOnline/AzureAD PowerShell license assignment cmdlets (Set-MsolUserLicense, Set-AzureADUserLicense) or Azure AD Graph assignLicense API fail with errors after retirement date. Scripts relying on legacy licensing APIs break.
rootCause: Microsoft retired MSOL and AAD Graph license assignment APIs (write operations) on 2023-03-31 (extended to 2024-10-31 for exception customers). New tenants blocked from 2022-11-01. These APIs are replaced by Microsoft Cloud Licensing via MS Graph.
solution: Migrate to Microsoft Graph PowerShell Set-MgUserLicense or Microsoft Graph API assignLicense endpoint. For PowerShell migration, see Find-MgGraphCommand for mapping old cmdlets. Docs: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/powershell/microsoftgraph/azuread-msoline-cmdlet-map
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/PowerShell Scenarios/MSOL and AAD Graph license assignment APIs retirement

symptom: Group Policy Advanced Audit settings not applying. auditpol /get /category:* shows settings not configured. GPO appears in gpresult /r but audit events are not logging. GPSVC log shows error 0x80070002 (FILE_NOT_FOUND) when processing Audit Policy CSE.
rootCause: An older GPO references the Advanced Audit Policy CSE ({F3CCC681-B74C-4060-9F26-CD84525DCA2A}) in its gPCMachineExtensionNames attribute, but the corresponding audit.csv file is missing from that GPO GPT folder (SYSVOL Policies {GUID} Machine Microsoft Windows NT Audit audit.csv).
solution: Run PowerShell script to find GPOs calling Advanced Audit CSE with missing audit.csv. For the problematic GPO: 1) Backup the GPO, 2) Open the GPC object in AD, 3) Remove [{F3CCC681-B74C-4060-9F26-CD84525DCA2A}{0F3F3735-573D-9804-99E4-AB2A69BA5FD4}] from the gPCMachineExtensionNames attribute.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Group Policy/Workflow: GPO: Unknown Solution Investigation/Workflow: GPO: Advanced Audit Setting not applying

symptom: Group Policy Advanced Audit settings not applying. GPSVC log shows error 0x8007000d (ERROR_INVALID_DATA) when processing the Audit Policy CSE. Settings were previously working.
rootCause: One of the GPOs has an audit.csv file with invalid/corrupted data in the GPT folder. The Advanced Audit Policy CSE fails to parse the corrupted audit.csv, causing all audit policy processing to fail.
solution: Identify the GPO with the corrupted audit.csv by checking GPSVC debug log for the GPO GUID associated with the 0x8007000d error. Disable that particular GPO to restore audit settings processing. Optionally fix or recreate the audit settings in that GPO.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Group Policy/Workflow: GPO: Unknown Solution Investigation/Workflow: GPO: Advanced Audit Setting not applying

symptom: User Group Policy settings not applying on Windows 10/11 clients (or Windows 7/8.1 with MS16-072 installed). GPResult shows GPO denied due to security filtering even though user is member of the filtered group (e.g., Domain Users).
rootCause: MS16-072 security update changed user GPO retrieval to use computer security context instead of user context. If Authenticated Users ACE (with Read permission) was removed from the GPO security filtering and replaced with a user-only group, the computer cannot read the GPO.
solution: Re-add Authenticated Users group with Read (GpoRead) permission to affected GPOs. PowerShell: Get-GPO -All, check for missing Authenticated Users, then Set-GPPermissions -TargetName 'authenticated users' -TargetType group -PermissionLevel GpoRead.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Group Policy/Workflow: GPO: Unknown Solution Investigation/Workflow: GPO: GPO Settings for users not applying - Security Filtering (MS16-072 related)

symptom: Group Policy fails to apply with error during gpupdate. Event 1129 logged in Event Log. GPSVC log shows GetLdapHandle Failed to connect DC with 81. Network trace shows TCP SYN retransmits to DC on port 389.
rootCause: Network connectivity issue preventing the client from reaching the Domain Controller on LDAP port 389. The TCP handshake fails (SYN packets go unanswered), causing the GP client service to be unable to retrieve GPO settings from AD via LDAP.
solution: 1) Enable gpsvc debug logging and collect network trace. 2) Correlate LDAP connection failure with network trace showing blocked port. 3) Involve customer network team to unblock required ports: TCP/UDP 389 (LDAP), TCP 636 (LDAPS), TCP 3268/3269 (GC), TCP/UDP 445 (SMB), Dynamic ports.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Group Policy/Workflow: GPO: Unknown Solution Investigation/Workflow: GPO: GPO not applying - Network issues

symptom: Group Policy settings not applying to certain machines. GPResult shows GPO denied with reason False WMI Filter. GPO applies correctly on some OS versions but not others.
rootCause: A WMI filter is configured on the GPO that evaluates to false on the target machine OS version/architecture. WMI filters are evaluated during GPO processing and if they return false, the entire GPO is skipped. Common: WMI filter targeting specific OS blocks GPO on newer OS versions.
solution: 1) Check GPResult for False WMI Filter denial. 2) In GPSVC debug log, locate the GPO GUID and WMI filter evaluation result. 3) Review WMI filter query in GPMC and verify it matches target OS. 4) Update WMI filter to include desired OS versions or remove if unneeded. 5) Verify with gpupdate /force.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Group Policy/Workflow: GPO: Unknown Solution Investigation/Workflow: GPO: GPO settings not applying - Security or WMI Filtering

symptom: Windows LAPS Event ID 10001: Local Administrator Password DLL failed to initialize. SAMRSRV.dll unable to load LAPS.dll during boot.
rootCause: LAPS.dll cannot be loaded by LSASS.exe during boot due to file corruption, missing DLL, or blocked loading.
solution: Collect Procmon on boot to view LoadImage operation by LSASS.exe. Enable SAMSRV logging and collect data during reboot to troubleshoot.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Windows LAPS/Windows LAPS - LAPSv2/Windows LAPS - Storing Password - Active Directory/LAPS - Error Events

symptom: Windows LAPS Event ID 10006: Password encryption required but AD domain not at 2016 domain functional level. Password not updated.
rootCause: Windows LAPS encrypts managed account password by default, requiring Windows Server 2016 DFL. Domain is at a lower functional level.
solution: Verify domain functional level and raise to Windows Server 2016 or higher.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Windows LAPS/Windows LAPS - LAPSv2/Windows LAPS - Storing Password - Active Directory/LAPS - Error Events

symptom: Windows LAPS Event ID 10011: LAPS failed querying Active Directory for the current computer state.
rootCause: Windows LAPS periodically queries AD every hour for computer state. Network issues, AD replication problems, or DC unavailability cause failure.
solution: Check LAPS operational log (Event Viewer > Application and Services > Microsoft > Windows > LAPS > Operational). Run Get-LapsDiagnostics -CollectNetworkTrace -ResetPassword.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Windows LAPS/Windows LAPS - LAPSv2/Windows LAPS - Storing Password - Active Directory/LAPS - Error Events

symptom: Windows LAPS Event ID 10012: AD schema has not been updated with necessary LAPS attributes.
rootCause: AD schema lacks Windows LAPS attributes. Schema was never extended or AD replication has not converged to the target DC.
solution: Run Update-LapsADSchema. Check Event ID 10055 in LAPS log to find target DC. Verify AD replication convergence.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Windows LAPS/Windows LAPS - LAPSv2/Windows LAPS - Storing Password - Active Directory/LAPS - Error Events

symptom: Windows LAPS Event ID 10013: LAPS failed to find the configured local administrator account on the machine.
rootCause: Managed local admin account in GPO/Intune does not exist on machine, or trailing spaces in account name configuration.
solution: Open Local Users and Groups to verify account. Check for trailing spaces in GPO/MDM account name and in the actual account.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Windows LAPS/Windows LAPS - LAPSv2/Windows LAPS - Storing Password - Active Directory/LAPS - Error Events

symptom: Windows LAPS Event ID 10017: LAPS failed to update Active Directory with the new password. Current password not modified.
rootCause: Computer account lacks self-permission to write password attributes on its OU, or LDAP connectivity issues to DC.
solution: Verify self-permission with Set-LapsADComputerSelfPermissions -identity OU. Collect Get-LapsDiagnostics and Wldap32 ETL on client + AD ETL on DC.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Windows LAPS/Windows LAPS - LAPSv2/Windows LAPS - Storing Password - Active Directory/LAPS - Error Events

symptom: Windows LAPS Event ID 10019: LAPS failed to update local admin account with the new password.
rootCause: LAPS found the managed user but encountered error changing password. Local password policy or account constraints prevent the change.
solution: Translate error code from event. Test resetting another local account password. Create alternate managed account, reconfigure LAPS, reboot, then test original account.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Windows LAPS/Windows LAPS - LAPSv2/Windows LAPS - Storing Password - Active Directory/LAPS - Error Events

symptom: Windows LAPS Event ID 10025: Azure discovery failed. Machine cannot reach Azure AD endpoints for password backup.
rootCause: Azure AD/Hybrid joined machine cannot connect to enterpriseregistration.windows.net for LAPS password backup to Entra ID.
solution: Verify connectivity to Azure AD endpoints. Check firewall rules, proxy settings, and network connectivity.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Windows LAPS/Windows LAPS - LAPSv2/Windows LAPS - Storing Password - Active Directory/LAPS - Error Events

symptom: Windows LAPS Event ID 10027: Unable to create acceptable new password. Password length/complexity policy mismatch.
rootCause: LAPS password settings (Intune/GPO) conflict with machine local or domain password policy requirements.
solution: Run secpol.msc > Account Policies > Password Policy. Align LAPS password settings with machine password policy.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Windows LAPS/Windows LAPS - LAPSv2/Windows LAPS - Storing Password - Active Directory/LAPS - Error Events

symptom: Windows LAPS Event ID 10034: Configured encryption principal is an isolated/ambiguous name. Cannot manage account password.
rootCause: Authorized Password Decryptors in GPO/Intune uses ambiguous name instead of domain-qualified format (user@domain.com or domain\user).
solution: Update GPO/Intune Authorized Password Decryptors setting to use DomainName\Username format.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Windows LAPS/Windows LAPS - LAPSv2/Windows LAPS - Storing Password - Active Directory/LAPS - Error Events

symptom: Windows LAPS Event ID 10056: Failed to locate a writable domain controller for password update.
rootCause: Network issues, AD site misconfiguration, or all DCs unavailable/read-only prevent discovery of a writable DC.
solution: Run nltest /dsgetdc:yourdomain.com /force /writable. Troubleshoot DNS, network, or AD site configuration.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Windows LAPS/Windows LAPS - LAPSv2/Windows LAPS - Storing Password - Active Directory/LAPS - Error Events

symptom: Windows LAPS Event ID 10057: Unable to bind over LDAP to the domain controller for password operations.
rootCause: Machine account authentication to DC fails during LDAP bind. Computer account password, Kerberos, or network issues.
solution: Troubleshoot as machine auth issue using auth scripts. Verify Group Policy updates work. Check computer account password sync.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Windows LAPS/Windows LAPS - LAPSv2/Windows LAPS - Storing Password - Active Directory/LAPS - Error Events

symptom: Windows LAPS Event ID 10065: LDAP_INSUFFICIENT_RIGHTS when updating legacy LAPS password attribute in emulation mode.
rootCause: In legacy emulation mode, machine account lacks self-permissions on OU to write ms-Mcs-AdmPwd attribute.
solution: Run Update-AdmPwdComputerSelfPermission on the OU. Verify machine account self-permission.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Windows LAPS/Windows LAPS - LAPSv2/Windows LAPS - Storing Password - Active Directory/LAPS - Error Events

symptom: w32tm /resync fails with 'The computer did not resync because no time data was available', w32tm /query /source shows 'Local CMOS clock' or 'Free-running System Clock', Event ID 47 shows 'peer is unreachable'
rootCause: Network connectivity issue - firewall or network devices blocking NTP UDP port 123 traffic between client and NTP server. No NTP response visible in network capture.
solution: Perform side-to-side network capture on both NTP client and server to confirm packet delivery. Check TTL via ping to identify intermediate network devices. Involve customer network team to track packet path and identify blocking/dropping point. As workaround, test with a local NTP server (same-site DC or any Windows machine with NtpServer Enabled=1) to isolate network issue.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Windows Time/Workflow: Windows Time: Looking for known solutions/"No time data was available" resync error

symptom: w32tm /resync fails with 'no time data was available', network capture shows NTP packets received at network layer but w32time service cannot process them. w32time.log shows NTP samples received but no synchronization occurs.
rootCause: Third-party filter drivers (antivirus, security agents like CrowdStrike CSAgent) performing packet inspection, preventing NTP packets from reaching the application layer where w32time resides.
solution: Use 'fltmc' command to list installed filter drivers. Cross-reference with Microsoft Allocated Filter Altitudes documentation. Uninstall (not just disable) the suspected third-party filter driver, reboot machine, and retry synchronization. If resolved, provide summary email for customer to share with vendor. Note: disabling is insufficient - full uninstall and reboot required.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Windows Time/Workflow: Windows Time: Looking for known solutions/"No time data was available" resync error

symptom: w32tm /resync fails with 'no time data was available'. w32tm /query /configuration shows 'The service has not been started (0x80070426)' or NtpClient Enabled shows 0.
rootCause: Windows Time service (w32time) is stopped or the NtpClient time provider is disabled in registry (HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Services\W32Time\TimeProviders\NtpClient\Enabled = 0).
solution: Start the w32time service with 'net start w32time'. If NtpClient is disabled, set registry value Enabled to 1 at HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Services\W32Time\TimeProviders\NtpClient, restart w32time service, and retry resync.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Windows Time/Workflow: Windows Time: Looking for known solutions/"No time data was available" resync error

symptom: w32tm /resync fails with 'no time data was available' on client DC. w32time.log shows 'Packet test 8 failed (bad value for root delay or root dispersion)' and 'Ignoring packet that failed tests from [server]'. NTP server responded but response was rejected.
rootCause: NTP server (e.g., PDC) experienced an abrupt time change causing excessive time difference (>5000ms sustained for 900 seconds). NTP server stops providing reliable time to clients. System Event ID 50 may appear on NTP server indicating 'time difference greater than 5000 milliseconds'.
solution: Fix time issue on the NTP server first. Check NTP server for System Event ID 50. Use 'w32tm /monitor' to check offset between DCs. Once NTP server time is stabilized, client DCs will automatically recover synchronization.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Windows Time/Workflow: Windows Time: Looking for known solutions/"No time data was available" resync error

symptom: w32tm /resync fails with 'no time data was available'. w32time.log shows 'NtpProvider: Created 0 sockets' and 'NTP sample vector is empty'. No System events generated during reproduction.
rootCause: A third-party application (time sync utility or other software) is occupying UDP port 123, preventing the Windows Time service from binding to the port and creating listening sockets.
solution: Run 'Get-Process -Id (Get-NetUDPEndpoint -Localport 123).owningprocess' to identify the conflicting process. Verify with 'tasklist /svc | findstr W32Time' that PID differs. Stop/reconfigure the conflicting application to use a different port, then restart w32time service.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Windows Time/Workflow: Windows Time: Looking for known solutions/"No time data was available" resync error

symptom: w32tm /resync fails with 'no time data was available' on a machine that cannot find a good time source within its Active Directory site.
rootCause: Registry CrossSiteSyncFlags at HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Services\W32Time\TimeProviders\NtpClient is set to 0 (NCCSS_None), preventing Windows Time service from selecting a time source outside the machine's AD site. Default value is 2 (NCCSS_All).
solution: Change CrossSiteSyncFlags registry value to 2 (default, allows cross-site synchronization) at HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Services\W32Time\TimeProviders\NtpClient, restart w32time service.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Windows Time/Workflow: Windows Time: Looking for known solutions/"No time data was available" resync error

symptom: High root dispersion values (several seconds) on Windows Domain Controllers. Unix/hardware devices using chrony fail to synchronize with DCs reporting 'No suitable source for synchronisation'. Event ID 266 with Reason Code 2 (network topology changes) appears frequently in Time-Service Operational log.
rootCause: Frequent network interface changes (e.g., Teredo adapter add/delete operations generating thousands of events) cause Windows Time service to repeatedly rediscover time sources, resulting in stale time samples and accumulating root dispersion values.
solution: Disable Teredo interface via GPO: gpedit.msc > Computer Configuration > Administrative Templates > Network > TCPIP Settings > IPv6 Transition Technologies > Set Teredo State = Enabled/Disabled State. Run gpupdate /force. Clean up duplicated firewall rules with 'netsh advfirewall firewall delete rule name="Core Networking - Teredo (ICMPv6-In)"' and Out variant. For diagnosis, capture TSS NET_NETIO + ADS_W32Time and engage NET team.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Windows Time/Workflow: Windows Time: Looking for known solutions/High Root Dispersion values

symptom: Group Policy Preferences (GPP) drive mapping fails - drive not visible after GPO applied, GPP debug log shows 'The network path was not found' or 'The network connection does not exist', network trace shows 'Bad Network Name'
rootCause: Incorrect share path configured in GPP drive mapping (e.g., extra trailing space in the UNC path), causing network name resolution failure
solution: Enable GPP debug logging for Drive Maps via GPO. Check the User.log file in the trace folder for error messages. Analyze network trace for 'Bad Network Name' in Tree Connect frames. Verify and correct the share path in the GPP configuration, removing any extra spaces or typos.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Group Policy/Workflow: GPO: Unknown Solution Investigation/Workflow: GPO: GPP settings not applying

symptom: IE Site-to-Zone assignment GPO settings do not apply on first user login but work on second login or after gpupdate /force. GPResult shows GPO applied but IE Zonemapping extension not processed on first logon.
rootCause: The GPO contains deprecated Internet Explorer Maintenance CSE (Client-Side Extension) settings alongside GPP IE settings. The IE Maintenance CSE ({A2E30F80-D7DE-11d2-BBDE-00C04F86AE3B}) takes precedence and conflicts with GPP on first login.
solution: Remove the deprecated IE Maintenance CSE from the GPO: 1) Backup the GPO 2) Rename the SYSVOL Policies {GPO_GUID} User MICROSOFT IEAK folder 3) Edit the gPCUserExtensionNames attribute on the GPC object in AD to remove {A2E30F80-D7DE-11d2-BBDE-00C04F86AE3B} and {FC715823-C5FB-11D1-9EEF-00A0C90347FF}. Alternatively, recreate the GPO.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Group Policy/Workflow: GPO: Unknown Solution Investigation/Workflow: GPO: IE settings are missing

symptom: IE Group Policy Preferences setting reverted on first login on Windows 2008 R2 Terminal Servers. Setting applies correctly only from second login. Process Monitor shows ie4uinit.exe reverting the registry value after GPP sets it.
rootCause: ie4uinit.exe (IE Per-User Initialization Utility) resets IE settings for new user profiles during first login on Windows 7/2008R2. This is by-design behavior that overwrites GPP changes.
solution: 1) Replace IE desktop icon with script that runs reg add to re-apply the setting or triggers GPUpdate before launching IE. 2) Upgrade Terminal Server to Windows Server 2012R2 or newer where ie4uinit no longer resets these settings.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Group Policy/Workflow: GPO: Unknown Solution Investigation/Workflow: GPO: IE settings are missing

symptom: Azure Files SMB mount fails with Access Denied after Kerberos RC4 enforcement update (April 2026). Storage account configured via AzFilesHybrid version below v0.2.2. KDC refuses RC4 TGT/Service Ticket.
rootCause: Older Azure Files deployments were hard-coded to use RC4-HMAC for Kerberos authentication. After enforcement, KDC refuses RC4 tickets.
solution: Route to Azure Files/Storage team. Update the storage account encryption to AES-256 via Azure portal or PowerShell. Directory Services cannot fix this via AD configuration changes.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Kerberos/Kerberos RC4 Deprecation/Collaboration Scenarios

symptom: Linux/NAS/SAN file share authentication fails after Kerberos RC4 enforcement. Keytab generated with only RC4 encryption types. krb5.conf restricts to legacy cipher types.
rootCause: Keytab files on Linux/NAS/SAN were generated containing only RC4 encryption types, which are blocked after DC enforcement update.
solution: 1) Verify computer object msDS-SupportedEncryptionTypes includes AES (0x18/24 decimal) 2) Regenerate keytab on Linux/NAS to include AES keys 3) Upgrade Samba to 4.24+ which defaults to AES. Route to Networking team.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Kerberos/Kerberos RC4 Deprecation/Collaboration Scenarios

symptom: Application fails with KDC_ERR_ETYPE_NOSUPP (0x12) after Kerberos RC4 enforcement. Users see repeated credential prompts or sudden spike in NTLM fallback traffic.
rootCause: Client application only supports RC4 but KDC enforces AES-only. No common encryption type for session. Silent Kerberos failure causes NTLM fallback or infinite credential prompt loop.
solution: Identify the RC4-dependent client/application. Update to support AES encryption. If legacy app, contact vendor for update. Check KDCSVC Event 203/204 on DCs to confirm RC4 block. Avoid NTLM fallback as it introduces relay attack risk.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Kerberos/Kerberos RC4 Deprecation/Collaboration Scenarios

symptom: Windows Hello for Business (WHfB) or CloudExperienceHost not functioning correctly, provisioning fails, or enrollment issues on Windows device.
rootCause: CloudExperienceHost or TokenBroker application package is corrupted or not properly registered on the device.
solution: Re-register the packages via PowerShell (run as user context): Get-AppXPackage *CloudExperienceHost* | Foreach {Add-AppxPackage -DisableDevelopmentMode -Register $($_.InstallLocation)\AppXManifest.xml -ForceApplicationShutdown} and Get-AppxPackage *BrokerPlugin* | Foreach {Add-AppxPackage -DisableDevelopmentMode -Register $($_.InstallLocation)\AppXManifest.xml -ForceApplicationShutdown}
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Windows Hello and Modern Credential Providers/WHfB/WHFB: Tips & Hints/Re-register package

symptom: Runas command fails with error 1326 'The user name or password is incorrect' when running as on-premises AD account (domain\user) on Azure AD Joined (AADJ) device.
rootCause: AADJ device has no password credential provider for on-premises domain format. The runas command with domain\user format is not supported on cloud-only AADJ devices.
solution: Use AzureAD\ prefix instead: runas /profile /user:AzureAD\<upn> cmd or runas /profile /user:AzureAD\<email> cmd. For right-click Run As, use AzureAD\<upn> format.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Windows Hello and Modern Credential Providers/WHfB/WHFB: Tips & Hints/Runas on AADJ using AD account error 1326

symptom: Windows Hello prompts/toast notifications appear repeatedly on Windows 10 2004 devices, asking user to set up Windows Hello Face or fingerprint.
rootCause: Known bug in face credential provider (facecredprov) in Windows 10 2004 OS. The CreateEnrollmentPromptCount logic has a bug that causes repeated prompts.
solution: Workaround 1 (recommended): Disable biometric authentication via UseBiometrics policy in Passport for Work CSP. Workaround 2: Set registry HKLM\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Authentication\LogonUI\FaceLogon CreateEnrollmentPromptCount=3. Or disable Hello notification via HKCU\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Notifications\Settings\Windows.SystemToast.HelloFace Enabled=0.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Windows Hello and Modern Credential Providers/WHfB/WHFB: Tips & Hints/Toast notification for provisionning

symptom: WHfB SmartCard emulation (virtual smart card) persists after setting DisableSmartCardNode=1 and resetting PIN. certutil -scinfo still shows VSC present.
rootCause: The WHfB virtual smart card (vSC) is provisioned/deprovisioned only when the WHfB container is created or destroyed. Non-destructive PIN reset does not destroy the container, so existing credentials retain SmartCard emulation.
solution: 1) Set DisableSmartCardNode=1 2) Run certutil -deletehellocontainer 3) Reboot device 4) Log in with username/password 5) Re-enroll WHfB 6) Force AAD Connect sync (delta) and AD replication 7) Verify with certutil -scinfo that VSC is no longer present.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Windows Hello and Modern Credential Providers/WHfB/WHFB: Tips & Hints/Turn off SmartCard emulation

symptom: Customer requires RSA key sizes larger than 2048 bits or ECC keys for Windows Hello for Business to meet BSI/compliance requirements. TPM KSP fails to enroll ECC certificate with error 0x80090027 NTE_INVALID_PARAMETER.
rootCause: Windows Hello for Business does not support ECC keys or RSA key sizes larger than 2048 bits. Microsoft Platform Crypto Provider (TPM KSP) returns incorrect values for ECC keys, causing enrollment failure.
solution: WHfB is limited to RSA 2048 for certificate trust deployment. For ECC certificate needs outside WHfB, use Microsoft Software Key Storage Provider as workaround. Add customer to UAT feedback item (Feedback 466065) requesting larger key size support.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Windows Hello and Modern Credential Providers/WHfB/WHFB: Tips & Hints/WHFB Support for key length larger than 2048 bit

symptom: WHfB PIN history policy not enforced - users can reuse previous PINs on devices with Enhanced Security Settings (ESS) or VBS enabled. Applies to Windows 11 24H2+ with VBS.
rootCause: PIN history is not supported on devices with ESS enabled because WHfB uses Virtualization-based Security (VBS) to isolate credentials, preventing PIN history tracking. Starting with Windows 11 24H2, this applies to all VBS-enabled devices.
solution: This is a known platform limitation. PIN history cannot be enforced on ESS/VBS-enabled devices. No workaround available.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Windows Hello and Modern Credential Providers/WHfB/WHFB: Tips & Hints/WHFB support of Pin History with ESS

symptom: WHfB provisioning logs Event ID 7201 (primary account PRT prerequisite check failed) or Event ID 7211 (secondary account PRT prerequisite check failed).
rootCause: The device cannot find a Primary Refresh Token (PRT) for the required account. Event 7201: AAD PRT missing (hybrid) or ADFS PRT missing (on-prem). Event 7211: secondary account PRT unavailable.
solution: For hybrid deployments: verify AAD PRT for primary account and ADFS PRT for secondary account. For on-prem: verify ADFS PRT is present. Check device registration status and federation configuration.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Windows Hello and Modern Credential Providers/WHfB/WHFB: Tips & Hints/What is the purposes of events id 7201 | 7211

symptom: AADJ device fails to access corporate website requiring NTLM authentication. WHfB security popup appears, user enters PIN/face auth, but access fails without further credential prompt. Error 0xc000005e STATUS_NO_LOGON_SERVERS.
rootCause: On AADJ device with key trust, Kerberos cannot determine on-prem domain name from credentials. DC locator fails using WORKGROUP name, returns STATUS_NO_LOGON_SERVERS. NTLM fallback fails because logon session lacks NTOWF secret. WININET does not re-prompt.
solution: Configure KDC Proxy to give AADJ clients access to on-prem DC without direct line of sight: 1) Set up Kerberos KDC Proxy Service on domain-joined server 2) Install RDS Gateway role 3) Configure IIS cert binding 4) Configure client GPO Specify KDC proxy servers under Computer Config System Kerberos.
sourceRef: Supportability/WindowsDirectoryServices/WindowsDirectoryServices:/Windows Hello and Modern Credential Providers/WHfB/WHFB: Tips & Hints/Whfb and "kdc proxy" feature
